FireGameEngine Guide
🔥 Line-by-line engine reference

The FireGame Engine

A hand-built 2D Donkey Kong-style platformer engine — a singleton core wiring Three.js rendering, a Rapier2D physics world, and a Vue presentation shell, all glued together by an event bus and a contact-table collision system. This document walks every file, every function, and every hard-won line so you can open any source file and know exactly what it does and how to change it.

TypeScript · strict Three.js r162 · WebGL Rapier2D · @dimforge compat Vue 3 · UI shell mitt · event bus dat.gui + stats.js · debug Vite · bundler

The one idea: a clean engine / game seam

Everything in this codebase serves a single architectural conviction: the engine layer (src/engine/) is a reusable platformer engine that never names a single Donkey-Kong concept, and the game layer (src/game/) supplies all the rules, entities, feel and content, wiring itself into the engine from the outside. The whole guide is organised around that seam.

🧩Singleton core

Experience owns the canvas, scene, camera, renderer, physics, input, resources and debug — created once via GetInstance()/Configure().

📡Event bus

A typed mitt emitter decouples subsystems. The engine emits tick/resize; the game speaks gameStart, gameOver, switchLevel, loading events and more.

💥Contact table

Physics contacts are routed through a generic (typeA,typeB) → handler registry. The game registers concrete rules; the engine never hard-codes a reaction.

🔁Finite-state machines

A tiny generic StateMachine drives the player (idle/run/jump/fall/climb) and the barrels (roll/descend/bounce) through pure handler functions.

🏗️GLB-driven levels

Levels are modelled in Shapr3D/Blender, exported to GLB; a parser turns folder names + glTF extras into typed LevelData the factories spawn.

🎚️Config-driven game

Entity factories, contact rules, the level registry, input bindings and "feel" profiles live in src/game/config & attributes — the control panel.

How to read this guide

Start with Architecture for the map and the diagrams. Want the editorial take first? Jump to What's Impressive and What Could Be Better — an honest, evidence-backed assessment of the engine's wins and its roadmap. Then dive into the Engine Layer and Game Layer sections — each documents its files function-by-function with real, quoted source. Finish in Recipes & Reference for step-by-step "how to add X" guides, the full event catalog, and the decision-log decoder.

The decision-log codes. Throughout the source you'll see comments like (C5), (D3), (D28). These reference the author's private decision log — each marks a deliberate, hard-won choice (e.g. C5 = the camera consumes a plain snapshot, never the game Player; D3 = the engine never names a game type, the game injects its palette). This guide surfaces and decodes every one — see the decision log.

Callout legend

🏆 Hard-won victory — a clever fix or a decision-code rationale.
⚠️ Gotcha — an ordering dependency or footgun.
⛔ Warning — something that will bite if ignored.
📝 Note — context worth knowing.
💡 Tip — a practical pointer.
Rendering this file. Diagrams (Mermaid) and code highlighting (Prism) load from a CDN, so view this guide with an internet connection for the full experience. The content is fully readable without it.

The Big Picture: engine vs game, the singleton, the wiring

FireGame is split into two layers that never blur together: a reusable engine/ that knows nothing about Donkey Kong, and a game/ that supplies the rules. A single Experience singleton owns the one canvas, scene, and physics world; a global mitt event bus decouples everything; and the whole machine is wired together from App.vue in one onMounted pass. This section is the map you read before any other.

src/engine/core/experience.ts src/main.ts src/App.vue src/game/gameDirector.ts

Orientation: the two-layer architecture

Before any one file, hold the shape of the whole thing in your head. There are two layers and a strict import rule between them:

  • engine/ — reusable, game-agnostic. The renderer, the Rapier physics world, the camera, the resource/GLB loader, the input device reader, the finite-state-machine base, the contact registry, the debug coordinator. It never imports from game/. When the engine needs something game-specific it exposes a seam — an interface, a registry, a late-bound hook — and lets the game plug in.
  • game/ — the Donkey Kong rules. The GameDirector, the player/enemy entities and their FSM states, the level registry, the contact rules, the feel profiles, the F-key bindings. It imports freely from engine/ and fills every seam the engine left open.
Note. The leak that became a seam. The engine's GLB parser (resourceLoader.ts) is type-blind: it no longer carries a hand-maintained table mirroring the game's EntityType strings. Instead the game injects its level-node vocabulary — LEVEL_NODE_TYPES, a name-token → game-type map whose values come straight from EntityType so the two cannot drift — via Resources.RegisterLevelTypes() at boot. The engine still never imports the game enum; it just looks each node up in whatever map the game handed it. What used to be the one place the engine named something game-shaped is now a proper seam like every other.

How the layers own each other — every arrow points "down" into the engine, never up:

flowchart TD
  subgraph shell["Vue shell (presentation)"]
    main["main.ts"]
    app["App.vue (onMounted wiring)"]
  end

  subgraph game["game/ (Donkey Kong rules)"]
    director["GameDirector"]
    rules["contactRules / inputBindings"]
    cfg["levelRegistry / feel profiles / entityFactories"]
    ent["Player / Enemy / sensors / platforms"]
  end

  subgraph engine["engine/ (reusable, game-agnostic)"]
    exp["Experience (singleton)"]
    subsys["Scene / Camera / Renderer / Physics / Resources / Input / Debug / Time / Sizes"]
    bus["Emitter (mitt event bus)"]
    reg["ContactRegistry seam"]
  end

  main --> app
  app --> exp
  app --> director
  app --> rules
  director --> exp
  director --> ent
  rules --> reg
  exp --> subsys
  game -. "decoupled via" .-> bus
  engine -. "decoupled via" .-> bus
  engine -. "never imports" .-x game
Tip. The litmus test. If you ever find yourself wanting to import something from game/ inside an engine/ file, stop: the correct move is to add a seam (a registry entry, an interface the game implements, or a late-bound callback) and let App.vue wire the game side in.

src/engine/core/experience.ts 134 LOC

Experience is the engine's root object: the one place that owns the canvas, the THREE.Scene, the Rapier world, and every engine subsystem. It is a singleton — a private constructor plus a static GetInstance() — so that anyone, anywhere (the camera, the physics world, the game director) can reach the same scene/world without it being threaded through every constructor. Construction is split in two: GetInstance() hands back the bare object; the real wiring lives in an async Configure() because the physics world needs await RAPIER.init(). It imports only engine modules — never anything from game/.

private static instance: Experience; private constructor() {}

The singleton mechanism. The constructor is private, so new Experience() is a compile error everywhere outside this file. The only way to obtain the instance is the static accessor below. 

// Class prop instance and "new" blocking constructor for Singleton
private static instance: Experience;
private constructor() {}

public static GetInstance(): Experience

Lazily creates the instance on first call and returns the same one forever after. This is what App.vue calls first, and what every engine/game module calls to reach the shared scene/world. 

public static GetInstance(): Experience {
  if (!Experience.instance) {
    Experience.instance = new Experience();
  }
  return Experience.instance;
}
Why a singleton. There is exactly one canvas, one WebGL renderer, one THREE.Scene, and one Rapier World. A singleton makes that physically true: Physics and Camera both call GetInstance() and necessarily get the same scene to bind into. The alternative — passing the scene/world through every constructor — invites two worlds drifting out of sync.

The subsystems Experience owns

All fields are declared with the definite-assignment ! because they are null until Configure() runs. Every one of these is documented in its own section; here is the ownership roster:

FieldTypeOwns / doesDetailed in
DebugDebugdat.GUI panel + Stats.js; registry for game debug modules, log sink, type colorsThe Debug Coordinator
SizesSizesWindow size; emits resizeCore: Sizes & Time
TimeTimerAF loop; clamped delta; emits tickCore: Sizes & Time
InputInputKeyboard device state (held booleans)Input
ResourcesResourceLoaderTexture/GLB batch loader + GLB level parserResources & the GLB Pipeline
TargetElementHTMLCanvasElement | nullThe DOM canvas from the Vue refthis section
SceneTHREE.SceneThe single render scene everything binds toRendering
CameraCameraOrthographic/perspective follow cameraCamera
RendererRendererWebGL renderer bound to the canvasRendering
PhysicsPhysicsRapier2D world, event queue, Contacts registryPhysics & the Contact Table
OnGameUpdate(() => void) | nullThe one late-bound seam the engine calls into the game each framethis section

public OnGameUpdate: (() => void) | null = null;

This is the single most important field in the file and the thesis of the whole architecture. The engine's per-frame loop needs to run the game's logic, but the engine core must not name a game class. So instead of import GameDirector, the engine exposes a nullable callback that the game assigns from outside. The engine calls it; it never knows what is on the other end. 

// Late-bound game update hook. The game layer assigns this; the engine just
// calls it each frame between physics and renderer. Keeps the engine core
// from naming any game class.
public OnGameUpdate: (() => void) | null = null;

It is assigned in App.vue to a closure that drives the game's input bindings and the GameDirector, and it is invoked with optional-chaining (this.OnGameUpdate?.()) inside the tick handler so that an unconfigured engine simply renders nothing-game and does not crash.

public async Configure(canvas: HTMLCanvasElement | null, resources: Resource[])

The real constructor. Instantiates every subsystem in a deliberate order, captures the canvas, builds the scene, and subscribes the two engine-level event handlers (resize, tick). It is async purely because Physics.Configure() awaits RAPIER.init() (the WASM module). Parameters: canvas — the DOM canvas element from the Vue template ref, or null if not yet mounted; resources — the game's asset manifest, passed straight to the ResourceLoader so the engine names none of the game's assets. Returns: a promise that resolves once physics is ready. 

public async Configure(
  canvas: HTMLCanvasElement | null,
  resources: Resource[]
) {
  this.Debug = new Debug();
  this.Sizes = new Sizes();
  this.Time = new Time();
  this.Input = new Input();
  this.Resources = new ResourceLoader(resources);

  this.TargetElement = canvas;

  this.Scene = new THREE.Scene();
  this.Camera = new Camera(new THREE.Vector3(0, 25, 100));
  this.Renderer = new Renderer();
  this.Physics = new Physics();
  await this.Physics.Configure();

Line-level notes:

  • Debug first. It reads import.meta.env.DEV / the #debug hash to set IsActive; later subsystems (notably Physics.Configure) check Debug.IsActive to decide whether to attach wireframes, so Debug must exist before them.
  • The ResourceLoader is handed the game's manifest, not a hardcoded one. The engine no longer names randy/enemy/dkGraphicsData anywhere — those { name, type, path } entries live in the game's assetManifest.ts (GAME_ASSETS), where the paths are Vite ?url imports resolved to bundled, content-hashed URLs. getAssetPath now simply returns source.path; a different game supplies its own manifest and reuses the engine unchanged (reusability mile, #5).
  • The camera's start position (0, 25, 100) is the only camera-placement value baked into the engine; per-level camera starts are applied later by the GameDirector.
  • Physics is constructed then awaited. The two-step new Physics() then await ...Configure() mirrors this very class's own pattern, again because of the async RAPIER.init().

Configure then registers the two engine-level event listeners and stores the handler refs for cleanup:

this.onResize = () => {
  this.Camera.Resize();
  this.Renderer.Resize();
};
Emitter.on("resize", this.onResize);

this.onTick = () => {
  if (this.Debug.IsActive) {
    this.Debug.Stats?.begin();
  }

  this.Physics.Update();
  this.OnGameUpdate?.();
  this.Renderer.Update();

  if (this.Debug.IsActive) {
    this.Debug.Stats?.end();
  }
};
Emitter.on("tick", this.onTick);
Hard-won victory. The frame order is the whole game in one line. Physics.Update() → OnGameUpdate?.() → Renderer.Update(). Physics steps the world first, then the game reads this frame's freshly-solved positions to update entities and camera, then the renderer draws. The Stats.begin()/end() bracket wraps the whole frame so the FPS/MS monitor measures real work. The handler refs (this.onResize, this.onTick) are stored as fields specifically so Destroy() can Emitter.off the exact same function instances — an inline arrow could never be removed.
Gotcha. The engine subscribes to tick, but it does not produce it. Time owns the requestAnimationFrame loop and emits tick; Sizes owns the window listener and emits resize. Experience is a subscriber. That is the event bus doing its job: the clock does not know who listens, and the loop does not know who clocks it.

public Destroy()

Symmetric teardown. Removes its own two listeners first (using the stored refs), then tells each subsystem to clean up in a sane order, and finally tears down the debug panel only if it was active. 

public Destroy() {
  Emitter.off("resize", this.onResize);
  Emitter.off("tick", this.onTick);

  this.Sizes.Destroy();
  this.Time.Destroy();
  this.Input.Destroy();

  this.Camera.Destroy();
  this.Physics.Destroy();
  this.Renderer.Destroy();
  this.Resources.Destroy();

  if (this.Debug && this.Debug.IsActive) {
    this.Debug.Destroy();
  }
}

Note the order: stop the clock and input first (so no more frames fire mid-teardown), then the render/physics/resource chain. The Debug.IsActive guard avoids destroying a panel that was never built in a production (non-debug) build.

Modifying this: to add a new engine subsystem, declare a public X!: X; field, instantiate it in Configure() (mind the ordering — anything that reads Debug.IsActive or Scene must come after them), tick it inside the onTick handler at the right point in the physics→game→render order, and tear it down in Destroy(). Resist the urge to import anything game-specific here — if the new subsystem needs game knowledge, give it a registry/interface seam instead and let App.vue feed it.

src/main.ts 5 LOC

The literal entry point named in index.html. It does exactly one thing: mount the Vue app. It is intentionally trivial — none of the engine wiring lives here, because the engine needs a real, mounted <canvas> element to attach to, and that only exists after Vue has rendered. So bootstrap is deferred to App.vue's onMounted. 

import { createApp } from 'vue'
import './style.css'
import App from './App.vue'

createApp(App).mount('#app')
Note. The order matters even here: importing ./style.css before mounting ensures the global stylesheet (and the canvas's full-bleed layout) is present when the component renders. The #app selector is the mount root in index.html.

Modifying this: almost never. If you wanted to install a Vue plugin (router, store) it would go here as createApp(App).use(plugin).mount('#app') — but the game's own lifecycle does not belong here; it belongs in App.vue.

src/App.vue 177 LOC

The wiring harness and the presentation shell. This Vue 3 <script setup> component is the only place the two layers are stitched together. Its <template> owns the loading overlay, the status text, and the reset button; its onMounted hook runs the entire bootstrap sequence. Everything game-specific that the engine needs — the asset manifest, the level-node type vocabulary, the contact rules, the collision-log sink, the debug color palette, the GameDirector, the F-key bindings, the per-frame game hook — is injected here (the manifest into Configure itself; the rest right after). The Vue layer communicates with the game purely through the event bus; it never reaches into game internals.

Reactive state (the presentation layer)

refDrives
webglRefThe <canvas> handed to Experience.Configure
statusRefThe "Game Win!" / "Game Over!" status text
isResetButtonVisibleShows/hides the Reset button
isLoadingShows/hides the full-screen loading overlay (starts true)
loadProgress{ loaded, total, item } for the real progress bar during the initial multi-asset load; null for single-GLB level loads (falls back to a plain spinner)

onMounted(async () => { ... }) — the bootstrap sequence

This is the spine of the whole application. The ordering inside it is not incidental; almost every line is placed to avoid a specific race. Walk it top to bottom.

1. Loading-screen listeners FIRST — before Configure.

Emitter.on("loadingStarted", () => {
  isLoading.value = true;
  loadProgress.value = null;
});
Emitter.on("loadingFinished", () => {
  isLoading.value = false;
});
Emitter.on("loadingProgress", (progress) => {
  loadProgress.value = progress;
});
Gotcha. Subscribe before you can be notified. These go up first so a very fast boot load cannot fire loadingFinished before anyone is listening — mitt does not buffer or replay events, so a notification with zero listeners is simply lost forever. This is the same hazard that governs StartLoading() being called last (below).

2. Get the singleton, configure the engine, and inject the level vocabulary.

const webglExperience = Experience.GetInstance();
// The game supplies its own asset manifest; the engine names none of these.
await webglExperience.Configure(webglRef.value, GAME_ASSETS);

// Inject the game's level-node type vocabulary into the engine GLB parser.
webglExperience.Resources.RegisterLevelTypes(LEVEL_NODE_TYPES);

webglRef.value is the now-mounted canvas; GAME_ASSETS is the game's boot manifest (assetManifest.ts), so the engine names none of its own assets. The await matters: nothing below may touch webglExperience.Physics until Configure has finished RAPIER.init(). The first thing it does afterward is hand the engine's GLB parser the game's level-node vocabulary via RegisterLevelTypes(LEVEL_NODE_TYPES) — the parser stays type-blind and the game's EntityType strings live in one place. It must run before any level loads, i.e. before StartLoading (below) triggers the first one.

3. Register the game's contact rules into the engine's contact registry.

registerContactRules(webglExperience.Physics.Contacts);

contactRules.ts declares the cross-entity table — Enemy→Player = gameOver, Enemy→TrashCan = destroy + ignite, Player→Teleporter/CameraSensor/WinFlag = the matching reaction. The engine's Physics.handleCollisionEvents dispatches every physics contact into this registry; it never names a game concept. This is the "no hard-coded if/else" collision routing the engine is built around (detailed in Physics & the Contact Table).

4. Register the collision-log sink and the debug color palette.

webglExperience.Debug.RegisterCollisionLogSink(new CollisionLogger());
webglExperience.Debug.SetPhysicsTypeColors(DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS);
Hard-won victory (D3). The engine never names a game type. The debug wireframe palette maps an entity-type string → a THREE.Color. Rather than baking that map into PhysicsDebug (which would force the engine to enumerate game types), the game injects the map via SetPhysicsTypeColors, and PhysicsDebug looks each collider's type up in it opaquely. Same idea for the log sink: CollisionLogger owns the game-specific formatting; the engine's Debug just forwards events to whatever sink implements the CollisionLogSink interface. Both calls are safe even when debug is inactive — they only store; PhysicsDebug reads them solely while rendering, which only happens when active.

5. Construct the game director and the F-key bindings.

const gameDirector = new GameDirector();
const inputBindings = new InputBindings(webglExperience.Input);

webglExperience.Debug.RegisterModule(new LevelDebug(gameDirector));

GameDirector is constructed after Configure so that the scene/camera/resources it pulls from the singleton already exist. InputBindings is handed the engine's Input device reader and converts F-key press edges into game events (F1→gameReset, F2→switchLevel, F3→manualCameraControl). RegisterModule with a LevelDebug wires a dat.gui level-select dropdown — but it is a no-op store when debug is inactive, so production builds pay nothing.

6. Install the per-frame game hook — the one seam.

webglExperience.OnGameUpdate = () => {
  inputBindings.Update();
  gameDirector.Update();
};
This single assignment is the engine↔game seam. The engine's tick handler calls this.OnGameUpdate?.() between physics and render. By assigning a closure here, App.vue gives the engine a way to run game logic every frame without the engine ever importing GameDirector or InputBindings. Input bindings run first (so an F-key press is observed before the director acts on the resulting event this frame).

7. Game-flow listeners that drive the UI shell.

Emitter.on("gameWin", () => { /* status = "Game Win!"; show Reset */ });
Emitter.on("gameOver", () => { /* status = "Game Over!"; show Reset */ });
Emitter.on("gameReset", () => { /* clear status; hide Reset */ });
Emitter.on("gameStart", () => { /* clear status; hide Reset */ });

These are pure presentation reactions. Note both the GameDirector and App.vue listen to gameStart / gameOver / etc.: the director changes simulation state (pause physics, stop spawning) while App.vue changes screen state (status text, reset button). Neither calls the other; they meet only at the event name. The gameStart listener clears stale win/over UI so a freshly-loaded level never shows a leftover "Game Over!".

8. Kick off asset loading LAST.

webglExperience.Resources.StartLoading();
Hard-won victory. Load last, after every listener is wired. StartLoading() is deliberately not called inside ResourceLoader's constructor. If every asset happened to resolve during the await window in Configure (e.g. assets cached), the resourcesReady event would fire into zero listeners — and the GameDirector's boot handler (which loads the default level) subscribes to exactly that event. mitt does not replay, so the boot would hang on the loading screen forever. Loading last guarantees the listener exists before the completion event can fire.

The full boot sequence, end to end

sequenceDiagram
  participant Main as main.ts
  participant Vue as App.vue (onMounted)
  participant Exp as Experience
  participant Bus as Emitter
  participant Res as ResourceLoader
  participant Dir as GameDirector

  Main->>Vue: createApp(App).mount('#app')
  Note over Vue: register loadingStarted/Finished/Progress listeners
  Vue->>Exp: GetInstance()
  Vue->>Exp: await Configure(canvas, GAME_ASSETS)
  Note over Exp: build Debug/Sizes/Time/Input/Resources<br/>Scene/Camera/Renderer/Physics (await RAPIER.init)<br/>subscribe resize + tick
  Vue->>Res: RegisterLevelTypes(LEVEL_NODE_TYPES)
  Vue->>Exp: registerContactRules(Physics.Contacts)
  Vue->>Exp: Debug.RegisterCollisionLogSink / SetPhysicsTypeColors
  Vue->>Dir: new GameDirector()
  Note over Dir: subscribes resourcesReady, gameStart, gameOver, ...
  Vue->>Exp: OnGameUpdate = () => { inputBindings.Update(); director.Update() }
  Vue->>Res: StartLoading()  (LAST)
  Res->>Bus: loadingProgress (per asset)
  Res->>Bus: resourcesReady (all loaded/failed)
  Bus->>Dir: resourcesReady -> loadLevelByIndex(DEFAULT)
  Note over Dir: parse GLB, spawn entities, apply feel + camera
  Dir->>Bus: gameStart
  Bus->>Dir: gameStart -> unpause physics, enable spawning
  Bus->>Vue: gameStart -> clear status, hide Reset

The template

The markup is small: a status line and Reset button (top-left), the WebGL <canvas>, and a full-screen loading overlay shown v-if="isLoading". When loadProgress is set (initial multi-asset load) it renders a real loaded / total bar driven by barWidth(); otherwise it shows a pulsing "Loading…" for single-GLB level swaps. The Reset button's handleButtonClicked simply emits gameReset — it does not touch the game directly; the director and the shell both react to the event.

Modifying this: this is where new game→engine wiring goes. To add a new contact rule, edit contactRules.ts (it is already registered here). To add a new debug module, construct it and pass it to Debug.RegisterModule alongside LevelDebug. To add a new per-frame game system, fold it into the OnGameUpdate closure. The two inviolable rules: keep StartLoading() the last line, and register any listener for a boot/completion event before the thing that emits it.

src/game/gameDirector.ts 546 LOC

The game layer's central controller — the counterpart to Experience on the game/ side. It owns the player and all level entities, drives the level lifecycle (load / unload / switch), runs the frame-driven enemy spawner, and translates game-flow events into simulation state. It reaches the engine the canonical way — Experience.GetInstance() in its constructor, then caches Scene, Time, Camera, Resources — and it is driven by the engine through the OnGameUpdate seam. This section maps its role and seams; the line-by-line bodies (spawning cadence, GLB load path, camera mapping) are covered in the dedicated GameDirector and entity sections.

constructor()

Pulls the engine singleton and caches the subsystems it needs, creates the level's GraphicsObject overlay holder, then wires every game-flow listener via setupEventHandlers(). 

this.experience = Experience.GetInstance();
this.scene = this.experience.Scene;
this.time = this.experience.Time;
this.camera = this.experience.Camera;
this.resources = this.experience.Resources;
this.GraphicsObject = new GameObject();

this.setupEventHandlers();

Because App.vue constructs the director after Configure, every one of these GetInstance() reads returns a fully-built subsystem. Construct it earlier and you would cache undefined.

private setupEventHandlers()

The director's entire reaction surface lives here — it is a pure event consumer. The events it listens to:

EventReaction
resourcesReadyBoot: await loadLevelByIndex(DEFAULT_LEVEL_INDEX) (which emits gameStart on success)
gameStartPhysics.SetPaused(false); enable enemy spawning
gameOverPhysics.SetPaused(true); stop spawning
gameWinFreeze identically to game over (so a stray barrel can't flip "Win" to "Over")
gameResetUnpause; resetGameState()
switchLevelswitchToNextLevel() (F2 cycle)
gameObjectRemovedremovedGameObject.Destroy()

It also starts a 5-second setInterval that prunes destroyed enemies from the Enemies array (kept separate from the frame loop so it keeps running after game over).

Note. The boot handler is why StartLoading() must run last. This resourcesReady subscription is the listener App.vue is protecting when it defers asset loading to the final line — it is the chain resourcesReady → load default level → gameStart.

public Update() — the body of the OnGameUpdate hook

Called every frame by the engine (through the closure App.vue installed on OnGameUpdate). The ordering inside is deliberate: bail while a level is loading, guard that the player exists, then player → camera → spawn → enemies → level entities → ladder state. 

if (this.isLoading) return;
if (!this.Player?.PhysicsBody) return;

this.Player.Update();
this.camera.Update(this.cameraTargetFromPlayer(this.Player));
this.trySpawn();

const firstTrashCan = this.TrashCans[0];
this.Enemies.forEach((enemy) => enemy.Update(this.Player, firstTrashCan));
Hard-won victory (C5). The camera is engine-pure. The director never hands the camera a game Player; it maps the player into a plain CameraTarget snapshot (x, y, velocityX, followState, isGrounded) via cameraTargetFromPlayer. That is why camera.ts imports nothing from game/ — it consumes scalars, not a game type. Player is updated before the camera so the follow reads this frame's position (the reverse order left the camera one frame stale).

private async loadLevelByIndex(index: number)

The single funnel for every level load — boot, the F2 cycle, and the dat.gui select all pass through here. It brackets the load with loadingStarted / loadingFinished (so the Vue overlay covers it), tears down the current level, parses the GLB, applies the registry entry's feel profile and camera-follow mode, and on success emits gameStart.

Hard-won victory (D5). One boolean, old-school. Loads are async and callers are fire-and-forget, so an isLoading guard serializes overlapping loads (F2 mashing / selecting mid-load). Without it a second load would interleave teardown and respawn → doubled entities, a leaked ambient light, a mismatched index. The same flag freezes Update() while a level rebuilds.
Gotcha. A failed load must not reject out of here. The whole body is wrapped in try/catch/finally: a missing/corrupt GLB is logged loud but swallowed, because rejecting would skip the boot handler's gameStart (leaving physics paused) and surface as an unhandled promise rejection. The finally always closes the loading overlay, so the screen never hangs. And gameStart is emitted only on the success path, so a failed load never wrongly unpauses.

private trySpawn() / SpawnEnemy() / SpawnCrazyEnemy()

Frame-driven enemy spawning, advanced off Time.Delta rather than a wall-clock setInterval (one clock, and a fresh boot spawns without needing a reset).

Hard-won victory (D28). One unified barrel array. Normal and "crazy" barrels are both Enemy now, distinguished only by their starting FSM state — the old separate CrazyEnemy class was folded in. The cadence counters are advanced before the async spawn so a stutter frame can't double-spawn.

The spawning cadence, FSM states, and oil-can targeting are detailed in the Enemy section.

public Destroy()

Unsubscribes every game-flow listener (Emitter.off by name), clears the cleanup interval, and unloads the current level. The mirror image of setupEventHandlers().

Modifying this: add a new game-flow reaction by subscribing in setupEventHandlers() and unsubscribing in Destroy() — keep them paired. Add a new level by editing the levelRegistry, not this file. Add a new level-placed entity type by giving it an entityFactory and a typed query array, not a new branch here — the spawn pass is data-driven over ENTITY_FACTORIES. Never call Physics.SetPaused or emit flow events from elsewhere if you can route it through these handlers; one funnel keeps the state machine honest.

Threads to follow next

From this overview, the natural next reads, by name:

  • The Event Bus — eventBus.ts: the typed mitt emitter, split into engine-level and game-level event maps, that decouples every line above.
  • Core: Sizes & Time — who actually emits resize and tick, and the clamped delta the physics step depends on.
  • Physics & the Contact Table — physics.ts + contactRegistry.ts: how handleCollisionEvents dispatches into the rules App.vue registered.
  • Resources & the GLB Pipeline — resourceLoader.ts: StartLoading, resourcesReady, and the GLB→LevelData parser.
  • The Debug Coordinator — debug.ts: the DebugModule / CollisionLogSink seams and the injected type-color palette (D3).
  • GameDirector (in depth) and the Player/Enemy FSM sections for the bodies deferred here.

The Frame Loop & Lifecycle

Everything that moves in FireGame is driven by a single heartbeat: Time calls requestAnimationFrame and emits a "tick" event; Experience.onTick turns that one event into a fixed-timestep accumulator that advances the simulation in constant 1/60 slices, then computes an interpolation alpha and runs a single per-frame render pass that smooths visuals between the last two sim states and renders once; and the game layer plugs its work in through two late-bound hooks (OnGameUpdate per sim-step, OnRenderUpdate per frame). This section traces that pass top to bottom — who drives it, the exact ordering and why it is exactly that order, how delta-time and resize are wired, and how the whole thing tears itself down symmetrically on Destroy.

src/engine/core/time.ts src/engine/core/sizes.ts src/engine/core/experience.ts src/game/gameDirector.ts
Note. The four files here answer three questions: who ticks (Time), what order the tick runs in (Experience), and what the game does inside its slice of the tick (GameDirector). Sizes is the parallel story for the resize signal. The decoupling seam between engine and game is two nullable function pointers: Experience.OnGameUpdate (run per fixed sim-step) and Experience.OnRenderUpdate (run once per frame, with the interpolation alpha). The engine never names a game class; the game assigns these hooks in App.vue. See Engine Core for per-subsystem detail and GameDirector & Game Flow for the level/spawn machinery. 
sequenceDiagram
    participant RAF as window.rAF
    participant Time
    participant Bus as Emitter (mitt)
    participant Exp as Experience.onTick
    participant Phys as Physics
    participant Game as OnGameUpdate
    participant IB as InputBindings
    participant GD as GameDirector
    participant RU as OnRenderUpdate
    participant Rend as Renderer
    RAF->>Time: callback
    Time->>Time: Elapsed, clamp FrameDelta (max 1/30)
    Time->>Bus: emit "tick" (in try/catch)
    Bus->>Exp: onTick()
    Exp->>Exp: Debug.Stats.begin()
    Exp->>Exp: accumulator += FrameDelta
    loop SIM: 0..N fixed steps (accumulator, max 5)
        Exp->>Exp: Time.Delta = 1/60 (fixed)
        Exp->>Phys: Update() (step world at Delta + drain contacts)
        Exp->>Game: OnGameUpdate()
        Game->>IB: Update() (F-key edges)
        Game->>GD: Update() (player, spawn, enemies, platforms, ladder)
        Exp->>Exp: accumulator -= 1/60
    end
    Exp->>Exp: alpha = accumulator / dt
    Exp->>RU: OnRenderUpdate(alpha)
    RU->>GD: RenderUpdate(alpha)
    GD->>GD: InterpolateGraphics(alpha): player + enemies
    GD->>GD: camera.Update(target from interpolated player)
    Exp->>Rend: Update() (render scene ONCE)
    Exp->>Exp: Debug.Stats.end()
    Time->>RAF: requestAnimationFrame(next) (outside try)

src/engine/core/time.ts 45 LOC

Time is the engine's clock and the sole driver of the frame loop. It owns a Three.js THREE.Clock, computes the clamped real per-frame FrameDelta, exposes the constant fixed simulation step FixedTimestep (1/60), and on every animation frame emits a single global "tick" event through the Event Bus (../events/eventBus). It is constructed by Experience.Configure and knows nothing about who listens — the only coupling outward is the emitted event. Lifecycle: the constructor schedules the first frame; tick reschedules itself forever — even if a "tick" listener throws, the reschedule sits outside the try/catch so the loop never dies; Destroy cancels the pending frame to stop the loop dead.

public static readonly FixedTimestep = 1 / 60; public Clock; public Elapsed; public Delta; public FrameDelta;

The fields any subsystem reads off the singleton's Time. FixedTimestep is the constant simulation step (1/60) the integrators march at; Clock is the underlying monotonic timer; Elapsed is total seconds since start. The two deltas are deliberately split: Delta is the simulation timestep the integrators read (player/enemy/physics multiply into it) — the frame loop sets it equal to FixedTimestep before each fixed step, so all integration uses a constant dt regardless of display refresh rate, and it is never 0. FrameDelta is the real, clamped wall-clock time since the last rendered frame; it drives the fixed-step accumulator only and is never fed into integration. Two private fields back the math: previous (last frame's Elapsed, used to subtract a delta) and animationFrameId (the handle Destroy cancels).

constructor()

Builds the clock, seeds Elapsed from it, sets Delta to the fixed step (Time.FixedTimestep), zeroes FrameDelta, and schedules the first frame. Crucially it does not call tick() synchronously — it waits one animation frame so the first real delta has a non-zero window to subtract against. 

constructor() {
  this.Clock = new THREE.Clock();
  this.Elapsed = this.Clock.getElapsedTime();
  this.Delta = Time.FixedTimestep; // integrators default to the fixed step
  this.FrameDelta = 0;
  this.previous = 0;

  // Instead of calling tick() immediately, wait 1 frame for delta time subtraction
  this.animationFrameId = window.requestAnimationFrame(() => {
    this.tick();
  });
}

Line-level notes: this.Delta = Time.FixedTimestep is the seed value — never 0. Physics.Update feeds Time.Delta straight into World.timestep, and a zero timestep would freeze the simulation on frame one. Deferring the first tick() by a frame means previous (0) and the first Elapsed are measured a real frame apart, so the very first computed FrameDelta is meaningful rather than 0.

Hard-won victory. Delta seeds to Time.FixedTimestep (1/60), never 0 — Physics.Update feeds Time.Delta straight into World.timestep, so any consumer that reads Time.Delta before the first tick (e.g. an entity constructed during Configure) gets a usable 16.6ms instead of a frozen 0.

private tick(): void

The heartbeat. Refreshes Elapsed, computes the clamped FrameDelta, advances previous, emits the global "tick" event inside a try/catch, then re-arms itself with another requestAnimationFrame. This is the only place "tick" is ever emitted in the whole codebase. 

private tick() {
  this.Elapsed = this.Clock.getElapsedTime();
  // Clamp so a suspended tab doesn't produce a huge catch-up delta. This clamp
  // also bounds how many fixed steps a single frame can run — no spiral of death.
  this.FrameDelta = Math.min(this.Elapsed - this.previous, 1 / 30);
  this.previous = this.Elapsed;

  // A throw in any tick listener must NOT stop the loop: the next frame is
  // scheduled below regardless, so one transient bug can't black-screen the game.
  try {
    Emitter.emit("tick");
  } catch (error) {
    console.error("Frame loop error (loop continues):", error);
  }

  // Recursively keep calling tick
  this.animationFrameId = window.requestAnimationFrame(() => {
    this.tick();
  });
}

Line-level notes: Math.min(this.Elapsed - this.previous, 1 / 30) upper-bounds FrameDelta at 1/30s (~33ms). The browser suspends rAF on a backgrounded tab, so when the tab returns the raw Elapsed - previous could be many seconds — feeding that into the accumulator would try to run a huge burst of fixed steps. Clamping bounds FrameDelta (and so the per-frame step count) to one slow frame's worth instead — the first line of defense against a spiral of death, with Experience.maxStepsPerFrame as the backstop. The "tick" emit is wrapped in a try/catch and the next-frame requestAnimationFrame sits outside it, so a throwing listener logs an error but can never stop the loop.

Gotcha. The clamp means real elapsed wall-time and the simulated time drift apart after any stall. That is intentional: the simulation advances in fixed 1/60 steps off the accumulator, not wall-clock — which is exactly why GameDirector's enemy spawner counts in Time.Delta (the fixed step) and not a setInterval (see trySpawn).

public Destroy(): void

Cancels the pending requestAnimationFrame via the stored animationFrameId. Because tick only reschedules at its own tail, cancelling the one outstanding frame is sufficient to halt the loop permanently — no flag, no in-flight callback can resurrect it. 

public Destroy() {
  // Stop the animation frame loop
  window.cancelAnimationFrame(this.animationFrameId);
}

Modifying this: the fixed-timestep accumulator that decouples render rate from physics rate now lives in Experience.onTick; Time's job is just to hand it a clean, clamped FrameDelta and the constant FixedTimestep. Experience.onTick now computes that alpha (the leftover accumulator / dt fraction) right after the step loop and hands it to OnRenderUpdate, so the per-frame render pass interpolates between the last two sim states. Keep the 1/30 clamp and never let Delta reach 0; both are load-bearing for the physics step.

src/engine/core/sizes.ts 33 LOC

Sizes is Time's sibling for screen geometry: it captures the window dimensions and device pixel ratio, and emits a "resize" event whenever the window changes. Like Time, it is constructed in Experience.Configure and is decoupled from its consumers — it just announces "resize" on the bus and lets Experience.onResize fan it out to Camera and Renderer. Lifecycle: constructor reads the initial size and registers the DOM listener; Destroy removes it.

public Width: number; public Height: number; public PixelRatio: number;

The current viewport, refreshed on every resize. Renderer reads all three (setSize / setPixelRatio) and Camera.Resize reads Width/Height for its aspect ratio. The private field onResize holds the listener reference so Destroy can unregister the exact same function.

constructor()

Seeds the three fields from window, defines the resize handler (which re-reads the same three fields and emits "resize"), and registers it on the window's "resize" DOM event. 

constructor() {
  this.Width = window.innerWidth;
  this.Height = window.innerHeight;
  this.PixelRatio = Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2);

  this.onResize = () => {
    this.Width = window.innerWidth;
    this.Height = window.innerHeight;
    this.PixelRatio = Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2);

    Emitter.emit("resize");
  };

  window.addEventListener("resize", this.onResize);
}

Line-level notes: Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2) caps the pixel ratio at 2. On a 3x or 4x display the renderer would otherwise allocate an enormous backbuffer for marginal visual gain — the cap keeps fill-rate sane. The handler is stored on this.onResize (not an inline arrow at addEventListener) for one reason: removeEventListener needs the identical reference later.

public Destroy(): void

Removes the DOM listener using the stored handler reference, ending the resize signal.

public Destroy() {
  window.removeEventListener("resize", this.onResize);
}
Note. Sizes emits "resize"; it does not touch the camera or renderer. Experience owns the wiring (onResize => Camera.Resize(); Renderer.Resize()). That is the same decoupling pattern as Time → "tick" → onTick: the core emitter never names its listeners.

Modifying this: if you add a fit-to-container mode (rendering into a sized div rather than full window), read from the target element here instead of window.innerWidth/Height, but keep emitting the same "resize" event — downstream Camera/Renderer code needs no change.

src/engine/core/experience.ts 122 LOC

Experience is the singleton that wires every engine subsystem together and defines the canonical per-frame order. It imports and owns Debug, Sizes, Time, Input, ResourceLoader, the THREE Scene, Camera, Renderer, and Physics — but imports nothing from game/. The game plugs in through one nullable hook, OnGameUpdate. Lifecycle is deliberately split: a private constructor (singleton guard), an async Configure that does the real construction and registers the "tick"/"resize" listeners, and a symmetric Destroy that unwinds everything in reverse.

private static instance: Experience; private constructor() {}

The singleton machinery. The constructor is private so no one can new Experience(); the static instance holds the one copy. Every subsystem that needs back-references (e.g. Renderer, Physics, every GameObject) calls Experience.GetInstance() rather than receiving the world by constructor injection — the singleton is the dependency-resolution mechanism.

public OnGameUpdate: (() => void) | null = null;

One of two decoupling seams between engine and game (its sibling is OnRenderUpdate, below). The engine calls this inside the fixed-step loop — once per sim-step, 0..N times a frame, right after Physics.Update(); the game assigns it in App.vue after constructing its GameDirector. Because it is a nullable function pointer, the engine core compiles and runs with zero knowledge of any game class. 

// Late-bound game update hook. The game layer assigns this; the engine just
// calls it each frame between physics and renderer. Keeps the engine core
// from naming any game class.
public OnGameUpdate: (() => void) | null = null;

For the concrete assignment, see App.vue: OnGameUpdate = () => { inputBindings.Update(); gameDirector.Update(); }. That is where the "InputBindings before GameDirector" ordering inside the game slice is actually decided.

public OnRenderUpdate: ((alpha: number) => void) | null = null;

The second seam, and the new one. Where OnGameUpdate runs per sim-step, OnRenderUpdate runs once per frame — after the whole fixed-step loop, before Renderer.Update() — and receives the interpolation alpha (accumulator / dt, in [0, 1)). The game uses it to lerp visuals between the last two sim states and to drive the camera, so rendering stays smooth above the sim rate. 

// Late-bound per-FRAME render hook, called once after the fixed-step sim loop
// with the interpolation alpha (accumulator / fixed step). The game uses it to
// interpolate visuals and drive the camera, so rendering stays smooth above the
// sim rate. Stays engine-pure — names no game class.
public OnRenderUpdate: ((alpha: number) => void) | null = null;

For the concrete assignment, see App.vue: OnRenderUpdate = (alpha) => director.RenderUpdate(alpha), wired alongside OnGameUpdate.

public static GetInstance(): Experience

Lazy singleton accessor: creates the instance on first call, returns the same one thereafter. Note this only constructs the empty shell — all the real subsystem wiring happens in Configure, not here. 

public static GetInstance(): Experience {
  if (!Experience.instance) {
    Experience.instance = new Experience();
  }
  return Experience.instance;
}

public async Configure(canvas: HTMLCanvasElement | null): Promise<void>

The "real constructor." It is async because Physics.Configure must await RAPIER.init() (the WASM physics module). Parameter canvas is the DOM target the renderer mounts into, captured from the Vue ref. Construction order matters: Sizes/Time/Input first (they have no engine deps), then the Scene, then Camera (the renderer reads it), then Renderer, then Physics (awaited). The two event listeners are registered last, after everything they touch exists. 

public async Configure(canvas: HTMLCanvasElement | null) {
  this.Debug = new Debug();
  this.Sizes = new Sizes();
  this.Time = new Time();
  this.Input = new Input();
  // ... ResourceLoader([randy, enemy, dkGraphicsData]) ...
  this.TargetElement = canvas;

  this.Scene = new THREE.Scene();
  this.Camera = new Camera(new THREE.Vector3(0, 25, 100));
  this.Renderer = new Renderer();
  this.Physics = new Physics();
  await this.Physics.Configure();

Constructing Time here is what actually starts the loop — new Time() schedules the first requestAnimationFrame. But note: at this moment onTick is not registered yet, so the first few ticks emitted before the listener is attached are harmlessly dropped. The Camera is seeded at (0, 25, 100); the ResourceLoader paths are intentionally empty (the real URLs come from a bundled ?url import map keyed by name — see ResourceLoader).

The back half of Configure registers the two engine event handlers, storing each on a field for cleanup:

// Sizes resize event — store ref for cleanup
this.onResize = () => {
  this.Camera.Resize();
  this.Renderer.Resize();
};
Emitter.on("resize", this.onResize);

// Time tick event — store ref for cleanup
this.onTick = () => {
  if (this.Debug.IsActive) {
    this.Debug.Stats?.begin();
  }

  // Fixed-timestep simulation. Accumulate real frame time and advance the sim
  // (physics + game update) in constant FixedTimestep steps, then render once.
  // This makes physics and movement frame-rate-independent and the sim
  // reproducible. FrameDelta is clamped in Time, so the per-frame step count is
  // bounded; maxStepsPerFrame is a final safety net.
  this.accumulator += this.Time.FrameDelta;
  const dt = Time.FixedTimestep;
  let steps = 0;
  while (this.accumulator >= dt && steps < this.maxStepsPerFrame) {
    this.Time.Delta = dt; // integrators read this as their timestep
    this.Physics.Update();
    this.OnGameUpdate?.();
    this.accumulator -= dt;
    steps++;
  }

  // Render once per frame, interpolated between the last two sim states by how
  // far we are into the next step (alpha in [0,1)). Buttery above the sim rate,
  // no judder below — render and sim rates are fully decoupled.
  const alpha = this.accumulator / dt;
  this.OnRenderUpdate?.(alpha);
  this.Renderer.Update();

  if (this.Debug.IsActive) {
    this.Debug.Stats?.end();
  }
};
Emitter.on("tick", this.onTick);

This onTick body is the frame: accumulate the real FrameDelta, run physics + game update 0..N times at a constant dt = 1/60, then compute the interpolation alpha and run the per-frame render pass (OnRenderUpdate + Renderer.Update) once. The exact order — and the reason for it — is the whole point of this section:

#CallWhy here
1Debug.Stats?.begin()Open the stats.js frame timer. Gated on Debug.IsActive; ?. tolerates a missing panel.
2accumulator += Time.FrameDeltaBank this frame's real clamped elapsed time. The accumulator is the only place wall-clock time enters the sim.
3while (accumulator >= dt && steps < maxStepsPerFrame)The fixed-step loop — runs 0..N times. Drains whole 1/60 slices out of the accumulator until under one step's worth, capped at 5 steps so a long stall can't spiral.
3aTime.Delta = dtPin the simulation timestep to the constant 1/60 inside the loop, so every integrator reads the same fixed dt this step.
3bPhysics.Update()Step the Rapier world (at Time.Delta) and drain contact events first, so the world state the game reads this step is already advanced and collisions already dispatched.
3cOnGameUpdate?.()The game's SIM slice: InputBindings.Update() then GameDirector.Update(). Reads the freshly-stepped physics, runs entity logic, moves the kinematic player. (Camera + visual interpolation are not here — they moved to the per-frame render pass below.)
3daccumulator -= dt; steps++Consume one fixed slice and count it against the per-frame cap.
4alpha = accumulator / dtThe interpolation fraction — how far ([0, 1)) the leftover accumulator sits into the next sim step. Drives the visual lerp so motion stays smooth between fixed steps.
5OnRenderUpdate?.(alpha)The per-frame render pass: GameDirector.RenderUpdate(alpha) interpolates the player + enemy meshes between their last two sim states, then moves the camera to follow the interpolated player.
6Renderer.Update()Render the scene once, after the step loop and the render pass, so the frame on screen reflects this tick's interpolated positions — never mid-loop or last tick's.
7Debug.Stats?.end()Close the stats.js timer, measuring the full begin→end span.
Gotcha. Inside each fixed step, physics → game is not negotiable, and the single Renderer.Update() comes after the whole step loop. If Renderer.Update() ran before OnGameUpdate, the screen would lag one frame behind simulation. If physics ran after the game, the player's CharacterController would move against a stale world and contact dispatch would be a step late.
Hard-won victory — fixed timestep. Banking real time in the accumulator and stepping the sim in constant 1/60 slices makes physics and movement frame-rate-independent and reproducible: a 144Hz display and a 60Hz display run the identical sequence of sim steps, just rendered at different cadences. The FrameDelta clamp in Time plus maxStepsPerFrame (5) together bound the catch-up work, so a long stall can never spiral into an ever-growing step debt.
Hard-won victory — render interpolation. The sim still steps at a fixed 1/60, but the screen no longer snaps to those steps. After the loop, alpha = accumulator / dt measures how far we are into the next step, and the per-frame OnRenderUpdate(alpha) lerps each entity's mesh between its last two sim states. Render and sim rates are now fully decoupled — buttery on a 144Hz display, no judder when the display outruns the sim — without touching the deterministic fixed-step physics underneath.
Hard-won victory. Every Stats, OnGameUpdate, and OnRenderUpdate call uses optional chaining (?.). The engine genuinely runs with no debug panel and no game attached — that is what lets the engine/ layer be reused on its own. The handlers are stored on this.onResize/this.onTick precisely so Destroy can pass the same references to Emitter.off — an inline arrow could never be unregistered.

public Destroy(): void

The symmetric teardown. It unwinds in a deliberate order: first remove Experience's own "resize"/"tick" listeners (so no late tick can touch half-freed subsystems), then stop the signal-producers (Sizes, Time, Input), then free the heavy resources (Camera, Physics, Renderer, Resources), then the debug panel. 

public Destroy() {
  // Remove our own event listeners first
  Emitter.off("resize", this.onResize);
  Emitter.off("tick", this.onTick);

  // Clear subsystem event listeners
  this.Sizes.Destroy();
  this.Time.Destroy();
  this.Input.Destroy();

  // Camera then physics then renderer then resources
  this.Camera.Destroy();
  this.Physics.Destroy();
  this.Renderer.Destroy();
  this.Resources.Destroy();

  // Debug menu
  if (this.Debug && this.Debug.IsActive) {
    this.Debug.Destroy();
  }
}

Line-level notes: removing onTick before Time.Destroy() is belt-and-braces — even if one more frame is in flight, the handler is already detached, and then Time.Destroy() cancels the pending rAF so no further ticks are emitted at all. Physics.Destroy() calls World.free() to release the Rapier WASM world; Renderer.Destroy() disposes the WebGL context. The order matches construction reversed, which is why Camera (the renderer's dependency) is torn down alongside but the renderer's GL dispose comes after physics frees its debug wireframe.

Note. GameDirector has its own Destroy (off-ing the game events, clearing the cleanup interval, unloading the level). Experience.Destroy does not call it — the two teardown chains are parallel, mirroring the two halves of the event map (EngineEventMap vs GameEventMap in Event Bus).

Modifying this: to add an engine subsystem, construct it in Configure (after its dependencies), call it from the right slot inside onTick, and free it in Destroy in reverse. Do not import anything from game/ here — if the game needs new per-frame work, route it through OnGameUpdate, not a new field on Experience. Adding a member to the tick? Decide its slot relative to physics (reads stale or fresh world?) and the renderer (must it be visible this frame?).

src/game/gameDirector.ts 546 LOC

GameDirector is the game layer's central controller: it owns the player and all entity arrays, drives level load/unload, runs the frame-driven enemy spawner, and — most relevant to this section — defines the internal order of game logic across two passes: Update (the SIM pass, run per fixed step) and RenderUpdate (the per-frame pass that interpolates visuals and drives the camera). It is constructed in App.vue after Experience.Configure (so the scene/camera exist), grabs the engine singletons it needs in its constructor, and is invoked via OnGameUpdate (per sim-step) and OnRenderUpdate (per frame). This guide documents only its frame-loop role in depth; level management and spawning are covered in GameDirector & Game Flow.

constructor()

Caches the engine singletons it leans on (Experience, Scene, Time, Camera, ResourceLoader), creates the GraphicsObject overlay holder, and registers all its game-event listeners. Note it pulls this.time = this.experience.Time — the same Time instance whose Delta the spawner counts in. 

this.experience = Experience.GetInstance();
this.scene = this.experience.Scene;
this.time = this.experience.Time;
this.camera = this.experience.Camera;
this.resources = this.experience.Resources;
this.GraphicsObject = new GameObject();

this.setupEventHandlers();

public Update(): void

The game's SIM pass, called from OnGameUpdate right after InputBindings.Update() — so it runs per fixed step, 0..N times a frame. Its internal order is as load-bearing as Experience.onTick's. After two guards, it runs: player → spawn → enemies → platforms → ladder. The camera and visual interpolation are no longer here — they moved to RenderUpdate (the per-frame pass). The ordering comments in the source are the real rationale, quoted below. 

// SIM update — runs inside the fixed-timestep loop (0..N times per frame), so all
// movement integrates at the constant step. Camera + visual interpolation are NOT
// here; they run once per FRAME in RenderUpdate, decoupled from the sim rate.
public Update() {
  // Freeze game logic while a level is loading ...
  if (this.isLoading) return;

  // Guard: Player must be ready
  if (!this.Player?.PhysicsBody) return;

  this.Player.Update();

  // Spawn + update enemies (runtime-spawned, not level-data), driven by the frame
  // clock here. Compact destroyed barrels in place right after — promptly, and
  // keeping the array reference stable (was a 5s wall-clock sweep).
  this.trySpawn();
  const firstTrashCan = this.TrashCans[0];
  this.Enemies.forEach((enemy) => enemy.Update(this.Player, firstTrashCan));
  GameUtils.CompactDestroyedObjects(this.Enemies);

  // Update every level-data entity that has an update, in one uniform loop.
  // Runs AFTER player.update() so the platform one-way toggle reads this
  // frame's player state. ...
  const updatableLevelEntities: UpdatableEntity[] = this.Platforms;
  for (const e of updatableLevelEntities) {
    e.Update?.(this.Player, firstTrashCan);
  }

  // Update ladder detection (manual for now, will convert to sensor callbacks later)
  this.Player.UpdateLadderState(
    GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggered(this.LadderTopSensors),
    GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggeredObjectFullyInside(this.LadderCoreSensors, this.Player),
    GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggered(this.LadderBottomSensors)
  );
}

The order, step by step, and why each step sits where it does:

#StepWhy this position
0aif (this.isLoading) return;Hard freeze while a level tears down/respawns — entities are mid-rebuild and the Vue overlay covers the canvas.
0bif (!this.Player?.PhysicsBody) return;Nothing below is safe before the player's physics body exists (it's created during level load).
1Player.Update()First — moves the kinematic body and recomputes contact/state this step. The per-frame render pass interpolates from what it produces.
2trySpawn()Frame-clock barrel cadence (advances counters, may spawn).
3Enemies.forEach(...); CompactDestroyedObjects(Enemies)Each barrel reads the player (target) and the oil can it aims for; destroyed barrels are then swap-removed in place (stable array ref).
4Platforms[i].Update?.(player, ...)After player — the one-way platform toggle reads this frame's player state.
5Player.UpdateLadderState(top, core, bottom)Pushes the ladder-sensor results back into the player for next frame's climbing logic.
Hard-won victory — camera follows the interpolated player. The camera moved out of the SIM Update and into the per-frame RenderUpdate, where it runs after InterpolateGraphics. So instead of snapping to the stepped sim position (which would judder when the display outruns the sim), it follows the player's smooth, interpolated render position — the source comment: "Follow the INTERPOLATED render position ... so the camera tracks the smooth visual player, not the stepped sim position."
Hard-won victory — C5 camera decouple. The camera is engine-pure: it never imports anything from game/. Instead cameraTargetFromPlayer flattens the Player into a plain CameraTarget snapshot (scalars + a CameraFollowState enum) that camera.Update consumes. That is the C5 decision — the camera knows nothing of Player or PlayerState.
Gotcha — platforms after player. "Runs AFTER player.update() so the platform one-way toggle reads this frame's player state." A one-way (drop-through) platform decides whether it is currently pass-through based on the player's position/intent this frame. Update the platforms first and the toggle lags a frame, letting the player clip or stick at the worst moment. The same loop is also where the WinFlag used to live — it now wins via the contact table, so it no longer needs a per-frame shapecast (a deliberate removal noted in the source).
Note. Step 5 is explicitly interim: "manual for now, will convert to sensor callbacks later." Ladder contact is currently polled from the sensor arrays via GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggered* and pushed into the player, rather than driven by the contact-table callbacks the rest of the collisions use.

public RenderUpdate(alpha: number): void

The per-frame pass, called from OnRenderUpdate once after the whole fixed-step loop with the interpolation alpha. Same two guards as Update, then it lerps the moving entities between their last two sim states and points the camera at the interpolated player. This is what decouples render rate from sim rate: the sim steps at a fixed 1/60, but the visuals are resampled every frame. 

public RenderUpdate(alpha: number) {
  if (this.isLoading) return;
  if (!this.Player?.PhysicsBody) return;

  this.Player.InterpolateGraphics(alpha);
  for (const enemy of this.Enemies) {
    enemy.InterpolateGraphics(alpha);
  }

  // Camera follows the interpolated visual position (C5 decouple: plain snapshot).
  this.camera.Update(this.cameraTargetFromPlayer(this.Player));
}

Order matters here too: InterpolateGraphics writes each entity's RenderTranslation before camera.Update reads it through cameraTargetFromPlayer, so the camera tracks the smooth visual player, not the stepped sim body.

private cameraTargetFromPlayer(player: Player): CameraTarget

The C5 mapping function. It translates the player's FSM State into the engine's CameraFollowState enum and packs only the scalars the camera needs. Return: a plain CameraTarget struct — no game types cross the boundary. 

return {
  // Follow the INTERPOLATED render position (set by InterpolateGraphics just
  // before this in RenderUpdate) so the camera tracks the smooth visual player,
  // not the stepped sim position.
  x: player.RenderTranslation.x,
  y: player.RenderTranslation.y,
  velocityX: player.NextTranslation.x,
  followState,
  isGrounded: player.IsTouching.ground,
};

x/y read the player's RenderTranslation — the interpolated visual position written by InterpolateGraphics in RenderUpdate moments earlier — so the camera tracks the smooth visual player, not the stepped sim body. velocityX reads NextTranslation.x (the player's intended horizontal velocity) and isGrounded from the player's shapecast contact flags, both from the latest sim step.

private trySpawn(): void

Frame-driven enemy spawning, called once per frame from Update. It replaces an old wall-clock setInterval with a single clock: Time.Delta. It bails immediately if spawning is off or physics is paused, then accumulates timeSinceLastSpawn and fires when the interval elapses. 

private trySpawn() {
  if (!this.isSpawningEnemies || this.experience.Physics.IsPaused) return;

  this.timeSinceLastSpawn += this.time.Delta;

  // First barrel: Kong's opening throw — a crazy barrel aimed at the oil can.
  if (this.currentSpawnInterval === 0) {
    this.timeSinceLastSpawn = 0;
    this.currentSpawnInterval = Math.random() * (4 - 3) + 3; // 3-4 seconds
    this.spawnOilCanBarrel();
    return;
  }

  // Subsequent barrels: mostly normal, every 8th crazy.
  if (this.timeSinceLastSpawn >= this.currentSpawnInterval) {
    this.timeSinceLastSpawn = 0;
    this.currentSpawnInterval = Math.random() * (3 - 2) + 2; // 2-3 seconds
    this.enemyCount++;
    if (this.enemyCount % 8 === 0) {
      this.SpawnCrazyEnemy();
    } else {
      this.SpawnEnemy();
    }
  }
}
Hard-won victory — one clock, not two. The header comment: "Replaces the old wall-clock setInterval: one clock now (Time.Delta), and a fresh boot spawns without needing a reset. ... The cadence counters are advanced BEFORE the (async) spawn so a stutter frame can't double-spawn." Because timeSinceLastSpawn is reset to 0 before the awaited spawn call, a long frame can't satisfy the interval twice. And because cadence rides the same clamped Time.Delta as everything else, pausing physics (which freezes Delta consumption via the IsPaused guard) cleanly halts spawning too.
Note (D28). Normal and crazy barrels are one unified Enemy type now, distinguished only by their starting FSM state — "D28 fold-in of the old CrazyEnemy." The director keeps a single Enemies: Enemy[] array; SpawnEnemy vs SpawnCrazyEnemy differ only in the constructor flags.

public Destroy(): void

The game-side half of the symmetric teardown. It offs every game-flow listener it registered, clears the periodic cleanup interval, and unloads the current level. This runs independently of Experience.Destroy. 

public Destroy() {
  Emitter.off("resourcesReady");
  Emitter.off("gameStart");
  Emitter.off("gameOver");
  Emitter.off("gameReset");
  Emitter.off("switchLevel");
  Emitter.off("gameObjectRemoved");

  clearInterval(this.cleanupIntervalId);
  this.UnloadLevelData();
}
Gotcha. The setInterval cleanup (every 5s, prunes destroyed enemies) is a wall-clock timer — the one place the game still uses real time rather than Time.Delta. It deliberately keeps running after gameOver (so corpses get reaped on the freeze screen) and is only stopped here in Destroy via clearInterval. Forget this and you leak a timer holding the whole director alive.

Modifying this: when adding per-frame game logic, slot it into Update with the same question Experience asks — does it need this frame's player state (put it after Player.Update()) or last frame's? Anything time-based should count in this.time.Delta, never setInterval, so it honors pause and the 1/30 stall clamp. Register new game-flow listeners in setupEventHandlers and mirror them with an Emitter.off in Destroy.

Lifecycle: Configure → running → Destroy

Stepping back from per-frame detail, the whole app moves through three phases. App.vue's onMounted registers loading listeners, calls Experience.Configure (which starts Time's loop and registers onTick/onResize), wires the game's contact rules, debug sinks, GameDirector, InputBindings, and the OnGameUpdate / OnRenderUpdate hooks, then kicks off asset loading last. From there the frame loop runs until teardown. 

flowchart TD
    A["App.vue onMounted"] --> B["Experience.GetInstance()"]
    B --> C["await Experience.Configure(canvas)"]
    C --> C1["new Time() starts rAF loop"]
    C --> C2["Emitter.on tick / resize"]
    C --> D["registerContactRules + debug sinks"]
    D --> E["new GameDirector / InputBindings"]
    E --> F["OnGameUpdate = InputBindings.Update then GameDirector.Update; OnRenderUpdate = GameDirector.RenderUpdate"]
    F --> G["Resources.StartLoading() (last)"]
    G --> H["resourcesReady -> loadLevelByIndex -> gameStart"]
    H --> LOOP["running: each frame = accumulate FrameDelta, 0..N fixed 1/60 SIM steps (Physics + OnGameUpdate), then alpha + OnRenderUpdate (interpolate + camera), Renderer once"]
    LOOP --> LOOP
    LOOP --> Z["Experience.Destroy + GameDirector.Destroy"]
    Z --> Z1["off tick/resize, Time.Destroy cancels rAF"]
    Z --> Z2["free Physics world, dispose Renderer, clear interval"]
Tip. The ordering inside onMounted is itself a hard-won detail: loading listeners are attached before Configure, and Resources.StartLoading() is called last, so a fast boot can't fire resourcesReady/loadingFinished before anyone is listening. See Bootstrap & App Shell for the full App.vue walk-through.

Collisions: groups, the contact table & sensors

This is the spine of the big refactor (commit "Replace collision routing with contact table"). A physics contact becomes a game reaction in four hops, and the engine never learns a single game word along the way: Rapier collision groups decide which pairs are even allowed to touch; the engine ContactRegistry is a generic (typeA, typeB) -> handler table; the game registers concrete rules in contactRules.ts; and each collider carries userData (a routing identity) plus contactPoints (per-surface flags) so a handler can resolve the real entities. This killed the old hard-coded if/else routing and, with the kinematic two-sided dispatch, finally fixed the barrel/enemy bug.

src/game/config/contactRules.ts src/engine/entities/contactRegistry.ts src/game/types/gameCollisionGroups.ts src/engine/types/userData.ts src/engine/types/contactPoints.ts

The mental model, end to end. Read it once and every file below slots into a box. Note the two producers of contact events that converge on the same registry — Rapier's own event queue (for dynamic movers like barrels) and the player's kinematic character controller (for the player-as-mover). That convergence is the whole fix. 

flowchart TD
  G["gameCollisionGroups: group & mask bitmasks<br/>decide which pairs may touch"] --> STEP
  STEP["Physics.Update(): World.step(EventQueue)"] --> DRAIN["handleCollisionEvents()<br/>drainCollisionEvents"]
  DRAIN --> RESOLVE["collider handle -> GameObject<br/>(gameObjectRegistry)"]
  KCC["Player.updateTranslation()<br/>KCC numComputedCollisions()<br/>diff vs last frame -> enter/exit edges"] --> RESOLVE2["collider -> GameObject<br/>GetGameObjectFromCollider"]
  RESOLVE --> DISPATCH["Contacts.Dispatch(phase, x, y)"]
  RESOLVE2 --> DISPATCH
  DISPATCH --> LOOKUP["for each rule: rule.a(x) && rule.b(y)<br/>else rule.a(y) && rule.b(x)"]
  LOOKUP --> HANDLER["game handler(a, b)<br/>reads userData.type, casts to entity"]
  HANDLER --> EMIT["Emitter.emit('gameOver' / 'gameWin' / ...)<br/>or mutate entity (can.IsOnFire, TeleportToPosition)"]
  EMIT --> GD["GameDirector / Vue react"]
Note. Two kinds of "collision sensing" coexist in this engine and they must not be conflated. (1) The contact table documented here is event-driven: enter/exit verdicts between two entities (Enemy kills Player, barrel ignites the can). (2) The player's own ground/wall/ceiling sensing is shapecast-based and fills contactPoints every frame (see Player in the Game sections). Ladders are a third path — polled "fully inside" intersection checks (D9), deliberately not in the contact table. Keep the three straight.

src/game/types/gameCollisionGroups.ts 30 LOC

The bitmask gate that runs before any contact event exists. This is a tiny game-layer const map (it lives under game/types, not engine — the engine names no group) consumed by the entity constructors via GameObject.setCollisionGroup / setCollisionMask. Rapier only generates a contact between two colliders when their group/mask bits agree; everything downstream (the registry, the rules) never fires for a pair the bitmasks already filtered out. No lifecycle of its own — it is pure data imported wherever an entity is built (Player, Enemy, Platform, TrashCan).

const CollisionGroups = { ... } as const

Four named bit constants, written in binary (0b…) so the bit positions are legible at a glance. Two halves of the Rapier interaction word: an object's group says what it is; its mask says what it collides with. The header states the exact interaction test the engine relies on. 

// Collision occurs when (a.group & b.mask) AND (b.group & a.mask); combine with bitwise OR.

const CollisionGroups = {
  // Player's main collision box — solid collisions with platforms and enemies
  PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX: 0b0000000000000001, // Bit 0

  // Bit 1 is free — was PLAYER_HIT_BOX (the player's 62.5% hit sensor), removed
  // when death detection moved to the full bounding box via the contact table.

  // Enemy collision group — collides with the player's bounding box and platforms
  ENEMY: 0b0000000000000100, // Bit 2

  // Platform collision group — regular platforms, one-way platforms, walls, etc.
  PLATFORM: 0b0000000000001000, // Bit 3

  // Default collision group — collides with everything
  DEFAULT: 0b1111111111111111,
} as const;

Line notes. Bit 0 / Bit 2 / Bit 3 are deliberately spaced (1 is left as a documented hole — see the victory below). DEFAULT is all sixteen low bits set, used as both group and mask for bystander solids that should just behave normally (the TrashCan sets group=DEFAULT, mask=DEFAULT so an enemy can fall in). as const narrows the values to literal types so the keys cannot drift.

Entitygroup (what it IS)mask (what it HITS)set in
PlayerPLAYER_BOUNDING_BOXPLATFORM | ENEMYplayer.ts ctor
Enemy (barrel)ENEMYPLATFORM | PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOXenemy.ts ctor
Platform / Wall / OneWayPLATFORMDEFAULTplatform.ts ctor
TrashCanDEFAULTDEFAULTtrashCan.ts ctor

Read the symmetry across the table: Player's mask lists ENEMY and Enemy's mask lists PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX — both halves of (a.group & b.mask) AND (b.group & a.mask) must be true, so a single missing bit silently disables a whole interaction. The barrel's mask is mutated at runtime, too: while DescendLadder() it drops PLATFORM (to pass through girders) but keeps PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX so it can still kill you mid-descent.

Hard-won victory — the free Bit 1. Death used to come from a separate 62.5%-size "hit box" sensor on the player (the old PLAYER_HIT_BOX) polled every frame. When death moved onto the full bounding box and into the contact table ("any touch registers"), that sensor and its poll became dead code. The bit was not renumbered — it is left as a labelled gap so future bits keep their positions and any stale 0b…10 mask in a saved level can never accidentally re-enable a hitbox that no longer exists.

Modifying this: add a new interaction by allocating the next free bit (Bit 1, then Bit 4…) as a new 0b… constant, then OR it into the relevant entities' group/mask in their constructors. Never reuse DEFAULT's value for a specific class — that opts the object into colliding with everything. Remember both directions must agree, or no contact (and therefore no rule) ever fires.

src/engine/types/userData.ts 10 LOC

The identity stamped onto every Rapier physics body — the bridge from an anonymous collider handle back to a game meaning. Rapier callbacks hand you a raw Collider, never your TS class, so instanceof is unavailable at the dispatch boundary; this opaque string pair is how routing survives. The engine defines the shape and reads it as opaque strings; the meaning of both fields is assigned entirely by the game layer (via EntityType).

export default interface UserData { type: string; name: string }

Two fields, two jobs. type is the shared routing flag: many entities carry the same type (every barrel is "Enemy"), so one contact rule matches them all. name is the per-instance id — it defaults to the type and is only overridden via SetName when you need to single out one specific entity (e.g. logging). 

export default interface UserData {
  // TYPE flag — shared routing identity; game-assigned, engine-opaque.
  type: string;

  // Per-INSTANCE identifier; defaults to the type until SetName overrides it.
  name: string;
}

Where it is written: GameObject.createObjectPhysics seeds userData = { type: name, name: name } (both start equal); SetType(newType) overwrites both (re-stamps routing identity and mirrors it to name); SetName(newName) overwrites only name. Where it is read: getUserData and matchesType in physicsHelpers — the latter is exactly what the contact-rule predicates call.

Note. Per-entity numeric metadata is intentionally not carried here. The header is explicit: it lives as named, typed fields on the entity classes (e.g. Platform.FloorLevel, Platform.IsEdge, LadderSensor.Direction), set at spawn from the GLB texture-name meta bag by the entity factories. This replaced an older anonymous value0/1/2/3 hop — the reason Player.getPlatform() resolves a real Platform instead of reading magic indices off userData.

Modifying this: resist the urge to widen this interface with game data. If a handler needs a number or a flag, add a typed field to the entity class and resolve the object with Physics.GetGameObjectFromCollider, then read the field. Keeping UserData to two opaque strings is what lets the engine stay game-blind.

src/engine/types/contactPoints.ts 34 LOC

The per-frame surface state a character accumulates — distinct from the contact table. Where the registry answers "which two entities just touched, run a verdict", ContactPoints answers "what is the player resting against / inside this frame". It is the geometry side of collisions: a flat struct of booleans the player rebuilds every frame from shapecasts (and from polled ladder sensors), then the state handlers read to drive movement. Owned by Player as IsTouching.

export default interface ContactPoints { ground; ceiling; leftSide; rightSide; edgePlatform; ladderCore; ladderTop; ladderBottom }

Eight booleans in three groups. The basic four are cardinal surfaces; one platform-type flag; three ladder zones. Each is the input to a specific mechanic.

fieldtrue when…drives
groundstanding on solid groundenables jump, resets coyote time, idle/run anim
ceilinghead against a ceilingcancels upward velocity
leftSide / rightSidetouching a wall on that sideblocks horizontal movement / wall slide
edgePlatformstanding on a platform flagged as an edgeedge-specific animations/mechanics
ladderCoreinside a ladder's climbable coreup/down climbing
ladderTopat a ladder topclimb -> stand transition (above)
ladderBottomat a ladder bottomclimb -> stand transition (below)

Lifecycle each frame: Player.resetCollisions() zeroes all eight via Object.assign(this.IsTouching, {...}) (assign, not replace, so dat.gui keeps its binding), then getShapeCastCollisions() sets ground / leftSide / rightSide / ceiling / edgePlatform, and the three ladder fields are pushed in by UpdateLadderState(top, core, bottom) from the GameDirector's polled sensor checks.

Gotcha. The ladder fields here are filled by polling, not by the contact table. A reader chasing "where does ladderCore get set?" will find nothing in contactRules.ts — by design (D9). The contact registry handles cross-entity verdicts; the player's standing surface is shapecast/poll territory. Two systems, one word ("contact") — don't cross the wires.

Modifying this: add a field here only if it's a per-frame surface the player rests against or sits inside. Then set it in getShapeCastCollisions() (and zero it in resetCollisions()). A new event between two entities does not belong here — that's a contact rule.

src/engine/entities/contactRegistry.ts 50 LOC

The generic engine half: a declarative "when an A contacts a B, run this" table layered over the physics event dispatch. It is constructed once by Physics.Configure() as Physics.Contacts = new ContactRegistry(), filled by the game at startup (contactRules.ts, via App.vue), and driven by Physics.handleCollisionEvents calling Dispatch. It imports only GameObject and names zero game concepts — same spirit as the engine StateMachine: a reusable mechanism the game configures with data. Deferred per-line physics wiring lives in the Engine "Physics" section; here is the conceptual model and its API.

export type ContactMatch = (gameObject: GameObject) => boolean

A predicate deciding whether a GameObject matches one side of a rule. The game supplies these (almost always byType("Enemy") etc.).
