Keeping "which objects count as the A side" as a function — not a string equality baked into the engine — is what lets the engine stay game-blind while still letting the game match by type, by name, by interface, or anything else.

type ContactPhase = "enter" | "exit" | "sensorEnter" | "sensorExit"

The four physics phases a rule can listen to. enter/exit are solid collisions; sensorEnter/sensorExit are non-solid sensor intersections. Note what's absent: there is no per-frame "stay" — Rapier only emits start/stop, so a rule that needs continuous truth (ladders) must poll instead.

type ContactHandler = (a: GameObject, b: GameObject) => void  /  interface ContactRule { a; b; phase; run }

A handler runs when both sides match and receives them in the rule's own declared (a, b) order — never reversed, no matter which body actually moved (the key ergonomic guarantee, made good by Dispatch). A ContactRule is just the bundle: two predicates, a phase, a handler.

public Add(a: ContactMatch, b: ContactMatch, phase: ContactPhase, run: ContactHandler)

Declare a rule. Pushes { a, b, phase, run } onto the private rules array. That's the entire registration API — rules are plain data, so adding game behavior never edits this file. 

private rules: ContactRule[] = [];

public Add(a: ContactMatch, b: ContactMatch, phase: ContactPhase, run: ContactHandler) {
  this.rules.push({ a, b, phase, run });
}

public Dispatch(phase: ContactPhase, x: GameObject, y: GameObject)

Called by Physics for every contact event (and by the player's KCC path). Walks all rules, skips those whose phase differs, and for the rest performs the two-sided match. This is the heart of the symmetry handling. 

public Dispatch(phase: ContactPhase, x: GameObject, y: GameObject) {
  for (const rule of this.rules) {
    if (rule.phase !== phase) {
      continue;
    }

    if (rule.a(x) && rule.b(y)) {
      rule.run(x, y);
    } else if (rule.a(y) && rule.b(x)) {
      rule.run(y, x);
    }
  }
}

Line notes — read the else if carefully, it is doing two jobs at once:

  • Order-independence. Physics has no idea whether the event arrived as (enemy, player) or (player, enemy). The first branch tries a=x, b=y; if that fails the second tries the swap. Either way the rule fires, and rule.run is called with arguments in the rule's declared order — so a handler written (enemy, can) => … always gets enemy first.
  • Fire-once for asymmetric pairs. Because the second test is an else if, a single event can only satisfy one branch. For a typical rule whose two predicates are different types (Enemy vs Player) exactly one branch can ever be true for a given (x, y), so the handler runs once per event, not twice.
Gotcha — same-type rules would only fire once. The else if means if you ever declared a rule whose both predicates match the same object (e.g. byType("Enemy") on both sides for enemy-vs-enemy), the first branch would already be true for (x, y) and the swapped branch would be skipped — you'd get one call, not a call for each ordering. None of today's rules are symmetric, so this is correct as written; just know the constraint before adding an A-equals-B rule.

Modifying this: almost never. This file is finished and game-agnostic. New behavior is added as data in contactRules.ts. The only reasons to touch ContactRegistry are a new phase (which also needs a matching call site in Physics.handleCollisionEvents) or a different matching strategy in Dispatch — both rare and both engine-level decisions.

src/game/config/contactRules.ts 68 LOC

The game half: the declarative table of who dies / wins / teleports / ignites. It imports the engine's ContactRegistry and matchesType, the game's EntityType strings, the global Emitter, and the few entity classes it needs to cast to in handlers (Player, Teleporter, CameraSensor, TrashCan). Lifecycle: a single function called once at startup from App.vue — registerContactRules(webglExperience.Physics.Contacts) — after the engine is configured. The header draws the boundary precisely: an entity's own movement reactions (a barrel reversing at a wall) live with the entity; only cross-entity verdicts live here.

const byType = (type: string): ContactMatch => (go: GameObject) => matchesType(go, type)

The single predicate factory used by every rule. It closes over a type string and returns a ContactMatch that asks "does this object carry that userData.type?" via physicsHelpers.matchesType. This is the join between the four userData identity and the registry's predicate slot — "match any entity carrying this TYPE flag (e.g. every enemy)". 

const byType = (type: string): ContactMatch => (go: GameObject) =>
  matchesType(go, type);

export default function registerContactRules(contacts: ContactRegistry)

Registers all of this game's contact rules into the passed registry. No return value; pure side-effect on contacts. Five Add calls — the complete cross-entity rule book. Read them as a table.

#side aside bphasehandler effect
1ENEMYPLAYERenterEmitter.emit("gameOver")
2ENEMYTRASH_CANentercan.IsOnFire = true + emit("gameObjectRemoved", enemy)
3TELEPORTERPLAYERsensorEnterplayer.TeleportToPosition(dest.x, dest.y)
4CAMERA_SENSORPLAYERsensorEntersensor.ApplyCameraOnEnter()
5WIN_FLAGPLAYERsensorEnterEmitter.emit("gameWin")

Rule 1 — Enemy touches Player → game over. Solid enter phase; the handler is fire-and-forget onto the event bus.

contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.ENEMY),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "enter",
  () => Emitter.emit("gameOver")
);

The handler ignores its (a, b) args — it only needs to know the touch happened. "Any touch registers": because Dispatch is two-sided, this fires whether the player ran into the barrel or the barrel rolled into the player. gameOver is a GameEventMap event consumed by GameDirector (which pauses physics — see the victory below).

Rule 2 — Enemy falls into a TrashCan → enemy destroyed, can ignites. The only handler that both mutates an entity and emits.

contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.ENEMY),
  byType(EntityType.TRASH_CAN),
  "enter",
  (enemy, can) => {
    (can as TrashCan).IsOnFire = true;
    Emitter.emit("gameObjectRemoved", enemy);
  }
);

Here the (a, b) order earns its keep: the rule declares a = ENEMY, b = TRASH_CAN, so the handler can name them (enemy, can) and trust the registry to pass them that way regardless of which moved. The can as TrashCan cast is safe precisely because byType(TRASH_CAN) already proved the type. Setting IsOnFire = true ripples back into Enemy.DecideTakeLadder() — barrels always take ladders until the can is lit, then roll the dice — the difficulty ramp lives in that one boolean. The gameObjectRemoved event lets GameDirector tear the barrel down.

Rule 3 — Player enters a Teleporter → teleport to its destination. First sensorEnter rule.

contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.TELEPORTER),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "sensorEnter",
  (teleporter, player) => {
    const dest = (teleporter as Teleporter).Destination;
    (player as Player).TeleportToPosition(dest.x, dest.y);
  }
);

The handler reads geometry off the entity, not off userData: Teleporter.Destination is a typed Vector2 the factory filled from the texture-name dest=x_y meta. Player.TeleportToPosition lives on GameObject (computes the delta and does a relative kinematic setTranslation).

Rule 4 — Player enters a CameraSensor → reposition the camera.

contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.CAMERA_SENSOR),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "sensorEnter",
  (sensor) => (sensor as CameraSensor).ApplyCameraOnEnter()
);

The cleanest decoupling in the file: the rule names no camera API at all, it just tells the zone "you were entered, do your thing." CameraSensor.ApplyCameraOnEnter() calls camera.SetBaselinePosition(x, y) from its authored cam=x_y anchor. The contact table doesn't even know the Camera exists.

Rule 5 — Player reaches the WinFlag → win the level.

contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.WIN_FLAG),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "sensorEnter",
  () => Emitter.emit("gameWin")
);

Mirror of Rule 1 on the sensor phase. The WinFlag used to be a solid box that self-detected with a per-frame four-direction shapecast; once the contact system existed it became a pass-through sensor and that proof-of-concept hack was deleted. You walk into the goal now.

Hard-won victory — the barrel/enemy bug. The old routing was a hard-coded if/else ladder inside the collision handler, branching on which body was which. It missed the Enemy→Player kill when only the player moved into the enemy: the player is a kinematic character controller, which keeps a skin gap from solids and so makes Rapier emit no collision event for the player-as-mover (confirmed by runtime logging: ccBlockedBy=[Enemy] yet zero Rapier events). The fix is the pair of mechanisms feeding one table: Rapier's drainCollisionEvents covers the barrel-as-mover, and Player.updateTranslation walks CharacterController.numComputedCollisions() itself for the player-as-mover — so the same declarative rule fires in both directions. But that controller list is a per-frame "currently touching" set; dispatching it raw would re-fire gameOver every frame you leaned on the enemy. So the player now diffs this frame's contacts against last frame's (two reused Sets, swapped each frame so nothing allocates): a contact that is new dispatches "enter", one that vanished dispatches "exit" — so an enter rule fires once on contact (not every frame) and rules finally get a real exit edge (contactRules declares only enter/sensorEnter today, so exit is a no-op there for now). Destroyed objects are skipped on both edges. The whole loop is guarded by if (!this.Physics.IsPaused) so the frozen game-over screen doesn't churn. 
// Player.updateTranslation() — second producer, now dispatching enter/exit EDGES:
if (!this.Physics.IsPaused) {
  this.currentContacts.clear();
  const numCollisions = this.CharacterController.numComputedCollisions();
  for (let i = 0; i < numCollisions; i++) {
    const collision = this.CharacterController.computedCollision(i);
    if (!collision?.collider) {
      continue;
    }
    const other = this.Physics.GetGameObjectFromCollider(collision.collider);
    if (other && !other.IsBeingDestroyed) {
      this.currentContacts.add(other);
    }
  }

  // New contacts this frame → "enter".
  for (const other of this.currentContacts) {
    if (!this.previousContacts.has(other)) {
      this.Physics.Contacts.Dispatch("enter", this, other);
    }
  }
  // Contacts gone since last frame → "exit".
  for (const other of this.previousContacts) {
    if (!this.currentContacts.has(other) && !other.IsBeingDestroyed) {
      this.Physics.Contacts.Dispatch("exit", this, other);
    }
  }

  // Swap the two sets (no allocation); next frame clears `current` before refill.
  const swap = this.previousContacts;
  this.previousContacts = this.currentContacts;
  this.currentContacts = swap;
}
Note — sensors must opt in. A sensor with no callback still has to arm physics events to participate in the table. Teleporter, CameraSensor, and WinFlag each call this.enableContactEvents() in their constructors for exactly this reason (they define no OnSensorEnter, so the auto-arm in GameObject.enablePhysicsEvents wouldn't fire). LadderSensor pointedly does not — it is polled (D9), so it arms no events and appears in no rule.
Gotcha — ladders are not here, by design. Despite "sensor" sounding like contact-table territory, the three ladder zones are absent from this file. Their "fully inside" climb gate needs continuous truth every frame, and the registry only sees enter/exit edges — so ladders stay on the polling path (GameSensor.IsFullyInside / Enemy.checkLadderIntersections). Looking for a ladder rule here is the classic wrong turn.

Modifying this: to add a cross-entity interaction, (1) ensure both entities carry a stable EntityType via SetType, (2) ensure their collision group/mask bits actually let them touch (gameCollisionGroups), (3) ensure non-callback sensors call enableContactEvents(), then (4) add one contacts.Add(byType(A), byType(B), phase, handler) here. Keep handlers thin: emit an event or call a method on the resolved entity; never put movement reactions or per-frame logic in a rule — that's the entity's job. Reach for enter/exit for solids and sensorEnter/sensorExit for sensor zones; if you need "while inside", you need a poll, not a rule.

The Finite-State-Machine pattern

Every moving creature in FireGame runs on the same nine-line engine primitive: a host object owns a string State field, the StateMachine holds a map of state → handler function, and once per frame it dispatches to the handler for the current state. Handlers read input and physics, mutate the body, and reassign host.State to transition next frame. The engine knows nothing about idle, jumping, or barrels — the player and the enemy each instantiate the SAME generic class with their own state set.

src/engine/entities/stateMachine.ts src/engine/types/playerStates.ts

src/engine/entities/stateMachine.ts 17 LOC

This is the entire generic FSM — a router, nothing more. It is a pure engine primitive: it imports nothing, references no game concept, and is parameterised over two type variables so it works for any host that exposes a string State plus a matching handler map. The game constructs one per entity: Player news up a StateMachine<Player, PlayerState> in its attribute-init (see the Player entity section), and Enemy news up a StateMachine<Enemy, EnemyState> in its init (see the Enemy section). The host owns the lifecycle; the machine has no Destroy() and holds no resources — it is just two references and a method.

The design seam here is deliberate and worth stating plainly: the host owns its State; the machine owns dispatch. A handler never asks the machine to transition — it simply reassigns host.State = SomeOtherState, and the very next Update() routes to the new handler. That single rule is what keeps every state body decoupled from every other and from the machine itself.

class StateMachine<H extends { State: S }, S extends string>

Two type parameters do all the heavy lifting. H is the host type, constrained to any object that has a State field of type S. S is the union of legal state strings (e.g. PlayerState = "idle" | "running" | ...). Because the handler map is typed Record<S, (host: H) => void>, TypeScript forces you to supply a handler for EVERY member of the union — forget one state and the construction call fails to compile. That compile-time exhaustiveness is the quiet payoff of the generics.

export default class StateMachine<H extends { State: S }, S extends string> {
  constructor(
    private host: H,
    private handlers: Record<S, (host: H) => void>
  ) {}

Line notes: both host and handlers are private constructor parameters — TypeScript's shorthand that declares and assigns the fields in one stroke, so there is no body. The host is stored by reference, so when the player's State mutates anywhere, the machine sees it on the next read — no syncing, no setter.

Note. The constraint is capital-State ({ State: S }), matching Player.State and Enemy.State. The file's top-of-file prose comment still says lowercase “host.state” in a couple of places — that is stale wording, not a second field. The only field the type system (and the dispatch below) reads is State.

public Update(): void

Called once per frame by the host's own Update() (the player calls it inside updatePlayerState(); the enemy calls this.fsm.Update() directly). It reads the host's current state ONCE, looks up the handler, and invokes it with the host. That is the whole loop.

public Update() {
  // Read State once: a handler that changes host.State transitions on the NEXT
  // update, it does not re-dispatch within this one.
  this.handlers[this.host.State]?.(this.host);
}

Line notes: the optional-chain ?. is the safety valve — if the current state has no registered handler (a typo, a half-added state), dispatch is a silent no-op rather than a crash. More importantly, because this.host.State is read exactly once per call, a handler that flips host.State mid-body does NOT cause the new state's handler to also run this frame. Transitions cost exactly one frame of latency, always. Several state bodies lean on this: they set the next state AND seed a value for that state (e.g. idle sets NextTranslation.y = JumpPower and switches to JUMPING) then return, trusting next frame to pick up the new handler.

Gotcha. The single-read rule means a handler that wants its successor to act this same frame must do that work itself before returning. The enemy does exactly this: handleEnemyRolling, on deciding to descend, sets State = DESCENDING AND immediately calls enemy.DescendLadder() so the barrel starts moving on the transition frame instead of stalling for one tick. Symmetrically handleEnemyDescending calls RollHorizontally() the instant it flips back to ROLLING. If you forget, the entity freezes for a frame at every transition.

How two entities reuse the one class

The reuse is the point of the generics — the same 17 lines drive a kinematic-character player and a dynamic-rigidbody barrel. The wiring is identical in shape, only the type arguments and handler maps differ:

// Player (game/entities/player/player.ts) — kinematic, input-driven
this.fsm = new StateMachine<Player, PlayerState>(this, {
  [PlayerStates.IDLE]: handlePlayerIdle,
  [PlayerStates.RUNNING]: handlePlayerRunning,
  [PlayerStates.FALLING]: handlePlayerFalling,
  [PlayerStates.JUMPING]: handlePlayerJumping,
  [PlayerStates.CLIMBING]: handlePlayerClimbing,
});

// Enemy (game/entities/enemy/enemy.ts) — dynamic, autonomous
this.fsm = new StateMachine<Enemy, EnemyState>(this, {
  [EnemyStates.ROLLING]: handleEnemyRolling,
  [EnemyStates.DESCENDING]: handleEnemyDescending,
  [EnemyStates.BOUNCING]: handleEnemyBouncing,
});

The handler signature (host) => void is what lets the bodies live in their own files (one file per state, under player/states/ and enemy/states/). A handler is just a free function that receives the entity and mutates it. No base class, no inheritance, no switch — the map IS the dispatch table.

flowchart TD
    A["entity.Update()"] --> B["refresh per-frame inputs<br/>(input snapshot / ladder poll)"]
    B --> C["fsm.Update()"]
    C --> D{"handlers[host.State]"}
    D --> E["state handler runs:<br/>read input + physics,<br/>mutate body"]
    E --> F{"reassign host.State?"}
    F -- "yes" --> G["transition lands NEXT frame"]
    F -- "no" --> H["stay in state"]
    E --> I["entity finishes Update<br/>(move body, sync sprite)"]

The barrel / enemy FSM

The enemy proves the “works for any host” claim. A DK barrel comes in two flavors chosen at spawn (D28: data-driven — the old separate CrazyEnemy class was folded into one Enemy picked by a boolean). A NORMAL barrel starts ROLLING and oscillates ROLLING ↔ DESCENDING as it rolls girders and occasionally takes a ladder; a CRAZY barrel starts BOUNCING and never leaves it. Because the flavor is encoded purely as the starting state and a crazy barrel never enters a normal state (nor vice-versa), State === BOUNCING is itself the “is this crazy?” discriminator — no separate flag. The full bodies live in the Enemy section; here is the shape:

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ROLLING: NORMAL barrel spawn
    [*] --> BOUNCING: CRAZY barrel spawn
    ROLLING --> DESCENDING: inside ladder and take-ladder dice pass
    DESCENDING --> ROLLING: reach ladder bottom
    BOUNCING --> BOUNCING: kick up on girder contact
    note right of BOUNCING
        Terminal for the barrel's whole life.
        Crazy barrels never take ladders.
    end note
    note left of ROLLING
        Wall-reverse and edge-turn are NOT
        transitions: they live in the enemy's
        collision callbacks, flipping a
        direction sign, not the State.
    end note
Note. The enemy FSM mirrors the player FSM on the game side on purpose (D3 / ADR-0003): the barrel's reactive movement — reverse at walls, turn at edges, bounce on girders — lives in its collision callbacks (OnCollisionEnter/Exit), while its cross-entity verdicts (kills the player, ignites the trash can) are NOT in the FSM at all. Those route through the declarative contact table (see the Contact rules / Contact registry sections). The FSM owns only the entity's own behavior, never how it interacts with others.
Hard-won victory (D28). Collapsing normal and crazy barrels into one state-driven class deleted an entire parallel CrazyEnemy type. The spawn site just passes crazy: boolean; the constructor maps it to a starting State. Adding a third barrel flavor later is a new state + handler, not a new class hierarchy — exactly the leverage the generic machine was built to give.

Modifying this: resist the urge to add features here. If you want entry/exit hooks, transition guards, or transition logging, the temptation is to grow this class — but every host already expresses entry work as “seed a value then return” and exit work as the next handler's first lines, so a richer machine would duplicate that. The one safe extension is purely additive: a new state is a new key in the host's handler map plus its constant in the host's state enum. Because the map is typed Record<S, ...>, the compiler will refuse to construct the machine until you supply the new handler — lean on that. Do NOT make Update() re-dispatch in a loop after a transition; the one-frame-per-transition guarantee is load-bearing for both the player and enemy bodies.

src/engine/types/playerStates.ts 24 LOC

The player's state alphabet: a frozen constant object mapping symbolic names to their string values, plus a derived union type. It is the S in StateMachine<Player, PlayerState> — the set of legal player states — and it is imported by player.ts and by all five handlePlayer* bodies so that transitions are written as player.State = PlayerStates.FALLING rather than bare strings. It lives under engine/types/ by convention even though the states themselves are game-flavored; the engine ships the type slot, the game fills it. (The enemy's equivalent, enemyStates.ts, lives game-side — see the Enemy section — reflecting that barrel states are wholly a game concept.)

const PlayerStates = { IDLE, RUNNING, JUMPING, FALLING, CLIMBING } as const

Five string constants under an as const assertion. The assertion is what makes each value a literal type ("idle", not string), which in turn makes the union below precise enough to drive the exhaustive handler map.

const PlayerStates = {
  IDLE: "idle",
  RUNNING: "running",
  JUMPING: "jumping",
  FALLING: "falling",
  CLIMBING: "climbing",
} as const;

export type PlayerState = typeof PlayerStates[keyof typeof PlayerStates];

export default PlayerStates;

Line notes: typeof PlayerStates[keyof typeof PlayerStates] is the idiom that turns the object's values into a union type — "idle" | "running" | "jumping" | "falling" | "climbing". The object is the runtime value (default export, used for PlayerStates.IDLE); the type is the named export (used wherever a PlayerState annotation is needed). The same enum-as-object pattern appears in enemyStates.ts, gameObjectType.ts, and friends — it is the project's house style for closed string sets.

The state table

Each constant carries an inline doc comment naming its intended exits. Here they are, alongside the handler file that implements each and what the body actually does — read the next column as “where to look,” not as the full logic, which the Player States section walks line by line.

StateValueHandlerRole
IDLE"idle"handlePlayerIdle.tsStanding on ground; pins downward gravity to hug slopes, re-arms coyote + buffer.
RUNNING"running"handlePlayerRunning.tsHorizontal ground movement via applyHorizontalMovement; paces the run animation by speed.
JUMPING"jumping"handlePlayerJumping.tsAscending; rise gravity, variable height (early-release latch), apex hang.
FALLING"falling"handlePlayerFalling.tsDescending; accelerates to terminal fall speed; hosts coyote & buffer jumps.
CLIMBING"climbing"handlePlayerClimbing.tsOn a ladder; vertical-only movement; exits to IDLE at the ends.

The player FSM (as the handlers actually implement it)

This diagram is drawn from the handler bodies, not the enum comments — see the gotcha below for where the two diverge. Every edge is a player.State = ... assignment in a handler:

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> RUNNING: horizontal input
    IDLE --> FALLING: walk off edge
    IDLE --> JUMPING: jump and buffer available
    IDLE --> CLIMBING: ladder core and up or down
    RUNNING --> IDLE: input released and slow
    RUNNING --> FALLING: leave ground
    RUNNING --> JUMPING: jump and buffer available
    RUNNING --> CLIMBING: ladder core and up or down
    JUMPING --> FALLING: natural apex or ceiling hit
    FALLING --> IDLE: land, idle input
    FALLING --> RUNNING: land, still moving
    FALLING --> JUMPING: coyote jump
    CLIMBING --> IDLE: reach top or bottom, or leave ladder
Gotcha. The enum's inline comments describe a slightly fuller graph than the current handlers build. Three intended edges are not (yet) implemented: JUMPING → CLIMBING, JUMPING → IDLE/RUNNING (jumping never lands directly — it always passes through FALLING), and CLIMBING → FALLING / CLIMBING → JUMPING (climbing only ever exits to IDLE). Conversely, the handlers add an edge the comments omit: FALLING → JUMPING via coyote time. Trust the diagram above (it follows the code); treat the enum comments as the author's aspirational map. The comment on JUMPING that says “jump released or max jump time” is likewise stale — handlePlayerJumping notes the apex now “emerges from physics, not a fixed MaxJumpTime timer.”

How transitions are gated: coyote-time, jump buffering, variable height

These three feel mechanics are not states — they are flags on the Player that gate the transitions into JUMPING and shape the arc once there. The grounded states (IDLE, RUNNING) re-arm them; the airborne states (FALLING, JUMPING) spend and clear them. Conceptually:

  • Coyote-time — a short grace window (CoyoteTime = 0.033s, two frames, set in baseFeel.ts) during which you can still jump just after walking off a ledge. IDLE/RUNNING set CoyoteAvailable = true every grounded frame; on leaving the ground they stamp TimeFallWasEntered = Time.Elapsed. FALLING then permits a jump only while CoyoteAvailable && Time.Elapsed < TimeFallWasEntered + CoyoteTime, consuming it by setting CoyoteAvailable = false. That is the FALLING → JUMPING edge above.
  • Jump buffering — a jump pressed slightly before landing still fires the instant you touch down. The player shapecasts downward each frame; when ground sits within BufferJumpRange (1.5 units, DK profile) and the player is descending, GroundWithinBufferRange goes true and FALLING arms BufferJumpAvailable. IDLE and RUNNING gate their jump on isJump && BufferJumpAvailable, so a buffered press launches on the first grounded frame.
  • Variable jump height — releasing the button mid-rise cuts the jump short. JUMPING latches EndedJumpEarly = true once !isJump and you are past the MinJumpTime floor (0.05s), which boosts rise gravity by JumpEndedEarlyGravityModifier so the apex lands lower. Every jump entry (in IDLE, RUNNING, and the coyote branch in FALLING) resets EndedJumpEarly = false so the next jump starts un-cut.
Tip. The pattern to internalise: grounded handlers re-arm, airborne handlers spend. IDLE/RUNNING each end with the same three lines — CoyoteAvailable = true; EndedJumpEarly = false; if (!isJump) BufferJumpAvailable = true; — and JUMPING opens by clearing both CoyoteAvailable and BufferJumpAvailable. Because re-arming happens only on grounded frames, the flags decay naturally the moment you are airborne, which is exactly what gives coyote-time its hard time bound. The full line-by-line of each gate is in the Player States section.
Note (D10). Every handler reads the player's input snapshot (player.Input), refreshed at the top of Player.Update() via inputDevice.CaptureInto(this.Input) — not the raw device. This is the input-as-data seam: a network, replay, or AI source could fill the same struct, and the FSM would not know the difference. It is also why the FSM is testable in isolation — states are pure functions of (input snapshot, physics flags). See the Input section.

Modifying this: adding a player state is a three-touch change — add the constant here (the as const object), write its handlePlayerXxx.ts body under player/states/, and register it in the handler map in player.ts. The Record<PlayerState, ...> typing means you cannot forget the third step: omit the new handler and new StateMachine<Player, PlayerState>(...) fails to compile. Update the inline transition comments when you do — they have already drifted from the handlers once (see the gotcha), and they are the only human-readable map of the graph.

What's Impressive — the Wins

An honest, evidence-backed account of what this engine gets right. Not cheerleading — every claim below points at real code (and real build numbers). This is the part worth being proud of.

measured from a production vite buildgrep-verified seams

It is astonishingly small and runs literally anywhere

A complete physics platformer — a WebGL renderer, a full Rust-built physics engine, a reactive UI framework, and all the game code — compiles to a single bundle that fits in your browser cache. These are real numbers from npx vite build on this repo, not estimates.

2.43 MB
total raw bundle
(js + wasm + css)
~766 KB
gzipped over the wire
(core, excl. lazy DRACO)
1
JS chunk ships it all
(Rapier WASM base64-inlined)
0
native dependencies
(pure web + WASM)
ArtifactRawGzippedNotes
assets/index-*.js2.30 MB785 KBThree.js (tree-shaken) + Vue runtime + Rapier2D WASM inlined as base64 + all engine + all game code, minified into one file.
assets/index-*.css7 KB2 KBThe entire stylesheet (Tailwind, purged).
draco/…/draco_decoder.wasm192 KB63 KBLazy-loaded — only fetched if a level GLB uses DRACO compression. Most plays never download it.
index.html454 B303 BThe whole HTML shell.
Hard-won victory. A from-scratch 2D physics platformer in under 800 KB gzipped, where roughly a megabyte of that (raw) is a serious deterministic physics solver, is genuinely lean. There is no app to install, no store, no native runtime: the URL is the game. It runs on any device with a modern browser and WebGL2 — desktop, laptop, phone, tablet, a kiosk, an embedded panel — same binary, no porting.

Why "runs anywhere" is true and not marketing: the entire dependency set is pure JavaScript + WebAssembly with zero native or platform-specific packages.

// package.json — the complete runtime dependency list
"@dimforge/rapier2d-compat": "^0.14.0",  // physics, shipped as portable WASM
"mitt": "^3.0.1",                         // ~200 bytes of event bus
"three": "^0.162.0",                      // WebGL rendering
"vue": "^3.4.21"                          // UI shell

Four dependencies. Rapier's -compat build loads its WASM with an async await RAPIER.init() (physics.ts:31), so it boots in any browser without a build-time WASM step. That is the whole portability story, and it holds up.

The engine/game separation is real, not aspirational

Lots of codebases claim a clean architecture in comments. This one's central invariant — the dependency arrow only ever points game → engine — actually holds when you grep for violations. That is the hardest thing to keep true over a refactor, and it's true here.

Hard-won victory — the grep passes. grep -r "game/" src/engine/ finds nothing. No file under src/engine/ imports anything from src/game/. The engine could be lifted into a different 2D platformer without dragging a single Donkey-Kong concept along. The discipline is even self-documented at resourceLoader.ts:427: "the engine deliberately cannot import the game's EntityType enum (engine names zero game concepts…)".

Three places show this isn't luck — it's a repeated, deliberate pattern of inverting the dependency so the engine receives game knowledge as plain data instead of importing it:

SeamHow the coupling is invertedEvidence
Camera follow (the C5 decouple)The camera reads a plain CameraTarget DTO of scalars; the game maps its Player into it each frame. The camera's easing keeps all its per-state branching but names zero game concepts.camera.ts:218 Update(target?: CameraTarget); gameDirector.ts:192 cameraTargetFromPlayer()
Collision reactionsPhysics fires generic contact events; a game-supplied ContactRegistry table decides what they mean. The engine never hard-codes "barrel hits player = game over".contactRules.ts (game) registers into contactRegistry.ts (engine)
Debug wireframe colors (D3)The game injects an EntityType → color palette; PhysicsDebug looks each collider up in the injected map and even depends on a structural Physics type, not the concrete class.physicsDebug.ts:36 SetTypeColors(...); App.vue:59 injects DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS

Debug tooling is exactly where game knowledge usually leaks into an engine (hardcoded type colors, bespoke log formats). Here the game injects both its color palette and its CollisionLogger sink (App.vue:54), and the engine renders and logs without knowing a single type name. That's a high bar, cleared.

The core abstractions are small, generic, and genuinely reusable

The two load-bearing engine primitives — the FSM and the contact table — are tiny, fully generic, and import nothing game-specific. They're the kind of code you'd copy into the next engine unchanged.

class StateMachine<H extends { State: S }, S extends string>

The whole finite-state-machine engine is one generic class whose Update() is a single line — dispatch to the handler for the host's current state. No engine imports, no singleton, trivially testable. It powers both the player (idle/run/jump/fall/climb) and the barrels (roll/descend/bounce) with zero duplication.

// stateMachine.ts — the entire dispatch
Update() { this.handlers[this.host.State]?.(this.host); }

ContactRegistry.Dispatch(phase, x, y)

A declarative (A, B) → run table that fires symmetric rules exactly once with a stable argument order, allocating nothing on the hot path. This is what replaced the old hand-written collision if/else ladder — and it's the fix that finally killed the barrel/enemy collision bug.

// contactRegistry.ts — two-sided match, fired once, no temporaries
if (rule.a(x) && rule.b(y))      { rule.run(x, y); }
else if (rule.a(y) && rule.b(x)) { rule.run(y, x); }

Two more abstractions punch above their weight:

  • Data-driven level spawning. gameDirector.ts:249 just iterates parsed rows and dispatches ENTITY_FACTORIES[data.type](...) — adding a placeable thing is (mostly) a new factory entry, not director surgery. The engine GLB parser stays game-blind by carrying an uninterpreted meta?: Record<string,string> bag (levelData.ts) that each factory reads only its own keys from.
  • Input as data. The device fills a plain InputState snapshot via CaptureInto(state) (input.ts:273), and the player reads that buffer — not the keyboard directly. The seam is explicitly designed for "keyboard today; network/replay/AI later" (inputState.ts:1). That one decision is what makes AI barrels and future netcode a fill-the-struct-differently problem instead of a rewrite.

Choosing Rapier was the right call

A platformer lives or dies on its collision and movement. Reaching for Rapier2D — a modern, Rust-built, WASM-compiled, determinism-capable solver — instead of hand-rolling AABB sweeps or pulling in a heavier non-deterministic engine is a mature, forward-looking choice.

What it buys you, concretely:

  • A real solver, for free. Continuous collision, sensors/intersections, kinematic character control, collision-group bitmasks — all production-grade, none of it your maintenance burden.
  • A foundation that's now deterministic. Rapier is built for cross-platform determinism, and the engine now exploits it — a fixed-timestep accumulator drives the sim and a seeded PRNG drives gameplay randomness (see Recently Shipped). So replay, deterministic tests, and lockstep netcode are all on the table; a non-deterministic engine would have closed those doors permanently.
  • WASM portability. Same solver, same numerics, every platform — no native build matrix.
Hard-won victory. The timestep hazard was clearly understood, not ignored — and is now resolved with a fixed-step accumulator. The supporting care remains: the camera uses frame-rate-correct exponential smoothing (camera.ts, 1 - Math.exp(-rate * Δt)) and the clock clamps delta and never feeds zero (time.ts). The author knew where the dragons were.

The craft details that show real care

Beyond the big architecture, a dozen small decisions reveal a builder who sweats the right things.

🎚️ The "Feel Lab" — live tuning with paste-back

Easily the best DX touch in the codebase. playerDebug.ts builds writable dat.GUI sliders that handlers read fresh every frame, so a slider mutates the movement feel instantly — and dumpFeelValues() prints a ready-to-paste attributes block, closing the loop from "tweak by hand" to "commit the numbers." That is how good platformer feel actually gets made.

📦 Zero-allocation hot paths

The per-frame code that matters is allocation-aware: the SpriteAnimator early-returns on unchanged state and only touches the texture inside its timing branch; the camera mutates one cached Vector3 rather than newing one each frame; collision resolution is an O(1) collider-handle → GameObject Map lookup, never a scan; and ContactRegistry.Dispatch allocates nothing. (There are still hot spots to fix — see performance — but the instincts are right.)

🧭 Self-documenting decision discipline

The (C5)/(D3)/(D28) decision-log tags aren't decoration — they're a culture of writing down why a shape is deliberate so future-you doesn't "simplify" a hard-won decoupling back into a bug. Combined with comments that name the exact bug a line prevents, this codebase teaches its own reasoning. Most don't.

And the quiet ones: levels auto-register via an eager import.meta.glob (drop a GLB in assets/levels/, it appears), the resource loader is fail-soft so a single 404 advances the bar and still boots the game rather than hanging, and every engine subsystem stores its event-handler refs so it can precisely off() them in Destroy() — the correct pattern for a long-lived global bus.

The bottom line: the architecture is sound, the seams are real, the footprint is tiny, and the foundation can carry far more game than currently sits on it. The next section is about how much farther — read it as a roadmap from someone who likes this engine, not a teardown.

What Could Be Better — an Honest Roadmap

Conceptual critique from someone who has read every file and likes where this is going. Two cohorts of fixes have already shipped (see below); what remains is debatable on purpose — agree, disagree, or defer. Each item is grounded in real code, ranked by payoff, and paired with a concrete direction. Severity is about impact on the engine's stated direction (reusable, portable, expandable), not "bug count."

✅ shipped already implemented (see Recently Shipped)
High blocks a stated goal or hides a real landmine
Medium a real ceiling you'll hit as it grows
Low worth doing, not urgent

✅ Recently shipped

Two batches of fixes are done and in the codebase (each typechecked, built, and adversarially reviewed) — removed from the roadmap below.

Batch 1 — determinism & safety

✓
Fixed-timestep accumulator + seeded PRNG — sim runs in constant 1/60 steps (frame-rate-independent feel, reproducible); a seeded mulberry32 replaces the unseeded global RNG. experience.ts · time.ts · physics.ts · mathHelpers.ts · gameDirector.ts
✓
Idempotent Destroy() + crash-proof frame loop (try/catch around the tick emit); own cloned GPU buffers (no disposing shared geometry); reusability mile (injected asset manifest + level-type vocabulary). Bonuses: dead timestep clamp removed, DRACO path BASE_URL-relative, GraphicsObject destroy-latch fixed.

Batch 2 — lifecycle, perf & smoothness

✓
Buttery render interpolation. A per-frame OnRenderUpdate(alpha) hook lerps moving entities between their last two sim states (alpha = accumulator/dt); the camera moved to the render pass and follows the interpolated position. Smooth above the sim rate (144 Hz), no judder below — with shortest-arc rotation and a teleport snap so resets/teleports jump, not slide. experience.ts (OnRenderUpdate) · gameObject.ts (InterpolateGraphics) · gameDirector.ts (RenderUpdate) · camera.ts
✓
Killed the per-frame allocations (the GC stutter). Hoisted the shapecast/KCC/ladder predicate closures to bound fields and reuse scratch {x,y} vectors across the Rapier calls; in-place compaction every frame replaced the 5 s wall-clock sweep. player.ts · enemy.ts · gameUtils.ts (CompactDestroyedObjects)
✓
KCC contact edges. The player's character-controller contacts now dispatch true enter/exit edges (diffed against last frame) instead of "enter" every frame — so an "on enter, do X once" rule fires once. player.ts (currentContacts/previousContacts)
✓
Bus hygiene + real teardown. Stored handler refs so off removes exactly ours (the gameWin leak fixed); App.vue onUnmounted now wires Destroy(); Configure has a re-entry guard and resets the singleton on Destroy (defensively). gameDirector.ts · App.vue · experience.ts
✓
Failed-load rollback (parse + validate graphics BEFORE unload; roll back on throw); InputSource interface (Player depends on it — touch/gamepad now a drop-in); light entity-store (unified teardown via levelEntityArrays(), typed arrays kept); type-hatch cleanups (destroyAll<T>, Items: GLTF|Texture, dropped the any casts).
Render interpolation note. It ships with interpolation now, so high-refresh is smooth. The one micro-nit left (harmless): for interpolated entities, syncGraphicsToPhysics still writes the mesh transform each sim step before InterpolateGraphics overwrites it — a few redundant writes, no visible effect.

1 · True multi-instance / dependency injection

Bus ownership, teardown wiring, the Configure guard, and a second update phase are all ✅ shipped. What's left is the deep one: the engine is still a global singleton, just a resettable one.

High One Experience + one module-global Emitter per page — no DI

eventBus.ts — const Emitter = mitt(...) exported global | experience.ts — private static instance (now nullable/resettable, but still a singleton)

Problem. Destroy() now resets the instance so a fresh boot works (HMR/restart), but you still can't run two worlds at once or inject a world per test: a headless netcode sim, a server-authoritative tick, an editor preview pane, and split-screen all need >1 Experience and a non-global bus. Every test would also share the one global emitter.

Direction. Make the bus an instance owned by Experience (this.Events = mitt()) and thread the Experience/world handle into subsystems and GameObject instead of GetInstance(). This is the prerequisite for headless/multi-world/testable use — and the largest single change, so do it when one of those futures is actually on the table.

Also here (Low): the two update phases (OnGameUpdate sim + OnRenderUpdate render) cover today's needs; if a third consumer appears (editor overlay, debug HUD, netcode pre/post-physics), promote them to a small ordered, multi-subscriber hook registry with named phases.

2 · The last reusability seam

Assets, level types, and the input source are all injected/abstracted now (✅ shipped). One seam remains.

Medium The engine event-bus module still owns the game's event vocabulary

eventBus.ts lives in engine/ yet enumerates gameStart/gameOver/gameWin/switchLevel and imports GameObject for the gameObjectRemoved payload

Problem. A second game must edit (or fork) an engine file to change its event vocabulary, and the engine bundle imports GameObject for one payload type. The discipline is documented but not enforced by the type system.

Direction. Make the bus generic over a game-supplied event map — engine exports only EngineEventMap (tick/resize/resources*), game/ defines GameEventMap and composes the typed emitter. Mirrors the asset-manifest / level-type / InputSource injections you already did.

3 · Expandability ceilings

Where the current design resists the next features. (Teardown duplication is reduced — levelEntityArrays() shipped — but the rest of these are untouched and not wrong for today's DK clone.)

High A single Player is hardcoded — "multiple players" is a rewrite

gameDirector.ts — public Player!: Player; one camera target, one ladder-state push, one feel apply; contactRules "Enemy→Player = gameOver" is one-shot

Problem. Every player-facing system assumes one instance. Co-op, split-screen, or a ghost replay would touch all of them.

Direction. Promote to Players: Player[] iterated in Update/RenderUpdate; make the camera target a function of the array (or a designated follow target); move lose/win/feel into per-player state.

High GameObject is a god base-class; inheritance forces all of it onto every entity

gameObject.ts — render + physics + transform + interpolation + collision-group bit-math + dispose, all in one base every entity extends

Problem. A pure sensor that needs no mesh, or a future logic-only/pooled entity, still inherits the entire render+physics+dispose surface, and the shape/material unions are closed (a new collider shape edits the base).

Direction. Move toward composition — split Transform, a Renderable (mesh/material/interpolate/dispose), and a PhysicsBody (collider/groups) into components a GameObject has, not is. Opens an ECS path if you ever want one.

Also here (Medium): the typed-array entity store got a light unification (teardown via levelEntityArrays()); a fully generic Map<type, GameObject[]> would make adding an entity purely additive, at the cost of casts (deferred — your explicit typed arrays read better). Runtime-spawned barrels still live outside the factory registry (new Enemy(...) hardcoded) — a Spawner + runtime factory would unify them; enemy "flavor" is a constructor boolean discriminated by FSM state — define enemy archetypes as data (feel + start state + handler map + resource), mirroring FEEL_PROFILES. And there's still no serialization / save-load / scene-snapshot — extract a Scene/World with toSnapshot()/fromSnapshot() to unlock checkpoints, save/load, and (with the fixed step + interpolation now in place) rollback netcode.

4 · Performance & memory

The hot paths are now allocation-free (the player/enemy per-frame churn and the shared-geometry dispose are ✅ shipped). What's left is lower-stakes.

Medium Barrels are compacted but not pooled — spawn/destroy still churns bodies & meshes

SpawnEnemy/SpawnCrazyEnemy build a fresh rigid body + collider + cloned mesh each spawn; Destroy tears them all down (every 2–3s)

Problem. In-place compaction removed the per-frame array churn and the 5s linger, but each barrel's birth and death still allocates/frees a Rapier body, a collider, and a cloned mesh group. At DK barrel rates it's fine; it's the next lever if you push entity counts.

Direction. A small barrel pool: reuse a fixed set of rigid bodies/meshes, re-seeding position + FSM state on spawn instead of constructing/destroying. (More complex than today's compaction — defer until you actually need it.)

Also here (Low): cameraTargetFromPlayer still news a fresh CameraTarget object each frame (reuse one struct on the director); PhysicsDebug rebuilds line geometry + typed arrays and walks the whole scene graph every frame (pre-size + throttle — debug-only); and there's no InstancedMesh/batching for identical barrels (caps swarm-scale).

5 · Correctness

The KCC enter/exit edges, the failed-load rollback, and the type escape hatches are ✅ shipped. One implicit invariant remains.

Medium Contact handlers destroy entities synchronously mid-rule-iteration

contactRules.ts — barrel→TrashCan emits gameObjectRemoved (→ enemy.Destroy()) from inside ContactRegistry.Dispatch, while the physics event drain / KCC diff is still iterating

Problem. Dispatch re-evaluates each rule's predicates fresh, so a later rule acting on a pair after a prior rule destroyed one side is currently safe only because matchesType is null-safe and the current rule set has disjoint pairs. It's an invariant held by luck, not structure.

Direction. Queue gameObjectRemoved for end-of-frame destruction (a deferred-destroy list drained once per tick) instead of destroying synchronously inside the contact dispatch. Cheap, and it makes the invariant explicit.

6 · Developer experience, testing & tooling

The biggest remaining gap, and the one you chose to defer. The fixed timestep + seeded PRNG now make deterministic tests possible for the first time, and the extracted levelNodeTypes map makes a level validator easy.

High Zero tests, zero CI, no typecheck/test/lint scripts

package.json scripts = dev / build / preview only

Problem. Every refactor (the log shows many) is validated only by manually playing the game — no regression net, no gate. (You've been validating these batches by typecheck+build+review by hand; a harness makes that repeatable.)

Direction. Add vitest + a typecheck script (vue-tsc --noEmit) + minimal CI. Seed it on the clean seams: StateMachine routing, ContactRegistry two-sided dispatch, mathHelpers (the seeded PRNG is a one-line golden-sequence test), and the GLB parser.

High State handlers can't be unit-tested without booting the whole app

a handler can only run by booting Experience or faking a Player with `as unknown as Player`; jump math is entangled with rendering + the singleton

Problem. The most valuable thing to test (movement/jump math) is the hardest to reach.

Direction. Define a narrow PlayerMovementContext interface (only what handlers read/write: IsTouching, NextTranslation, Time, Input, the feel scalars, a playAnim(state) callback) and type handlers against that, not the concrete Player. With the fixed timestep, the asserted arc is now stable.

Also here (Medium): binary GLB levels have no in-repo validator — add a tiny Node CLI that imports ParseLevel headless (feed it the extracted levelNodeTypes map) and flags unknown types / dupes / zero-size colliders / unrecognized meta. No eslint/prettier gate. And level-load failure is still surfaced only as a logged string — emit a structured levelLoadFailed event (mirroring resourceLoadFailed) so an embedding app or a test can observe it.

Closing thought. Two batches in, the pattern holds: the decoupling, the contact table, the input-as-data seam, and the FSM are exactly why a fixed timestep, render interpolation, an InputSource interface, and idempotent teardown were small, surgical changes rather than rewrites. Everything left on this list rides the same seams — a test harness, a generic event bus, multiple players, and serialization are each one clean abstraction away.

Engine Core — Experience, Time, Sizes

The beating heart of the engine. Experience is a lazy singleton that owns and wires every engine subsystem; Time drives the single requestAnimationFrame loop and emits "tick"; Sizes tracks the viewport and emits "resize". Nothing here names a single game concept — the game plugs itself in through two late-bound hooks, OnGameUpdate (per sim step) and OnRenderUpdate (per rendered frame), and the global event bus.

src/engine/core/experience.ts src/engine/core/time.ts src/engine/core/sizes.ts src/main.ts

src/engine/core/experience.ts 164 LOC

What it is: the engine's composition root — a lazily-constructed singleton that instantiates, holds, and tears down every engine subsystem (Debug, Sizes, Time, Input, ResourceLoader, Scene, Camera, Renderer, Physics). Who constructs it: nobody, directly — the private constructor is blocked; the Vue shell (src/App.vue → onMounted) calls Experience.GetInstance() then await Configure(canvas, GAME_ASSETS) — handing the engine the game's asset manifest rather than letting the engine name those assets itself. Why these imports: it imports each subsystem class because it is the one place that news them up and assembles the dependency graph; it imports Emitter (see eventBus, Engine Layer) only to subscribe its two per-frame/per-resize closures. Lifecycle: GetInstance() → Configure() (the real, async constructor) → frames flow via the "tick" subscription → Destroy() unwinds everything in a deliberate order.

Note. The header comment sums it up: "Overall handler that mounts a webgl render to a dom canvas element." Every other engine file reaches back here via Experience.GetInstance() to find the shared Scene, Sizes, Time, etc. That back-reference is the reason this is a singleton rather than a passed-around object.

Fields

The class declares its subsystems with definite-assignment assertions (!) because they are populated in Configure(), not in the constructor. TypeScript can't see that, so the ! tells it "trust me, this is assigned before use." 

// Class prop instance and "new" blocking constructor for Singleton.
// Nullable so Destroy() can clear it and a fresh GetInstance()/Configure() works
// (HMR, tests, a restart) instead of handing back a torn-down instance.
private static instance: Experience | undefined;
private constructor() {}

// engine subsystems, instantiated in Configure()
public Debug!: Debug;
public Sizes!: Sizes;
public Time!: Time;
public Input!: Input;
public Resources!: ResourceLoader;

public TargetElement!: HTMLCanvasElement | null;

public Scene!: THREE.Scene;
public Camera!: Camera;
public Renderer!: Renderer;
public Physics!: Physics;

Line-level notes:

  • private static instance + private constructor() {} together enforce the singleton: outside code physically cannot write new Experience(). It is typed Experience | undefined so Destroy() can null it back out and a later GetInstance() rebuilds a clean instance (HMR, tests, restart) rather than handing back a gutted one.
  • TargetElement is the raw DOM <canvas> handed in by Vue; Renderer reads it back off the singleton as this.experience.TargetElement to build the WebGLRenderer.
  • Scene is held here (not inside Renderer) because everyone needs it — Camera adds itself to it, Physics binds meshes to it, the game adds level geometry to it.
Hard-won victory — the OnGameUpdate seam. This single nullable field is the one thread the engine pulls to run the game, without ever importing it: 
// Late-bound game update hook. The game layer assigns this; the engine just
// calls it each frame between physics and renderer. Keeps the engine core
// from naming any game class.
public OnGameUpdate: (() => void) | null = null;
The engine ships knowing nothing about GameDirector, InputBindings, or Donkey Kong. The game assigns OnGameUpdate in App.vue, and the tick loop calls it with optional-chaining (this.OnGameUpdate?.()) so a frame that fires before the game is wired simply does nothing instead of throwing. This is the central decoupling seam of the whole codebase.
Hard-won victory — the OnRenderUpdate sibling seam. A second late-bound field lets the engine render smoothly above the sim rate without naming the game: 
// Late-bound per-FRAME render hook, called once after the fixed-step sim loop
// with the interpolation alpha (accumulator / fixed step). The game uses it to
// interpolate visuals and drive the camera, so rendering stays smooth above the
// sim rate. Stays engine-pure — names no game class.
public OnRenderUpdate: ((alpha: number) => void) | null = null;
Where OnGameUpdate fires per fixed sim step (0..N times a frame), OnRenderUpdate fires exactly once per rendered frame, after the loop, with an alpha in [0,1) — how far the accumulator has progressed into the next step. The game uses it to lerp meshes between the last two physics states and to drive the camera off that interpolated position, so a 144 Hz display stays buttery while the sim still ticks at a constant 1/60. The same optional-chaining (this.OnRenderUpdate?.(alpha)) means a frame that fires before the game is wired no-ops instead of throwing.
Gotcha — why the handler refs exist. 
// Stored event handler references for cleanup
private onResize!: () => void;
private onTick!: () => void;
mitt's off() removes a listener by identity. If you subscribe an inline arrow and later try to Emitter.off("tick", () => {...}) with a fresh arrow, you remove nothing — different function object. So the closures are captured into onResize/onTick fields at subscribe time and the exact same references are passed to off() in Destroy(). Forgetting this leaks a RAF-driven tick handler that keeps stepping physics on a torn-down world. The same pattern recurs in Sizes and in Camera (onManualCameraControl).
Note — fixed-timestep accumulator state. Two private fields back the per-frame loop: 
// Fixed-timestep accumulator state (see onTick): carries leftover real time
// between frames; maxStepsPerFrame caps catch-up so a long stall can't freeze.
private accumulator = 0;
private maxStepsPerFrame = 5;
accumulator holds real time not yet consumed by a fixed step; maxStepsPerFrame bounds how many 1/60 steps a single frame may run. Both are read only inside onTick (covered below).

public static GetInstance(): Experience

Purpose: the only sanctioned way to obtain the Experience. Parameters: none. Returns: the one-and-only instance, creating it on first call (lazy init). 

public static GetInstance(): Experience {
  if (!Experience.instance) {
    Experience.instance = new Experience();
  }
  return Experience.instance;
}

The classic lazy singleton. Because the constructor is empty, GetInstance() is cheap and side-effect-free — all the heavy assembly is deferred to Configure(). That split matters: subsystems like Renderer and Physics call GetInstance() in their constructors to read shared state, and those constructors run during Configure() — so the instance must already exist (it does, since App.vue calls GetInstance() first).

public async Configure(canvas: HTMLCanvasElement | null, resources: Resource[]): Promise<void>

Purpose: the real constructor. It exists as a separate async method because one subsystem — Physics — must await RAPIER.init() (a WASM load), and JavaScript constructors cannot be async. Parameters: canvas — the DOM canvas element from the Vue ref (may be null; it is stored as-is and the Renderer casts it); resources — the game's boot asset manifest (a Resource[]), injected by the caller so the engine names none of the game's assets. Returns: a promise that resolves once Rapier's WASM is initialized and both engine event handlers are subscribed.

The body opens with an idempotency guard — a second Configure() (an HMR reload, a restart) is warned-and-ignored rather than overwriting live subsystems and leaking the previous physics world: 

// Idempotent: a second Configure() (HMR / restart) is ignored rather than
// overwriting live subsystems and leaking the previous world. Destroy first.
if (this.isConfigured) {
  console.warn("Experience.Configure() ignored — already configured. Call Destroy() first.");
  return;
}
this.isConfigured = true;

The isConfigured flag is set true here and reset to false in Destroy(), so the only sanctioned way to re-run Configure() is to tear the engine down first. Then the body assembles the graph in a strict order. First the dependency-free leaves: 

this.Debug = new Debug();
this.Sizes = new Sizes();
this.Time = new Time();
this.Input = new Input();

Debug goes first because later subsystems (Physics, Camera) check this.experience.Debug.IsActive in their constructors to decide whether to wire debug overlays. Sizes and Time are pure leaves — they only touch window and the event bus.

Then the resource manifest — the one the caller passed in:

this.Resources = new ResourceLoader(resources);

The engine no longer hardcodes a randy/enemy/dkGraphicsData list here; it just forwards the injected resources array to the ResourceLoader. The actual manifest lives game-side in src/game/config/assetManifest.ts (exported as GAME_ASSETS), keeping the engine asset-name-blind. Note Configure builds the ResourceLoader but does not start loading; App.vue calls Resources.StartLoading() last, after every listener is wired.

Note — reusability seam. Because the manifest is a parameter rather than a literal, a different game can drive this same engine just by passing its own Resource[]. The Resource type is exported from resourceLoader.ts and imported here precisely so the signature can name it without naming any asset.

Then the rendering/physics quartet — order is load-bearing here:

this.TargetElement = canvas;

this.Scene = new THREE.Scene();
this.Camera = new Camera(new THREE.Vector3(0, 25, 100));
this.Renderer = new Renderer();
this.Physics = new Physics();
await this.Physics.Configure();

Line-level ordering notes (each later line depends on an earlier one):

  • TargetElement must be set before new Renderer(), because the Renderer constructor reads this.experience.TargetElement to build its WebGLRenderer({ canvas }).
  • Scene must exist before new Camera(...): the Camera constructor does this.scene.add(this.PerspectiveCamera).
  • Camera must exist before new Renderer(): the Renderer caches this.camera = this.experience.Camera and renders through this.camera.Instance.
  • The camera's new THREE.Vector3(0, 25, 100) is the engine's default initial position; the game's level data later teleports it via Camera.TeleportToPosition.
Hard-won victory — why Physics.Configure() is awaited. Rapier2D ships as WebAssembly; Physics.Configure() does await RAPIER.init() before it can build new RAPIER.World(...) (see physics.ts). If Experience.Configure did not await it, control would return to App.vue and the first "tick" could fire this.Physics.Update() against an undefined World — an instant crash. Awaiting here is what lets the rest of the boot (registering contact rules, constructing GameDirector) safely assume a live physics world. It is also why Configure itself had to become async.

Finally, the two engine event subscriptions — the closures captured into the stored refs:

// Sizes resize event — store ref for cleanup
this.onResize = () => {
  this.Camera.Resize();
  this.Renderer.Resize();
};
Emitter.on("resize", this.onResize);

// Time tick event — store ref for cleanup
this.onTick = () => {
  if (this.Debug.IsActive) {
    this.Debug.Stats?.begin();
  }

  // Fixed-timestep simulation. Accumulate real frame time and advance the sim
  // (physics + game update) in constant FixedTimestep steps, then render once.
  // This makes physics and movement frame-rate-independent and the sim
  // reproducible. FrameDelta is clamped in Time, so the per-frame step count is
  // bounded; maxStepsPerFrame is a final safety net.
  this.accumulator += this.Time.FrameDelta;
  const dt = Time.FixedTimestep;
  let steps = 0;
  while (this.accumulator >= dt && steps < this.maxStepsPerFrame) {
    this.Time.Delta = dt; // integrators read this as their timestep
    this.Physics.Update();
    this.OnGameUpdate?.();
    this.accumulator -= dt;
    steps++;
  }

  // Render once per frame, interpolated between the last two sim states by how
  // far we are into the next step (alpha in [0,1)). Buttery above the sim rate,
  // no judder below — render and sim rates are fully decoupled.
  const alpha = this.accumulator / dt;
  this.OnRenderUpdate?.(alpha);
  this.Renderer.Update();

  if (this.Debug.IsActive) {
    this.Debug.Stats?.end();
  }
};
Emitter.on("tick", this.onTick);

The onTick body is the engine's per-frame pipeline — now a fixed-timestep accumulator rather than a single pass. Each frame:

  1. Debug.Stats?.begin() — opens the stats.js FPS/MS sample window (optional-chained; Stats only exists when debug is active).
  2. accumulator += Time.FrameDelta — banks the real, clamped wall-clock time elapsed since the last frame (see time.ts).
  3. The while loop — while at least one fixed step's worth of time is banked (and we're under maxStepsPerFrame), it sets Time.Delta = FixedTimestep (the constant 1/60 the integrators read), runs Physics.Update() then OnGameUpdate?.(), and drains one dt from the accumulator. So physics + game logic run 0..N times per rendered frame, always at a constant step — the sim is frame-rate-independent and reproducible.
  4. const alpha = accumulator / dt then OnRenderUpdate?.(alpha) — once per frame, after the loop drains, it computes the interpolation factor (how far the leftover accumulator has progressed into the next fixed step, in [0,1)) and hands it to the per-frame render hook. The game uses alpha to lerp meshes between the last two physics states and to drive the camera off that interpolated position — smooth motion above the sim rate, no judder below.
  5. Renderer.Update() — draws Scene through Camera.Instance once per frame, after the sim has caught up.
  6. Debug.Stats?.end() — closes the sample window.
Hard-won victory — fixed timestep, variable render. Stepping physics by a constant 1/60 instead of the raw frame delta means a 144 Hz monitor and a 30 Hz one simulate identically — jump arcs, gravity, and contact resolution no longer drift with refresh rate, and a recorded input sequence replays deterministically. The render still runs once per frame, so high-refresh displays stay smooth. The two new private fields carry the state: accumulator (leftover real time between frames) and maxStepsPerFrame = 5 (a cap that prevents a "spiral of death" if a frame stalls — the sim falls behind in slow motion instead of locking up trying to catch up).
Gotcha — physics → game → render is non-negotiable. Within each step, physics runs before the game update, and the render happens only after the loop. Reorder these and you get a one-frame lag (rendering before the game moves its meshes) or stale collisions (game logic reading last frame's contacts). The pipeline reads as: simulate the world in fixed steps, let the game react each step, then paint once. 
flowchart TD
  RAF["Time RAF loop"] -->|emit tick| H["Experience.onTick"]
  H --> S1["Debug.Stats?.begin()"]
  S1 --> ACC["accumulator += FrameDelta"]
  ACC --> W{"accumulator >= 1/60 <br/>and steps < 5?"}
  W -->|yes| P["Physics.Update (Delta = 1/60)"]
  P --> G["OnGameUpdate?.()"]
  G --> D["accumulator -= 1/60"]
  D --> W
  W -->|no| AL["alpha = accumulator / 1/60"]
  AL --> OR["OnRenderUpdate?.(alpha)"]
  OR --> R["Renderer.Update (once)"]
  R --> S2["Debug.Stats?.end()"]
  P -.->|drains contacts| C["ContactRegistry.Dispatch"]
  G -.->|game wires| GD["InputBindings + GameDirector"]
  OR -.->|game wires| RD["InterpolateGraphics + Camera"]

public Destroy(): void

Purpose: unwind everything Configure() built, in an order that avoids touching a torn-down subsystem. Parameters: none. Returns: void. 

public Destroy() {
  // Remove our own event listeners first
  Emitter.off("resize", this.onResize);
  Emitter.off("tick", this.onTick);

  // Optional chaining so a Destroy after a partially-failed Configure (e.g. the
  // physics WASM init threw mid-init) tears down whatever exists instead of
  // cascading on the first missing subsystem.
  this.Sizes?.Destroy();
  this.Time?.Destroy();
  this.Input?.Destroy();

  // Camera then physics then renderer then resources
  this.Camera?.Destroy();
  this.Physics?.Destroy();
  this.Renderer?.Destroy();
  this.Resources?.Destroy();

  // Debug menu
  if (this.Debug && this.Debug.IsActive) {
    this.Debug.Destroy();
  }

  // Allow a fresh GetInstance()/Configure() after teardown (HMR, tests, restart).
  this.isConfigured = false;
  Experience.instance = undefined;
}

Line-level teardown order (the comments narrate the intent):

  • Own listeners first — off("resize") and off("tick") with the stored refs. This stops the pipeline before any subsystem it calls is freed, so no half-destroyed Physics/Renderer gets a final tick.
  • Time.Destroy() cancels the RAF (see time.ts) so no further "tick" is even emitted. Sizes.Destroy() removes the window resize listener.
  • Camera → Physics → Renderer → Resources, each optionally-chained (this.Camera?.Destroy()). Camera unsubscribes its manualCameraControl handler and removes itself from the scene; Physics frees the Rapier World (itself a guarded World?.free()); Renderer disposes the WebGL context; Resources releases GPU textures/geometries. The ?. matters because a Destroy() after a partially-failed Configure() (say the physics WASM init threw mid-boot) should tear down whatever did get built instead of cascading on the first missing subsystem.
  • Debug last, guarded by this.Debug && this.Debug.IsActive — its Destroy() tears down dat.GUI + the stats.js DOM node, which exist only when debug was active.
  • Reset the singleton last of all — isConfigured = false and Experience.instance = undefined, so the next GetInstance() news up a clean instance and Configure() can run again (HMR, tests, a restart) instead of reviving a gutted object.
Hard-won victory — a clean restart. Destroy() now resets the singleton: isConfigured = false and Experience.instance = undefined. A later GetInstance() therefore builds a fresh instance and Configure() runs cleanly again — exactly what HMR, tests, and a full restart need. Paired with the isConfigured guard at the top of Configure(), the contract is symmetric: you cannot double-configure a live engine, and you cannot get back a gutted one after teardown. (Level switching still goes through GameDirector, not here — this remains a full teardown, not a soft reset.)

Modifying this: to add a new engine subsystem, (1) declare a public Foo!: Foo field, (2) new it in Configure() at the right spot in the ordering — after anything it reads off the singleton in its constructor and before anything that reads it — and (3) call this.Foo.Destroy() in Destroy() in reverse-dependency order. If the subsystem subscribes to bus events, store the handler in a private field and off() it in its own Destroy() — never inline the arrow. Do not reach for a game class here; keep the engine name-blind by routing game logic through OnGameUpdate and the event bus.

src/engine/core/time.ts 64 LOC

What it is: the engine's single heartbeat. It wraps a THREE.Clock, owns the one and only requestAnimationFrame loop, computes a clamped per-frame FrameDelta (real wall-clock time), and emits the global "tick" event each frame. Who owns it: Experience.Configure() news up a single Time. Why these imports: THREE only for THREE.Clock (a tiny high-resolution elapsed-time helper); Emitter to broadcast "tick" so the engine never needs a reference to its listeners. Lifecycle: constructor schedules the first frame → tick() recurses every frame emitting "tick" → Destroy() cancels the RAF.

Note — the Delta split. Time now exposes two distinct durations. FrameDelta is the real, clamped time per rendered frame and drives Experience.onTick's accumulator. Delta is the simulation timestep the integrators read — the frame loop sets it to the constant FixedTimestep (1/60) before each fixed step. They are different numbers doing different jobs: FrameDelta measures wall time; Delta is the fixed step size physics integrates by.

Fields

// The fixed simulation timestep. The frame loop advances the sim in constant
// steps of this size so physics + gameplay feel are frame-rate-independent and
// reproducible (see Experience.onTick).
public static readonly FixedTimestep = 1 / 60;

public Clock: THREE.Clock;
public Elapsed: number;
// The SIMULATION timestep the integrators read (player/enemy/physics). The frame
// loop sets this to FixedTimestep before each fixed step, so all integration uses
// a constant dt regardless of display refresh rate. Never 0.
public Delta: number;
// The REAL wall-clock time since the last rendered frame (clamped). Drives the
// fixed-step accumulator only; NOT used for integration.
public FrameDelta: number;

private previous: number; // last frame's elapsed time, used for delta
private animationFrameId: number = 0;
  • FixedTimestep — the constant 1/60s simulation step, static readonly so the frame loop and any integrator can reference the one canonical value. This is the dt physics is stepped by.
  • Elapsed — total seconds since the clock started (monotonic).
  • Delta — the simulation timestep; the frame loop assigns it FixedTimestep before each fixed step, and physics + every integration calc downstream reads this. Never 0.
  • FrameDelta — the real, clamped seconds since the previous rendered frame; it feeds the accumulator in Experience.onTick and is not used for integration.
  • previous — last frame's Elapsed, the subtrahend for FrameDelta.
  • animationFrameId — the handle returned by requestAnimationFrame, kept so Destroy() can cancel the exact pending frame.

constructor()

Purpose: seed the clock and schedule the first frame — but not run a tick yet.

constructor() {
  this.Clock = new THREE.Clock();
  this.Elapsed = this.Clock.getElapsedTime();
  this.Delta = Time.FixedTimestep; // integrators default to the fixed step
  this.FrameDelta = 0;
  this.previous = 0;

  // Instead of calling tick() immediately, wait 1 frame for delta time subtraction
  this.animationFrameId = window.requestAnimationFrame(() => {
    this.tick();
  });
}
Hard-won victory — seed Delta = FixedTimestep, never 0. The integrators read Delta as their timestep, so it must be a sane non-zero value from the very first step — even before the accumulator loop has assigned it. Seeding it to the constant 1/60 (Time.FixedTimestep) guarantees a zero timestep can never reach physics. FrameDelta starts at 0 because there is no prior frame to measure against yet; the first real tick() fills it in.
Gotcha — the deliberate one-frame delay. The constructor schedules tick() via RAF rather than calling it inline. If it ran tick() immediately, previous would still be 0 while Elapsed is already a few milliseconds, producing a wrong first delta. Waiting one frame lets the clock advance so the first computed FrameDelta is a real inter-frame duration.

private tick(): void

Purpose: the per-frame core — refresh Elapsed, compute the clamped real FrameDelta, emit "tick", and re-arm the next frame. Parameters: none. Returns: void (drives side effects via the event bus and RAF). 

private tick() {
  this.Elapsed = this.Clock.getElapsedTime();
  // Clamp so a suspended tab doesn't produce a huge catch-up delta. This clamp
  // also bounds how many fixed steps a single frame can run — no spiral of death.
  this.FrameDelta = Math.min(this.Elapsed - this.previous, 1 / 30);
  this.previous = this.Elapsed;

  // A throw in any tick listener must NOT stop the loop: the next frame is
  // scheduled below regardless, so one transient bug can't black-screen the game.
  try {
    Emitter.emit("tick");
  } catch (error) {
    console.error("Frame loop error (loop continues):", error);
  }

  // Recursively keep calling tick
  this.animationFrameId = window.requestAnimationFrame(() => {
    this.tick();
  });
}

Line-level notes:

  • Elapsed = Clock.getElapsedTime() reads the monotonic clock first.
  • FrameDelta = Math.min(Elapsed - previous, 1/30) — the raw inter-frame wall time, clamped to a maximum of 1/30s (~33ms). Note this writes FrameDelta, not Delta — Delta is owned by the accumulator loop in Experience.onTick.
  • previous = Elapsed advances the baseline for next frame's subtraction.
  • Emitter.emit("tick") — the broadcast, now wrapped in try/catch. Its single engine subscriber is Experience.onTick; the game never subscribes to "tick" directly — it rides the OnGameUpdate hook inside that handler. This is what keeps the frame pipeline in one ordered place.
  • The trailing requestAnimationFrame re-arms the loop and overwrites animationFrameId with the new pending handle. Critically it sits outside the try, so the next frame is always scheduled.
Hard-won victory — clamp FrameDelta to 1/30. The comment: "a suspended tab doesn't produce a huge catch-up delta." A backgrounded tab pauses RAF; on return, Elapsed - previous could be many seconds. Since FrameDelta feeds the fixed-step accumulator, an unclamped value would queue hundreds of physics steps in one frame — a "spiral of death." Capping it bounds the per-frame step count (and onTick's maxStepsPerFrame is the final backstop), so the worst case is a brief slow-motion catch-up, never a freeze or physics explosion.
Hard-won victory — the loop survives a throwing listener. The emit("tick") is wrapped in try/catch but the re-arming requestAnimationFrame is deliberately outside it. Before this, a single exception thrown deep inside a tick subscriber (say, a game-logic bug) would propagate out of tick() and the loop would never reschedule — the screen froze. Now the error is logged and the next frame still fires, so one transient bug degrades gracefully instead of black-screening the game.

public Destroy(): void

Purpose: stop the heartbeat. Parameters: none. Returns: void.

public Destroy() {
  // Stop the animation frame loop
  window.cancelAnimationFrame(this.animationFrameId);
}

Cancels the pending frame by its stored handle. Because tick() reassigns animationFrameId every frame, this always targets the next scheduled callback — so the recursion stops cleanly and no further "tick" is emitted. Called by Experience.Destroy() early, so the pipeline halts before any subsystem is freed.

Modifying this: the fixed-timestep accumulator already lives downstream in Experience.onTick — Time's job is just to emit one "tick" per rendered frame with an honest, clamped FrameDelta. Keep the FrameDelta clamp (it bounds the per-frame step count) and keep Delta seeded to a non-zero value so an integrator that reads it before the first fixed step can't divide by or step by 0. Leave the reschedule outside the try/catch so a throwing listener can never stop the loop. If you change the emit name, update the lone subscriber in Experience.Configure and the event map in eventBus.

src/engine/core/sizes.ts 33 LOC

What it is: the viewport tracker. It captures window width/height and a capped device pixel ratio, listens for the DOM resize event, and re-broadcasts it as the engine "resize" event. Who owns it: Experience.Configure(). Why the one import: Emitter — the only thing it talks to besides window; it never reaches into Camera or Renderer, it just announces "the viewport changed" and lets them react. Lifecycle: constructor reads initial size + subscribes the DOM listener → handler updates fields and emits "resize" → Destroy() removes the DOM listener.

Fields

public Width: number;
public Height: number;
public PixelRatio: number;

private onResize: () => void; // stored handler so we can remove it later

Width/Height are read by Renderer (setSize) and Camera (aspect ratio). onResize is the stored handler ref — same off()-by-identity reasoning as in Experience, but here it's removeEventListener rather than Emitter.off.

constructor()

Purpose: snapshot the current viewport and wire the resize listener.

constructor() {
  this.Width = window.innerWidth;
  this.Height = window.innerHeight;
  this.PixelRatio = Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2);

  this.onResize = () => {
    this.Width = window.innerWidth;
    this.Height = window.innerHeight;
    this.PixelRatio = Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2);

    Emitter.emit("resize");
  };

  window.addEventListener("resize", this.onResize);
}

Line-level notes:

  • PixelRatio = Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2) — capped at 2. On a 3x phone or Retina display, rendering at full DPR triples the pixel count for negligible visual gain (and this is a pixel-art game where antialiasing is off anyway). Capping at 2 protects the fill-rate budget. The cap is applied both at init and in the handler, so dragging a window between monitors of different DPR is handled.
