  • The handler updates all three fields then Emitter.emit("resize") — so subscribers always read fresh numbers. The sole subscriber is Experience.onResize, which calls Camera.Resize() then Renderer.Resize() (camera recomputes its projection matrix before the renderer resizes its buffer).
Note — fan-out via the bus, not direct calls. Sizes could call Camera.Resize() itself, but it doesn't — it emits. That keeps Sizes a pure, game-and-render-agnostic leaf: anything that cares about viewport changes subscribes to "resize" without Sizes ever knowing it exists.

public Destroy(): void

Purpose: detach the DOM listener so a torn-down engine stops reacting to window resizes.

public Destroy() {
  window.removeEventListener("resize", this.onResize);
}

Passes the same this.onResize reference captured in the constructor — which is exactly why it was stored on a field. An inline arrow here would silently fail to remove the listener, leaking a handler that emits "resize" into a dead bus. Called from Experience.Destroy().

Modifying this: to support rendering into a sub-element rather than the full window, swap window.innerWidth/Height for a ResizeObserver on the target element — but keep the field-update-then-emit shape and the stored-handler/Destroy() contract intact. Leave the DPR cap; raising it above 2 noticeably hurts perf for no visual gain on this pixel-art renderer.

src/main.ts 5 LOC

What it is: the Vite entry point — the entire bootstrap is mounting the Vue app. Notably, it touches none of the engine directly: it mounts App.vue, and App.vue is where the engine actually boots (inside onMounted). This is the cleanest possible seam between "load the page" and "start the game engine." 

import { createApp } from 'vue'
import './style.css'
import App from './App.vue'

createApp(App).mount('#app')

Line-level notes:

  • import './style.css' — pulls global styles (Tailwind layers) into the bundle as a side-effect import.
  • createApp(App).mount('#app') — mounts the root component onto the #app element in index.html. Everything engine-side happens later, triggered by App.vue's onMounted.
Note — where the engine actually starts. The real boot sequence lives in App.vue → onMounted, and its ordering is itself a hard-won design. Reproduced here because it is the canonical example of consuming Experience: 
const webglExperience = Experience.GetInstance();
// The game supplies its own asset manifest; the engine names none of these.
await webglExperience.Configure(webglRef.value, GAME_ASSETS);

// The game injects its level-node type vocabulary into the engine GLB parser.
// Must run before any level loads (StartLoading below triggers the first).
webglExperience.Resources.RegisterLevelTypes(LEVEL_NODE_TYPES);

registerContactRules(webglExperience.Physics.Contacts);
webglExperience.Debug.RegisterCollisionLogSink(new CollisionLogger());
webglExperience.Debug.SetPhysicsTypeColors(DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS);

const gameDirector = new GameDirector();
const inputBindings = new InputBindings(webglExperience.Input);
webglExperience.Debug.RegisterModule(new LevelDebug(gameDirector));

webglExperience.OnGameUpdate = () => {
  inputBindings.Update();
  gameDirector.Update();
};
// ...event listeners wired...
webglExperience.Resources.StartLoading();
Hard-won victory — boot ordering in App.vue. Three ordering decisions make this robust, each guarding against a fire-before-listen race:
  • Loading-screen listeners are registered FIRST, before Configure, so a fast boot can't emit loadingFinished before anyone is listening.
  • OnGameUpdate is assigned only after GameDirector / InputBindings exist — which is only possible because Configure was awaited, so Scene/Camera/Physics already exist when the game director's constructor runs.
  • Resources.StartLoading() is called LAST, after every resourcesReady/loadingProgress listener is wired, so the completion event can't fire before its listener exists.
The whole sequence is a study in "subscribe before you can possibly emit."

Modifying this: keep main.ts a thin mount. New engine wiring belongs in App.vue's onMounted, between await Configure() and StartLoading(); preserve the "listeners first, OnGameUpdate after construction, loading last" ordering.

Events & Input — the mitt bus and the keyboard layer

Two of the engine's smallest files carry an outsized share of its decoupling. eventBus.ts is a single typed mitt emitter that lets any subsystem talk to any other without holding a reference — the engine half of the map never names a game concept, the game half does. input.ts is the raw keyboard device: it captures keydown/keyup into held-state booleans and snapshots them into a plain InputState bag, so controllers read input as data instead of polling the live device.

src/engine/events/eventBus.ts src/engine/input/input.ts src/engine/types/inputSource.ts src/engine/types/inputState.ts

src/engine/events/eventBus.ts 45 LOC

This file IS the application's single global event bus — one mitt emitter instance, exported as a default named Emitter. Nobody constructs it; it is a module-level singleton created the first time the module is imported, and every import Emitter from ".../eventBus" across the codebase resolves to that same object. It imports exactly one thing — GameObject — and only so the gameObjectRemoved event can carry a strongly-typed payload. Its entire job is to publish a typed event map so that Emitter.emit("tick") and Emitter.on("tick", fn) are compile-checked: a typo in an event name, or a payload of the wrong shape, is a build error rather than a silent no-op.

Note. The map is deliberately split into two halves — EngineEventMap and GameEventMap — that are then merged. This is the same engine/game seam that runs through the whole project (see Engine Core — Experience and Game Director). The engine half is allowed to be imported by a fresh project with no game; the game half is the Donkey-Kong vocabulary layered on top. Keeping them as two named types documents which events a reusable engine may legitimately fire.

type EngineEventMap = { tick; resize; resourcesReady; resourceLoadFailed; loadingProgress }

The engine-pure events. Every value type here is the payload that travels with the event: void means "no payload, this is a pure signal." None of these names mention a game concept, which is the invariant that lets the engine layer stay game-agnostic. 

type EngineEventMap = {
  tick: void;
  resize: void;
  resourcesReady: void;
  resourceLoadFailed: string;

  // Per-asset progress for the initial resource load (textures/models)...
  // Engine-pure: `item` is a generic resource key, never a game concept.
  loadingProgress: { loaded: number; total: number; item: string };
};
EventPayloadEmitted byListened by
tickvoidTime (time.ts) once per requestAnimationFrameExperience (experience.ts:98) — drives the whole frame
resizevoidSizes (sizes.ts) on window resizeExperience (experience.ts:82) → calls Camera.Resize() + Renderer.Resize()
resourcesReadyvoidResourceLoader (resourceLoader.ts:118,140) when the last asset settlesGameDirector (gameDirector.ts:100) — boots the game
resourceLoadFailedstring (the failed asset's name)ResourceLoader (resourceLoader.ts:129)no current listener — the hook exists for a future error UI
loadingProgress{ loaded, total, item }ResourceLoader (resourceLoader.ts:81,110,132)App.vue (App.vue:40) — drives the real progress bar
Tip. loadingProgress.item is typed as string, a generic resource key, on purpose — the comment in the source calls this out explicitly. The engine never knows it is loading "randy" or "dkGraphicsData"; it only forwards the name it was handed. That is what keeps the progress event engine-pure.

type GameEventMap = { gameObjectRemoved; gameStart; gameOver; gameWin; gameReset; switchLevel; manualCameraControl; loadingStarted; loadingFinished }

The game-specific vocabulary: flow control, level switching, and the loading-overlay bracket. The one payload here is gameObjectRemoved: GameObject — the single reason this file imports GameObject. 

type GameEventMap = {
  gameObjectRemoved: GameObject;

  gameStart: void;
  gameOver: void;
  gameWin: void;
  gameReset: void;

  switchLevel: void;
  manualCameraControl: void;

  // Loading-screen signals... Emitted by GameDirector around level loads.
  loadingStarted: void;
  loadingFinished: void;
};
EventPayloadEmitted byListened by
gameObjectRemovedGameObjectcontactRules (enemy→trashcan, contactRules.ts:38), Player.Destroy (player.ts:480), GameDirector teardown (gameDirector.ts:281,356)GameDirector (gameDirector.ts:136) — disposes the entity
gameStartvoidGameDirector (gameDirector.ts:435)App.vue (:102) + GameDirector (:106)
gameOvervoidcontactRules enemy↔player (contactRules.ts:28)App.vue (:84) + GameDirector (:112)
gameWinvoidcontactRules winflag sensor (contactRules.ts:66)App.vue (:76) + GameDirector (:119)
gameResetvoidInputBindings F1 (inputBindings.ts:29), App.vue button (:118)App.vue (:92) + GameDirector (:125)
switchLevelvoidInputBindings F2 (inputBindings.ts:35)GameDirector (:131)
manualCameraControlvoidInputBindings F3 (inputBindings.ts:41)Camera (camera.ts:95)
loadingStartedvoidGameDirector (gameDirector.ts:389)App.vue (:31) — shows overlay
loadingFinishedvoidGameDirector (gameDirector.ts:444)App.vue (:37) — hides overlay

type AppEvents = EngineEventMap & GameEventMap;

The full event surface is the intersection-type merge of both maps. As the source comment notes, the & here "just means all the keys from both maps" — for two object types with disjoint keys, the TypeScript intersection is the union of their fields. mitt<AppEvents>() is then parameterised on this combined map so both engine and game events are typed by the one emitter. 

type AppEvents = EngineEventMap & GameEventMap;

const Emitter = mitt<AppEvents>();

export default Emitter;
Gotcha. Emitter is a true singleton because ES modules are evaluated once and cached. That is the feature — every subsystem shares one bus — but it also means listeners leak across the object lifecycle unless explicitly removed. Long-lived owners therefore store their handler refs and call Emitter.off(name, ref) in their Destroy(): see Experience (experience.ts:103-104), Camera (camera.ts:329), ResourceLoader (resourceLoader.ts:524), and GameDirector (gameDirector.ts:536-541). An anonymous inline listener cannot be removed by name — that is why these classes hoist the callback into a stored field.
Note. One asymmetry worth remembering: GameDirector.Destroy calls off for resourcesReady, gameStart, gameOver, gameReset, switchLevel, and gameObjectRemoved — but not gameWin, and manualCameraControl is owned/removed by Camera instead. If you add a new long-lived listener, add the matching off in the same owner's teardown or it will accumulate across resets.

Modifying this: to add an event, put it in the correct half of the map — engine-pure signals go in EngineEventMap (no game words allowed), game-flow signals go in GameEventMap. Give it a payload type, never leave it untyped; use void for a pure signal. Then emit with Emitter.emit("name", payload) and subscribe with Emitter.on("name", fn), and — if the subscriber outlives a single load — store the handler ref and remove it in Destroy(). Do not add a second emitter instance; the whole design depends on one shared bus.

src/engine/input/input.ts 290 LOC

This file IS the raw keyboard device. It is constructed once by Experience in Configure() (experience.ts:55, this.Input = new Input()) and exposed as Experience.Input. It imports the InputState type plus the InputSource interface it implements — it still has no idea what a "player" or a "jump" means; it only knows that certain physical keys are up or down right now. It registers two window listeners in its constructor, exposes a pile of held-state booleans and combo helper methods, and offers one snapshot method (CaptureInto) that copies the current intent into a plain data bag. Lifecycle: constructor wires listeners; there is no per-frame Update() (the device is event-driven, not polled); Destroy() unhooks the window listeners.

Note. The engine Input exposes only held-state — booleans that are true for as long as the key is down. It deliberately knows nothing about press edges (the false→true transition of a single press) and nothing about game events. Edge detection and the mapping to game meaning live one layer up, in InputBindings (see Game Config — InputBindings) and in the player state handlers.
Note (the InputSource seam). Input now formally implements InputSource — a one-method engine interface (CaptureInto(state: InputState): void, engine/types/inputSource.ts) that names the capture seam. The payoff is at the consumer, not here: Player types its inputDevice as InputSource rather than as the concrete keyboard device, so a touch, gamepad, network, or replay source drops in just by implementing CaptureInto — no entity edits, because nothing depends on the keyboard class. 
// engine/types/inputSource.ts — the one method Player actually depends on
export default interface InputSource {
  // Fill the given InputState snapshot from this source for the current frame.
  CaptureInto(state: InputState): void;
}

// input.ts — the default (keyboard) implementation
export default class Input implements InputSource { ... }

interface Key { keyCode: string; isPressed: (pressed: boolean) => void; }

A private file-local interface describing one tracked key: its physical keyCode and a setter callback that writes the matching held-state field. Using KeyboardEvent.code (e.g. "KeyW", "Space", "Backquote") rather than .key makes the mapping layout-independent: W is the physical W slot regardless of QWERTY/AZERTY or the active locale. 

interface Key {
  keyCode: string; // KeyboardEvent.code, layout-independent (e.g. "KeyW", "Space")
  isPressed: (pressed: boolean) => void; // Input calls this when the key changes
}

// fields: private held-state + public per-key flags + private keys[] table

Two tiers of state. The private booleans back the directional combo logic and are never read directly by outside code — isLeftKeyPressed, isRightKeyPressed, isUpKeyPressed, isDownKeyPressed, isSpacebarPressed. The public booleans are read directly by debug/binding code that wants a single raw key — IsWKeyPressed/IsAKeyPressed/ IsSKeyPressed/IsDKeyPressed (camera fly, read by CameraDebug), IsQKeyPressed/IsEKeyPressed, Is1KeyPressed/Is2KeyPressed, IsTildePressed, and IsF1Pressed…IsF4Pressed (read by InputBindings). 

// private held-state, refined by the Is* combo checks below
private isLeftKeyPressed: boolean;
private isRightKeyPressed: boolean;
private isUpKeyPressed: boolean;
private isDownKeyPressed: boolean;
private isSpacebarPressed: boolean;

public IsWKeyPressed: boolean;  // ... A/S/D, Q/E, 1/2
public IsTildePressed: boolean;
public IsF1Pressed: boolean;    // ... F2/F3/F4

private keys: Key[]; // key-code -> pressed-flag setter table
Tip. Why is left/right/up/down private but W/A/S/D public? The arrow keys feed the combo-resolving methods (IsLeft(), IsLeftRightCombo() …) — outsiders are meant to ask the question ("am I moving left?"), not read the raw flag, so the raw flag is hidden. The WASD/F-key flags have no combo semantics; they are single raw keys, so they are exposed directly.

constructor()

No parameters. Zeroes every held-state and public flag to false, builds the keys table mapping each tracked keyCode to the closure that sets its flag, then stores bound listener references and registers them on window. The closures capture this, which is why each one is an arrow function. 

this.keys = [
  { keyCode: "ArrowLeft",  isPressed: (eventResult) => { this.isLeftKeyPressed = eventResult; } },
  { keyCode: "ArrowRight", isPressed: (eventResult) => { this.isRightKeyPressed = eventResult; } },
  // ... ArrowUp/ArrowDown, Space, KeyW/A/S/D, Digit1/2, KeyQ/E, Backquote, F1..F4
];

// Store bound references so they can be removed in destroy()
this.onKeyDownListener = (event: KeyboardEvent) => this.onKeyDown(event.code);
this.onKeyUpListener = (event: KeyboardEvent) => this.onKeyUp(event.code);

window.addEventListener("keydown", this.onKeyDownListener, false);
window.addEventListener("keyup", this.onKeyUpListener, false);
Hard-won victory. The two listeners are hoisted into onKeyDownListener / onKeyUpListener fields before being registered, precisely so Destroy() can pass the same function identity to removeEventListener. If the constructor had registered an inline arrow (window.addEventListener("keydown", e => this.onKeyDown(e.code))), the listener could never be removed — every engine teardown/rebuild would stack another live keyboard handler on window, and stale Input instances would keep mutating after they were meant to be dead.
Note. Listeners are bound to window, not the canvas, so input works regardless of focus within the page. Only fifteen physical keys are tracked; everything else falls through both loops untouched.

private onKeyDown(keyName: string) / private onKeyUp(keyName: string)

The two raw event handlers. Each receives event.code (already unwrapped by the bound listener), scans the keys table for a matching keyCode, and calls that entry's isPressed setter with true (down) or false (up). A linear scan over ~15 entries per keystroke is trivially cheap and keeps the table the single source of truth for which keys exist. 

private onKeyDown(keyName: string) {
  for (const key of this.keys) {
    if (keyName === key.keyCode) {
      key.isPressed(true);
    }
  }
}

Line note: there is no break after a match — harmless because keyCode values are unique, so at most one entry ever fires. Unrecognised keys simply complete the loop with no effect.

IsLeft() / IsRight() / IsLeftRightCombo() / IsNeitherLeftRight()

The horizontal intent resolvers. These collapse the two raw arrow flags into four mutually-exclusive questions. IsLeft() is true only when left is held and right is not — pressing both at once is its own distinct state (IsLeftRightCombo()), not "left wins." This matters because the player's running/jumping handlers treat "both pressed" and "neither pressed" the same way (decelerate), and they need to distinguish that from a clean single direction. 

public IsLeft() {
  if (this.isLeftKeyPressed && !this.isRightKeyPressed) {
    return true;
  }
  return false;
}
public IsLeftRightCombo() {
  if (this.isLeftKeyPressed && this.isRightKeyPressed) {
    return true;
  }
  return false;
}
Tip. The four methods partition the (left, right) truth table into exactly four cases — IsLeft, IsRight, IsLeftRightCombo, IsNeitherLeftRight — so a state handler can switch on intent without re-deriving the boolean algebra. IsJump() is the simple odd one out: it just mirrors isSpacebarPressed with no combo logic.

IsUp() / IsDown() / IsUpDownCombo() / IsNeitherUpDown() / IsJump()

The vertical equivalents, used for ladder climbing, plus the jump query. Same exclusive partitioning as the horizontal set: IsUp() requires up held and down clear, etc. IsJump() returns the raw spacebar held-state — edge handling (coyote time, jump buffering, variable jump height) is entirely the player layer's job, not the device's. 

public IsUp() {
  if (this.isUpKeyPressed && !this.isDownKeyPressed) {
    return true;
  }
  return false;
}
public IsJump() {
  if (this.isSpacebarPressed) {
    return true;
  }
  return false;
}

public CaptureInto(state: InputState): void

The decoupling seam. Given an existing InputState bag, it calls each of the nine intent methods and writes the result into the matching field — mutating the passed object in place, never allocating a new one. The Player calls this once per frame, before its state handlers read intent (player.ts:470, this.inputDevice.CaptureInto(this.Input)). 

// Snapshot input intent into InputState (mutated in place so existing refs stay valid).
// Per-entity input instead of the live device — the seam for network/replay/AI input.
public CaptureInto(state: InputState): void {
  state.isLeft = this.IsLeft();
  state.isRight = this.IsRight();
  state.isLeftRightCombo = this.IsLeftRightCombo();
  state.isNeitherLeftRight = this.IsNeitherLeftRight();
  state.isJump = this.IsJump();
  state.isUp = this.IsUp();
  state.isDown = this.IsDown();
  state.isUpDownCombo = this.IsUpDownCombo();
  state.isNeitherUpDown = this.IsNeitherUpDown();
}
Hard-won victory (D10 — input-as-data). Mutating in place rather than returning a fresh object means any reference already held to player.Input stays valid frame-to-frame — no churn, no dangling old snapshots. More importantly, because the player reads a plain InputState bag rather than calling the live device, the source of that bag can be swapped wholesale — a network packet, a replay buffer, or an AI controller could fill the same fields and the movement code would not change a line. The player keeps its own inputDevice ref separately (player.ts:135) purely to drive this one capture.

public Destroy()

Removes both window listeners by passing the stored bound references. Called from Experience.Destroy (experience.ts:109). No flags are reset — once destroyed the instance is discarded, so the only thing that matters is detaching from window so no zombie handler keeps firing. 

public Destroy() {
  window.removeEventListener("keydown", this.onKeyDownListener);
  window.removeEventListener("keyup", this.onKeyUpListener);
}

How the keyboard becomes a game event each frame:

sequenceDiagram
  participant W as window
  participant I as Input
  participant IB as InputBindings
  participant P as Player
  participant E as Emitter
  W->>I: keydown / keyup (event.code)
  I->>I: keys[] match -> set held flag
  Note over IB,P: each frame via Experience.OnGameUpdate
  IB->>I: read IsF1Pressed / IsF2Pressed / IsF3Pressed
  IB->>IB: edge = pressed && !wasPressed
  IB->>E: emit("gameReset" / "switchLevel" / "manualCameraControl")
  P->>I: CaptureInto(player.Input)
  I-->>P: nine intent booleans written in place
  P->>P: state handlers read player.Input.isLeft ...

Modifying this: to track a new key, add one entry to the keys table (a unique KeyboardEvent.code plus a setter) and declare the backing field — public for a raw single key, private if it participates in a new combo group. If it should produce game intent consumed by entities, add a field to InputState and a line to CaptureInto; if it should fire a game event, do the edge detection in InputBindings, not here. Resist adding edge or game logic to this file — its value is that it stays a dumb, game-blind device.

src/engine/types/inputState.ts 18 LOC

This file IS the data contract between the input device and whatever consumes intent. It is a pure interface — no class, no runtime code — describing a flat bag of nine booleans. Input.CaptureInto writes it; entity controllers read it. Nobody "owns" the type; instances are created by consumers (the Player builds one in initializePlayerAttributes, player.ts:140) and then refilled each frame.

export default interface InputState { isLeft; isRight; isLeftRightCombo; isNeitherLeftRight; isJump; isUp; isDown; isUpDownCombo; isNeitherUpDown }

Three logical groups: horizontal intent (4 booleans), the single jump intent, and vertical/ladder intent (4 booleans). The field names mirror the device's intent methods exactly — IsLeft() the method becomes isLeft the field — so reading intent costs one property access and no parentheses. 

export default interface InputState {
  // Horizontal intent (mutually-exclusive helpers mirror the input device)
  isLeft: boolean;
  isRight: boolean;
  isLeftRightCombo: boolean;
  isNeitherLeftRight: boolean;

  // Jump intent
  isJump: boolean;

  // Vertical intent (ladders)
  isUp: boolean;
  isDown: boolean;
  isUpDownCombo: boolean;
  isNeitherUpDown: boolean;
}
FieldGroupFilled fromRead by
isLeft / isRighthorizontalInput.IsLeft() / IsRight()running / jumping / falling handlers (move)
isLeftRightCombo / isNeitherLeftRighthorizontalIsLeftRightCombo() / IsNeitherLeftRight()handlers (decelerate when both/neither)
isJumpjumpInput.IsJump()idle/running (buffer jump), jumping/falling (variable height)
isUp / isDownverticalIsUp() / IsDown()idle/running (mount ladder), climbing handler
isUpDownCombo / isNeitherUpDownverticalIsUpDownCombo() / IsNeitherUpDown()climbing handler (hold position)
Note. The source's own header comment spells out the rename rationale: a handler reads player.input.isLeft where it used to call player.input.isLeft() — "same value, one less pair of parens." The deeper win is that the consumer no longer touches the live device at all, which is the input-as-data seam (D10) described under CaptureInto above.

Modifying this: add a field here only for a piece of intent an entity reads (e.g. a "dash" boolean). Keep it a flat, allocation-free bag of primitives — no methods, no nested objects — so CaptureInto stays a straight line-per-field copy and the per-entity snapshot remains cheap to mutate in place every frame. Whenever you add a field here, add the matching write in Input.CaptureInto, or the field will silently stay at its initial value.

Rendering — Renderer & SpriteAnimator

Two small, single-responsibility files own everything the player actually sees. renderer.ts is a thin lifecycle wrapper around one THREE.WebGLRenderer — it is created by the Experience singleton, rendered once per tick, resized on resize, and disposed on teardown. spriteAnimator.ts is a per-entity helper that turns a single static sprite-sheet texture into a flip-book: it slices the sheet into a tile grid and walks a THREE.SpriteMaterial's UV offset frame-by-frame. Both files share one obsessive theme — pixel-perfect, never-blurry rendering — and both encode that as inline CRITICAL comments rather than decision-codes.

src/engine/rendering/renderer.ts src/engine/rendering/spriteAnimator.ts

src/engine/rendering/renderer.ts 58 LOC

This file IS the engine's window onto the GPU — a wrapper that owns exactly one THREE.WebGLRenderer and nothing else. It is constructed by the Experience singleton (see Engine Core — Experience): this.Renderer = new Renderer(), immediately after the Scene and Camera exist and just before Physics. It imports Experience only to pull engine singletons out of the one global wiring hub, Sizes for width/height/pixel-ratio, and Camera because rendering needs a viewpoint — it does not import the game layer at all, which is what keeps it reusable. Its lifecycle is deliberately minimal: constructor grabs refs and builds the renderer, Resize() re-fits it to the window, Update() draws one frame, Destroy() frees the GL context.

Note. The renderer never subscribes to the event bus itself. The Experience owns the tick and resize subscriptions and calls Renderer.Update() / Renderer.Resize() directly. That keeps this class a passive device the coordinator drives, not an actor that wires itself into the world — the same pattern every engine subsystem follows.

private experience; private canvas; private sizes; private scene; private camera; private Instance!: THREE.WebGLRenderer

Five cached references plus the raw renderer. Everything except Instance is read straight off the Experience in the constructor, so this object never re-resolves a singleton at runtime. Instance is the only thing this class actually owns and is marked ! (definite-assignment) because it is built in setInstance() rather than inline. 

private experience: Experience;
private canvas: HTMLElement;
private sizes: Sizes;
private scene: THREE.Scene;
private camera: Camera;

private Instance!: THREE.WebGLRenderer; // the raw THREE WebGL renderer

Note scene is a THREE.Scene (the engine-shared draw list) while camera is the engine's own Camera wrapper — so rendering reaches through this.camera.Instance for the underlying THREE camera (see Camera).

constructor()

No parameters. Resolves the singleton, caches the canvas/sizes/scene/camera off it, then builds and sizes the renderer. The order matters: setInstance() creates the GL context bound to the canvas, and Resize() immediately fits it to the current window — there is no first-frame at the wrong size. 

this.experience = Experience.GetInstance();
this.canvas = this.experience.TargetElement as HTMLElement;
this.sizes = this.experience.Sizes;
this.scene = this.experience.Scene;
this.camera = this.experience.Camera;

this.setInstance();
this.Resize();

TargetElement is cast to HTMLElement because the Experience stores it generically; the WebGL renderer wants a concrete canvas element. Reading Sizes here (not window directly) is the seam that lets Sizes own the single source of truth for dimensions and pixel ratio.

private setInstance()

Builds and configures the one THREE.WebGLRenderer. This is the most opinionated method in the file: every option here exists to make pixel art crisp. 

this.Instance = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({
  canvas: this.canvas,
  // CRITICAL: Antialiasing MUST be disabled for pixel art!
  // Antialiasing smooths edges which makes pixel art look blurry...
  antialias: false,
});

this.Instance.setClearColor("#211d20");
this.Instance.setSize(this.sizes.Width, this.sizes.Height);
this.Instance.setPixelRatio(this.sizes.PixelRatio);

Line by line: canvas binds the GL context to the existing DOM canvas (rather than letting THREE create a detached one). setClearColor("#211d20") paints the background — a near-black warm charcoal — every frame before drawing. setSize sets the drawing-buffer resolution from Sizes, and setPixelRatio applies the (capped) device pixel ratio so HiDPI displays render sharp without over-rendering.

Hard-won victory: antialias: false. This is the single most important line in the renderer. MSAA smooths triangle edges, which on a magnified pixel-art sprite reads as a shimmering, blurry halo that crawls during motion. Disabling it is what makes a 16×-scaled firefighter look like crisp pixels instead of a smear. It is the renderer-side half of a fix that the SpriteAnimator completes with NearestFilter — you need both off/nearest or the art still blurs.
Gotcha. setPixelRatio must come after (or alongside) setSize, and both read from Sizes. If you ever set the renderer size from window.innerWidth directly you fork the source of truth and the canvas can desync from the camera's aspect on resize. Always go through Sizes.

public Resize()

Re-fits the renderer to the current window. Called by the Experience's resize handler (which also calls Camera.Resize()). It simply re-applies size and pixel ratio from the freshly updated Sizes values. 

this.Instance?.setSize(this.sizes.Width, this.sizes.Height);
this.Instance?.setPixelRatio(this.sizes.PixelRatio);

The ?. optional chaining guards the one race where Resize() could fire before Instance exists. In practice the constructor builds it first, but the guard keeps a stray early resize from throwing. Pixel ratio is re-applied here too, not just in setInstance, because dragging a window between a HiDPI and a standard monitor changes window.devicePixelRatio live.

public Update()

Draws exactly one frame: the shared scene viewed through the active camera. This is the engine's render call, invoked once per tick by the Experience. 

this.Instance?.render(this.scene, this.camera.Instance);

this.camera.Instance reaches past the engine's Camera wrapper to the underlying THREE camera that render() requires. The crucial detail is when this runs in the frame: the Experience's tick handler runs Physics.Update() first (which moves bodies and lets each game object sync its mesh to its physics transform), then the game's OnGameUpdate hook, then Renderer.Update() last — so the renderer always draws the world's final, settled state for that frame. 

sequenceDiagram
    participant T as Time (tick)
    participant E as Experience.onTick
    participant P as Physics.Update
    participant G as OnGameUpdate
    participant R as Renderer.Update
    T->>E: emit "tick"
    E->>P: step world, sync meshes
    E->>G: game logic / camera follow
    E->>R: render(scene, camera.Instance)
    R-->>T: frame drawn
Gotcha: render is last for a reason. If you ever move Renderer.Update() before Physics.Update(), you draw last frame's positions — one-frame visual lag and jitter on fast-moving barrels. The ordering in Experience's onTick is load-bearing.

public Destroy()

Frees the GPU resources held by the renderer. Called by the Experience's Destroy() in the documented teardown order (camera → physics → renderer → resources). 

// Dispose of the WebGL renderer
this.Instance.dispose();

dispose() releases the WebGL context and its GPU buffers. Note this does not dispose geometries, materials, or textures in the scene — those belong to the individual GameObjects and the ResourceLoader, each of which disposes its own (e.g. the SpriteAnimator below disposes its texture and material). Renderer only owns the context, so it only frees the context.

Modifying this: treat the four pixel-art settings as a unit — if you turn antialias back on you reintroduce blur, and changing setPixelRatio away from the Sizes value forks the source of truth. To add post-processing you would introduce an EffectComposer and swap Update()'s render for composer.render() (and resize the composer in Resize()). To change the background, edit the one setClearColor hex. Keep this file game-agnostic: no game import belongs here.

src/engine/rendering/spriteAnimator.ts 139 LOC

This file IS the engine's flip-book player: it takes one static sprite-sheet THREE.Texture, treats it as a grid of equal tiles, and animates a THREE.SpriteMaterial by sliding its UV offset from tile to tile over time. It imports only three — it knows nothing about players, states, or the game. One SpriteAnimator is constructed and owned per animated entity; for the hero it is built in Player.setSpriteAnimator() as new SpriteAnimator(this.Resources.Items.randy, 4, 6) (see Player). Lifecycle: constructor caches the grid dimensions and builds the material; ChangeState() swaps which frames play; ChangeAnimationTiming() scales playback speed; Update(deltaTime) advances the frame; Destroy() disposes the texture and material.

interface SpriteState { indicies: number[]; timing: number[] }

The data shape that drives one animation. indicies is the ordered list of tile ids to show (the misspelling is kept on purpose, per the comment, so it matches the rest of the codebase). timing is the parallel array of seconds each frame holds — same length as indicies, index-aligned. The game supplies these as a static table in spriteAnimations.ts (see Player); the engine never hard-codes any frame numbers. 

interface SpriteState {
  indicies: number[]; // tile indices, left-to-right top-to-bottom from 0
  timing: number[];   // seconds per frame; same length as indicies
}

How a sheet becomes a flip-book

The animator never crops or copies pixels. The texture stays whole; what moves is a small UV window. In the constructor the material's repeat is set to (1/tilesHorizontal, 1/tilesVertical) — for Randy that is (1/4, 1/6), a window exactly one tile wide and one tile tall. Every frame, Update() recomputes the window's offset so it lands on the desired tile. Here is the real sheet the offsets walk:

Randy is a 4×6 grid (24 tiles, ids 0..23), numbered left-to-right then top-to-bottom from 0 in the top-left. So row 0 is ids 0,1,2,3; row 1 is 4,5,6,7; and so on down to row 5 = 20,21,22,23. The empty cells are intentional gaps — the game's SpriteAnimations table only references the occupied ones:

AnimationindiciesSheet locationtiming (s)
IDLE_LEFT[1, 2]row 0, cols 1&2[5, 0.1]
IDLE_RIGHT[5, 6]row 1, cols 1&2[0.1, 5]
RUN_LEFT[8, 9, 10, 11]row 2, all 4[0.25×4]
RUN_RIGHT[12, 13, 14, 15]row 3, all 4[0.25×4]
JUMP_LEFT / FALL_LEFT[17, 18]row 4, cols 1&2[0.25, 0.25]
JUMP_RIGHT / FALL_RIGHT[21, 22]row 5, cols 1&2[0.25, 0.25]
Tip: the blink. Idle is a two-frame loop where one frame holds for 5 s and the other for 0.1 s — that 0.1 s frame is the eyes-closed tile, so Randy blinks once every ~5 seconds while standing. Notice IDLE_LEFT is [5, 0.1] but IDLE_RIGHT is [0.1, 5]: the long-hold frame is the second entry on the right side because the open-eyes tile happens to be the second id (6) there. The timing array is what encodes "which tile is the resting pose."

private currentTile; elapsedTime; runningTileIndex; tilesHorizontal; tilesVertical; timingMultiplier; public State; public Material!

Two indices are easy to confuse, so the field names are precise: currentTile is the tile id on the sheet (e.g. 13), while runningTileIndex is the position within State.indicies (e.g. the 2nd entry). elapsedTime accumulates deltaTime toward the current frame's duration. tilesHorizontal/tilesVertical are the grid dims. timingMultiplier scales every frame duration to speed up or slow down playback. State and Material are the only public members — the game reads/writes State and the entity's mesh binds to Material.

constructor(spriteSheet: THREE.Texture, tilesHorizontal: number, tilesVertical: number)

Parameters: the sheet texture (Randy's randy texture from ResourceLoader), and the grid dimensions. Zeroes the playback cursors, stores the grid, initialises State to an empty animation, sets timingMultiplier = 1, then builds the material. 

this.currentTile = 0;
this.elapsedTime = 0;
this.runningTileIndex = 0;
this.tilesHorizontal = tilesHorizontal;
this.tilesVertical = tilesVertical;

this.State = { indicies: [], timing: [] };
this.timingMultiplier = 1;

this.setMaterial(spriteSheet);

The empty starting State means a freshly built animator shows tile 0 (top-left) and does not advance until the game assigns a real state — which the Player does immediately after construction: this.SpriteAnimator.State = SpriteAnimations.IDLE_RIGHT.

private setMaterial(spriteSheet: THREE.Texture)

Builds the THREE.SpriteMaterial and locks in pixel-perfect filtering — the SpriteAnimator's half of the crispness fix that Renderer starts with antialias: false. 

spriteSheet.colorSpace = THREE.SRGBColorSpace;

this.Material = new THREE.SpriteMaterial({ map: spriteSheet });

this.Material.map!.magFilter = THREE.NearestFilter; // scaling UP: hard edges
this.Material.map!.minFilter = THREE.NearestFilter; // scaling DOWN: no blur
this.Material.map!.anisotropy = 1;                  // minimum: no angle filtering

// Show one tile from the sheet at a time
this.Material.map!.repeat.set(
  1 / this.tilesHorizontal,
  1 / this.tilesVertical,
);

colorSpace = SRGBColorSpace keeps the art's colours correct through THREE's colour pipeline. repeat.set(1/4, 1/6) shrinks the sampled UV rectangle to one tile — combined with the per-frame offset in Update(), that rectangle becomes the moving "window" over the sheet.

Hard-won victory: NearestFilter on BOTH min and mag. The comment is emphatic for a reason — THREE defaults minFilter to a mip-mapped linear filter. With the default, the sprite blurs the moment it is drawn even slightly smaller than its native size (camera pull-back, sub-pixel positions). Forcing NearestFilter on magFilter (zoomed in) and minFilter (zoomed out), plus anisotropy = 1 (no oblique-angle smoothing), is what guarantees Randy stays razor-sharp at every scale and never shimmers in motion. Miss any one of these three and the blur sneaks back.

public ChangeState(newState: { indicies: number[]; timing: number[] })

Swaps the playing animation. Called by the player state handlers (handlePlayerIdle, handlePlayerRunning, handlePlayerJumping, handlePlayerFalling — see the Player states) every frame with the appropriate SpriteAnimations.* object. 

// Skip if already in this state (don't reset the animation every frame)
if (this.State === newState) {
  return;
}

this.State = newState;
this.timingMultiplier = 1;
this.runningTileIndex = 0;
this.currentTile = this.State.indicies[this.runningTileIndex];
this.elapsedTime = this.State.timing[this.runningTileIndex]; // Force play new animation instead of waiting a loop

The reference-equality guard is the load-bearing line: handlers call ChangeState unconditionally each frame, but because SpriteAnimations.RUN_RIGHT is a single shared object, passing the same one short-circuits — so the animation does not restart from frame 0 sixty times a second. It only truly changes when the object identity differs. Resetting timingMultiplier to 1 here means a new state starts at base speed (running re-applies its own multiplier afterward).

Gotcha: that last line pre-loads elapsedTime. Setting elapsedTime = timing[0] means the very next Update() immediately satisfies the advance condition and steps to indicies[1] — the new animation visibly "moves" on the first frame instead of holding indicies[0] for its full duration first. The practical consequence: the first frame actually rendered after a state change is indicies[1], not indicies[0]. That is intended ("force play"), but it means index 0 is effectively the loop's starting pose, not its first-shown pose. Keep that in mind when ordering frames in spriteAnimations.ts.

public ChangeAnimationTiming(newTimingMultiplier: number)

Scales playback speed without changing which frames play. The running handler drives this so the run cycle speeds up with the player's velocity: handlePlayerRunning calls ChangeAnimationTiming(1 / (|NextTranslation.x| / AnimationScalingFactor)). 

// Don't change timing unless needed
if (this.timingMultiplier === newTimingMultiplier) {
  return;
}

this.timingMultiplier = newTimingMultiplier;

Every duration in Update() is multiplied by timingMultiplier, so a multiplier below 1 shortens frame holds (faster animation) and above 1 lengthens them (slower). The equality guard avoids redundant writes. AnimationScalingFactor (set to 1.6 in baseFeel.ts) is the game-feel knob that tunes how fast the legs churn relative to ground speed — a game-layer concern the engine stays ignorant of.

public Update(deltaTime: number)

Advances the flip-book by one tick. Called every frame from Player.updatePlayerState() as this.SpriteAnimator.Update(this.Time.Delta) right after the FSM runs, so the sprite reflects the state the handlers just chose. Accumulates time, and when the current frame's (scaled) duration is reached, steps to the next tile and recomputes the UV offset. 

this.elapsedTime += deltaTime;

if (
  this.State.timing[this.runningTileIndex] * this.timingMultiplier > 0 &&
  this.elapsedTime >=
    this.State.timing[this.runningTileIndex] * this.timingMultiplier
) {
  this.elapsedTime = 0;

  // Move to the next tile index, looping back to the beginning if necessary
  this.runningTileIndex =
    (this.runningTileIndex + 1) % this.State.indicies.length;

  this.currentTile = this.State.indicies[this.runningTileIndex];

  const offsetX =
    (this.currentTile % this.tilesHorizontal) / this.tilesHorizontal;
  const offsetY =
    (this.tilesVertical -
      Math.floor(this.currentTile / this.tilesHorizontal) -
      1) /
    this.tilesVertical;

  this.Material.map!.offset.x = offsetX;
  this.Material.map!.offset.y = offsetY;
}

Walking the two halves of the gate condition:

  • timing[runningTileIndex] * timingMultiplier > 0 — a zero (or negative) duration disables advancing, freezing on the current tile. This is the safety valve: a 0 in a timing array means "hold this frame forever," and it also prevents a degenerate >= 0 from advancing every single frame.
  • elapsedTime >= timing[...] * timingMultiplier — the frame's time is up. Reset elapsedTime to 0 and advance.

(runningTileIndex + 1) % indicies.length is the loop: it wraps the position cursor back to the first entry, so every animation here loops indefinitely. There is no one-shot mode — a non-looping animation would be expressed by parking on a frame with timing 0 (the gate above), not by a flag.

The offset math, and why Y is flipped

Tile ids count from the top-left, but THREE texture UVs originate at the bottom-left. So column is a straight modulo, while the row must be flipped:

  • column = currentTile % tilesHorizontal, then offsetX = column / tilesHorizontal.
  • rowFromTop = Math.floor(currentTile / tilesHorizontal), then offsetY = (tilesVertical - rowFromTop - 1) / tilesVertical — the (V - row - 1) term is the top-to-bottom → bottom-to-top flip.

Worked example — tile 13 (RUN_RIGHT frame 2), grid 4×6: column = 13 % 4 = 1 → offsetX = 1/4 = 0.25; rowFromTop = floor(13/4) = 3 → offsetY = (6 - 3 - 1)/6 = 2/6 ≈ 0.333. With repeat = (0.25, 0.1667) the sampled window is x ∈ [0.25, 0.5], y ∈ [0.333, 0.5] — the 4th row from the top, 2nd column. Exactly the tile shown in the sheet above. 

flowchart TD
    A["Update(deltaTime)"] --> B["elapsedTime += deltaTime"]
    B --> C{"elapsedTime >= timing * mult ?"}
    C -- no --> Z["hold current tile"]
    C -- yes --> D["elapsedTime = 0"]
    D --> E["runningTileIndex = (i+1) % indicies.length"]
    E --> F["currentTile = indicies[i]"]
    F --> G["offsetX = (tile % W) / W"]
    G --> H["offsetY = (V - floor(tile/W) - 1) / V"]
    H --> I["map.offset = (offsetX, offsetY)"]
Note. Only the texture offset changes per frame — never the geometry, the mesh, or the repeat. That is why this is cheap enough to run on every animated entity every frame: it is two float writes when a frame boundary is crossed, and nothing at all otherwise.

public Destroy()

Releases this animator's GPU resources. Called from Player.Destroy() as this.SpriteAnimator.Destroy() before the base GameObject teardown. 

if (this.Material.map) {
  this.Material.map.dispose();
}

this.Material.dispose();

Disposing Material.map (the texture) first, then the material, frees both the uploaded texture and the material program. Because each entity owns its own SpriteAnimator, each one disposes its own texture and material — the Renderer's dispose() only frees the GL context, not these, so without this method destroyed entities would leak their sheets.

Modifying this: to animate a new character, hand the constructor that sheet's texture and its real (tilesHorizontal, tilesVertical) — the offset math is fully driven by those two numbers, so no code changes are needed for a differently-shaped grid. To add new clips, add entries to the game's spriteAnimations.ts table (engine stays untouched). To make a clip play once and stop, give its final frame a timing of 0 so the gate freezes it. If you ever want frame indicies[0] to actually be the first shown frame, change the ChangeState pre-load from elapsedTime = timing[0] to elapsedTime = 0 — but expect a brief hold before the first advance. Keep all pixel-art filtering settings as-is; they are paired with the renderer's antialias: false.

Camera — engine-pure follow camera

A self-contained Three.js camera that knows how to follow a moving point with Mario-Wonder-style easing — look-ahead on X, intent-driven pan on Y — yet names zero game concepts. It never imports Player; it reads a plain CameraTarget snapshot of scalars handed to it each frame. That seam (roadmap C5) is the whole point of this section: the same camera serves a Donkey-Kong follow level, a fixed-zone level, or a future multiplayer follow without a single edit.

src/engine/camera/camera.ts src/engine/types/cameraTarget.ts

src/engine/camera/camera.ts 332 LOC

What it is: the engine's one camera class. It owns a PerspectiveCamera (the shipping camera) and an optional debug-only OrthographicCamera, a smoothed follow state, and a live-editable bag of tuning knobs. Who constructs it: the Experience singleton (Engine Core) news it up exactly once in Configure() — this.Camera = new Camera(new THREE.Vector3(0, 25, 100)) — and stores it as experience.Camera. Who drives it: the GameDirector (Game Layer) calls camera.Update(target) every frame; the Renderer (Engine Layer) reads camera.Instance to draw. Why these imports: THREE for the camera objects; Experience/Sizes/Time for the shared scene, viewport, and frame delta; Input/Debug only when the debug overlay is active; Emitter for the "manualCameraControl" toggle; and crucially CameraFollowState/CameraTarget from ../types/cameraTarget — the only shape it knows about its follow target. Lifecycle: constructor → (per frame) Update(target?) → Resize() on viewport change → Destroy().

Hard-won victory — C5 / D3. This file imports nothing from game/. Before the decouple, Update() reached into a concrete Player for CurrentPosition, NextTranslation, State and IsTouching.ground. Now it consumes the CameraTarget snapshot instead. What it prevents: a cyclic engine→game dependency, and the engine layer hard-coding Donkey-Kong's notion of "player." The camera's easing math is byte-for-byte the same; only its input shape changed.

interface CameraTuning & const MOVE_EPSILON

Module-level declarations (lines 16–29). CameraTuning is the live-editable feel bag — every field is surfaced in the Camera Debug GUI folder, so you can dial the pan while the game runs. The header comment encodes the design intent: X eases a look-ahead toward travel direction; Y follows only sustained / intentional vertical motion (climb, real fall, landing) and deliberately ignores jump arcs. The *Rate fields are responsiveness constants for the exponential smoother (see smooth()), and all distances are world units. 

interface CameraTuning {
  lookaheadX: number;        // horizontal offset placed ahead of the player
  lookaheadRate: number;     // how fast the look-ahead eases toward its goal
  horizontalRate: number;    // X follow responsiveness
  verticalRateGround: number;// grounded vertical follow (gentle)
  verticalRateUp: number;    // climbing — track up briskly
  verticalRateDown: number;  // falling — slower / capped descent
  climbLeadY: number;        // look-ahead ABOVE the player while climbing
  fallBufferY: number;       // player must drop this far below the camera before it follows
  fallLeadY: number;         // lead relative to the player while falling (negative = below)
}

// Below this |velocity| the player counts as "not moving" → X look-ahead eases to 0.
const MOVE_EPSILON = 0.01;

MOVE_EPSILON is the dead-zone that decides direction: in Update() a velocityX above +MOVE_EPSILON aims the look-ahead right, below -MOVE_EPSILON aims it left, and anything in between eases the offset back to zero so a standing player re-centers.

KnobDefaultAxisRole
lookaheadX8XHow far ahead of the player the camera leads while moving
lookaheadRate3XEase speed of the look-ahead offset itself
horizontalRate6XEase speed of the camera toward its X target
verticalRateGround4YGrounded / jumping vertical follow (gentle)
verticalRateUp6YClimbing follow (brisk)
verticalRateDown3YFalling follow (capped / slow)
climbLeadY2YLead above the player while climbing (show next handhold)
fallBufferY3YDrop distance below the camera before it starts following down
fallLeadY-2YLead into the fall (negative = below the player)

Fields

The follow state (lines 42–62) is split into the smoothed values the camera writes each frame and the anchors it eases toward. The ! definite-assignment markers exist because these are populated by the two private initialize* helpers the constructor calls, not inline. 

public InitialPosition: THREE.Vector3;

// Follow state
private isCameraFollowOn!: boolean;
private currentPosition!: THREE.Vector3; // the smoothed camera position we write each frame
private currentLookaheadX!: number;      // smoothed horizontal look-ahead offset
private baselineX!: number;              // horizontal rest anchor (CameraStart / CameraSensor zone)
private baselineY!: number;              // vertical rest anchor (player Y, or a pinned CameraSensor zone)
private hasSensorBaseline!: boolean;     // true while a CameraSensor has pinned the baseline

public Tuning!: CameraTuning;

public ManualControl: boolean;
public Instance: THREE.Camera;
public CameraType!: string;
private PerspectiveCamera!: THREE.PerspectiveCamera;
public OrthographicCamera?: THREE.OrthographicCamera;

Line-level notes: currentPosition is the source of truth the camera writes into Instance.position at the end of every Update() — easing happens on this vector, not directly on the THREE camera. baselineX/baselineY are the rest anchors; hasSensorBaseline records whether a CameraSensor (Game Layer) has pinned the vertical baseline (changes how grounded re-baselining behaves). Instance is the polymorphic handle the Renderer draws with — it points at PerspectiveCamera normally and OrthographicCamera only when toggled in debug.

constructor(initialPosition: THREE.Vector3)

Builds both follow state and the camera objects. Parameter: initialPosition — the world-space spawn point (Experience passes (0, 25, 100)); stored on InitialPosition and copied into every camera and the follow baselines. Returns: the instance. Walk-through: it pulls the shared Sizes/Scene/Time off the singleton (lines 66–69), then delegates to initializePerspectiveInstance() and initializeCameraFollow(). It defaults Instance to the perspective camera and CameraType to "perspective" (lines 77–78) — the orthographic camera is a debug-only luxury. 

// Default use the perspective camera
this.Instance = this.PerspectiveCamera;
this.CameraType = "perspective";
this.ManualControl = false;
this.LastToggleTime = 0;
this.AspectRatio = this.sizes.Width / this.sizes.Height;

// Debug
if (this.experience.Debug.IsActive) {
  this.initializeOrthographicInstance();
  this.Input = this.experience.Input;
  this.debug = this.experience.Debug;
  this.debug.InitCameraDebug(this);
}

// Events — store ref for cleanup in destroy()
this.onManualCameraControl = () => {
  this.ManualControl = !this.ManualControl;
};
Emitter.on("manualCameraControl", this.onManualCameraControl);

Line-level notes: the orthographic camera, the Input reference, and the GUI folder are only created when Debug.IsActive (lines 84–89). In a production build none of that exists, which is why OrthographicCamera, Input and debug are all optional (?) fields. The "manualCameraControl" handler is stored on this.onManualCameraControl rather than inlined so Destroy() can pass the same reference to Emitter.off — an anonymous closure could never be unsubscribed.

Gotcha — construction order. The constructor calls this.debug.InitCameraDebug(this), which binds dat.GUI controls directly to camera.Tuning and camera.Instance.position. That means initializeCameraFollow() (which creates Tuning) and initializePerspectiveInstance() (which creates the camera) must run before the debug block — and they do, at lines 73–74. Reorder them and the GUI binds to undefined.

private initializePerspectiveInstance(): void

Creates the shipping camera and adds it to the scene. A 35° vertical FOV, aspect from the live viewport, near 0.1 / far 500. It copies InitialPosition in and registers with the scene so the Renderer can find it. 

this.PerspectiveCamera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(
  35,
  this.sizes.Width / this.sizes.Height,
  0.1,
  500
);
this.PerspectiveCamera.position.copy(this.InitialPosition);
this.scene.add(this.PerspectiveCamera);

Note this is a perspective camera even though the game is 2D — the gameplay plane sits at a fixed depth and the perspective FOV gives a subtle parallax. The orthographic camera below is a flat top-down debugging aid, not the play view.

private initializeOrthographicInstance(): void

Debug-only. Seeds FrustumSize = 40 and ZoomFactor = 0.5 (the per-keypress zoom step the Camera Debug module uses), then builds an OrthographicCamera whose left/right bounds are scaled by AspectRatio so the view stays square-ish across window shapes. 

this.FrustumSize = 40;
this.ZoomFactor = 0.5;

this.OrthographicCamera = new THREE.OrthographicCamera(
  (-this.FrustumSize * this.AspectRatio) / 2,
  (this.FrustumSize * this.AspectRatio) / 2,
  this.FrustumSize / 2,
  -this.FrustumSize / 2,
  0.1,
  500
);
this.OrthographicCamera.position.copy(this.InitialPosition);
this.scene.add(this.OrthographicCamera);

The exact same frustum-bounds formula reappears in Resize() and in CameraDebug.zoom(); if you ever change the projection math, change it in all three.

private initializeCameraFollow(): void

Seeds the follow state and the default tuning. Follow starts off (loadLevelByIndex later calls SetCameraFollow(entry.cameraFollow) per level). The smoothed position is cloned from InitialPosition, the look-ahead starts at 0, and both baselines start at the initial X/Y with no sensor pin. 

this.isCameraFollowOn = false;
this.currentPosition = new THREE.Vector3().copy(this.InitialPosition);
this.currentLookaheadX = 0;
this.baselineX = this.InitialPosition.x;
this.baselineY = this.InitialPosition.y;
this.hasSensorBaseline = false;

// Starting feel values (dial live via the Camera Debug GUI folder).
this.Tuning = {
  lookaheadX: 8,  lookaheadRate: 3,  horizontalRate: 6,
  verticalRateGround: 4,  verticalRateUp: 6,  verticalRateDown: 3,
  climbLeadY: 2,  fallBufferY: 3,  fallLeadY: -2,
};

public SetCameraFollow(value: boolean): void

The mode switch. true = track the CameraTarget (player-follow level); false = behave as a fixed/zone camera that only eases toward the authored baseline. Called from GameDirector.loadLevelByIndex with the registry entry's cameraFollow flag, so follow-vs-fixed is a per-level authoring decision, not a hard-coded constant. 

public SetCameraFollow(value: boolean) {
  this.isCameraFollowOn = value;
}

public SwitchCamera(): void

Toggles Instance between the perspective and orthographic cameras and updates CameraType to match. Invoked by the ~ key handler in CameraDebug.Update. The orthographic branch is guarded by if (this.OrthographicCamera) because that camera only exists in debug builds — in a production build the method simply never has anything to switch to. 

if (this.Instance instanceof THREE.OrthographicCamera) {
  this.Instance = this.PerspectiveCamera;
  this.CameraType = "perspective";
} else {
  if (this.OrthographicCamera) {
    this.Instance = this.OrthographicCamera;
    this.CameraType = "orthographic";
  }
}

public SetBaselinePosition(x: number, y: number): void

Pins the camera's rest anchor to an authored CameraSensor zone. Parameters: x, y — the authored world spot (parsed from a cam=x_y tag on the sensor's texture). Sets both baselines and raises hasSensorBaseline. Caller: CameraSensor.ApplyCameraOnEnter, which fires from the declarative contact table when the player enters the zone. 

public SetBaselinePosition(x: number, y: number) {
  this.baselineX = x;
  this.baselineY = y;
  this.hasSensorBaseline = true;
}

The behavioral split lives in Update(), and the comment on this method spells it out: on a non-follow level the camera eases to this anchor on both axes (authored x,y spots); on a follow level only Y matters — it pins the grounded vertical baseline while follow keeps driving X off the player. Crucially, an intentional vertical move (climb or real fall) releases the pin by setting hasSensorBaseline = false again inside Update().

public Resize(): void

Recomputes the aspect ratio and the active camera's projection. Called from Experience's "resize" handler, which fires when Sizes emits on a window change. It first refreshes AspectRatio (needed by both cameras), then branches on which camera is currently active. 

this.AspectRatio = this.sizes.Width / this.sizes.Height;

// Orthographic camera
if (this.Instance instanceof THREE.OrthographicCamera) {
  this.Instance.left = (-this.FrustumSize * this.AspectRatio) / 2;
  this.Instance.right = (this.FrustumSize * this.AspectRatio) / 2;
  this.Instance.top = this.FrustumSize / 2;
  this.Instance.bottom = -this.FrustumSize / 2;
  this.Instance.updateProjectionMatrix();
  return;
}

// Perspective camera
if (this.Instance instanceof THREE.PerspectiveCamera) {
  this.PerspectiveCamera.aspect = this.AspectRatio;
  this.Instance.updateProjectionMatrix();
  return;
}

Line-level notes: only the active camera is reprojected — the inactive one is corrected lazily by SwitchCamera/zoom if it ever becomes active. The orthographic branch rewrites all four frustum edges (the same formula as initializeOrthographicInstance); the perspective branch only needs aspect. Both end with updateProjectionMatrix() — without it THREE keeps the stale matrix and the view stretches.

public TeleportToPosition(x: number, y: number, z: number): void

Hard-snaps the camera with no easing — sets currentPosition, both baselines, and the live Instance.position all at once, and clears any sensor pin. Used for cuts: GameDirector.setCameraStart on level load and resetGameState on reset. 

public TeleportToPosition(x: number, y: number, z: number) {
  this.currentPosition.set(x, y, z);
  this.baselineX = x;
  this.baselineY = y;
  this.hasSensorBaseline = false;
  this.Instance.position.set(x, y, z);
}
Gotcha — argument order at the CameraStart call site. The GLB stores positions Blender-style (Z-up). setCameraStart reads x = position[0], y = position[1], z = position[2] and then calls TeleportToPosition(x, z, y) — swapping Y and Z to convert into Three.js's Y-up world. resetGameState, by contrast, passes InitialPosition.x/.y/.z straight through. Same method, two coordinate conventions at the boundary; the swap lives in the game caller, keeping the engine method axis-agnostic.

private smooth(current: number, target: number, rate: number): number

The one easing primitive every follow channel runs through. Parameters: current (where we are), target (where we want to be), rate (responsiveness from Tuning). Returns: the next value, one frame closer. 

private smooth(current: number, target: number, rate: number): number {
  const alpha = 1 - Math.exp(-rate * this.Time.FrameDelta);
  return current + (target - current) * alpha;
}
Hard-won victory — frame-rate-independent lerp. A naive current + (target-current)*k with a constant k eases faster at high frame rates and slower on a laggy frame — the camera feel drifts with FPS. The 1 - e^(-rate·dt) form folds Time.FrameDelta in, so a given rate produces the same wall-clock approach speed whether the frame is 16 ms or 33 ms. It reads FrameDelta — the real per-frame wall-clock time — and deliberately not Time.Delta (the fixed 1/60 sim step), because the camera now runs in the per-FRAME render pass, easing at the display's actual refresh rate while following the interpolated player. FrameDelta is clamped to 1/30 upstream, so a huge tab-restore hitch can't make alpha overshoot toward 1 and teleport the camera.

public Update(target?: CameraTarget): void

The heart of the file — runs once per FRAME from GameDirector.RenderUpdate (the per-frame render pass, not the fixed-step sim Update where movement integrates). It builds the target via cameraTargetFromPlayer after the player's graphics have been interpolated for this frame, so the follow reads the smooth interpolated position (player.RenderTranslation) rather than the stepped sim position. Parameter: the optional CameraTarget snapshot. Returns: nothing — it mutates currentPosition and writes it into Instance.position.

Hard-won victory — render interpolation. The sim now advances in fixed 1/60 steps (0..N per frame), but the camera no longer rides that stepped position. GameDirector's per-frame RenderUpdate(alpha) first lerps each entity between its last two sim states into RenderTranslation, then calls Update with a target built off that interpolated player — so the follow is smooth above the sim rate (buttery on a 144 Hz display, no judder on a 60 Hz one) instead of snapping once per sim step.

1 — Guards & manual fly-cam (lines 222–236).

if (!target) {
  return;
}

if (this.debug) {
  this.debug.UpdateCameraDebug(this);

  // Manual fly-cam fully owns the camera while engaged
  if (this.ManualControl) {
    return;
  }
  this.PerspectiveCamera.rotation.y = 0;
}

The !target bail-out defends against the follow target not being ready yet (the game already guards Player?.PhysicsBody, so this is belt-and-suspenders). When debug is active it first refreshes the GUI readouts via UpdateCameraDebug; if ManualControl is on, the WASD fly-cam fully owns the camera and follow is skipped entirely. Otherwise it forces PerspectiveCamera.rotation.y = 0 each frame so any yaw left over from a previous fly-cam session is undone before follow positioning resumes.

2 — Follow branch, X look-ahead (lines 242–255).

if (this.isCameraFollowOn) {
  // X: velocity-driven look-ahead, eased
  let lookaheadGoal = 0;
  if (target.velocityX > MOVE_EPSILON) {
    lookaheadGoal = this.Tuning.lookaheadX;
  } else if (target.velocityX < -MOVE_EPSILON) {
    lookaheadGoal = -this.Tuning.lookaheadX;
  }
  this.currentLookaheadX = this.smooth(
    this.currentLookaheadX,
    lookaheadGoal,
    this.Tuning.lookaheadRate
  );
  targetX = target.x + this.currentLookaheadX;

The look-ahead goal snaps to ±lookaheadX by velocity sign (or 0 inside the MOVE_EPSILON dead-zone), but the actual currentLookaheadX eases toward it — so reversing direction swings the camera smoothly across center instead of popping. The X target is the player's X plus that eased offset.

3 — Follow branch, Y by intent (lines 257–304). This is the per-state vertical behavior. The default Y target is the baseline at the gentle ground rate; the switch on target.followState overrides per mode.

targetY = this.baselineY;
verticalRate = this.Tuning.verticalRateGround;

switch (target.followState) {
  case CameraFollowState.CLIMBING:
    targetY = target.y + this.Tuning.climbLeadY;
    verticalRate = this.Tuning.verticalRateUp;
    this.baselineY = target.y;
    this.hasSensorBaseline = false;
    break;

  case CameraFollowState.FALLING: {
    const fallThreshold = this.currentPosition.y - this.Tuning.fallBufferY;
    if (target.y < fallThreshold) {
      targetY = target.y + this.Tuning.fallLeadY;
      verticalRate = this.Tuning.verticalRateDown;
      this.baselineY = target.y;
      this.hasSensorBaseline = false;
    } else {
      // Small drops / early fall: hold vertical (don't yank the camera).
      targetY = this.currentPosition.y;
      verticalRate = this.Tuning.verticalRateDown;
    }
    break;
  }

  case CameraFollowState.JUMPING:
    targetY = this.baselineY;
    verticalRate = this.Tuning.verticalRateGround;
    break;

  default:
    if (target.isGrounded && !this.hasSensorBaseline) {
      this.baselineY = target.y;
    }
    targetY = this.baselineY;
    verticalRate = this.Tuning.verticalRateGround;
}
flowchart TD
  A["target.followState"] --> B{which mode?}
  B -->|CLIMBING| C["targetY = y + climbLeadY (brisk)\nre-baseline + release sensor pin"]
  B -->|FALLING| D{"y below currentY - fallBufferY?"}
  D -->|yes| E["targetY = y + fallLeadY (capped)\nre-baseline + release pin"]
  D -->|no| F["hold currentY (don't yank)"]
  B -->|JUMPING| G["targetY = baselineY (ignore arc)"]
  B -->|GROUNDED default| H{"grounded AND no sensor pin?"}
  H -->|yes| I["baselineY = y (re-baseline)"]
  H -->|no| J["keep baselineY (sensor-pinned)"]
  I --> K["targetY = baselineY (gentle)"]
  J --> K
followStateY targetratebaseline / pin effect
CLIMBINGy + climbLeadY (lead above)verticalRateUpre-baseline to y, release sensor pin
FALLING (past buffer)y + fallLeadY (lead below)verticalRateDownre-baseline to y, release sensor pin
FALLING (within buffer)currentPosition.y (hold)verticalRateDownno change
JUMPINGbaselineYverticalRateGroundno change (don't chase the arc)
GROUNDED (default)baselineYverticalRateGroundre-baseline to y only if grounded & unpinned

Line-level notes worth re-reading: FALLING uses a buffer — the camera only starts dropping once target.y falls below currentPosition.y - fallBufferY, so a short hop-down or the apex of a fall doesn't yank the view; below the buffer it leads into the fall by the negative fallLeadY so the landing zone is visible. JUMPING deliberately holds the baseline — the camera ignores the parabola so the screen doesn't bob on every jump. The grounded default re-baselines to the player's standing Y only when truly grounded and no CameraSensor has pinned the baseline — that is what lets the camera pan up as the player walks up terrain, while a pinned zone keeps the view locked. CLIMBING and a committed FALLING both clear hasSensorBaseline, which is the "intentional vertical move releases the pin" rule from SetBaselinePosition.

4 — Non-follow branch (lines 305–312).

} else {
  // Non-follow level: fixed/zone camera positioned by CameraStart + CameraSensor zones.
  targetX = this.baselineX;
  targetY = this.baselineY;
  verticalRate = this.Tuning.verticalRateGround;
}

When follow is off, the player is never read for positioning — the camera eases toward the authored baselineX/baselineY on both axes, which is exactly what a CameraSensor sets via SetBaselinePosition. This is the fixed/zone-camera mode.

5 — Smooth & write (lines 314–327).

this.currentPosition.x = this.smooth(
  this.currentPosition.x, targetX, this.Tuning.horizontalRate
);
this.currentPosition.y = this.smooth(
  this.currentPosition.y, targetY, verticalRate
);
// z stays fixed at the initial depth
this.Instance.position.copy(this.currentPosition);

Both branches feed a single, shared smoothing tail: X always eases at horizontalRate, Y eases at whichever verticalRate the branch chose, and Z is never touched (the play plane sits at a fixed depth). The final copy is the only place the THREE camera's position is written during follow — everything upstream operated on the private currentPosition vector.

Note — why the camera reads velocityX not a direction enum. Look-ahead direction comes from the raw signed horizontal velocity (NextTranslation.x on the game side), not from a "facing" flag. That keeps the camera honest about momentum — a player who's pinned against a wall has near-zero velocity and the look-ahead correctly eases back to center even though they're still "facing" that way.

public Destroy(): void

Unwinds the constructor: unsubscribes the stored "manualCameraControl" handler and removes the active camera from the scene. Called from Experience.Destroy (camera first, then physics, renderer, resources). 

public Destroy() {
  Emitter.off("manualCameraControl", this.onManualCameraControl);
  this.scene.remove(this.Instance);
}
Warning. scene.remove(this.Instance) removes only the active camera. In a debug build the inactive (e.g. orthographic) camera is left attached to the scene. Harmless in practice — the whole scene is torn down — but worth knowing if you start counting scene children.

Modifying this: to change feel, edit the Tuning defaults in initializeCameraFollow (or just dial them live in the Camera Debug folder and copy the numbers back). To add a new vertical behavior, add a case to the switch in Update and a matching member to the CameraFollowState map — then map your game state to it in cameraTargetFromPlayer. Do not import anything from game/ here: the C5 seam is load-bearing. If you need a new piece of follow input, add a scalar field to CameraTarget rather than reaching for the Player.

src/engine/types/cameraTarget.ts 25 LOC

What it is: the decoupling contract — a tiny types file that defines the engine's vertical follow modes (CameraFollowState) and the per-frame data shape the camera consumes (CameraTarget). Who builds it: the GameDirector (Game Layer) constructs one every frame from its Player; the camera only ever reads it. Why it exists: it is the entire surface area of the C5 decouple — by routing follow through plain scalars, the engine Camera names zero game concepts and imports nothing from game/ (D3).

Hard-won victory — C5 + D3 + D15 in one file. The header comment is the design rationale verbatim: "Plain data snapshot so the engine Camera names ZERO game concepts (D3) — no Player/PlayerStates import." Each field is annotated with the exact Player expression it replaced, so you can trace the seam line by line:

const CameraFollowState / type CameraFollowState

The engine-side vocabulary of vertical-follow modes — deliberately named in camera terms (GROUNDED/CLIMBING/FALLING/JUMPING), not player terms. The game maps its own PlayerStates onto these. 

export const CameraFollowState = {
  GROUNDED: "grounded",
  CLIMBING: "climbing",
  FALLING: "falling",
  JUMPING: "jumping",
} as const;

export type CameraFollowState =
  (typeof CameraFollowState)[keyof typeof CameraFollowState];

This is the same same-named const + type idiom used by playerStates.ts and gameObjectType.ts: CameraFollowState is usable as both a value (CameraFollowState.CLIMBING in the switch) and a type annotation (followState: CameraFollowState). The as const freezes the literals so the derived type is the string-union "grounded" | "climbing" | ..., not string.

ModeCamera behavior (from the in-file comment)
GROUNDEDrest at baseline (re-baseline to target Y while grounded)
CLIMBINGtrack up with a lead
FALLINGfollow down past a buffer, capped
JUMPINGhold the baseline (don't chase the arc)

interface CameraTarget

The per-frame follow snapshot. A pure data shape (camelCase fields, D15) carrying exactly the scalars the camera's easing reads — no more, no less — so the branching in Camera.Update survives the decouple unchanged. 

export interface CameraTarget {
  x: number;          // world X to follow (was player.CurrentPosition.x)
  y: number;          // world Y to follow (was player.CurrentPosition.y)
  velocityX: number;  // horizontal velocity -> look-ahead direction (was NextTranslation.x)
  followState: CameraFollowState; // vertical-follow mode (mapped from the game's state)
  isGrounded: boolean;// truly on the ground -> grounded re-baseline (was IsTouching.ground)
}

Each comment is a migration breadcrumb: x/y were player.CurrentPosition, velocityX was NextTranslation.x, isGrounded was IsTouching.ground, and followState is the one field that isn't a raw copy — it's derived on the game side.

The mapping seam — GameDirector.cameraTargetFromPlayer

The companion piece lives in the game layer. Each frame — in the per-frame render pass, right after the player's graphics have been interpolated — the GameDirector translates PlayerStates → CameraFollowState and packs the scalars into a fresh CameraTarget: 

private cameraTargetFromPlayer(player: Player): CameraTarget {
  let followState: CameraFollowState;
  switch (player.State) {
    case PlayerStates.CLIMBING: followState = CameraFollowState.CLIMBING; break;
    case PlayerStates.FALLING:  followState = CameraFollowState.FALLING;  break;
    case PlayerStates.JUMPING:  followState = CameraFollowState.JUMPING;  break;
    default:                    followState = CameraFollowState.GROUNDED;
  }
  return {
    // Follow the INTERPOLATED render position (set by InterpolateGraphics just
    // before this in RenderUpdate) so the camera tracks the smooth visual player.
    x: player.RenderTranslation.x,
    y: player.RenderTranslation.y,
    velocityX: player.NextTranslation.x,
    followState,
    isGrounded: player.IsTouching.ground,
  };
}

Note the default catch-all: IDLE and RUNNING both fall through to GROUNDED, because the camera draws no distinction between standing still and walking — both rest at the baseline. This switch is the only place the two vocabularies meet; a different game (or a multiplayer follow that averages two players' positions) would write its own version of this one method and the engine camera would never know.

Tip. Want to debug what the camera is "seeing"? The values flowing through this snapshot are exactly the player position/state/velocity already surfaced in the Player Debug readouts, and the resulting camera transform is mirrored live in the Camera Debug position fields.

Modifying this: add a field only when the camera genuinely needs a new scalar (and wire it in cameraTargetFromPlayer + read it in Camera.Update). To add a follow mode, extend the CameraFollowState map, add a switch case in both Camera.Update and cameraTargetFromPlayer. Keep this file free of any import from game/ or even from three — its purity is the reason the camera is reusable.

Physics — the Rapier2D world

Physics wraps a single Rapier2D rigid-body world: it boots the WASM solver, steps it on a fixed timestep, and — the heart of the engine — drains Rapier's per-step collision event queue, resolves each colliding collider back to the owning GameObject, and fans the event out two ways: to the object's own override callbacks AND into the declarative ContactRegistry contact table. It is the engine half of the contact-table system; the game half lives in Config: contactRules. ContactPoints is the tiny per-surface boolean vocabulary the gameplay layer reads off all of this.

src/engine/physics/physics.ts src/engine/types/contactPoints.ts

src/engine/physics/physics.ts 237 LOC

This file is the engine's physics subsystem: one RAPIER.World, one RAPIER.EventQueue, a registry mapping collider handles back to game objects, and the dispatch logic that turns raw Rapier events into engine-level callbacks and contact-rule firings. It is owned by the singleton Core: Experience, which does this.Physics = new Physics() and then await this.Physics.Configure() inside its own async Configure() (see Core: Experience), and which drives Physics.Update() first on every tick. Its imports tell the whole story of its job:

  • RAPIER from "@dimforge/rapier2d-compat" — the WASM-backed physics engine. The compat build is chosen deliberately: it bundles the WASM and exposes RAPIER.init() so the world can be created after an await instead of needing a separate fetch/instantiate step.
  • Experience — the singleton back-reference, used to reach Scene (where debug meshes live), Time.Delta (the timestep), and Debug.
  • GameObject — the type the registry resolves collider handles to; the dispatcher reads its OnCollisionEnter/Exit, OnSensorEnter/Exit hooks and IsBeingDestroyed flag (see Entities: GameObject).
  • ContactRegistry — the generic, game-blind contact-rule table this class instantiates and dispatches into (see Entities: ContactRegistry).
  • Debug — optional; only wired when the debug panel is active.

Lifecycle is the engine's standard four-phase shape, but with the constructor replaced by an async Configure() because WASM init cannot be awaited in a real constructor: new Physics() does nothing, Configure() builds the world, Update() steps + drains every frame, Destroy() frees the WASM world. 

sequenceDiagram
    participant T as Time (tick)
    participant E as Experience.onTick
    participant P as Physics.Update
    participant W as RAPIER.World
    participant Q as EventQueue
    participant R as ContactRegistry
    T->>E: Emitter.emit("tick")
    loop 0..N fixed 1/60s steps
        E->>P: Physics.Update()
        P->>W: timestep = Time.Delta (fixed 1/60)
        P->>W: World.step(EventQueue)
        W->>Q: queue collision + sensor events
        P->>Q: drainCollisionEvents(cb)
        Q-->>P: (handle1, handle2, started)
        P->>P: resolve handles to GameObjects
        P->>P: call OnCollision/OnSensor hooks
        P->>R: Contacts.Dispatch(phase, a, b)
    end

private experience; private debug?; public Scene; public World; public EventQueue; public IsPaused; public Contacts; private gameObjectRegistry

The instance state. Note the ! definite-assignment on the public handles — they are filled in Configure(), not the constructor, so TypeScript is told "trust me, these exist by use time."

private experience!: Experience; // engine singleton back-reference
private debug?: Debug; // optional debug overlay helper
public Scene!: THREE.Scene; // Three.js scene physics meshes bind to

public World!: RAPIER.World; // Rapier 2d physics world
public EventQueue!: RAPIER.EventQueue; // per-step collision event buffer
public IsPaused!: boolean; // when true, Update() skips the step

// Declarative "when an A contacts a B, run this" table. The game fills it; the
// engine just dispatches contact events into it (see handleCollisionEvents).
public Contacts!: ContactRegistry;
