// GameObject registration system - maps collider handles to GameObjects
private gameObjectRegistry!: Map<number, GameObject>;

Line-level notes:

  • World and EventQueue are public because game-side entities reach through Physics.World to create bodies/colliders (e.g. Entities: GameObject's createObjectPhysics calls this.Physics.World.createRigidBody(...)), and Debug: PhysicsDebug walks World.forEachCollider to draw wireframes.
  • Contacts is public so the game can populate it once at startup — Config: contactRules receives experience.Physics.Contacts and calls .Add(...) on it.
  • gameObjectRegistry is the crux of event resolution: Rapier hands you only opaque numeric collider handles in events; this map turns a handle back into the rich GameObject. It is keyed by collider handle, not body handle, because one rigid body may carry several colliders.

public async Configure(): Promise<void>

The real, async constructor. No parameters; returns a promise the caller (Core: Experience) awaits so nothing touches World before the WASM is live. Body, in order:

this.experience = Experience.GetInstance();
this.Scene = this.experience.Scene;

await RAPIER.init();
this.World = new RAPIER.World({ x: 0.0, y: -9.81 });
this.EventQueue = new RAPIER.EventQueue(true);
this.IsPaused = false;

// Initialize the declarative contact-rule table (game fills it at startup)
this.Contacts = new ContactRegistry();

// Initialize GameObject registry for collision event mapping
this.gameObjectRegistry = new Map<number, GameObject>();

// Debug
if (this.experience.Debug.IsActive) {
  this.debug = this.experience.Debug;
  this.debug.InitPhysicsDebug(this);
}
  • await RAPIER.init() — loads/instantiates the Rapier WASM module. Everything below depends on it; this single line is why the whole method (and Experience.Configure) must be async.
  • new RAPIER.World({ x: 0.0, y: -9.81 }) — gravity vector. Negative-Y is "down" in this engine's world space (a standard 2D screen-up convention), at roughly Earth gravity. This is the only place gravity is set; tweak the constant to make the whole game feel heavier/floatier (though most "feel" lives in Attributes: baseFeel via the character controller, not raw gravity).
  • new RAPIER.EventQueue(true) — the boolean is Rapier's autoDrain flag: the queue auto-clears between steps so stale events from a prior frame never leak into the next. We rely on this and never manually clear it.
  • this.Contacts = new ContactRegistry() — created empty here; the engine never adds rules. The game injects them post-configure.
  • The debug block is the seam to Debug: PhysicsDebug: only when Debug.IsActive do we keep a debug reference and call InitPhysicsDebug(this), which hands this (the live Physics) to PhysicsDebug so it can read Scene and World.
Hard-won victory — async over constructor. Rapier's WASM cannot be initialized synchronously. By making the entry point async Configure() instead of a constructor, and having Experience await this.Physics.Configure() before wiring the tick handler, we guarantee no frame ever runs World.step() against an uninitialized solver. This is why new Physics() is a no-op.
Gotcha — ordering. this.experience = Experience.GetInstance() must run first, and this.Scene is read from it immediately, so Experience must have set its Scene before calling Physics.Configure(). It does: this.Scene = new THREE.Scene() is assigned a few lines before this.Physics.Configure() in Experience.Configure.

public RegisterGameObject(gameObject: GameObject): void

Records every collider of a game object's rigid body into gameObjectRegistry so future events can be resolved. Called by Entities: GameObject at the end of createObjectPhysics (after the body + collider exist).

if (!gameObject.PhysicsBody) {
  console.warn("Cannot register GameObject without a physics body");
  return;
}

// Register all colliders for this rigid body
const numColliders = gameObject.PhysicsBody.numColliders();
for (let i = 0; i < numColliders; i++) {
  const collider = gameObject.PhysicsBody.collider(i);
  this.gameObjectRegistry.set(collider.handle, gameObject);
}

Loops over all colliders, not just collider 0, so a multi-collider body (e.g. a sensor collider plus a solid collider) maps every handle to the same owner. The guard returns early (with a warning) if there is no body — registration is meaningless without one.

public UnregisterGameObject(gameObject: GameObject): void

The mirror of registration: deletes every collider handle of the body from the map. Called by Entities: GameObject inside Destroy(), before World.removeRigidBody() — the colliders must still exist to enumerate their handles.

if (!gameObject.PhysicsBody) {
  return;
}

const numColliders = gameObject.PhysicsBody.numColliders();
for (let i = 0; i < numColliders; i++) {
  const collider = gameObject.PhysicsBody.collider(i);
  this.gameObjectRegistry.delete(collider.handle);
}
Gotcha — unregister before removeRigidBody. World.removeRigidBody() destroys the colliders, after which numColliders()/collider(i) can no longer give you the handles to delete. GameObject.Destroy calls UnregisterGameObject(this) first, then removes the body — reverse that order and you leak stale handles in the map.

private getGameObject(colliderHandle: number): GameObject | undefined

The internal one-line lookup used by the event dispatcher: return this.gameObjectRegistry.get(colliderHandle);. Returns undefined for any handle that was never registered (e.g. a body created directly against World without going through RegisterGameObject) — the dispatcher treats that as "skip."

public GetGameObjectFromCollider(collider: RAPIER.Collider): GameObject | undefined

The public sibling of getGameObject — same map lookup, but takes a RAPIER.Collider object and reads its .handle. It exists for one specific, subtle case the comment spells out:

// Resolve a collider to its owning GameObject (or undefined if unregistered).
// Lets a kinematic character controller feed its OWN contacts into the contact
// registry: a kinematic body keeps a skin gap from solids it moves into, so
// Rapier emits no collision event for it as the mover — but the controller's
// computed collisions still report what it touched. The game can resolve those
// to GameObjects and Dispatch them, so the same contact rules fire either way.
public GetGameObjectFromCollider(
  collider: RAPIER.Collider
): GameObject | undefined {
  return this.gameObjectRegistry.get(collider.handle);
}
Hard-won victory — kinematic mover gets no event. The player uses a Rapier kinematic character controller, which moves with a skin gap and resolves penetration itself. Because it never actually overlaps the solids it slides against, Rapier's EventQueue emits no collision event with the player as the mover. The controller's computedCollision(i) list still reports what it touched, though — so the player can call GetGameObjectFromCollider(hit.collider) and Contacts.Dispatch(...) manually, and a rule like "enemy touches player → game over" fires whether the enemy rolled into the player (a real event) or the player walked into the enemy (a computed collision). Same table, both directions. See Architecture: Collisions.

private handleCollisionEvents(): void

The engine's contact-dispatch core, called once per step from Update(). It drains the EventQueue and, for each event, resolves both colliders to game objects, decides sensor-vs-solid, fires the two objects' override hooks, and dispatches the matching declarative contact rules. There is no hard-coded "if player and enemy…" anywhere — that knowledge lives entirely in the game's ContactRegistry rules.

The drain callback signature is Rapier's: (handle1, handle2, started) — two collider handles and a boolean that is true for "contact began" and false for "contact ended."

this.EventQueue.drainCollisionEvents((handle1, handle2, started) => {
  const collider1 = this.World.getCollider(handle1);
  const collider2 = this.World.getCollider(handle2);

  // Skip if colliders don't exist
  if (!collider1 || !collider2) {
    return;
  }

  const gameObject1 = this.getGameObject(handle1);
  const gameObject2 = this.getGameObject(handle2);

  // Skip if either GameObject is not registered or is being destroyed
  if (
    !gameObject1 ||
    !gameObject2 ||
    gameObject1.IsBeingDestroyed ||
    gameObject2.IsBeingDestroyed
  ) {
    return;
  }

Two guard layers before any dispatch:

  • Colliders still alive — World.getCollider(handle) returns falsy if that collider was removed earlier in the same drain (e.g. a contact rule destroyed one body, and a later event still references it). Skipping avoids touching a freed handle.
  • Both registered and neither dying — an unregistered handle resolves to undefined; a GameObject.IsBeingDestroyed === true means it is mid-teardown.
Hard-won victory — the IsBeingDestroyed guard. GameObject.Destroy sets this.IsBeingDestroyed = true as its very first line, before removing the body. Combined with the check here, this prevents the classic bug where a contact handler runs against a half-torn-down object (mesh disposed, body removed) and dereferences something undefined. It is the exact mechanism behind commit b0436b4's "finally fix barrel/enemy bug." It also means dispatch is naturally re-entrancy-safe: a rule that destroys a body during the drain can't trigger a follow-up event against the corpse.

Next, route sensor vs. solid:

// Check if either collider is a sensor to route to appropriate callbacks
const isSensor1 = collider1.isSensor();
const isSensor2 = collider2.isSensor();
const isSensorEvent = isSensor1 || isSensor2;

if (isSensorEvent) {
  // This is a sensor intersection event
  if (started) {
    if (isSensor1 && gameObject1.OnSensorEnter) {
      gameObject1.OnSensorEnter(gameObject2);
    }
    if (isSensor2 && gameObject2.OnSensorEnter) {
      gameObject2.OnSensorEnter(gameObject1);
    }
    // Fire any matching declarative contact rules (two-sided internally)
    this.Contacts.Dispatch("sensorEnter", gameObject1, gameObject2);

    if (this.debug && this.debug.LogSensors) {
      this.debug.LogSensorEvent(gameObject1, gameObject2, "enter");
    }
  } else {
    // ... mirror: OnSensorExit + Dispatch("sensorExit", ...) + LogSensorEvent(..., "exit")
  }
} else {
  // This is a solid collision event
  if (started) {
    if (gameObject1.OnCollisionEnter) gameObject1.OnCollisionEnter(gameObject2);
    if (gameObject2.OnCollisionEnter) gameObject2.OnCollisionEnter(gameObject1);
    this.Contacts.Dispatch("enter", gameObject1, gameObject2);
    // ... LogCollisionEvent(..., "enter")
  } else {
    // ... OnCollisionExit + Dispatch("exit", ...) + LogCollisionEvent(..., "exit")
  }
}

Line-level reasoning for the routing:

  • Sensor detection is a property of the collider, not a separate event stream. In Rapier a sensor collider produces collision events (when armed with ActiveEvents.COLLISION_EVENTS), not a distinct intersection channel — so this one drain loop handles both, and collider.isSensor() is the discriminator. If either collider is a sensor, the whole event is treated as a sensor/intersection event.
  • The override hooks are one-sided, gated on the sensor flag. For sensor events, only the side that is the sensor gets its OnSensorEnter/Exit called (isSensor1 && gameObject1.OnSensorEnter) — the object passing through a trigger should not receive a "you are a sensor" callback. For solid events both sides are notified symmetrically. Every hook is optional (the ? on the method) so the if (…OnCollisionEnter) presence-check is mandatory.
  • The four phase strings — "enter", "exit", "sensorEnter", "sensorExit" — are the bridge to the contact table. They match exactly the ContactPhase union in Entities: ContactRegistry. Contacts.Dispatch(phase, gameObject1, gameObject2) is two-sided internally: the registry tests each rule as both (a,b) and (b,a), so dispatch passes the raw pair in handle order and lets the table sort out direction. That's why a rule fires no matter which body Rapier listed first.
  • Logging is opt-in and game-owned. this.debug.LogSensors / LogCollisions are getters that delegate to a game-registered CollisionLogSink (see Debug: Debug), so the engine forwards log events without knowing the concrete logger. Off by default; toggled in the debug GUI.
Note — enter/exit only, no "stay." Rapier's collision events fire on transition, not per-frame. There is no "still touching" event. The gameplay layer that needs continuous "am I on the ground right now" state does not get it from here — it recomputes per frame from the kinematic controller and writes ContactPoints booleans (the second file in this section). GameObject.enablePhysicsEvents even documents this: "Physics only dispatches enter/exit (no per-frame 'stay')."
Gotcha — events only fire if the collider is armed. A collider emits nothing unless setActiveEvents(RAPIER.ActiveEvents.COLLISION_EVENTS) was called on it. GameObject arms this automatically for any entity that defines an OnCollision*/OnSensor* callback (enablePhysicsEvents), and exposes enableContactEvents() for entities that only want the declarative table. If a contact rule "isn't firing," check the collider is armed before suspecting this dispatcher.

public SetPaused(paused: boolean): void

Sets this.IsPaused = paused. That's all it does — and that minimalism is the point.

// Pause or resume the physics simulation. The game decides WHEN to call this
// (e.g. on game over / reset); the engine just obeys — it names no game events.
public SetPaused(paused: boolean): void {
  this.IsPaused = paused;
}
Note — decoupling seam. The engine deliberately does not subscribe to "gameOver"/"gameWin" on the event bus. The game's GameDirector decides when to pause and calls SetPaused(true); the engine stays ignorant of game concepts. IsPaused is public for reads (e.g. UI), SetPaused is the intent-revealing setter.

public Update(): void

The per-step advance. Experience's onTick now runs a fixed-step accumulator: it calls Physics.Update() 0..N times per frame — once per accumulated 1/60s step, each time before that step's OnGameUpdate — and runs Renderer.Update once at the end of the frame. So game logic and rendering always see a freshly-stepped world.

if (this.IsPaused) {
  return;
}

// Time.Delta is the FIXED simulation timestep (set by the frame loop), so each
// physics step is constant and reproducible. No clamp needed — the frame loop
// bounds how often we step (the old Math.min(..., 0.1) was dead code anyway,
// since Time.Delta was already capped well below 0.1).
this.World.timestep = this.experience.Time.Delta;
this.World.step(this.EventQueue);

// Process collision and sensor events
this.handleCollisionEvents();

// Run debug physics logic if needed
if (this.debug) {
  this.debug.UpdatePhysicsDebug(this);
}
  • Pause short-circuit — when paused, the world is not stepped and no events are drained, freezing the simulation while the render loop still runs.
  • Fixed timestep this.World.timestep = this.experience.Time.Delta — Time.Delta is no longer the raw frame delta; it is the fixed simulation step (1/60s) that Core: Experience's accumulator assigns immediately before each Update(). Every step is the same constant size, so the simulation is frame-rate-independent and reproducible. The old Math.min(Time.Delta, 0.1) cap was deleted as dead code: the accumulator can only ever feed this a constant 1/60, and the wall-clock catch-up is bounded upstream — Core: Time clamps FrameDelta (the value that drives the accumulator, not the integration step) to 1/30. A single giant step can no longer reach Rapier, so nothing tunnels through walls on a frame hitch.
  • World.step(this.EventQueue) — advances the solver and fills the (auto-drained) queue with this step's collision/sensor transitions. Passing the queue is what makes events available to the very next line.
  • handleCollisionEvents() runs immediately after the step, in the same frame, so callbacks and contact rules act on the just-resolved positions.
  • Debug last — UpdatePhysicsDebug(this) rebuilds the collider wireframe and refreshes object counts (see Debug: PhysicsDebug), only if a debug ref was captured in Configure.
Gotcha — never set Time.Delta to 0. A zero timestep stalls the solver, so Core: Time flags the field "Never 0." In practice the frame loop always assigns Delta = Time.FixedTimestep (1/60) before each step, and the constructor seeds it to the same value, so this line never receives 0 — but preserve that invariant if you ever touch the loop.
Hard-won victory — a true fixed timestep. This used to be a variable (clamped real-delta) step. It is now a genuine accumulator-based fixed timestep: Core: Experience's onTick accumulates FrameDelta and runs Physics.Update() (with the matching OnGameUpdate) in 0..N constant 1/60s steps per frame, rendering once, while Rapier always receives exactly that fixed step. The payoff is a simulation that is frame-rate-independent and reproducible — identical inputs play out identically on a 60Hz and a 144Hz display.

public Destroy(): void

Tears down the WASM world and the debug wireframe. Called by Experience.Destroy in its "camera then physics then renderer then resources" order.

// Dispose of the Rapier world (guarded so a Destroy after a half-failed
// Configure — e.g. RAPIER.init() threw before World was built — doesn't cascade).
this.World?.free();

// Tear down the debug wireframe (PhysicsDebug owns its own mesh)
if (this.debug) {
  this.debug.DestroyPhysicsDebug();
}
  • World?.free() releases the WASM memory backing every body, collider, and joint in one call — there is no per-body cleanup loop here because individual entities free their own bodies during their Destroy(); this is the final sweep of whatever remains. The ?. is deliberate: if Configure() aborted before World was built (e.g. RAPIER.init() threw), World is still undefined, and the optional chain makes Destroy() a safe no-op instead of throwing a second error on top of the first.
  • DestroyPhysicsDebug() removes and disposes the wireframe LineSegments mesh, which PhysicsDebug owns (Physics itself holds no debug mesh).
Note — teardown survives a half-failed boot. Guarding with this.World?.free() is part of the engine's broader teardown hygiene: Core: Experience now resets its singleton and uses optional chaining when tearing down subsystems, so a Configure() that died partway through can still be cleanly Destroy()ed and retried. Physics holds up its end of that contract — never assume World exists just because Destroy() was called.
Warning — contact-force events are not used here. Rapier's EventQueue can also surface contact-force events via drainContactForceEvents (impulse magnitudes between solids). This engine never drains them — only drainCollisionEvents is called — because the gameplay only needs enter/exit transitions and trigger overlaps, not force magnitudes. If you ever want "hard landing" damage scaled by impact force, that is where you'd add a second drain pass; today it does not exist, so don't expect a force-events code path.

Modifying this: To add a new kind of contact behavior, you almost never touch this file — add a rule to Config: contactRules (game data) or an OnCollision*/OnSensor* override on the entity. Touch handleCollisionEvents only to change the dispatch mechanism itself (e.g. add a drainContactForceEvents pass, or a new phase string — which must also be added to ContactPhase in Entities: ContactRegistry). To change global feel, edit the gravity vector in Configure or the fixed step (Time.FixedTimestep) that Core: Time defines. Keep this class game-blind: if you find yourself importing an EntityType or checking a specific name here, the logic belongs in the game layer instead.

src/engine/types/contactPoints.ts 34 LOC

A pure TypeScript interface — no runtime code — describing the set of surfaces a character can be touching right now, as eight booleans. It is the engine's contribution to the per-frame "where am I?" vocabulary; the engine defines the shape, the game owns an instance and decides what each flag means for gameplay. Concretely, Player (game layer) declares public IsTouching!: ContactPoints and rebuilds it every frame: the player's kinematic-controller collision pass writes ground/ceiling/leftSide/rightSide/edgePlatform, and the ladder sensor writes ladderCore/ladderTop/ladderBottom. Player state handlers (Player states: Idle/Running/Jumping/Falling/Climbing) read these to decide transitions, and PlayerDebug binds them into the dat.GUI as live checkboxes.

Note — this is the "stay" state the event queue won't give you. Because Rapier only emits enter/exit transitions (see handleCollisionEvents above), continuous "still on the ground" information has to be recomputed each frame. ContactPoints is that recomputed snapshot — the bridge between raw physics events and the gameplay state machine. It is reset to all-false and refilled every tick, so reading a stale value across frames is never a concern.
FieldGroupMeaning (from the doc comments)Set by
groundBasic directionTouching the ground; enables jumping, resets coyote time, drives the idle/run animation state.Player collision pass
ceilingBasic directionTouching a ceiling; cancels upward velocity, prevents jumping through ceilings.Player collision pass
leftSideBasic directionTouching a wall on the left; blocks leftward movement, can enable wall slide.Player collision pass
rightSideBasic directionTouching a wall on the right; blocks rightward movement, can enable wall slide.Player collision pass
edgePlatformSpecial platformStanding on an edge platform; allows edge-specific animations/mechanics.Player collision pass (reads Platform.IsEdge)
ladderCoreLadder zoneInside the climbable body of a ladder; can climb up/down with directional input.Ladder sensor
ladderTopLadder zoneAt the top of a ladder; transition from climbing to standing on the platform above.Ladder sensor
ladderBottomLadder zoneAt the bottom of a ladder; transition from climbing to standing on the ground below.Ladder sensor
 
export default interface ContactPoints {
  // ===== BASIC COLLISION DIRECTIONS =====
  ground: boolean;
  ceiling: boolean;
  leftSide: boolean;
  rightSide: boolean;

  // ===== SPECIAL PLATFORM TYPES =====
  edgePlatform: boolean;

  // ===== LADDER COLLISION ZONES =====
  ladderCore: boolean;
  ladderTop: boolean;
  ladderBottom: boolean;
}

Why these eight, and why split this way:

  • Four cardinal directions are the minimum a 2D platformer needs: ground (jump gating + coyote time), ceiling (head-bonk velocity cancel), and the two walls (movement blocking / wall slide). They come from the player's own per-frame collision query, not from the contact table — this is "self" state, not "who hit me."
  • edgePlatform is separated out because an edge tile is still "ground," but unlocks edge-specific behavior; the player sets it by resolving the ground collider to its Platform and reading platform.IsEdge.
  • The three ladder zones exist because a ladder is not one trigger but three overlapping sensors — a tall core for climbing plus thin top/bottom bands for the hand-off to standing. The state machine combines them, e.g. "in core, and not (at top while pressing up) and not (at bottom while pressing down)" to decide whether to enter the climbing state (see Player states: Running/Idle/Climbing). These three are written by the LadderSensor's sensor callbacks — the engine-side sensor events from this section's handleCollisionEvents flowing through to gameplay booleans.
Tip — naming convention. The fields stay camelCase deliberately (unlike the PascalCase public API elsewhere) because they mirror "real types" the way Rapier's own x/y/z vectors do; PlayerDebug notes this exact rationale where it binds them into the GUI.

Modifying this: Add a boolean here whenever the player needs a new persistent "am I touching X?" surface (e.g. onSlope, inWater). Then do two things or the flag is dead: (1) reset it to false in the player's per-frame reset block, and (2) set it true from whatever detects it — a collision pass or a sensor callback. Because this is just a type, adding a field is free at runtime; the cost is the gameplay code that must populate and read it. Keep it boolean and surface-oriented — numeric per-instance data does not belong here (per Types: userData, that lives as typed fields on entity classes).

Engine Entities — GameObject, GameSensor, ContactRegistry, StateMachine

This is the heart of the engine's entity layer — four classes and two type files that every game entity is built from, and not one of them names a game concept. GameObject is the base class that marries a Rapier rigid body to a Three.js mesh and syncs them every frame; GameSensor turns that body into a non-solid trigger; ContactRegistry is the declarative "when an A touches a B, run this" table that replaced a wall of hard-coded if/else; and StateMachine is the tiny generic FSM the player and enemies both run on. The two type files (gameObjectType, userData) are the opaque vocabulary the engine carries on the game's behalf.

src/engine/entities/gameObject.ts src/engine/entities/gameSensor.ts src/engine/entities/contactRegistry.ts src/engine/entities/stateMachine.ts src/engine/types/gameObjectType.ts src/engine/types/userData.ts

src/engine/entities/gameObject.ts 509 LOC

This file IS the base class for every spawnable thing in the world — the player, enemies, platforms, ladders, teleporters, win flags, trash cans all extend GameObject (directly or via GameSensor). Nobody constructs a bare GameObject; subclasses call its protected builders from their own constructors. It imports the engine singletons it needs at birth — Experience (for the shared Scene and Physics), Physics (to create bodies/colliders and register for collision callbacks) — plus GameObjectType and UserData (the opaque routing vocabulary) and getUserData from physicsHelpers. Lifecycle: constructor → initializeAttributes() grabs singletons and zeroes transforms; a subclass then calls createObjectPhysics(...) (rigid body + collider + registration), optionally CreateObjectGraphics(...) or createObjectGraphicsDebug(...) (attach a mesh); each sim step the owner calls syncGraphicsToPhysics() to copy the body's transform onto the mesh (and record the previous one), and each render frame it calls InterpolateGraphics(alpha) to lerp the mesh between those two transforms so motion stays smooth above the fixed sim rate; finally Destroy() tears down mesh, geometry, body and collider.

Note — the generic-ization. Read this file end to end and you will not find the words "player", "barrel", "ladder" or "Donkey Kong" anywhere. The recent refactor (commits "split webgl into game and engine" and "Replace collision routing with contact table") stripped every game name out of here. Identity is carried as two opaque strings (type / name in UserData) assigned by the game layer; shape is one of three primitives (GameObjectType); collision routing is data in the ContactRegistry. The base class is now reusable by a fresh project with no game.

// fields: engine refs, render side, physics side, cached transform, destroy flag

The fields split into five tiers. Engine refs (experience, scene, Physics) are the singletons grabbed at construction. Render side (geometry, material, mesh, spriteScale) is the optional Three.js half — a GameObject can exist as pure physics with no mesh. Physics side is PhysicsBody, the Rapier RigidBody. Cached transform (InitialSize/CurrentSize/InitialTranslation/CurrentTranslation/CurrentRotation) is mirrored off the body so other code never has to dig into collider.shape.halfExtents. And render interpolation (previousRenderTranslation/previousRenderRotation plus public RenderTranslation) holds the previous sim transform and the latest, so the per-frame render pass can lerp between them — and exposes RenderTranslation, the smoothed position other systems (notably the camera) follow instead of the stepped sim position. 

protected experience!: Experience;
protected scene!: THREE.Scene;
public Physics!: Physics;  // Made public for GameUtils temporary sensor checking

protected gameObjectType!: string;

protected mesh?: THREE.Mesh | THREE.Sprite | THREE.Group;
public PhysicsBody?: RAPIER.RigidBody;

public InitialSize!: RAPIER.Vector2;
public CurrentSize!: RAPIER.Vector2;
public InitialTranslation!: RAPIER.Vector2;
public CurrentTranslation!: RAPIER.Vector;
private CurrentRotation!: number;

// Render interpolation: the previous sim transform plus the latest, so the render
// pass can lerp between them and stay smooth above the sim rate. RenderTranslation
// is the interpolated position other systems (e.g. the camera) should follow.
protected previousRenderTranslation = { x: 0, y: 0 };
protected previousRenderRotation = 0;
public RenderTranslation = { x: 0, y: 0 };

public IsBeingDestroyed!: boolean; // true while tearing down, blocks callbacks

Line notes: Physics is public only because the temporary sensor-polling helpers in GameUtils (IsAnySensorTriggered*) reach through an entity to its world — the comment says so. CurrentSize caching is the explicit reason given in createObjectPhysics: "so I don't have to look for it in physicsBody.collider.shape.halfExtents later." And IsBeingDestroyed is the guard that makes teardown safe mid-frame — Physics.handleCollisionEvents and syncGraphicsToPhysics both bail when it is set (see the Destroy callout).

OnCollisionEnter?(other) / OnCollisionExit?(other) / OnSensorEnter?(other) / OnSensorExit?(other)

Four optional callback slots — declared, not implemented. A subclass that wants to react to a contact overrides the relevant one; if it does, enablePhysicsEvents() auto-arms the matching Rapier events. Physics.handleCollisionEvents calls these directly on the resolved GameObject: the solid pair fires OnCollisionEnter/Exit on both bodies, the sensor pair fires OnSensorEnter/Exit only on the side that is actually the sensor. 

public OnCollisionEnter?(other: GameObject): void;
public OnCollisionExit?(other: GameObject): void;
public OnSensorEnter?(other: GameObject): void;
public OnSensorExit?(other: GameObject): void;
Note — two ways to react to a contact. An entity's own reaction to the world it moves through (e.g. Enemy.OnCollisionEnter reverses at a wall, enemy.ts:138) lives in these overrides. Cross-entity verdicts (enemy kills player, player wins) live as data in contactRules and run through the ContactRegistry. Both are dispatched from the same Physics drain loop — the callbacks first, then Contacts.Dispatch(...).

constructor() / private initializeAttributes()

The constructor does exactly one thing: call initializeAttributes(). That method grabs the three engine singletons off Experience.GetInstance() — the shared Scene and the Physics world — and zeroes every cached transform field to a fresh Vector2(0,0), empties gameObjectType, and clears IsBeingDestroyed. It does not create a body or a mesh; that is the subclass's job, by calling createObjectPhysics after super(). 

private initializeAttributes() {
  this.experience = Experience.GetInstance();
  this.scene = this.experience.Scene;
  this.Physics = this.experience.Physics;

  this.InitialSize = new RAPIER.Vector2(0, 0);
  this.CurrentSize = new RAPIER.Vector2(0, 0);
  this.InitialTranslation = new RAPIER.Vector2(0, 0);
  this.CurrentTranslation = new RAPIER.Vector2(0, 0);
  this.CurrentRotation = 0;

  this.gameObjectType = "";
  this.IsBeingDestroyed = false;
}
Gotcha — singletons must exist first. The constructor assumes Experience is already configured and experience.Physics has run Configure() (Rapier init() is async). Any entity built before the physics world exists will read an undefined Physics. In practice entities are only ever spawned by GameDirector after resourcesReady, well past engine boot.

protected createObjectPhysics(name, gameObjectType, size, position, rotation, specifiedRigidBodyType = RAPIER.RigidBodyDesc.fixed())

The main physics builder — every subclass constructor calls it. Params: name seeds the per-instance id (defaults to "GameObject"); gameObjectType picks the collider shape (a GameObjectType value); size is full width/height; position is the spawn point; rotation is in radians; specifiedRigidBodyType defaults to a fixed body (walls/platforms) — dynamic bodies (enemies) pass RAPIER.RigidBodyDesc.dynamic().

Body of the method, in order: cache InitialTranslation/InitialSize/CurrentSize and the type; build a collider descriptor from the type via createCollider; create the rigid body in the world; set its translation and rotation (mirroring both into the Current* caches); stamp userData; attach the collider to the body; register with Physics so the collider handle resolves back to this GameObject; then auto-arm events. 

const collider = this.createCollider(size, gameObjectType);

this.PhysicsBody = this.Physics.World.createRigidBody(specifiedRigidBodyType);

this.PhysicsBody.setTranslation({ x: position.x, y: position.y }, true);
this.CurrentTranslation = this.PhysicsBody.translation();
this.PhysicsBody.setRotation(rotation, true);
this.CurrentRotation = this.PhysicsBody.rotation();

// type = shared routing flag; name = per-instance id, both default to `name`
this.PhysicsBody.userData = { type: name, name: name } as UserData;

this.Physics.World.createCollider(collider, this.PhysicsBody);
this.Physics.RegisterGameObject(this);   // collider.handle -> this
this.enablePhysicsEvents();              // arm events if callbacks exist
Note — type vs name both seeded from one arg. At creation both userData fields are set to name. The comment in the source explains the contract: type is the shared routing flag many entities can carry (one contact rule matches all of them), name is the per-instance id. They start equal; a subclass calls SetType() and/or SetName() afterward to differentiate. The engine treats both as opaque strings.
Gotcha — registration is per collider, by handle. RegisterGameObject walks numColliders() and maps each collider.handle → gameObject. The contact callbacks can only fire for a collider that is registered, which is why registration happens here, immediately after the collider is attached. Destroy() must call the matching UnregisterGameObject or the map leaks stale entries pointing at dead bodies.

private enablePhysicsEvents()

The auto-arm step. It inspects which of the four callback slots this instance defined and OR-s the matching Rapier ActiveEvents bit onto collider 0 — but only if at least one callback exists. Entities that define no callbacks (a plain wall) stay event-silent, which keeps the per-step event queue small. 

let eventsToEnable = 0;

if (this.OnCollisionEnter || this.OnCollisionExit) {
  eventsToEnable |= RAPIER.ActiveEvents.COLLISION_EVENTS;
}
// In Rapier, sensors use collision events, not separate intersection events
if (this.OnSensorEnter || this.OnSensorExit) {
  eventsToEnable |= RAPIER.ActiveEvents.COLLISION_EVENTS;
}

if (eventsToEnable > 0) {
  collider.setActiveEvents(eventsToEnable);
}
Note — there is no "stay" event. The source comment is explicit: physics only dispatches enter/exit, no per-frame "stay" (see Physics.handleCollisionEvents). Anything that needs continuous "am I overlapping right now?" must poll — which is exactly the GameSensor ladder path described below. Also note both sensor and collision callbacks arm the same COLLISION_EVENTS bit, because in Rapier a sensor reports through the collision event stream; Physics disambiguates by checking collider.isSensor().

protected enableContactEvents(colliderIndex = 0)

The manual counterpart to the auto-arm above. Entities that route purely through the declarative ContactRegistry — and therefore define no callback overrides — still need their collider to emit events, so they call this explicitly. Teleporter, CameraSensor and WinFlag all do exactly this right after setAsSensor(true) (teleporter.ts:31-33). 

protected enableContactEvents(colliderIndex: number = 0) {
  this.PhysicsBody?.collider(colliderIndex)?.setActiveEvents(
    RAPIER.ActiveEvents.COLLISION_EVENTS
  );
}
Gotcha — callback-defining entities must NOT call this. Enemy defines OnCollisionEnter, so enablePhysicsEvents() already armed it; its constructor comment literally says "no enableContactEvents needed" (enemy.ts:98). Calling it a second time is harmless (idempotent set) but signals confusion: if your entity has a callback, it is armed automatically; if it is rule-only, arm it here.

protected createCollider(size, gameObjectType)

Maps the opaque type string to a Rapier ColliderDesc. Three shapes only — ball, capsule, cuboid — and the default branch falls through to a cuboid, so any unrecognised type is a box rather than a crash. 

switch (gameObjectType) {
  case GameObjectType.SPHERE:
    return RAPIER.ColliderDesc.ball(size.width / 2);
  case GameObjectType.CAPSULE:
    return RAPIER.ColliderDesc.capsule(size.height / 2, size.width);
  case GameObjectType.CUBE:
  default:
    return RAPIER.ColliderDesc.cuboid(size.width / 2, size.height / 2);
}
Hard-won victory (D5 / "D-lean"). The source comment spells out the architectural diet: "Slopes are rotated cubes and complex shapes are overlapped cubes (no polyline or convex-mesh)." A whole class of geometry bugs — degenerate polylines, non-convex meshes that Rapier silently rejects, expensive convex decomposition — was eliminated by restricting the engine to cuboid/ball/capsule. Note the conversions: ball takes a radius (width/2), cuboid takes half-extents, and capsule takes a half-height plus radius.

protected setGeometry(geometry?) / protected setMaterial(material?, spriteScale?)

Trivial setters that stash the Three.js geometry/material (and optional sprite scale) on the instance so Destroy() can later dispose them. They are protected so subclasses and the debug-mesh builder can call them; they are split out from mesh creation so a subclass can supply a custom geometry/material before the mesh is built. 

protected setGeometry(geometry?: THREE.BoxGeometry | THREE.SphereGeometry) {
  this.geometry = geometry;
}
protected setMaterial(material?: THREE.MeshBasicMaterial | THREE.SpriteMaterial, spriteScale?: number) {
  this.material = material;
  this.spriteScale = spriteScale;
}

private createMeshGroup() / private async addMeshesToGroupAsync(meshes, batchSize) / private disposeMeshHelper(object)

The mesh-plumbing trio used by CreateObjectGraphics. createMeshGroup lazily makes the mesh an empty THREE.Group (idempotent — returns early if one exists). addMeshesToGroupAsync adds cloned meshes to that group in batchSize chunks, yielding to the event loop with await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 0)) between batches, then adds the whole group to the scene once. disposeMeshHelper frees one object's geometry and material(s), normalising the single-vs-array material case. 

private async addMeshesToGroupAsync(meshes: any[], batchSize: number) {
  for (let i = 0; i < meshes.length; i += batchSize) {
    const batchOfMeshes = meshes.slice(i, i + batchSize);
    batchOfMeshes.forEach((mesh) => this.mesh!.add(mesh));
    // Allow other operations to execute
    await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  }
  this.scene.add(this.mesh!);
}
Hard-won victory — async batching keeps the frame alive. The comment explains it: loading many meshes from the Blender scene synchronously blocks the main thread ("too many synchronous loads to THREE.Scene"). Slicing into batches of 5 and yielding a macrotask between them lets rendering and input breathe during a level load, so the loading overlay actually animates instead of freezing.

protected createObjectGraphicsDebug(meshColor, opacity = 1)

Builds a simple solid debug mesh sized to the collider, so a sensor or an otherwise-invisible body can be seen. opacity < 1 flips the material's transparent flag. It switches on the cached gameObjectType: a CUBE gets a BoxGeometry sized (CurrentSize.x, CurrentSize.y, 1), a SPHERE gets a SphereGeometry(CurrentSize.x); the default branch builds a mesh from whatever geometry/material were set earlier. It finishes by adding the mesh to the scene and calling syncGraphicsToPhysics() once to place it. 

case GameObjectType.CUBE:
  this.setGeometry(new THREE.BoxGeometry(this.CurrentSize.x, this.CurrentSize.y, 1));
  this.setMaterial(new THREE.MeshBasicMaterial({
    color: meshColor, opacity, transparent: isMaterialTransparent,
  }));
  this.mesh = new THREE.Mesh(this.geometry, this.material);
  break;
// ...
this.scene.add(this.mesh);
this.syncGraphicsToPhysics();

Line note: this is how the disabled debug visualisations in LadderSensor, Teleporter and CameraSensor work — they call createObjectGraphicsDebug("blue", 0.5) etc. (mostly left commented out in source) to paint the otherwise-invisible sensor volume.

protected syncGraphicsToPhysics(meshOffset = { x: 0, y: 0, z: 0 })

The per-sim-step bridge: copy the physics body's transform onto the Three.js mesh. The owner calls this once per fixed sim step from its Update() (Player, Enemy). Before it overwrites the Current* caches it now records them into previousRenderTranslation/previousRenderRotation — prev = last step's transform, current = this step's — so the per-frame render pass (InterpolateGraphics) has both endpoints to lerp between. meshOffset lets a subclass nudge the visual relative to the collider — notably the z is taken straight from the offset (2D physics has no z), so sprites can be layered front-to-back. 

if (this.IsBeingDestroyed || !this.PhysicsBody) {
  return;
}

// Record the previous sim transform so the render pass can interpolate toward
// the new one (see InterpolateGraphics).
this.previousRenderTranslation.x = this.CurrentTranslation.x;
this.previousRenderTranslation.y = this.CurrentTranslation.y;
this.previousRenderRotation = this.CurrentRotation;

this.CurrentTranslation = this.PhysicsBody.translation();
this.mesh?.position.set(
  this.CurrentTranslation.x + meshOffset.x,
  this.CurrentTranslation.y + meshOffset.y,
  meshOffset.z
);

this.CurrentRotation = this.PhysicsBody.rotation();
this.mesh?.rotation.set(this.mesh?.rotation.x, this.mesh?.rotation.y, this.CurrentRotation);
Gotcha — physics is the source of truth, never the mesh. Data flows one way: body → mesh. Nothing reads the mesh back into physics. The early-return on IsBeingDestroyed || !PhysicsBody is what makes it safe to call during teardown — a frame that fires after Destroy() nulled the body simply does nothing instead of dereferencing undefined. Also note CurrentTranslation is the stepped sim position refreshed here once per sim step, so code reading it (ladder containment math) gets last-step's exact position. The smoothed camera-follow position is a different field — RenderTranslation, written by InterpolateGraphics below.

public InterpolateGraphics(alpha, meshOffset = { x: 0, y: 0, z: 0 })

The per-frame render placement, the sibling to syncGraphicsToPhysics. Where the sync runs once per fixed sim step, this runs once per rendered frame for moving entities (Player, Enemies) and lerps the mesh between previousRenderTranslation and CurrentTranslation by alpha — the fraction (0..1) of how far into the next sim step the current frame falls (the engine computes it as accumulator / dt). Static entities are placed once by the sync and never need this. It also writes the interpolated point into RenderTranslation so the camera follows the smooth visual position rather than the stepped sim position. 

public InterpolateGraphics(
  alpha: number,
  meshOffset: { x: number; y: number; z: number } = { x: 0, y: 0, z: 0 }
) {
  if (this.IsBeingDestroyed || !this.mesh) {
    return;
  }

  const x =
    this.previousRenderTranslation.x +
    (this.CurrentTranslation.x - this.previousRenderTranslation.x) * alpha;
  const y =
    this.previousRenderTranslation.y +
    (this.CurrentTranslation.y - this.previousRenderTranslation.y) * alpha;

  this.RenderTranslation.x = x;
  this.RenderTranslation.y = y;

  this.mesh.position.set(x + meshOffset.x, y + meshOffset.y, meshOffset.z);

  // Interpolate rotation along the SHORTEST arc. The Rapier 2D angle wraps at ±π,
  // so a rolling barrel's angle jumps from ~+π to ~-π between steps; a naive lerp
  // would spin the long way round (a visible one-frame backspin). atan2(sin,cos)
  // wraps the delta into (-π, π] so we always take the short way.
  let dRot = this.CurrentRotation - this.previousRenderRotation;
  dRot = Math.atan2(Math.sin(dRot), Math.cos(dRot));
  const rot = this.previousRenderRotation + dRot * alpha;
  this.mesh.rotation.set(this.mesh.rotation.x, this.mesh.rotation.y, rot);
}
Hard-won victory — shortest-arc rotation (no one-frame backspin). Position lerps cleanly, but rotation does not: the Rapier 2D angle wraps at ±π, so a rolling barrel's angle jumps from roughly +π to roughly -π between two sim steps. A naive prev + (cur - prev) * alpha would read that as a near-2π delta and spin the barrel the long way round — a visible backspin every time the angle wraps. Computing the delta and folding it through Math.atan2(Math.sin(dRot), Math.cos(dRot)) wraps it back into (-π, π], so the interpolation always takes the short way and the barrel rolls forward smoothly.
Note — two transforms, two cadences. syncGraphicsToPhysics is the sim-rate truth (it samples the body and snapshots prev/current); InterpolateGraphics is the frame-rate smoothing (it blends those two). They guard differently on purpose: the sync needs the live body (!PhysicsBody bails) because it reads from it, while the interp only needs a mesh (!this.mesh bails) because it reads from the already-snapshotted caches. The owner wires them up as a pair — see GameDirector.Update (sim) vs GameDirector.RenderUpdate (frame).

protected setCollisionGroup(collisionGroups, colliderIndex = 0) / protected setCollisionMask(collisionMask, colliderIndex = 0)

The two halves of Rapier's 32-bit interaction filter. Rapier packs membership group in the low 16 bits and the filter mask in the high 16 bits of one collisionGroups() value. These methods let a subclass set one half without clobbering the other: setCollisionGroup reads the current mask (>> 16), writes the new group, and re-OR-s the mask back; setCollisionMask does the mirror, preserving the low-16 group (& 0xffff) and writing the mask (<< 16). 

// set group bits, preserve the current mask
const currentMask = this.PhysicsBody.collider(colliderIndex).collisionGroups() >> 16;
this.PhysicsBody.collider(colliderIndex)
  .setCollisionGroups(collisionGroups | (currentMask << 16));

// set mask bits, preserve the current group
const currentGroup = this.PhysicsBody.collider(colliderIndex).collisionGroups() & 0xffff;
this.PhysicsBody.collider(colliderIndex)
  .setCollisionGroups(currentGroup | (collisionMask << 16));
Tip — call order is free. Because each method preserves the other half, a subclass can call them in either order: Platform does setCollisionGroup(PLATFORM) then setCollisionMask(DEFAULT) (platform.ts:48-49); Enemy sets ENEMY group then a PLATFORM | PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX mask. The actual bit constants live game-side in gameCollisionGroups — the engine only knows how to splice the two 16-bit fields.

public SetType(newType?) / public SetName(newName?)

Post-construction identity edits, both reading the live userData via getUserData (from physicsHelpers). SetType sets the shared routing type and mirrors it onto name — so after a SetType the instance name is the type again; call SetName after to give it a distinct per-instance id. Both no-op on a falsy argument, so a subclass can pass an optional value straight through. 

public SetType(newType?: string) {
  if (!newType) { return; }
  const userData = getUserData(this.PhysicsBody);
  userData.type = newType;
  userData.name = newType;   // name re-mirrors to type
}
public SetName(newName?: string) {
  if (!newName) { return; }
  getUserData(this.PhysicsBody).name = newName;
}
Gotcha — order matters: SetType clobbers name. Because SetType rewrites name = newType, calling SetName("barrel_07") and then SetType(ENEMY) silently throws the name away. Always do SetType first, then SetName. getUserData returns a safe empty {type:"",name:""} if the body is undefined, so these never throw on a partially-built object — they just write into a throwaway.

private TeleportRelative(newX, newY) / public TeleportToPosition(targetX, targetY)

Movement helpers. TeleportRelative offsets the body from its cached CurrentTranslation; TeleportToPosition computes the delta to an absolute world point and defers to it, then snaps the render-interpolation state to the target so the move reads as an instant jump, not a one-frame slide. Public TeleportToPosition is what the teleporter contact rule calls (contactRules.ts:49) and what PlayerDebug's click-to-teleport uses (playerDebug.ts:379). 

public TeleportToPosition(targetX: number, targetY: number) {
  const deltaX = targetX - this.CurrentTranslation.x;
  const deltaY = targetY - this.CurrentTranslation.y;
  this.TeleportRelative(deltaX, deltaY);   // wake = true

  // Snap the render-interpolation state to the new position so the mesh JUMPS to
  // the teleport target instead of sliding across the level over one frame (reset,
  // teleporter). Without this, prev=old/curr=new would lerp the whole distance.
  this.CurrentTranslation = this.PhysicsBody!.translation();
  this.previousRenderTranslation.x = this.CurrentTranslation.x;
  this.previousRenderTranslation.y = this.CurrentTranslation.y;
  this.RenderTranslation.x = this.CurrentTranslation.x;
  this.RenderTranslation.y = this.CurrentTranslation.y;
}
Hard-won victory — teleports JUMP, they don't slide. Render interpolation lerps the mesh from previousRenderTranslation to CurrentTranslation every frame. A teleport (a reset back to spawn, a teleporter pad) sets current to the far-away target while previous still holds the old spot — so without intervention the next frame would smoothly glide the player across the whole level over one step. Collapsing all three (CurrentTranslation = previousRenderTranslation = RenderTranslation = target) zeroes the lerp distance, so the entity simply appears at the destination.
Note — relies on a fresh cache. The opening delta math reads CurrentTranslation, which is refreshed once per sim step in syncGraphicsToPhysics. Within a single frame that is last step's position; for a teleport that is fine (the delta self-corrects against the real value), but it is why setTranslation passes true to wake the body — a sleeping dynamic body must wake to honour the move. The snap at the end then re-reads the body's true post-move translation, so the interpolation endpoints are exact.

public async CreateObjectGraphics(resourceFromLoader)

Attaches real art (a loaded GLTF/Blender scene) to this body. Param: resourceFromLoader is a loaded resource whose .scene.children are the meshes — handed in by GameDirector from this.resources.Items.* (e.g. gameDirector.ts:521, enemy.CreateObjectGraphics(this.resources.Items.enemy)). It ensures the group exists, deep-clones each child mesh so the shared source asset is never reparented — and clones that mesh's geometry and material too, so this instance owns its own GPU buffers — batches them into the scene async, then syncs the group to the physics transform once. 

this.createMeshGroup();

const blenderMeshes: THREE.Mesh[] = [];
for (const child of resourceFromLoader.scene.children) {
  if (child?.isMesh) {
    const cloned: THREE.Mesh = child.clone();    // clone so the original keeps its parent
    cloned.geometry = child.geometry.clone();    // own geometry buffer
    cloned.material = Array.isArray(child.material)
      ? child.material.map((m: THREE.Material) => m.clone())
      : child.material.clone();                  // own material(s)
    blenderMeshes.push(cloned);
  }
}

await this.addMeshesToGroupAsync(blenderMeshes, 5);
this.syncGraphicsToPhysics();
Hard-won victory — clone the mesh and its GPU buffers. Two hazards stack here. First, adding a mesh to a new THREE.Group removes it from its old parent — and the shared loaded GLTF in ResourceLoader's cache is reused across many entities, so moving the original's children would empty the cached asset and leave every later spawn blank; cloning each child first keeps the source intact. Second, a plain .clone() still shares geometry and material with the source. So when a dead entity (e.g. a barrel that despawns every couple of seconds) called Destroy() and disposed those, it freed buffers still in use by the source asset and every other live clone — flickering or vanishing art. Cloning geometry and material too gives each instance its own buffers, so Destroy() frees only what that instance owns.

public Destroy()

Full teardown of both halves. The very first line is an idempotent guard: if IsBeingDestroyed is already set, it returns immediately, so a second (or re-entrant) Destroy() is a harmless no-op. Otherwise it sets IsBeingDestroyed = true, then disposes and removes the mesh and all its children from the scene, disposes the cached geometry/material, and finally unregisters from Physics and removes the rigid body. Removing the body also removes its attached colliders, so there is no manual collider cleanup. 

// Idempotent: a second Destroy() (e.g. a re-entrant "gameObjectRemoved") is a
// no-op instead of double-freeing the body or recursing.
if (this.IsBeingDestroyed) {
  return;
}

// Set immediately — guards concurrent collision callbacks
this.IsBeingDestroyed = true;

if (this.mesh) {
  this.disposeMeshHelper(this.mesh);
  this.scene.remove(this.mesh);
  this.mesh.children.forEach((child) => this.disposeMeshHelper(child));
  this.mesh.clear();
  this.mesh = undefined;
}

this.geometry?.dispose();
this.material?.dispose();

if (this.PhysicsBody) {
  this.Physics.UnregisterGameObject(this);          // drop handle -> this mapping
  this.Physics.World.removeRigidBody(this.PhysicsBody); // also frees colliders
  this.PhysicsBody = undefined;
}
Hard-won victory — the destroy flag is set first, on purpose. Physics events drain after World.step(), and a body can register a contact in the very step it is being destroyed. Physics.handleCollisionEvents bails if either party IsBeingDestroyed (physics.ts:115-121), and syncGraphicsToPhysics bails too. Setting the flag at the top of Destroy() — before the body still physically exists for the rest of the method — is what prevents a callback firing on a half-torn-down object (a use-after-free style bug that manifested as enemies "killing" the player on the same frame they fell into the trash can).
Note — the guard also makes Destroy idempotent. The early-return reuses that same IsBeingDestroyed latch: a second call returns before touching anything. That matters because a contact handler may Emitter.emit("gameObjectRemoved", ...), the director's handler calls .Destroy(), and a path that re-emits for the same object would otherwise recurse or double-free the body. Because the latch never resets, a destroyed instance is permanently dead — whoever reuses a slot (e.g. GameDirector rebuilding level graphics) constructs a fresh GameObject rather than re-destroying the old one.
Gotcha — order is load-bearing. Unregister before removeRigidBody: UnregisterGameObject reads numColliders() off the still-live body to find the handles to delete. Remove the body first and that lookup would have nothing to walk, leaking the registry entry.

Lifecycle of one entity, end to end:

flowchart TD
  A["subclass ctor calls super()"] --> B["initializeAttributes(): grab Experience/Scene/Physics, zero transforms"]
  B --> C["createObjectPhysics(): body + collider + RegisterGameObject + enablePhysicsEvents"]
  C --> D{has art?}
  D -- yes --> E["CreateObjectGraphics(): clone GLTF meshes, batch into scene"]
  D -- debug --> F["createObjectGraphicsDebug(): solid box/ball"]
  E --> G["each SIM step: owner.Update() -> syncGraphicsToPhysics()<br/>(snapshot prev = current, then current from body)"]
  F --> G
  G --> G2["each RENDER frame (moving entities): InterpolateGraphics(alpha)<br/>lerp mesh prev->current + write RenderTranslation"]
  G2 --> H["Destroy(): IsBeingDestroyed=true, dispose mesh, Unregister + removeRigidBody"]

Modifying this: add a new collider shape by extending GameObjectType and adding a case to createCollider (and a matching debug branch in createObjectGraphicsDebug if you want it visualised) — but honour the D5 diet: prefer composing cuboids/balls over reintroducing polyline/convex meshes. Keep this class game-blind: never reference an EntityType or game rule here — identity stays in UserData, routing stays in ContactRegistry. If you add a teardown step, put it in Destroy() after the IsBeingDestroyed = true line, and unregister before removing the body.

src/engine/entities/gameSensor.ts 38 LOC

This file IS the thin specialization of GameObject for non-solid trigger volumes — regions that detect things passing through them without physically blocking them. It extends GameObject and adds only two methods: setAsSensor (flip collider 0 into sensor mode) and IsFullyInside (an X-axis containment test). Game subclasses are LadderSensor, Teleporter, CameraSensor, WinFlag. It owns no constructor — subclasses still call createObjectPhysics(...) from GameObject and then setAsSensor(true).

protected setAsSensor(value: boolean)

Marks collider 0 as a sensor (or back to solid). A sensor produces no collision response — bodies pass through — but still emits collision events, which is how Rapier reports intersections. It also sets the active collision types so the sensor can detect the body kinds it cares about. 

protected setAsSensor(value: boolean) {
  if (!this.PhysicsBody) { return; }

  this.PhysicsBody.collider(0).setSensor(value);

  // detect kinematic AND fixed bodies (defaults miss them)
  this.PhysicsBody
    .collider(0)
    .setActiveCollisionTypes(RAPIER.ActiveCollisionTypes.KINEMATIC_FIXED);
}
Hard-won victory — KINEMATIC_FIXED active types. By default Rapier only reports dynamic-vs-dynamic intersections. The player moves under a kinematic character controller and most sensors are fixed bodies — without setActiveCollisionTypes(KINEMATIC_FIXED) a kinematic player crossing a fixed ladder/teleporter/win-flag sensor would fire nothing. This one line is what makes every sensor in the game actually trigger.
Gotcha — arming events is separate. setAsSensor only flips the sensor flag; it does not arm event emission. A sensor still needs either a defined OnSensorEnter/Exit override (auto-armed) or an explicit enableContactEvents() call. That is why Teleporter/WinFlag/CameraSensor call setAsSensor(true) then enableContactEvents(), while LadderSensor — which is polled, not event-driven — calls only setAsSensor(true) and arms nothing.

public IsFullyInside(other: GameObject): boolean

Returns true only if other is fully contained within this sensor horizontally — an X-axis AABB test using the cached CurrentTranslation/CurrentSize of both objects. Y is deliberately ignored. Used by GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggeredObjectFullyInside (gameUtils.ts:101) as the second half of the ladder climb gate. 

const sensorMinX = this.CurrentTranslation.x - this.CurrentSize.x / 2;
const sensorMaxX = this.CurrentTranslation.x + this.CurrentSize.x / 2;
const objectMinX = other.CurrentTranslation.x - other.CurrentSize.x / 2;
const objectMaxX = other.CurrentTranslation.x + other.CurrentSize.x / 2;

return objectMinX > sensorMinX && objectMaxX < sensorMaxX;
Hard-won victory (D9 — the "fully inside" ladder gate). The class header is explicit: ladder detection is intentionally not event-driven. The player may only mount a ladder when fully centred on it horizontally, and that is a continuous "am I inside right now?" question — exactly what enter/exit events cannot answer (there is no "stay" event). So the ladder path polls every frame via GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggered* + this X-only containment check, instead of reacting to a one-shot enter. The X-only test means the player can be at any height on the ladder and still "inside"; requiring full horizontal containment stops the player snapping onto a ladder they are only clipping the edge of.

Modifying this: keep this class tiny and game-blind. A new kind of sensor is a new subclass in game/entities/, not a new method here. If you need a Y-axis or full-AABB containment variant, add it as a sibling method (e.g. IsFullyInsideY) rather than changing the X-only semantics that the D9 ladder gate depends on. Remember the two-step setup contract: createObjectPhysics → setAsSensor(true) → arm events (or rely on a callback override).

src/engine/entities/contactRegistry.ts 50 LOC

This file IS the declarative contact table that replaced the old wall of hard-coded collision if/else. It is a generic engine class that names no game concept (same spirit as StateMachine): the game declares rules as data in contactRules.ts, and Physics owns the one instance as Physics.Contacts (constructed in physics.ts:37). It imports only GameObject, for the handler signature. Lifecycle: created once in Physics.Configure; filled once at startup by registerContactRules (called from App.vue); then Dispatch-ed every step from Physics.handleCollisionEvents.

type ContactMatch = (gameObject: GameObject) => boolean type ContactPhase = "enter" | "exit" | "sensorEnter" | "sensorExit" type ContactHandler = (a: GameObject, b: GameObject) => void interface ContactRule { a; b; phase; run }

The vocabulary. A ContactMatch is a predicate that decides whether a GameObject fills one side of a rule — in practice byType(EntityType.ENEMY) built on physicsHelpers.matchesType. ContactPhase is the four physics moments a rule can listen to, matching the two solid + two sensor branches in Physics. A ContactRule bundles the two side-predicates, the phase, and the handler. 

export type ContactMatch = (gameObject: GameObject) => boolean;
type ContactPhase = "enter" | "exit" | "sensorEnter" | "sensorExit";
type ContactHandler = (a: GameObject, b: GameObject) => void;

interface ContactRule {
  a: ContactMatch;
  b: ContactMatch;
  phase: ContactPhase;
  run: ContactHandler;
}

public Add(a: ContactMatch, b: ContactMatch, phase: ContactPhase, run: ContactHandler)

Declares one rule by pushing it onto the private rules array: "when an a and a b reach phase, run run(a, b)." This is the entire write API; the game calls it once per rule at startup. There is no removal API — the table is built once and lives for the session. 

private rules: ContactRule[] = [];

public Add(a: ContactMatch, b: ContactMatch, phase: ContactPhase, run: ContactHandler) {
  this.rules.push({ a, b, phase, run });
}
Note — rules are data, verdicts are decoupled. The handlers in contactRules.ts mostly just Emitter.emit("gameOver" / "gameWin" / "gameObjectRemoved", ...) — they decide what happened and let the event bus decide who reacts. So a contact never directly reaches into game flow; it raises an event. That is the second decoupling seam stacked on top of the first.

public Dispatch(phase: ContactPhase, x: GameObject, y: GameObject)

Called by Physics for every contact event of a given phase, with the two colliding GameObjects in whatever order Rapier reported them. It scans all rules, skips those of a different phase, and for a phase-match tests the pair both ways: (x,y) against (a,b), else (y,x). The first orientation that matches fires the handler with the args in the rule's declared order. 

public Dispatch(phase: ContactPhase, x: GameObject, y: GameObject) {
  for (const rule of this.rules) {
    if (rule.phase !== phase) {
      continue;
    }
    if (rule.a(x) && rule.b(y)) {
      rule.run(x, y);
    } else if (rule.a(y) && rule.b(x)) {
      rule.run(y, x);
    }
  }
}
Hard-won victory — the else if is the whole trick. Physics has no idea who is "a" and who is "b"; it just reports two handles. Testing both orientations means a rule fires regardless of which body moved into which ("any touch registers" — the enemy-kills-player rule must work whether the player ran into the enemy or the barrel rolled into the player). Crucially it is else if, not two ifs: for a symmetric pair where both predicates could match either body, a plain second if would fire the handler twice. The else if guarantees exactly one invocation per rule per event, and always with args in the rule's own (a, b) order — so the handler can safely cast a to the enemy type and b to the player type.
Note — dispatch is two-tiered. Physics.handleCollisionEvents first calls the per-entity callbacks (OnCollisionEnter etc.) on each body, then calls Contacts.Dispatch(...). So an entity's own movement reaction and the cross-entity verdict both run from the same drained event, callbacks first. The kinematic-controller path (Physics.GetGameObjectFromCollider) also lets the player feed its own computed contacts into Dispatch so rules fire even when Rapier emits no event for the kinematic mover.

One contact event, from world step to game verdict:

sequenceDiagram
  participant W as "World.step()"
  participant Ph as Physics
  participant CR as ContactRegistry
  participant H as "rule.run(a,b)"
  participant E as Emitter
  W->>Ph: drainCollisionEvents(h1,h2,started)
  Ph->>Ph: resolve handles -> GameObjects, skip if destroyed
  Ph->>Ph: call OnCollisionEnter/OnSensorEnter callbacks
  Ph->>CR: Dispatch("enter", go1, go2)
  CR->>CR: for each rule of this phase
  CR->>H: a(x)&&b(y) ? run(x,y) : a(y)&&b(x) ? run(y,x)
  H->>E: emit("gameOver" / "gameWin" / "gameObjectRemoved")

Modifying this: you almost never edit this file — new game behavior is a new contacts.Add(...) line in contactRules.ts, not a code change here. Keep the class generic: no EntityType, no event names. If you ever need rule removal (e.g. per-level rule sets), add an Remove/Clear method and have GameDirector rebuild the table on level switch — but preserve the else if symmetry in Dispatch, it is the bug-prevention that the whole table rests on.

src/engine/entities/stateMachine.ts 17 LOC

This file IS the engine's entire finite-state-machine — seventeen lines, fully generic, naming no state and no entity. It is a router: given a host that owns a string State field and a map from each state to a handler function, Update() calls the handler for the host's current state, once. The Player and Enemy each construct one in their attribute init (player.ts:161, enemy.ts:128) and drive it from their per-frame Update() (fsm.Update()). The host owns the state; handlers transition by reassigning host.State.

export default class StateMachine<H extends { State: S }, S extends string>

Two type parameters tie the machine together: H is the host type, constrained to "anything with a State: S field", and S is the union of state strings. The constructor stores the host and a Record<S, (host: H) => void> handler map — a total map, so TypeScript forces a handler for every state at the call site. Player instantiates it as StateMachine<Player, PlayerState>, Enemy as StateMachine<Enemy, EnemyState>. 

export default class StateMachine<H extends { State: S }, S extends string> {
  constructor(
    private host: H,
    private handlers: Record<S, (host: H) => void>
  ) {}
Tip — the Record<S, ...> is a compile-time completeness check. Because the handler map is keyed by the full state union, adding a new value to PlayerStates without adding its handler to the new StateMachine(...) map is a build error, not a silent missing-case at runtime. The FSM can never dispatch to a state that has no handler.

public Update()

Reads host.State once and invokes the matching handler with the host as its argument. The optional-chain ?. means a host whose state has no handler simply does nothing rather than throwing (belt-and-braces on top of the compile-time Record guarantee). 

public Update() {
  // Read State once: a handler that changes host.State transitions on the NEXT
  // update, it does not re-dispatch within this one.
  this.handlers[this.host.State]?.(this.host);
}
Hard-won victory — "read State once" prevents same-frame cascades. A handler that sets host.State = next does not re-dispatch within the same Update() — the new state's handler runs next frame. This is deliberate: it gives every state exactly one tick of execution, so a chain like idle→running→jumping in one frame is impossible. Without it, a handler that transitions could fall straight into the next handler, and a poorly-ordered pair of transitions could loop forever inside a single frame.
Note — the FSM only routes; the host owns the state. There is no transition() method, no enter/exit hooks, no state stack. Transitions are just player.State = PlayerStates.FALLING inside a handler (e.g. handlePlayerJumping.ts:14). This minimalism is the point — see Architecture — the generic FSM for how the player's five states and the enemy's three states both reuse this one class. 
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> idle
  idle --> running
  idle --> jumping
  idle --> falling
  idle --> climbing
  running --> idle
  running --> jumping
  running --> falling
  running --> climbing
  jumping --> falling
  falling --> idle
  falling --> running
  falling --> jumping
  climbing --> idle
  note right of idle : host owns State; handler reassigns it; transition lands NEXT Update()

Modifying this: resist the urge to grow it. New behavior is a new state value plus a new handler in the game layer's state map, not a feature here. If you genuinely need enter/exit hooks or a transition history, build them as a richer host contract (e.g. handlers returning the next state) rather than special-casing a game entity inside this class — its value is that Player and Enemy share it verbatim. Keep the "read State once per Update" semantics; the single-tick-per-state guarantee depends on it.

src/engine/types/gameObjectType.ts 14 LOC

This file IS the closed vocabulary of collider shapes the engine knows how to build — three string constants in a frozen as const object. It is consumed by GameObject.createCollider (shape selection) and createObjectGraphicsDebug (debug-mesh selection), and passed in by every entity constructor as the gameObjectType argument to createObjectPhysics. Pure data — no class, no runtime logic.

const GameObjectType = { CUBE: "cube", SPHERE: "sphere", CAPSULE: "capsule" } as const

Each constant names a Rapier collider primitive. The header comments map them straight across: CUBE → cuboid, SPHERE → ball, CAPSULE → capsule. 

const GameObjectType = {
  // Rectangular/box collider — players, platforms, walls, slopes (rotated cubes).
  CUBE: "cube",
  // Circular collider — rolling enemies, projectiles, round objects.
  SPHERE: "sphere",
  // Rounded-rectangle collider — smooth-moving characters (won't catch on ledges).
  CAPSULE: "capsule",
} as const;
TypeRapier shapeUsed byConstruction (in createCollider)
CUBEColliderDesc.cuboidplatforms, walls, ladder/teleporter/camera sensors, slopescuboid(width/2, height/2) — half-extents
SPHEREColliderDesc.ballrolling enemies (barrels)ball(width/2) — radius
CAPSULEColliderDesc.capsulesmooth characters (won't snag on ledges)capsule(height/2, width)
Hard-won victory (D5 / "D-lean" — three shapes only). The file header records the diet directly: "CUBE, SPHERE, CAPSULE only … slopes built from rotated/overlapped cubes, polyline/convex purged." Restricting the engine to three convex primitives killed an entire family of geometry bugs (degenerate/non-convex meshes Rapier rejects, costly decomposition) and means createCollider's default branch can safely fall through to a cuboid for any unknown type.

Modifying this: adding a type here is half the change — the other half is a matching case in GameObject.createCollider (and optionally createObjectGraphicsDebug). Before adding one, ask whether the shape can be composed from existing cuboids/balls instead; the whole engine leans on staying within these three convex primitives.

src/engine/types/userData.ts 10 LOC

This file IS the identity contract stamped onto every Rapier body's userData — the two opaque strings the contact system and physics queries read off a collider. It is a pure interface. It is written by GameObject.createObjectPhysics (initial stamp), SetType/SetName (edits), and read via physicsHelpers.getUserData/matchesType/isColliderType. The engine treats both fields as meaningless strings; only the game assigns them meaning (via EntityType).

export default interface UserData { type: string; name: string }

Two fields, deliberately split. type is the shared routing flag — many entities can carry the same type, so one contact rule (byType(EntityType.ENEMY)) matches all of them. name is the per-instance id for singling out one entity. Both default to the same value at spawn (createObjectPhysics stamps { type: name, name: name }) until SetName overrides the instance id. 

export default interface UserData {
  // TYPE flag — shared routing identity; entity-defined string, game-assigned.
  type: string;
  // Per-INSTANCE identifier — defaults to the type until SetName overrides it.
  name: string;
}
Note — numeric metadata does NOT live here. The header is emphatic: per-entity numeric data is not carried in userData (no more anonymous value0/value1/value3 bag). It lives as named, typed fields on the entity class — e.g. Platform.FloorLevel/IsEdge (platform.ts:11-13) — set at spawn from the level's meta bag that the GLB parser fills from each mesh's texture name. userData stays a two-string routing key, nothing more.
Gotcha — getUserData never throws, which can mask mistakes. physicsHelpers.getUserData returns a throwaway { type: "", name: "" } when the body is undefined. That keeps SetType/SetName from crashing on a half-built object — but it also means a write to a bodyless object silently goes nowhere. If a SetType seems to have no effect, check the body exists first (i.e. createObjectPhysics ran).

Modifying this: do not add fields here — that is the explicit design decision the header documents. New per-entity data belongs as a typed field on the entity subclass, populated from level meta. Keep UserData a pure two-string routing key so the engine stays game-agnostic and every contact rule keeps matching "by type."

Resources — loader & the GLB level parser

One engine class wears two hats. As an asset loader it batch-loads our textures and DRACO-compressed GLB models at boot, reporting per-item progress on the event bus and emitting a single resourcesReady when the manifest is done. As a level parser it walks a GLB scene graph exported from the level editor, resolves each collision cube's type from its node name and its metadata from its texture name, performs the one-and-only glTF → game axis conversion, and emits the generic LevelData record that GameDirector and the entityFactories consume. The engine names zero game concepts in either role.

src/engine/resources/resourceLoader.ts src/engine/types/levelData.ts

src/engine/resources/resourceLoader.ts 531 LOC

The largest single file in the engine, and the one with the most moving parts. It is constructed exactly once, by Experience.Configure(), which hands it the game's asset manifest — a Resource[] the game supplies (in this game three rows: randy texture, enemy model, dkGraphicsData model) — and stores it as Experience.Resources. From there the whole game reaches it as a singleton field. It imports THREE for the TextureLoader, GLTFLoader + DRACOLoader from the Three.js addons (DRACO shrinks the model files dramatically), the Emitter to broadcast loading lifecycle, and the LevelData type it emits. Crucially it now names zero game assets: the Vite ?url imports that bundle + content-hash the models live game-side in game/config/assetManifest.ts, so the loader just loads whatever URLs it is handed.

Lifecycle. The constructor only wires the loaders; it deliberately does not start loading. StartLoading() kicks off the batch (called last by App.vue). ParseLevel() is invoked on demand by GameDirector for every level load. Destroy() disposes every loaded GPU resource.

Note. The two responsibilities are independent. The asset manifest path (constructor → StartLoading → loadResource → Items) loads the three persistent assets used by the player sprite, enemy barrels, and graphics overlay. The level parser path (ParseLevel → parseScene → parseTypedNode → convertToLevelData) re-runs on every level switch and produces LevelData — it does not go through Items. The only thing they share is the same gltfLoader instance (DRACO already configured).

Type model: Resource, ResourceType, ParsedLevelObject

Three types frame everything. ResourceType is the loader selector — "texture" | "gltfModel". A Resource is one manifest row: a unique name (the Items key), its type, and a path (public-relative, an absolute URL, or a Vite-imported ?url string). It is the one exported type here, because the game declares its own asset manifest against it (see game/config/assetManifest.ts); the other two stay internal. ParsedLevelObject is the parser's intermediate row, the bridge between the raw glTF node and the final LevelData; note its position/rotation arrays already carry the game slot order, documented inline. 

interface ParsedLevelObject {
  name: string;                          // Object name from 3D software
  type: string;                          // Game object type (Platform, Wall, etc.)
  position: [number, number, number];    // game slots: [gameX, gameDepth, gameUp]
  rotation: [number, number, number];    // game slots: [-, gameRotation(z), -]
  width: number;                         // game horizontal extent (glTF X)
  height: number;                        // game vertical extent (glTF Y)
  depth: number;                         // game depth extent (glTF Z)
  meta?: Record<string, string>;         // Metadata parsed from the mesh's texture name
}

type ResourceType = "texture" | "gltfModel";

// Exported so the game can declare its asset manifest (game/config/assetManifest.ts).
export interface Resource {
  name: string;       // unique key to fetch the loaded asset from Items
  type: ResourceType; // picks the loader
  path: string;       // public-relative path, absolute URL, or Vite-imported URL
}

Private fields & public Items store

sources is the manifest; gltfLoader / textureLoader are the two Three.js loaders; toLoad and loaded are the progress counters; levelTypes is the game-injected name-token → game-type vocabulary the level parser reads, empty until RegisterLevelTypes runs. The one public field is the Items store — a flat { [name]: GLTF | Texture } map keyed by Resource.name. After boot, the game pulls assets straight out of it: this.Resources.Items.randy for the player's sprite sheet (player.ts), this.resources.Items.enemy for each barrel (GameDirector.SpawnEnemy), and this.resources.Items[entry.graphics] for the optional art overlay (GameDirector.loadLevelByIndex). 

private sources: Resource[];
private gltfLoader: GLTFLoader;
private textureLoader: THREE.TextureLoader;
// A loaded gltfModel is stored as the whole GLTF object (its .scene is read by
// CreateObjectGraphics); a texture is stored as a THREE.Texture.
public Items: { [key: string]: GLTF | THREE.Texture };
private toLoad: number;
private loaded: number;

// Level-node type vocabulary, INJECTED by the game (RegisterLevelTypes). The
// engine's GLB parser is type-blind — it no longer mirrors EntityType by hand.
private levelTypes: Record<string, string> = {};

constructor(sources: Resource[])

Stores the manifest, initializes an empty Items, sets toLoad = sources.length and loaded = 0, then builds the loaders.
