The GLTFLoader gets a DRACOLoader whose decoder path is import.meta.env.BASE_URL + "draco/gltf/" — that folder lives in public/ and ships draco_decoder.wasm + draco_wasm_wrapper.js, so the WASM decoder is fetched at runtime (it is not bundled). Resolving it against BASE_URL rather than an absolute "/draco/gltf/" means a sub-path deploy (e.g. GitHub Pages under /FireGame/) still finds the decoder instead of 404-ing and silently breaking every GLB load. The TextureLoader needs no configuration. 

this.gltfLoader = new GLTFLoader();
const dracoLoader = new DRACOLoader().setDecoderPath(
  import.meta.env.BASE_URL + "draco/gltf/"
);
this.gltfLoader.setDRACOLoader(dracoLoader);

this.textureLoader = new THREE.TextureLoader();
Hard-won victory — why the constructor must NOT auto-load. The closing comment is the load-bearing one: loading is deliberately not kicked off in the constructor. The caller invokes StartLoading() only after the resourcesReady listener (GameDirector) is subscribed. If every asset happened to resolve during the await window inside Experience.Configure (e.g. RAPIER.init()), the completion event would fire into zero listeners and be lost — mitt does not buffer or replay — hanging the boot on the loading screen forever. The fix is purely an ordering contract enforced across three files (see the boot sequence below). 
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as App.vue onMounted
    participant Exp as Experience.Configure
    participant RL as ResourceLoader
    participant Bus as Emitter (mitt)
    participant GD as GameDirector
    App->>App: Emitter.on(loadingStarted/Finished/Progress)
    App->>Exp: await Configure(canvas, GAME_ASSETS)
    Exp->>RL: new ResourceLoader(GAME_ASSETS)
    Note over RL: constructor wires loaders only - NO loading
    App->>RL: RegisterLevelTypes(LEVEL_NODE_TYPES)
    App->>GD: new GameDirector()
    GD->>Bus: Emitter.on(resourcesReady)
    App->>RL: StartLoading()  (LAST)
    RL->>Bus: loadingProgress 0/total
    RL->>Bus: loadingProgress n/total (per asset)
    RL->>Bus: resourcesReady (once loaded==toLoad)
    Bus->>GD: loadLevelByIndex(DEFAULT_LEVEL_INDEX)

public StartLoading(): void

Begins the batch. First it seeds a 0/total loadingProgress event so the Vue bar can paint immediately, before any asset resolves. Then it loops the manifest, resolves each row's URL via getAssetPath, warns + skips rows with no path, and dispatches each through loadResource. 

Emitter.emit("loadingProgress", { loaded: 0, total: this.toLoad, item: "" });

for (const source of this.sources) {
  const assetPath = this.getAssetPath(source);
  if (!assetPath) {
    console.warn(`⚠️ No valid path for resource: ${source.name}`);
    continue;
  }
  this.loadResource(source, assetPath);
}
Gotcha — call order is a hard contract. App.vue calls StartLoading() as the very last line of onMounted, after GameDirector has subscribed to resourcesReady and after the three loading-screen listeners are registered. Move it earlier and you re-introduce the lost-event hang the constructor note warns about.

private loadResource(source: Resource, path: string): void

Loads one asset and owns the progress/completion bookkeeping. It builds two callbacks then dispatches by type. onLoad stores the file in Items[source.name], increments loaded, emits a loadingProgress with the resolved item name, and — when loaded === toLoad — emits the single resourcesReady. onError is the resilience path: it logs, still increments loaded, emits resourceLoadFailed with the asset name, advances the bar, and also checks for completion. 

const onLoad = (file: GLTF | THREE.Texture) => {
  this.Items[source.name] = file;
  this.loaded++;
  Emitter.emit("loadingProgress", { loaded: this.loaded, total: this.toLoad, item: source.name });
  if (this.loaded === this.toLoad) {
    Emitter.emit("resourcesReady");
  }
};

const onError = (error: any) => {
  console.error(`❌ Failed to load ${source.name}:`, error);
  this.loaded++;                                   // count it so a 404 can't hang the loader
  Emitter.emit("resourceLoadFailed", source.name);
  Emitter.emit("loadingProgress", { loaded: this.loaded, total: this.toLoad, item: source.name });
  if (this.loaded === this.toLoad) {
    Emitter.emit("resourcesReady");
  }
};

if (source.type === "gltfModel") {
  this.gltfLoader.load(path, onLoad, undefined, onError);
} else if (source.type === "texture") {
  this.textureLoader.load(path, onLoad, undefined, onError);
} else {
  console.warn(`⚠️ Unknown resource type for ${source.name}`);
}
Hard-won victory — a 404 must not hang the loader. Counting failed loads toward loaded is the whole point of the onError branch: a single missing asset would otherwise leave loaded < toLoad forever, resourcesReady would never fire, and the boot would freeze on the loading screen. A failed asset is "done" as far as progress is concerned — the game starts anyway, and anyone who cares hears resourceLoadFailed.
Note — no more as any. onLoad's parameter is typed as the GLTF | THREE.Texture union — exactly what Items stores. Three's load callbacks are typed per-loader (GLTF for the GLTF loader, Texture for the texture loader), and because a handler taking the wider union accepts either narrower payload, both loaders take onLoad directly. The old onLoad as any casts (a relic of the looser Object3D | Texture field type) are gone.

private getAssetPath(source: Resource): string | undefined

Resolves a manifest row to a real URL — now a thin normalizer, because the game's manifest already supplies the URL. A bundled (Vite ?url) asset arrives as a full hashed path and an absolute URL is taken as-is; a plain public-folder path just gets a leading slash for clean resolution from public/. A row with no path returns undefined (and StartLoading warns + skips it). The old name-keyed bundled-asset map — which hardcoded dkGraphicsData/enemy/randy — is gone; the engine no longer names a single game asset. 

if (!source.path) return undefined;
if (source.path.startsWith("/") || source.path.includes("://")) {
  return source.path;
}
return `/${source.path}`;

The ?url imports that trigger Vite to emit content-hashed copies into dist/assets/ now live in game/config/assetManifest.ts, which builds the GAME_ASSETS: Resource[] array with each row's path already set to the resolved bundle URL. So getAssetPath sees a real path for every row, and the old path: "" placeholder is gone.

Tip — adding a new bundled asset. No engine edit at all now: in game/config/assetManifest.ts, import x from "../../assets/.../x.glb?url" and add a { name: "x", type, path: x } row to GAME_ASSETS. For a public/-served asset, skip the import and give the row a real public-relative path instead. The loader takes whatever URL you hand it.
Note — reusability. Hoisting the asset list out of the engine and into GAME_ASSETS is what lets a different game reuse engine/ unchanged: the loader became a pure "load these URLs by name" service that names none of this game's assets.

— The GLB level parser —

Everything below is the second hat. The entry point is ParseLevel; the pipeline is three private stages. 

flowchart TD
    A["ParseLevel(glbPath)"] --> B["loadGLB - gltfLoader.load -> gltf"]
    B --> C["parseScene(gltf.scene) - recursive walk"]
    C --> D["parseTypedNode - per typed node"]
    D --> E["readMeta - texture-name key=value"]
    D --> F["world transform decompose + axis remap"]
    D --> G["ParsedLevelObject[]"]
    G --> H["convertToLevelData - dedup keys"]
    H --> I["LevelData record"]
    I --> J["GameDirector.LoadLevelData -> ENTITY_FACTORIES"]

public async ParseLevel(glbPath: string): Promise<LevelData>

The public parser. glbPath is any URL — in practice the registry's levelUrl() hands it a hashed /assets/… bundle URL, but a raw public path or a ?url import works just as well. It awaits the GLB, walks the scene into intermediate rows, converts to LevelData, and returns. Called from GameDirector.LoadGLBLevel. 

const gltf = await this.loadGLB(glbPath);
const parsedObjects = this.parseScene(gltf.scene);
const levelData = this.convertToLevelData(parsedObjects);
return levelData;

private loadGLB(path: string): Promise<any>

A thin Promise wrapper around the callback-style gltfLoader.load, so ParseLevel can await it. Resolves with the raw GLTF object ({ scene, animations, … }); rejects on error. Reuses the same DRACO-configured gltfLoader as the asset path.

private parseScene(node: THREE.Object3D): ParsedLevelObject[]

The recursive scene-graph walk, and one of the most carefully-reasoned functions in the codebase. The rule: a node's TYPE comes from its NAME — specifically the first "_"-token resolved through lookupCanonicalType — not from its folder path. Editors flatten and rename the group hierarchy on glTF export, so the node name is the one stable channel. If the name resolves to a known type, the node becomes one game object built from its entire subtree's geometry and we do not recurse into it. If it doesn't resolve: a leaf Mesh is a genuinely mis-named cube (warn + skip), while an untyped group is structural and we walk through its children. 

const type = this.lookupCanonicalType(node.name);
if (type) {
  const parsed = this.parseTypedNode(node, type);
  if (parsed) objects.push(parsed);
  return objects;                       // typed node: do NOT recurse
}

if (node instanceof THREE.Mesh) {
  this.warnUnknownType(node.name);      // mis-named leaf cube
  return objects;
}

for (const child of node.children) {    // structural group: walk through
  for (const childObj of this.parseScene(child)) objects.push(childObj);
}
Hard-won victory — the vanishing multi-material platform. That single "stop at the typed node" rule covers two export shapes. A normal single-material cube exports as one THREE.Mesh named e.g. "Platform". But a multi-material cube (a metadata texture on only some faces) is split by Three into per-face primitive meshes under an unnamed group that keeps the "Platform" name — the pieces are named "mesh_N". Reading the group name and merging the split primitives in parseTypedNode recovers it as one collider; without this rule the unnamed pieces were silently skipped and the platform vanished.

private parseTypedNode(node, type): ParsedLevelObject | null

The heart of the parser — it turns one typed node + its subtree into a single ParsedLevelObject with correct position, rotation, size, and metadata, performing the one-and-only glTF → game axis conversion. Returns null for a GraphicsObject (no physics/gameplay) or a node with no meshes. Walk-through:

1. Early-out for graphics-only.

if (type === "GraphicsObject") {
  return null;
}

2. Collect every mesh in the subtree. For a normal cube that's just the node; for a multi-material cube it's the per-face primitives Three split out. They share the node's local frame, so they're merged below. 

const meshes: THREE.Mesh[] = [];
node.traverse((child) => { if (child instanceof THREE.Mesh) meshes.push(child); });
if (meshes.length === 0) return null;

const meta = this.readMeta(meshes);

3. Decompose the WORLD transform. Using matrixWorld (parents included), not the local matrix, so a cube nested under a translated/rotated group still resolves correctly. 

node.updateWorldMatrix(true, false);
const worldTranslation = new THREE.Vector3();
const worldQuaternion = new THREE.Quaternion();
const worldScale = new THREE.Vector3();
node.matrixWorld.decompose(worldTranslation, worldQuaternion, worldScale);

const rotation = new THREE.Euler().setFromQuaternion(worldQuaternion);
Gotcha — read the WORLD quaternion, not the local one. For a tilted cube (a slope) built in the editor's X×Y plane, the angle lands in rotation.z (rotation about glTF Z, the game's depth axis). Reading the local quaternion was the old bug — it ignored any parent-group rotation.

4. Size from the LOCAL geometry box, scaled by world-scale magnitude. Each primitive's bounding box is unioned in the shared local frame, then multiplied by abs(worldScale). Using the local extent rather than a world AABB (setFromObject) keeps a rotated slope's size true instead of inflating it to its tilted envelope; the Math.abs guards a mirrored element (negative decomposed scale) from producing a negative half-extent — a degenerate collider. 

const localBox = new THREE.Box3();
for (const mesh of meshes) {
  mesh.geometry.computeBoundingBox();
  if (mesh.geometry.boundingBox) localBox.union(mesh.geometry.boundingBox);
}

const size = new THREE.Vector3();
localBox.getSize(size).multiply(
  new THREE.Vector3(Math.abs(worldScale.x), Math.abs(worldScale.y), Math.abs(worldScale.z))
);

5. Position = geometry WORLD CENTER, not node origin. The level editor bakes pivot offsets into the vertices of stretched / multi-cube elements, so the node origin is often a corner. Centering on the geometry (local center → applyMatrix4(matrixWorld)) makes the collider land exactly where the cube is drawn — WYSIWYG — and stays correct under rotation. 

const position = new THREE.Vector3();
localBox.getCenter(position).applyMatrix4(node.matrixWorld);

6. The single glTF → game axis translation. This is the only place axes are remapped. BlockBench/glTF and this 2D game share axes: gameX = glTF X (horizontal), gameUP = glTF Y (vertical), gameDEPTH = glTF Z (into-screen). Downstream readers consume a fixed slot order, so the remap happens once here. 

const parsedObject: ParsedLevelObject = {
  name: node.name,
  type: type,
  position: [position.x, position.z, position.y],   // [ gameX, gameDEPTH, gameUP ]
  rotation: [rotation.x, -rotation.z, rotation.y],   // slope angle -> slot[1], negated
  width: size.x,   // game horizontal <- glTF X
  height: size.y,  // game vertical   <- glTF Y
  depth: size.z,   // game depth      <- glTF Z
  meta,
};
ParsedLevelObject slotValueglTF sourceRead by
position[0]gameX (horizontal)position.xfactories position[0] → x
position[1]gameDEPTH (into-screen)position.zsetCameraStart position[1]
position[2]gameUP (vertical)position.yfactories position[2] → up
rotation[1]slope angle (negated)-rotation.zfactories -rotation[1]
widthhorizontal extentsize.xfactory data.width
heightvertical extentsize.yfactory data.height
depthdepth extentsize.zfactory data.depth
Hard-won victory — the double-negative that keeps slopes the right way round. A slope's angle is a rotation about glTF Z, landing in rotation[1]. The parser negates it here; then the entity factory applies -data.rotation[1] (see entityFactories.createPlatform), so the two negations net to +rotation.z and a BlockBench CCW rotation reads the same direction in-game. Without the parser-side negation it came out mirrored. Single-axis slopes only — flip this sign if a slope ever tilts the wrong way.
Note — the old "Z-up" bug. A previous version treated glTF Z as "up", forcing you to build levels along the editor's Z axis. The comment block here is the record of that fix: the parser now maps the editor's natural X (wide) × Y (tall) plane straight onto the game's horizontal × vertical plane.

Type resolution & metadata

public RegisterLevelTypes(types: Record<string, string>): void
private lookupCanonicalType(name: string): string | undefined

The type vocabulary is now injected by the game, not held in the engine. RegisterLevelTypes(types) stores the name-token → game-type map into this.levelTypes; App.vue calls it at boot with LEVEL_NODE_TYPES (from game/config/levelNodeTypes.ts), before any level parses. lookupCanonicalType takes name.split("_")[0], lowercases it, and reads it out of this.levelTypes — so "OneWayPlatform", "OneWayPlatform_2" (a user instance suffix), and "OneWayPlatform_1" (Three's auto-dedup of duplicate names) all resolve to the same type. Exact-token matching keeps OneWayPlatform distinct from Platform — the old fuzzy "onewayplatform".includes("platform") mis-routed it. 

private levelTypes: Record<string, string> = {};

public RegisterLevelTypes(types: Record<string, string>) {
  this.levelTypes = types;
}

private lookupCanonicalType(name: string): string | undefined {
  const token = name.split("_")[0].toLowerCase();
  return this.levelTypes[token];
}

The token map below now lives game-side in game/config/levelNodeTypes.ts and is handed to the loader via RegisterLevelTypes; its values come straight from EntityType:

Cube name token (case-insensitive)Emitted type stringBecomes (game side)
wallWallcreateWall → Platform (SetType WALL)
platformPlatformcreatePlatform (solid)
onewayplatformOneWayPlatformcreatePlatform (isOneWay)
trashcanTrashCancreateTrashCan
winflagWinFlagcreateWinFlag
teleporterTeleportercreateTeleporter
camerasensorCameraSensorcreateCameraSensor
laddertopsensorLadderTopSensorcreateLadderSensor
laddercoresensorLadderCoreSensorcreateLadderSensor
ladderbottomsensorLadderBottomSensorcreateLadderSensor
playerstartPlayerStartGameDirector.setPlayerStart (no factory)
camerastartCameraStartGameDirector.setCameraStart (no factory)
graphics / graphicsobjectGraphicsObjectparser returns null (skipped)
Hard-won victory — the hand-synced mirror is gone. This map used to be a CANONICAL_TYPES constant inside the engine, hand-mirrored from the game's EntityType and prone to silent drift. Now levelNodeTypes.ts builds it game-side with its values sourced straight from EntityType (so the two cannot drift) and injects it via RegisterLevelTypes. The engine keeps naming zero game concepts — it just reads whatever vocabulary it is handed. Only level-placeable types (those with an entity factory), the spawn points, and GraphicsObject belong in that map; runtime-only types like Enemy (the barrel spawner) intentionally do not.

private warnUnknownType(name: string): void

Called for a leaf mesh whose name didn't resolve. Logs the offending name + token and lists the valid type names (deduped from the injected levelTypes values), with a hint to name the cube by its type. This deliberately replaced an old fallback that spawned an invisible solid platform for any unknown cube — far more surprising than a skip + warning.

private readTextureName(mesh): string · readMeta(meshes) · parseMetadata(textureName)

The metadata pipeline. Because collision geometry and graphics are separate layers, a collision mesh's texture name is a free data carrier. Three.js copies the glTF texture name verbatim onto material.map.name (it does not sanitize it the way it does node names), so =, ., _ and spaces all survive — which is what makes key=value metadata possible.

readTextureName pulls map.name off the first material and strips a trailing image extension. readMeta merges every textured primitive of a (possibly multi-material) cube into one bag (later face wins on conflict). parseMetadata splits on whitespace / comma / semicolon, treats a bare token as a flag mapping to "1", and otherwise splits key=value with keys lower-cased. Values stay strings; per-type interpretation happens game-side. 

// readTextureName: keep "=", ".", "_" but drop a trailing image extension —
// otherwise "edge=1.png" would parse the value as "1.png" (NaN) and read as 0.
return name.replace(/\.(png|jpe?g|webp|gif|bmp|tga)$/i, "");

// parseMetadata
for (const token of textureName.trim().split(/[\s,;]+/)) {
  if (!token) continue;
  const eq = token.indexOf("=");
  if (eq === -1) {
    meta[token.toLowerCase()] = "1";                       // bare flag
  } else {
    meta[token.slice(0, eq).toLowerCase()] = token.slice(eq + 1);
  }
}
Gotcha — "_" is NOT a token separator. Multi-number values use "_" between components (e.g. cam=3.5_2_-1, dest=10_55) so they stay intact through the split. The game side (metaCoords in entityFactories) splits those on "_" later. Conversely, the type-token split in lookupCanonicalType does split on "_" — that's the instance-suffix channel. Same character, two different roles in two different name fields (texture name vs node name).
Hard-won victory — the silent ".png" zero. A texture that inherited its image's filename ("edge=1.png") would parse the value as "1.png" — not-a-number — so the flag silently read as 0. The extension strip in readTextureName is the fix.

Authoring contract vs. the implemented reader

The authoring guide (devAssets/GLB_LEVEL_FORMAT.md) describes a folder-driven type system and glTF extras metadata — the original Shapr3D contract. The parser as implemented resolves type from the node name and metadata from the texture name, because editors flatten folders and rename groups on export. Read the markdown for the authoring intent (folder layout, the conceptual property names below), but the tables above reflect what the code actually consumes.

Authoring folder (GLB_LEVEL_FORMAT.md)Intended typeConceptual metadata (extras)
Platforms/PlatformfloorLevel, isEdge, enablePoint → game keys floor/edge/oneway
OneWayPlatforms/OneWayPlatformsame as Platform
Walls/Wall—
Ladders/Top·Core·Bottom/Ladder{Top,Core,Bottom}Sensordirection → game key dir (-1 left, 1 right, 0 both)
Sensors/Cameras/CameraSensortarget [X,Y,Z] → game key cam=x_y_z
Entities/teleporter…Teleporterdestination [X,Y] → game key dest=x_y
Entities/player_startPlayerStart—
Entities/camera_startCameraStart—
Entities/trash_can…TrashCan—
Entities/win_flagWinFlag—
Graphics/GraphicsObjectskipped (no physics)

The game-side interpretation of each metadata key lives in entityFactories.ts: metaNum(meta,"floor"), metaNum(meta,"edge"), metaNum(meta,"oneway") for platforms; metaNum(meta,"dir") for ladders; metaCoords(meta,"cam") for camera sensors; metaCoords(meta,"dest") for teleporters. The engine parser never touches any of those keys.

private convertToLevelData(parsedObjects): LevelData

Folds the intermediate rows into the final LevelData dictionary keyed by name, passing width/height/depth/position/rotation/type/meta straight through. Its one piece of real logic is key disambiguation: the dictionary key must be unique per cube or the loser is silently dropped. 

let key = obj.name;
for (let n = 2; key in levelData; n++) {
  key = `${obj.name}#${n}`;
}
if (key !== obj.name) {
  console.warn(`⚠️ Duplicate cube name "${obj.name}" ... kept as "${key}" so it isn't dropped.`);
}

levelData[key] = {
  width: obj.width, height: obj.height, depth: obj.depth,
  position: obj.position, rotation: obj.rotation,
  type: obj.type, meta: obj.meta ?? {},
};
Hard-won victory — the GLTFLoader name-dedup collision. GLTFLoader dedups duplicate node names, but its counter is per-base-name. So a hand-suffixed cube ("Platform_2") can collide with a plain cube whose name auto-deduped to the same string (the 3rd "Platform" → "Platform_2"). The #n suffix guarantees every cube survives. It is only the map key — entities are matched by type, never by name — so it is purely cosmetic to gameplay.

public Destroy(): void · private disposeObject3D(root): void

Frees all loaded GPU memory. Destroy first Emitter.off("resourcesReady") (drops the boot listener), then loops Items: a THREE.Texture gets dispose(); a GLTF object (detected by the "scene" in item duck-type, since Items' union doesn't capture the GLTF wrapper) is walked by disposeObject3D, which traverses and disposes every mesh's geometry and material(s). 

Emitter.off("resourcesReady");
for (const key in this.Items) {
  const item = this.Items[key];
  if (item instanceof THREE.Texture) {
    item.dispose();
  } else if (item && typeof item === "object" && "scene" in item) {
    const gltf = item as { scene: THREE.Object3D };
    this.disposeObject3D(gltf.scene);
  }
}

Called from Experience.Destroy last, after camera/physics/renderer. Note this disposes the persistent boot assets; per-level entity teardown is GameDirector's UnloadLevelData, a separate concern.

Modifying this: Most edits no longer touch this file at all. To add a new boot asset, add a row to GAME_ASSETS in game/config/assetManifest.ts (a ?url import for a bundled asset, or a real public-relative path for a public/ one). To add a new level-placeable type, edit two game files in lockstep: add the lowercased token → type mapping to LEVEL_NODE_TYPES in game/config/levelNodeTypes.ts (its value sourced from EntityType), and add the matching entity factory keyed by that exact type string. Forget the factory and the cube parses but spawns nothing; forget the LEVEL_NODE_TYPES entry and the cube warns + skips. To add a new metadata key, no engine change is needed — the bag is generic; just read it game-side with metaNum/metaCoords. The engine here stays game-agnostic: it loads whatever URLs and resolves whatever type tokens it is handed, and interprets no game key — that boundary is the whole point.

src/engine/types/levelData.ts 35 LOC

The contract type for a parsed level: a dictionary mapping entity names to properties. It is the seam between the parser (ResourceLoader.ParseLevel emits it) and the consumers (GameDirector iterates it; the entity factories read each row). It imports nothing and is pure structure. GLB is the single permanent source of LevelData — there is no JSON path anymore.

interface LevelEntity

One GameObject to create. width / depth / height are the size components; position and rotation are number[] in the game slot order the parser emits and the factories read; type is the entity-type string (the verbatim EntityType values); meta is the optional generic key→value bag. 

interface LevelEntity {
  width: number;            // X axis; horizontal size for 2D
  depth: number;            // Y slot in this array; often minimal for 2D
  height: number;           // Z slot in this array; vertical size for 2D
  position: number[];       // [horizontal, depth, vertical]
  rotation: number[];       // Euler radians; usually [0,0,0] or [0, rotation, 0]
  type: string;             // Platform, Wall, OneWayPlatform, ...Sensor, PlayerStart, ...
  meta?: Record<string, string>;  // texture-name key=value bag, e.g. "floor=1 oneway=8"
}

interface LevelData {
  [entityName: string]: LevelEntity;
}
Note — the meta bag replaced numbered fields. The file comment records the history: meta superseded an old generic value0-3 index scheme. The engine emits this bag and never interprets a game key; the factories own per-type interpretation (floor/edge/oneway for platforms, dir for ladders, cam/dest for camera/teleporter). That keeps levelData.ts game-agnostic even though it documents the game keys for the reader's benefit.
Gotcha — the comment's axis labels describe array SLOTS, not literal X/Y/Z. The depth/height doc comments say "Y axis" / "Z axis", but that's the position-array slot meaning in this 2D system, where position = [horizontal, depth, vertical]. In real glTF terms the parser fills height from glTF Y and depth from glTF Z (see parseTypedNode). Always cross-reference the parser's slot table above rather than reading these labels as Cartesian axes.

Modifying this: This interface is consumed untyped on the factory side (data: any, by deliberate design), so adding an optional field is safe and non-breaking. Renaming or reordering position/rotation slots is not safe: the slot order is a hard contract shared by parseTypedNode, setPlayerStart/setCameraStart, and every entity factory — change one and you must change all of them together.

Helpers — math & physics utilities

Two tiny, dependency-light modules of mostly-pure functions the engine leans on everywhere: mathHelpers.ts holds game-state-free number math (a framerate-independent move-towards, a seeded mulberry32 PRNG, and a coin-flip built on it), and physicsHelpers.ts holds the thin layer that reads opaque identity off Rapier bodies and colliders so the contact table and physics queries can match entities "by type" without ever importing a game class. The game layer never calls these directly — it reaches them through the GameUtils facade.

src/engine/helpers/mathHelpers.tssrc/engine/helpers/physicsHelpers.ts

src/engine/helpers/mathHelpers.ts 49 LOC

A leaf module: "Generic pure math utility functions, no game-state dependencies." It imports nothing and is imported by nothing in the engine layer directly — instead the game-layer GameUtils facade re-exports the two game-facing helpers (GameUtils.MoveTowardsPoint and GameUtils.CalculatePercentChance) so game code funnels through one entry point, while the seeded-PRNG controls (seedRandom and randomRange) are imported straight by the game's GameDirector to pin and reset the seed. It has no constructor or lifecycle — five named exports plus a single module-level piece of state, rngState, the value the PRNG advances. moveTowards is still stateless and side-effect-free; the PRNG functions are deliberately stateful so one fixed seed reproduces one fixed sequence.

Note. Until recently this file carried no decision-code comments; the seeded PRNG added the first — a block explaining why randomness is seeded here (a replay, a lockstep peer, or a deterministic test would all desync on the first Math.random() roll). The older design lesson still holds: math that knows nothing about the Donkey-Kong rules stays in engine/, and the game reaches it through GameUtils.

export function moveTowards(current: number, target: number, maxDelta: number): number

Steps current toward target by at most maxDelta, never overshooting. Parameters: current the present value; target the value to approach; maxDelta the largest step allowed this call (callers pass accel * Time.Delta, which is what makes the motion framerate-independent). Returns: the new value — exactly target once it is within reach, otherwise one step closer.

if (Math.abs(target - current) <= maxDelta) {
  return target;
}

return current + Math.sign(target - current) * maxDelta;

Line-level notes: the guard on line 10 is the anti-overshoot clamp — if the remaining distance is no larger than one step, snap exactly to target instead of stepping past and oscillating around it. The return on line 15 uses Math.sign(target - current) to pick the direction (+1 when target is above, -1 when below, 0 when equal — though equality is already caught by the guard) and scales maxDelta by it, so the step always heads toward the target regardless of which side current sits on.

Tip. This is the engine's equivalent of Unity's Mathf.MoveTowards. Unlike lerp, the step size is absolute (a fixed delta), not a fraction of the remaining gap — so motion is linear and reaches the target in finite time rather than asymptotically. That is exactly why it is used for acceleration toward a target speed: the "feel" attributes set the rate, and the value arrives crisply.

Where it is used. Exposed as GameUtils.MoveTowardsPoint (src/game/gameUtils.ts:14) and called three times, always with a per-frame Time.Delta step:

CallerWhat it eases
applyHorizontalMovement.ts:30player.NextTranslation.x toward targetSpeed by acceleration * player.Time.Delta — the core run/accelerate/decelerate ramp.
handlePlayerFalling.ts:113player.NextTranslation.y — easing vertical velocity during a fall.
handlePlayerClimbing.ts:61player.NextTranslation.y — easing climb speed up/down a ladder.
 
// applyHorizontalMovement.ts
player.NextTranslation.x = GameUtils.MoveTowardsPoint(
  player.NextTranslation.x,
  targetSpeed,
  acceleration * player.Time.Delta
);
Gotcha. The framerate-independence lives in the caller, not here. moveTowards is a pure step; it is the * Time.Delta at every call site that makes the ramp identical at 30fps and 144fps. Forget the Delta and a value will accelerate as fast as the machine renders. See Time for the delta source and baseFeel for where acceleration comes from.

Below moveTowards sits the file's other half: a small seeded pseudo-random generator. Three exports — seedRandom, random, and randomRange — share a single module-level integer, rngState (initialised to the golden-ratio constant 0x9e3779b9), and implement mulberry32, a tiny, fast, well-distributed 32-bit PRNG. They are the only stateful code in the module, and that statefulness is exactly the point.

Hard-won victory. This is the randomness half of the determinism work; its sibling is the fixed timestep. Math.random() has no seed, so a replay, a lockstep peer, or a deterministic test would desync on the very first roll. Routing every gameplay draw through a seeded mulberry32 means the same seed always replays the same sequence — and GameDirector seeds it at boot and re-seeds on reset, so a fresh run is reproducible from frame zero.

export function seedRandom(seed: number): void

Pins the generator's state. Parameter: seed any number (the game uses a fixed constant). Returns: nothing — it overwrites the module-level rngState. Same seed in, same random() sequence out.

rngState = seed >>> 0;

Line-level note: the >>> 0 coerces the seed to an unsigned 32-bit integer — mulberry32 is all 32-bit math, so a float or a negative input still lands on a well-defined state. GameDirector calls this once in its constructor with a fixed RNG_SEED and again inside resetGameState(), so a reset replays the run identically.

export function random(): number

The seeded drop-in for Math.random(): one mulberry32 step that advances rngState and returns the next value in [0, 1). Returns: a uniform float in [0, 1), fully determined by the current seed.

rngState = (rngState + 0x6d2b79f5) | 0;
let t = rngState;
t = Math.imul(t ^ (t >>> 15), t | 1);
t ^= t + Math.imul(t ^ (t >>> 7), t | 61);
return ((t ^ (t >>> 14)) >>> 0) / 4294967296;

Line-level notes: each call bumps rngState by the odd constant 0x6d2b79f5 (the | 0 keeps it in signed-32-bit range), then runs the mulberry32 avalanche — two Math.imul mixes interleaved with xorshifts (>>> 15, >>> 7, >>> 14) to scramble the bits. The final unsigned 32-bit result is divided by 4294967296 (2^32) to map it into [0, 1). Because the whole state and arithmetic are integer, the same seed produces the same stream on every machine and JS engine — no floating-point drift.

export function randomRange(min: number, max: number): number

A seeded uniform draw in a half-open range. Parameters: min inclusive lower bound; max exclusive upper bound. Returns: a float in [min, max).

return min + random() * (max - min);

Line-level note: a plain affine remap of random()'s [0, 1) onto [min, max) — and because it reads the same seeded stream, any interval it feeds is reproducible too. GameDirector's trySpawn() uses randomRange(3, 4) and randomRange(2, 3) for its barrel-spawn cadence, so spawn timing now replays exactly under a fixed seed.

export function percentChance(probability: number): boolean

A weighted coin flip. Parameter: probability in the range 0 to 1 (0 = never, 1 = always). Returns: true with that probability, false otherwise.

return random() < probability;

Line-level note: the seeded random() yields a uniform value in [0, 1), so < probability is true for exactly that fraction of draws. The strict < (not <=) means probability === 0 is correctly never true; and because random() never returns 1.0, probability === 1 is always true. The bounds behave exactly as you'd want with no clamping needed. Swapping the built-in Math.random() for the seeded generator is what makes the barrel's ladder-descent coin flip reproducible — same seed, same descents.

Where it is used. Exposed as GameUtils.CalculatePercentChance (src/game/gameUtils.ts:16) and used in exactly one spot: enemy.ts:213, inside DecideTakeLadder(), to decide whether a rolling barrel descends a ladder it is sitting on.

// enemy.ts — DecideTakeLadder()
if (!this.currentTrashCan?.IsOnFire) return true;
return GameUtils.CalculatePercentChance(LADDER_TAKE_CHANCE);
Note. The barrel is guaranteed to take the ladder while the oil can is unlit (the relentless DK opening), then drops to a fixed LADDER_TAKE_CHANCE roll once the first barrel lights the can. The probability constant and that condition belong to the game; this helper is only the dice. See enemy.

Modifying this: add new pure number utilities (e.g. a clamp, lerp, or range-remap) here, never in the game layer — the test is simply "does it reference any game concept?" If yes, it belongs in GameUtils or an entity. Keep every function side-effect-free apart from the seeded-PRNG family (seedRandom/random/randomRange/percentChance), which deliberately share the module-level rngState — any new randomness must draw from random(), never Math.random(), or it will break replay determinism. When you add a helper game code needs, re-export it from GameUtils so game code keeps a single import surface.

src/engine/helpers/physicsHelpers.ts 27 LOC

The other leaf module: "Helpers for Rapier bodies/colliders: extract userData, check names, collision math." It imports Collider and RigidBody from @dimforge/rapier2d-compat (the physics primitives it reads), the engine's UserData interface (the identity shape stamped onto every body), and GameObject (so one overload can take an entity directly). Its whole job is to read the opaque userData bag off a Rapier handle and answer "what type is this?" — the single seam that lets the contact registry and raycast callbacks match entities without the engine ever knowing what an "enemy" or a "wall" is. No constructor, no lifecycle: three pure functions.

Note. UserData carries two strings: type (a shared routing flag — many entities can share one type so a single contact rule matches them all) and name (a per-instance id). These helpers only ever read type. The values are assigned by the game layer; here they are just opaque strings. See UserData.

export function getUserData(physicsBody?: RigidBody): UserData

Reads the identity bag off a Rapier RigidBody, returning a safe empty default when the body is missing. Parameter: physicsBody — optional, because a GameObject may not have built its body yet. Returns: the body's live UserData reference, or a fresh { type: "", name: "" } when there is no body.

if (physicsBody) {
  return physicsBody.userData as UserData;
}

// Return empty default data
return { type: "", name: "" };

Line-level notes: line 10 casts physicsBody.userData (typed unknown by Rapier) to UserData and returns it by reference — that reference identity is load-bearing, because callers mutate it in place to rename or retype the entity. Line 14's default is a brand-new object whose mutations go nowhere, which makes a bodyless call a safe no-op rather than a null-deref.

Where it is used. Engine-internal only — both call sites live in GameObject:

// gameObject.ts — SetType()
const userData = getUserData(this.PhysicsBody);
userData.type = newType;
userData.name = newType;   // mirror name to type until SetName overrides

// gameObject.ts — SetName()
getUserData(this.PhysicsBody).name = newName;

SetType grabs the live reference and writes both fields (a fresh entity's name defaults to its type); SetName chains straight off the return to overwrite just name. Both only work because the function returns the same object the body holds.

Gotcha. The by-reference return is a double-edged sword. When PhysicsBody is undefined, getUserData hands back a throwaway default, so SetName on a body-less object silently does nothing — the write lands on an object that is immediately garbage-collected. Always set the type/name after the body exists. This is why SetType/SetName are called during entity construction once the Rapier body is built, not before.

export function matchesType(gameObject: GameObject, type: string): boolean

Answers "does this entity carry the given TYPE flag?" — the GameObject-facing matcher the contact table is built on. Parameters: gameObject the entity to test; type the routing flag to compare against. Returns: true iff the entity's userData.type equals type.

const userData = gameObject.PhysicsBody?.userData as UserData | undefined;
return userData?.type === type;

Line-level notes: it does not reuse getUserData — instead it optional-chains gameObject.PhysicsBody?.userData and casts to UserData | undefined, then optional-chains again on line 20. The difference is deliberate: matchesType only needs a boolean answer and must never allocate a default object just to compare, and a missing body must read as "no match" (undefined === type is false) rather than matching the empty-string default. Reading raw avoids both the allocation and the false-positive-on-"" trap.

Where it is used. It is the engine primitive behind the game's contact table. contactRules.ts wraps it in a one-liner byType factory that produces the ContactMatch predicates fed to contacts.Add(...):

// contactRules.ts
const byType = (type: string): ContactMatch => (go: GameObject) =>
  matchesType(go, type);

contacts.Add(byType(EntityType.ENEMY), byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "enter", () => Emitter.emit("gameOver"));
Hard-won victory. This one function is the whole point of the contact-table refactor (commit "Replace collision routing with contact table"). Every collision rule is now a pair of byType predicates plus a callback — there is no hard-coded if (a instanceof Enemy && b instanceof Player) ladder anywhere. New interactions are data: add a row, not a branch. The engine stays ignorant of every game type because matchesType compares opaque strings. See contactRegistry for the two-sided dispatch that consumes these predicates.

export function isColliderType(collider: Collider, type: string): boolean

The collider-based sibling of matchesType: same question, but starting from a Rapier Collider rather than a GameObject. This is the form Rapier's shapecast and intersection callbacks hand you. Parameters: collider the shape returned by a query; type the routing flag. Returns: true iff the collider's parent body's userData.type equals type.

const userData = collider.parent()?.userData as UserData | undefined;
return userData?.type === type;

Line-level notes: the only structural difference from matchesType is line 25's collider.parent()? — identity is stamped on the RigidBody, not the individual Collider, so you must hop up to the parent before reading userData. The same optional-chaining + UserData | undefined cast keeps a parentless collider (Rapier can return one) reading as "no match" instead of throwing.

Where it is used. Exposed as GameUtils.IsColliderType (gameUtils.ts:19) and used heavily wherever the game inspects raw query results — chiefly the player's ground/wall/ceiling casts and the enemy's wall/platform/ladder probing:

CallerTypical use
player.ts (lines 356, 387, 397, 407, 451)Filtering down/left/right/up raycasts — e.g. ignore one-way platforms on side casts, detect a wall under the feet, spot an enemy collider.
enemy.ts (lines 144–198, 281, 299)Barrel logic: reverse at a WALL, ride a PLATFORM/ONE_WAY_PLATFORM, decide ladder descent.
 
// player.ts
GameUtils.IsColliderType(downCast.collider, EntityType.WALL) === false &&
...
if (GameUtils.IsColliderType(collider, EntityType.ENEMY)) { ... }
Note. matchesType and isColliderType are the same predicate from two starting handles. matchesType serves the contact table (which already has whole GameObjects in hand); isColliderType serves manual queries (which only get bare colliders back). Keeping both means neither call site has to do the GameObject↔Collider bookkeeping itself.
flowchart TD
  subgraph engine ["engine/helpers/physicsHelpers.ts"]
    GUD["getUserData(body)"]
    MT["matchesType(gameObject, type)"]
    ICT["isColliderType(collider, type)"]
  end
  GO["GameObject.SetType / SetName"] --> GUD
  CR["contactRules.byType()"] --> MT
  MT --> REG["ContactRegistry dispatch"]
  GU["GameUtils.IsColliderType"] --> ICT
  PL["Player raycasts"] --> GU
  EN["Enemy wall/ladder probes"] --> GU
Tip. The FOCUS brief mentions "shape/vector helpers" — note those do not live here. Geometry that needs entity dimensions (e.g. IsObjectFullyInsideSensor, which reads a Cuboid's halfExtents) sits in the game-layer GameUtils, because it reasons about gameplay shapes. This module stays minimal on purpose: it only does identity lookup, never spatial math.

Modifying this: add new identity/userData readers here (e.g. a getName or a body-vs-collider name matcher) and keep them pure and game-agnostic — compare opaque strings, never import an EntityType or any game class. If you add a function that returns userData by reference like getUserData, document the mutation contract; if you only need a boolean, follow matchesType/isColliderType and read raw with optional chaining so a missing body/parent reads as "no match" without allocating. Re-export anything game code needs through GameUtils.

Engine Debug — coordinator, Camera & Physics debug

The engine's debug layer is a tiny coordinator (Debug) plus two focused sub-modules (CameraDebug, PhysicsDebug). Debug owns the single dat.GUI panel and the stats.js monitor, gates everything behind a URL hash, and exposes dependency-injection seams so the game can plug in a debug-wireframe palette, a collision-log sink, and arbitrary GUI modules without the engine ever naming a single game concept (this is decision D3). GameObjectType is included here because it is the engine's collider-shape vocabulary that PhysicsDebug reads back when it draws.

src/engine/debug/debug.ts src/engine/debug/cameraDebug.ts src/engine/debug/physicsDebug.ts src/engine/types/gameObjectType.ts
Hard-won victory — D3, the "engine never names a game type" rule. Three seams keep engine/debug/ completely game-blind. The game pushes knowledge into the engine; the engine never reaches out for it:
  • SetPhysicsTypeColors(map) — the wireframe palette (Config: debugColors) keyed by the game's EntityType strings. PhysicsDebug looks each collider up in an opaque Map<string, THREE.Color> and falls back to gray; it never knows what "player" or "enemy" mean.
  • RegisterCollisionLogSink(sink) — the game's console-log formatter (Game Debug: collisionLogger) behind the CollisionLogSink interface; Physics routes events to it.
  • RegisterModule(module) — any game GUI panel behind the DebugModule interface (Game Debug: levelDebug / playerDebug).
All wiring happens once, in App.vue, after Experience.Configure(). The payoff: you can lift engine/ into a different game with zero edits to the debug layer. 
flowchart TD
    subgraph G["game/ layer (knows the rules)"]
      DC["debugColors.ts — palette"]
      CL["collisionLogger.ts — sink"]
      MOD["levelDebug / playerDebug — modules"]
    end
    subgraph E["engine/ layer (game-blind)"]
      D["Debug (coordinator)"]
      PD["PhysicsDebug"]
      CD["CameraDebug"]
      PH["Physics"]
      CAM["Camera"]
    end
    DC -->|SetPhysicsTypeColors| D
    CL -->|RegisterCollisionLogSink| D
    MOD -->|RegisterModule| D
    PH -->|InitPhysicsDebug / UpdatePhysicsDebug| D
    CAM -->|InitCameraDebug / UpdateCameraDebug| D
    D --> PD
    D --> CD
    PH -.->|LogCollisionEvent| D
    D -.->|forwards opaquely| CL

src/engine/debug/debug.ts 160 LOC

This file is the debug coordinator. It is the first subsystem the singleton Core: Experience constructs in Configure() (this.Debug = new Debug()) — first, because every other subsystem checks this.experience.Debug.IsActive in its own constructor to decide whether to wire its debug panel. Debug owns exactly two pieces of UI — the single dat.GUI panel (Ui) and the stats.js monitor (Stats) — and delegates all domain-specific drawing to two private sub-modules it instantiates: CameraDebug and PhysicsDebug. Its imports are deliberately minimal:

  • dat from "dat.gui" and Stats from "stats.js" — the panel and FPS/MS monitor it owns directly.
  • CameraDebug, PhysicsDebug — the engine-side sub-modules, wired in-engine (not game-registered).
  • GameObject — only as a type in the CollisionLogSink method signatures.
  • * as THREE — only for the THREE.Color in SetPhysicsTypeColors's signature; it never constructs one.

Lifecycle: the constructor decides IsActive and, if active, builds the panel + monitor. There is no per-frame Update() on Debug itself — instead Physics and Camera call UpdatePhysicsDebug/UpdateCameraDebug from their own update loops, so the debug code rides along on the subsystem it observes. Destroy() tears down the panel, monitor, and registered modules.

Note — the forward-typed cycle break. Camera and Physics are aliased to any at the top of the file: 
// Forward types to avoid circular imports — Camera/Physics all depend on
// Experience which depends on Debug. Sub-modules carry their own forward types.
type Camera = any;
type Physics = any;
Importing the real classes would close the loop Camera → Experience → Debug → Camera. Debug only ever passes these through to its sub-modules, so it doesn't need the real shapes; the sub-modules each carry their own structural forward type (see below).

export interface DebugModule { Init?(ui: dat.GUI): void; Destroy?(): void }

The shape a game GUI panel must satisfy to be registered. Both methods are optional: Init(ui) builds dat.GUI folders when the panel is ready; Destroy() is optional teardown. The engine knows only this shape — never a concrete panel class.

export interface CollisionLogSink { LogCollisions: boolean; LogSensors: boolean; LogCollisionEvent(obj1: GameObject, obj2: GameObject, eventType: "enter" | "exit"): void; LogSensorEvent(obj1: GameObject, obj2: GameObject, eventType: "enter" | "exit"): void; }

The shape of the game's collision/sensor console logger. Game Debug: collisionLogger implements this; Physics forwards log events here (no-op if none registered). The two boolean flags are the live toggles a debug panel flips. Note that Physics reads these flags through the Debug coordinator, not off the sink directly — which is what the next member is for.

public IsActive: boolean; public Ui?: dat.GUI; public Stats?: Stats; private camera = new CameraDebug(); private physics = new PhysicsDebug(); private modules: DebugModule[] = []; private logSink?: CollisionLogSink;

IsActive is the master gate read everywhere. Ui/Stats are optional because they only exist when active. The two sub-modules are constructed unconditionally (they're cheap, hold no state until Init). modules is the registered-game-panel store; logSink is the single optional collision logger.

public get LogCollisions(); public set LogCollisions(v); public get LogSensors(); public set LogSensors(v);

These four accessors are a delegating facade over the registered sink. Physics reads this.debug.LogCollisions to decide whether to log; a debug panel writes it to toggle. They forward to the sink and degrade safely when no sink is registered: 

public get LogCollisions() { return this.logSink?.LogCollisions ?? false; }
public set LogCollisions(v: boolean) { if (this.logSink) this.logSink.LogCollisions = v; }
public get LogSensors() { return this.logSink?.LogSensors ?? false; }
public set LogSensors(v: boolean) { if (this.logSink) this.logSink.LogSensors = v; }

The ?? false getter is the important line: with no sink, logging is simply off, and Physics's if (this.debug.LogCollisions) guard never fires. The setter no-ops without a sink, so a GUI bound to it can't crash on a missing logger.

constructor()

Decides whether debug mode is on and, if so, builds the panel and monitor. No parameters; no return. 

// Enable debug mode with #debug in URL, or always on in dev builds
this.IsActive = import.meta.env.DEV || window.location.hash === "#debug";

if (this.IsActive) {
  this.initPanel();
  this.initStats();
}

Two ways in: Vite's import.meta.env.DEV (always on while developing) or #debug in the URL hash (so a deployed production build can be inspected by adding the fragment). The check is a one-time read at construction — toggling the hash later does nothing until reload.

public RegisterModule(module: DebugModule)

The game-GUI-panel seam. Stores the module, then — only if the panel is live — runs its Init immediately so the folder appears. 

public RegisterModule(module: DebugModule) {
  this.modules.push(module);
  if (this.IsActive && this.Ui && module.Init) {
    module.Init(this.Ui);
  }
}

It always pushes, even when inactive — that's the "no-op store" the call site comments on (App.vue: "RegisterModule is a no-op store when debug is inactive"). The game can register unconditionally at boot; the engine decides whether the panel actually materializes. The this.IsActive && this.Ui guard means a module registered while inactive is kept but never inited (and so never torn down, since Destroy() only runs when active).

public RegisterCollisionLogSink(sink: CollisionLogSink)

The collision-logger seam. Stores the single game sink; that's all. From then on the LogCollisions/LogSensors accessors and the two Log*Event forwarders have somewhere to delegate to. 

public RegisterCollisionLogSink(sink: CollisionLogSink) {
  this.logSink = sink;
}

public InitCameraDebug(camera); public UpdateCameraDebug(camera); public InitPhysicsDebug(physics); public UpdatePhysicsDebug(physics); public DestroyPhysicsDebug();

Thin pass-throughs to the two in-engine sub-modules. Camera calls InitCameraDebug(this) from its constructor and UpdateCameraDebug(this) from its Update() — but both only when Experience.Debug.IsActive (see Engine Camera). Likewise Physics calls InitPhysicsDebug/UpdatePhysicsDebug/DestroyPhysicsDebug behind its own IsActive gate. 

public InitCameraDebug(camera: Camera) {
  this.camera.Init(this.Ui!, camera);
}
Gotcha — the this.Ui! non-null assertions. InitCameraDebug and InitPhysicsDebug assert this.Ui! is defined. That's safe only because the callers (Camera, Physics) gate their Init* calls behind Experience.Debug.IsActive, and IsActive being true is exactly the condition under which the constructor built this.Ui. Call InitCameraDebug directly while inactive and you'd hand undefined to dat.GUI. The ordering dependency — "panel exists ⇒ active" — is the contract that keeps the ! honest.

public SetPhysicsTypeColors(colors: Map<string, THREE.Color>)

The palette seam — the cleanest expression of D3. Forwards the game's entity-type→color map straight to PhysicsDebug.SetTypeColors, opaquely. 

// Inject the game's wireframe palette (entity-type -> color); engine forwards it opaquely to PhysicsDebug.
// Safe to call when inactive — PhysicsDebug only reads it while rendering, which only runs when active.
public SetPhysicsTypeColors(colors: Map<string, THREE.Color>) {
  this.physics.SetTypeColors(colors);
}

The key field is Map<string, THREE.Color> — a string key, never a typed enum. The engine cannot and does not import the game's EntityType. And unlike the Init* methods, this one is safe to call when inactive: it only stores a reference, and the stored map is read solely inside PhysicsDebug.Update(), which only ever runs when active. That's why App.vue can call it unconditionally right after Configure().

public LogCollisionEvent(obj1, obj2, eventType); public LogSensorEvent(obj1, obj2, eventType);

The forwarders Physics calls when its flags are on. Each is a single optional-chained delegation — a no-op if no sink is registered: 

public LogCollisionEvent(obj1: GameObject, obj2: GameObject, eventType: "enter" | "exit") {
  this.logSink?.LogCollisionEvent(obj1, obj2, eventType);
}
public LogSensorEvent(obj1: GameObject, obj2: GameObject, eventType: "enter" | "exit") {
  this.logSink?.LogSensorEvent(obj1, obj2, eventType);
}

Note the asymmetry that keeps logging cheap: Physics first checks this.debug.LogCollisions (the delegating getter) before calling these, so the ?. here is belt-and-suspenders rather than the primary guard.

public Destroy()

Tears down the panel, removes the stats DOM node, and destroys every registered module. Called by Core: Experience's Destroy(), but only inside an if (this.Debug && this.Debug.IsActive) guard. 

public Destroy() {
  this.Ui?.destroy();
  this.Stats?.dom.parentNode?.removeChild(this.Stats.dom);
  for (const module of this.modules) {
    module.Destroy?.();
  }
}

this.Stats?.dom.parentNode?.removeChild(...) deletes the monitor element via its parent rather than assuming it's a direct child of document.body. The sub-modules' physics wireframe is not torn down here — that goes through DestroyPhysicsDebug from Physics's own Destroy(), since PhysicsDebug owns a mesh that lives in the scene.

private initPanel(); private initStats();

Build the two pieces of owned UI. initPanel is one line — a fixed-width, non-hideable dat.GUI: 

private initPanel() {
  this.Ui = new dat.GUI({ width: 300, hideable: false });
}

initStats creates the stats.js monitor, positions it just left of the 300px panel (right:315px), forces all of its internal panels visible (FPS/MS/MB stacked rather than click-to-cycle), and appends it to the body: 

this.Stats.dom.style.cssText =
  "position:fixed;top:0;right:315px;cursor:pointer;opacity:0.9;z-index:10000";

const panels = this.Stats.dom.children;
for (let i = 0; i < panels.length; i++) {
  const panel = panels[i] as HTMLElement;
  panel.style.display = "block";
  panel.style.position = "relative";
}

document.body.appendChild(this.Stats.dom);

// Memory panel only exists behind a Chrome flag
const hasMemory = (performance as any).memory !== undefined;

The right:315px is the 300px GUI width plus a 15px gutter — they sit side by side. The loop overrides stats.js's default behavior (one panel shown, click to switch) so all metrics are visible at once. The performance.memory probe just tunes the console banner — that panel only exists when Chrome is launched with --enable-precise-memory-info.

Modifying this: to add a new game debug panel, implement DebugModule in game/debug/ and call Debug.RegisterModule(...) from App.vue — never import a game type here. To add a new engine debug sub-module (say an audio visualizer), mirror the CameraDebug/PhysicsDebug pattern: instantiate it as a private field, add Init/Update/Destroy pass-throughs that assert this.Ui!, and call them from the subsystem's own IsActive-gated loop. Keep the rule: anything the engine can't name belongs behind a registration seam, not an import.

src/engine/debug/cameraDebug.ts 167 LOC

A focused sub-module owned by Debug (private camera = new CameraDebug()). It does two jobs: build the 📷 Camera Debug folder (transform readouts + live follow-tuning knobs) in Init, and run a manual fly-cam (WASD/QE pan, 1/2 zoom-or-raise, ~ camera-type toggle) every frame in Update. It imports only * as THREE (vector math) and dat from "dat.gui". Like its sibling, it declares a structural forward type for Camera to break the import cycle.

Note — the structural Camera mirror. Rather than aliasing to any, CameraDebug spells out exactly the slice of Camera it touches: 
// Forward type — Camera → Experience → Debug → CameraDebug → Camera would cycle.
type Camera = {
  Instance: THREE.Camera; CameraType: string; ManualControl: boolean;
  FrustumSize: number; ZoomFactor: number; AspectRatio: number; LastToggleTime: number;
  OrthographicCamera?: THREE.OrthographicCamera;
  Input?: { IsWKeyPressed: boolean; /* ...IsSKey, IsAKey, ... IsTildePressed */ };
  Time: { Elapsed: number };
  SwitchCamera: () => void;
  Tuning: { lookaheadX: number; /* ...mirrors Camera.Tuning */ };
};
The field names are PascalCase to mirror the real Camera class, and the nested Input/Time/Tuning shapes mirror those classes too — so TypeScript still type-checks the access even though no import exists. If you add a knob to Camera.Tuning (see Engine Camera) you must add it here as well, or the GUI binding won't compile.

const MOVE_SPEED = 0.5; const ROTATE_SPEED = 0.02;

Module-level constants: world units of pan per frame, and radians of yaw per frame for A/D in perspective mode. Frame-based (not Time.Delta-scaled) — this is a developer fly-cam, not gameplay, so frame-rate-dependent speed is acceptable.

public Init(ui: dat.GUI, camera: Camera)

Builds the camera folder. ui is the shared panel; camera is the structural view bound by reference. Returns nothing. 

const folder = ui.addFolder("📷 Camera Debug");
folder.close();

folder.add(camera, "CameraType").name("Type").listen();
folder.add(camera, "ManualControl").name("Manual Control").listen();
folder.add(camera.Instance.position, "x").name("Position X").min(-1000).max(1000).step(0.1).listen();
// ...Position Y, Position Z, Zoom (FrustumSize)...

Every readout uses .listen() so dat.GUI polls the live value each frame — the position fields track the camera as the fly-cam or follow logic moves it. Then a nested 🎥 Camera Tuning sub-folder binds all nine camera.Tuning knobs (look-ahead, follow rates, climb/fall leads), letting you dial the Mario-style follow feel live while playing: 

const tuning = folder.addFolder("🎥 Camera Tuning");
tuning.add(camera.Tuning, "lookaheadX").name("Look-ahead X").min(0).max(30).step(0.5);
tuning.add(camera.Tuning, "lookaheadRate").name("Look-ahead Rate").min(0.5).max(15).step(0.5);
// ...horizontalRate, verticalRateGround/Up/Down, climbLeadY, fallBufferY, fallLeadY...
tuning.close();

These tuning binds omit .listen() — they're write-only inputs, not readouts, so there's no live value to poll. Both folders open closed (folder.close(), tuning.close()) to keep the panel tidy.

public Update(camera: Camera)

Per-frame manual camera movement, called from Debug.UpdateCameraDebug → from Camera.Update. It first builds a ground-plane basis, then reads the held WASD/QE/1/2 keys, then handles the ~ toggle. 

// Camera forward/right flattened onto the ground plane.
const forward = new THREE.Vector3(0, 0, -1).applyQuaternion(camera.Instance.quaternion);
forward.y = 0;
forward.normalize();

const right = new THREE.Vector3().crossVectors(forward, new THREE.Vector3(0, 1, 0)).normalize();

const isOrtho = camera.Instance instanceof THREE.OrthographicCamera;

forward.y = 0 flattens the look direction so W/S pan along the ground instead of diving toward it. The behavior then forks on isOrtho: in orthographic mode WASD pans the world axes and 1/2 zoom; in perspective mode W/S dolly along forward, A/D yaw (rotateY ± ROTATE_SPEED), Q/E strafe along right, and 1/2 raise/lower the Y position. 

if (camera.Input?.IsWKeyPressed) {
  if (isOrtho)
    camera.Instance.position.addScaledVector(new THREE.Vector3(0, 1, 0), MOVE_SPEED);
  else camera.Instance.position.addScaledVector(forward, MOVE_SPEED);
} else if (camera.Input?.IsSKeyPressed) {
  // ...mirror with -MOVE_SPEED...
} else if (camera.Input?.IsAKeyPressed) {
  if (isOrtho) camera.Instance.position.addScaledVector(right, -MOVE_SPEED);
  else camera.Instance.rotateY(ROTATE_SPEED);
}
// ...D, Q, E, 1, 2...

The whole movement block is one else if chain, so only one action fires per frame — diagonal pans aren't possible. camera.Input? is optional-chained because Camera only assigns this.Input when debug is active (see Engine Camera, constructor line 86). The tilde toggle is debounced against the engine clock: 

if (camera.Input?.IsTildePressed &&
    camera.Time.Elapsed - camera.LastToggleTime >= 0.25) {
  camera.SwitchCamera();
  camera.LastToggleTime = camera.Time.Elapsed;
  console.log(`📷 Switched to ${camera.CameraType} camera`);
}

The 0.25s gate stops a single key-hold from flickering perspective↔ortho every frame; LastToggleTime lives on Camera so it survives between frames.

Gotcha — who owns the camera while flying. This Update writes the camera transform directly, but it only takes effect because Camera.Update early-returns the moment ManualControl is true (after calling UpdateCameraDebug). So the manual fly-cam and the follow logic never fight: flip Manual Control in the panel (or the bound key) and the fly-cam fully owns the camera; flip it back and follow resumes. See Engine Camera.

private zoom(camera: Camera, zoomIn: boolean)

Orthographic-only zoom: nudges FrustumSize by ±ZoomFactor, clamps to a minimum of 1, then recomputes the ortho bounds from the aspect ratio and pushes the projection matrix. 

let delta = camera.ZoomFactor;
if (zoomIn) delta = -camera.ZoomFactor;
camera.FrustumSize = Math.max(1, camera.FrustumSize + delta);
const ortho = camera.OrthographicCamera!;
ortho.left = (-camera.FrustumSize * camera.AspectRatio) / 2;
ortho.right = (camera.FrustumSize * camera.AspectRatio) / 2;
ortho.top = camera.FrustumSize / 2;
ortho.bottom = -camera.FrustumSize / 2;
ortho.updateProjectionMatrix();

Zooming in means a smaller frustum, hence delta is negated when zoomIn. This duplicates the bound-math that Camera.Resize() uses (see Engine Camera) — the same four-line formula keeps the orthographic view square to the viewport.

Modifying this: adding a movement key means extending the else if chain and the Input field list on the structural Camera type (and the real Input class). Adding a new follow knob means adding it to Camera.Tuning, the CameraTuning interface in Engine Camera, and the structural mirror here, then one tuning.add(...) line. Because the type is structural, a typo silently drops a binding only at runtime — keep the mirror in lock-step.

src/engine/debug/physicsDebug.ts 179 LOC

The collider wireframe renderer, owned by Debug (private physics = new PhysicsDebug()). Each frame it walks the Rapier world, draws every collider's outline as line segments colored by the game's entity-type palette, and surfaces two object counters in a 🌍 World Debug folder. It owns a single THREE.LineSegments mesh — adds it to the scene, rebuilds its geometry every frame, and disposes it on teardown. Imports: * as THREE (the mesh + colors), RAPIER (collider shape accessors), dat from "dat.gui" (the counters). It declares a read-only structural Physics view — the scene it draws into and the world it walks: 

type Physics = {
  Scene: THREE.Scene;
  World: {
    colliders: { len: () => number };
    forEachCollider: (cb: (collider: RAPIER.Collider) => void) => void;
  };
};

Note Physics itself carries no debug fields — this module owns its own mesh, so the physics subsystem stays clean. This replaces Rapier's built-in debugRender, whose flat palette could only tell sensors from solids.

Hard-won victory — D3 lives here. The whole reason this hand-rolled renderer exists is to color colliders by game type while the engine stays type-blind: 
private static readonly DEFAULT_COLOR = new THREE.Color("#777777"); // gray fallback
// EntityType string -> color, injected by the game (D3: engine stays type-blind).
private typeColors = new Map<string, THREE.Color>();
The map starts empty and is filled only via SetTypeColors (← Debug.SetPhysicsTypeColors ← Config: debugColors). Any collider whose type isn't in the map draws gray. The engine never writes a single color literal for a game type.

private static readonly CIRCLE_SEGMENTS = 20; private mesh?: THREE.LineSegments<THREE.BufferGeometry, THREE.LineBasicMaterial>; private scene?: THREE.Scene; private counts = { renderObjectCount: 0, physicsObjectCount: 0 };

CIRCLE_SEGMENTS = arc resolution for ball/capsule outlines. counts is bound by reference into the GUI so .listen() reflects live updates without re-binding — the Update loop just mutates its two fields.

public SetTypeColors(colors: Map<string, THREE.Color>)

The receiving end of the D3 palette seam. Replaces the whole map by reference. Safe to call any time (even inactive) because the map is only read inside Update, which only runs when active. 

public SetTypeColors(colors: Map<string, THREE.Color>) {
  this.typeColors = colors;
}

public Init(ui: dat.GUI, physics: Physics)

Creates the wireframe mesh and the counter folder. Called by Physics.Configure() via Debug.InitPhysicsDebug, only when active. 

this.scene = physics.Scene;

this.mesh = new THREE.LineSegments(
  new THREE.BufferGeometry(),
  // vertexColors draws each segment with the per-vertex RGBA we build in
  // Update() — one color per collider, keyed by its entity type.
  new THREE.LineBasicMaterial({ vertexColors: true })
);
this.mesh.frustumCulled = false;
this.scene.add(this.mesh);

const folder = ui.addFolder("🌍 World Debug");
folder.open();
folder.add(this.counts, "renderObjectCount").name("Render Objects").listen();
folder.add(this.counts, "physicsObjectCount").name("Physics Objects").listen();

vertexColors: true is what lets one mesh carry many colors — each segment endpoint gets its own RGBA, so platforms, enemies, and sensors are different colors in a single draw call. frustumCulled = false stops Three.js from culling the mesh when its (frequently rebuilt, possibly stale) bounding sphere falls offscreen — the wireframe must always render. This folder .open()s by default (unlike the camera folder) since the counts are at-a-glance health numbers.

public Update(physics: Physics)

Recounts the scene and colliders, then rebuilds the entire wireframe from scratch. Called from Physics.Update() every frame. 

let entityCount = 0;
physics.Scene.traverse(() => entityCount++);
this.counts.renderObjectCount = entityCount;
this.counts.physicsObjectCount = physics.World.colliders.len();

renderObjectCount counts every node in the Three.js scene graph (traverse); physicsObjectCount is Rapier's live collider count — a quick way to spot leaks (numbers should fall back to baseline after a level unload). Then the rebuild: 

const positions: number[] = [];
const colors: number[] = [];

physics.World.forEachCollider((collider) => {
  const userData = collider.parent()?.userData as { type?: string } | undefined;
  const color =
    this.typeColors.get(userData?.type ?? "") ?? PhysicsDebug.DEFAULT_COLOR;
  this.appendColliderOutline(collider, color, positions, colors);
});

const geometry = this.mesh!.geometry;
geometry.setAttribute("position",
  new THREE.BufferAttribute(new Float32Array(positions), 2));
geometry.setAttribute("color",
  new THREE.BufferAttribute(new Float32Array(colors), 4));
this.mesh!.visible = true;

The type lookup is the heart of D3: collider.parent()?.userData.type is the game's EntityType string (set on the rigid body — see Entities: userData), and that string indexes typeColors, falling back to gray for any unmapped type. Two BufferAttribute item-sizes matter: position is 2 (Rapier2D is XY-only; the mesh sits at z=0) and color is 4 (RGBA, matching the four values seg() pushes per endpoint).

Gotcha — full rebuild every frame. positions/colors are fresh arrays each frame and wrapped in new Float32Arrays, so this allocates per frame. That's a deliberate simplicity-over-speed call for a dev-only overlay (it never ships to players), but it's why this is the kind of thing you'd never leave on in a perf profile.

private appendColliderOutline(collider, color, positions, colors)

Appends one collider's outline (cuboid / ball / capsule) to the shared buffers, in world pose. It reads the collider's translation and rotation once, precomputes cos/sin, and defines a local seg() closure that rotates a local-space segment into world space and pushes the color on both endpoints: 

const t = collider.translation();
const angle = collider.rotation();
const cos = Math.cos(angle);
const sin = Math.sin(angle);

const seg = (ax: number, ay: number, bx: number, by: number) => {
  positions.push(t.x + ax * cos - ay * sin, t.y + ax * sin + ay * cos);
  positions.push(t.x + bx * cos - by * sin, t.y + bx * sin + by * cos);
  colors.push(color.r, color.g, color.b, 1, color.r, color.g, color.b, 1);
};

That's a standard 2D rotation (x' = x·cos − y·sin, y' = x·sin + y·cos) plus the translation — so rotated cubes (the engine's slopes!) draw at their true angle. The colors.push writes 8 numbers: RGBA for endpoint A then RGBA for endpoint B, all opaque. Then it branches on the Rapier shape: 

switch (collider.shapeType()) {
  case RAPIER.ShapeType.Cuboid: {
    const h = collider.halfExtents();
    seg(-h.x, -h.y, h.x, -h.y); seg(h.x, -h.y, h.x, h.y);
    seg(h.x, h.y, -h.x, h.y);   seg(-h.x, h.y, -h.x, -h.y);
    break;
  }
  case RAPIER.ShapeType.Ball: {
    const r = collider.radius();
    const n = PhysicsDebug.CIRCLE_SEGMENTS;
    for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) { /* n chords around the circle */ }
    break;
  }
  case RAPIER.ShapeType.Capsule: {
    const r = collider.radius();
    const hh = collider.halfHeight();
    seg(-r, -hh, -r, hh); seg(r, -hh, r, hh);   // straight sides (axis = local Y)
    // ...two semicircle caps over +hh / under -hh...
    break;
  }
}

Cuboid = four sides from halfExtents; ball = CIRCLE_SEGMENTS chords; capsule = two straight sides plus rounded caps (axis is local Y, so caps bulge above +hh and below -hh). These three cases are exactly the three shapes the engine ever creates — see the GameObjectType article below. The comment pins this to Rapier 0.14 shape accessors (halfExtents(), radius(), halfHeight()); they've moved between versions, hence the version pin in VERSION.md.

public Destroy()

Removes the mesh from the scene and disposes both its geometry and material — the GPU-resource cleanup Three.js requires. Reached via Debug.DestroyPhysicsDebug from Physics.Destroy(). 

if (this.mesh) {
  this.scene?.remove(this.mesh);
  this.mesh.geometry.dispose();
  this.mesh.material.dispose();
  this.mesh = undefined;
}

Setting this.mesh = undefined makes the method idempotent and lets the next Init start clean. This is the one piece of debug teardown that does not go through Debug.Destroy() — it has to follow the physics world's lifecycle because the mesh lives in the shared scene.

Modifying this: to support a new collider shape, add a case RAPIER.ShapeType.X with the matching Rapier accessor and a few seg() calls — but first check whether the gameplay layer even creates it (today it never does; see below). To recolor, edit Config: debugColors, never this file. If you bump the Rapier version, re-verify halfExtents()/radius()/halfHeight() against the new API and update VERSION.md.

src/engine/types/gameObjectType.ts 14 LOC

The engine's entire collider-shape vocabulary — a frozen as const object with three members. It's the type GameObject.createCollider switches on to pick a Rapier collider, and (transitively) the set of shapes PhysicsDebug.appendColliderOutline needs to draw. Documented here because it closes the loop: the same three shapes that go in at collider creation come back out at debug-draw time. 

// GameObject physics collider shapes — CUBE, SPHERE, CAPSULE only (D5/D-lean);
// slopes built from rotated/overlapped cubes, polyline/convex purged.
// Maps to Rapier2D: CUBE->cuboid, SPHERE->ball, CAPSULE->capsule.
const GameObjectType = {
  CUBE: "cube",       // players, platforms, walls, slopes (rotated cubes)
  SPHERE: "sphere",   // rolling enemies, projectiles, round objects
  CAPSULE: "capsule", // smooth-moving characters (won't catch on ledges)
} as const;

export default GameObjectType;
MemberValueRapier shapeDrawn byUsed for
CUBE"cube"cuboid (ShapeType.Cuboid)4-segment box from halfExtents()players, platforms, walls, slopes (rotated cubes)
SPHERE"sphere"ball (ShapeType.Ball)20-chord circle from radius()rolling enemies, projectiles, round objects
CAPSULE"capsule"capsule (ShapeType.Capsule)2 sides + 2 semicircle capssmooth-moving characters (won't catch on ledges)
Hard-won victory — D5 / "D-lean". The header comment records a deliberate diet: only these three primitives survive; polyline and convex-mesh colliders were purged. Slopes are rotated cubes and complex shapes are overlapped cubes. The payoff cascades into this very section — PhysicsDebug's switch only ever needs three cases, and the 2D rotation in seg() is enough to draw an angled slope correctly. A polyline collider would have meant per-vertex outline code and far more surface for bugs.
Gotcha — two different "type" axes, don't conflate them. GameObjectType is the collider's geometry (engine concept, this file). The color palette is keyed by the game's EntityType (a separate enum in Game Types: entityType, e.g. PLAYER, ENEMY, LADDER_SENSOR). So PhysicsDebug draws each collider by its GameObjectType shape (via shapeType()) but colors it by its EntityType string (via userData.type). A red ball and a red box can share a color yet differ in shape; a box can be white (player) or green (platform) yet draw identically. Keeping the two axes orthogonal is what lets the engine own shapes while the game owns identity.

The consumer side, for reference — GameObject.createCollider maps each member to a Rapier ColliderDesc, with CUBE as the catch-all default: 

switch (gameObjectType) {
  case GameObjectType.SPHERE:  return RAPIER.ColliderDesc.ball(size.width / 2);
  case GameObjectType.CAPSULE: return RAPIER.ColliderDesc.capsule(size.height / 2, size.width);
  case GameObjectType.CUBE:
  default:                     return RAPIER.ColliderDesc.cuboid(size.width / 2, size.height / 2);
}

Modifying this: adding a shape is a three-site change kept intentionally honest by the diet — add the member here, a case in GameObject.createCollider (Entities: GameObject), and a draw case in PhysicsDebug.appendColliderOutline. But weigh it against D5 first: most "new shapes" are better expressed as rotated or overlapped cubes, which need no code at all.

Engine Types — the shared vocabulary

Nine tiny files, zero runtime behaviour, enormous decoupling power. These are the plain data shapes and string/number enums that let the engine and the game speak to each other without either holding a reference to the other's classes. Every one is either a const-object enum (the same-named const + type idiom), a data-shape interface, a one-method seam interface (InputSource), or both — the enums chosen so the same identifier works as a value (PlayerStates.IDLE) and as a type annotation (: PlayerState). Read this section as the dictionary the rest of the guide is written in.

src/engine/types/cameraTarget.ts src/engine/types/contactPoints.ts src/engine/types/gameObjectType.ts src/engine/types/inputSource.ts src/engine/types/inputState.ts src/engine/types/levelData.ts src/engine/types/playerDirection.ts src/engine/types/playerStates.ts src/engine/types/userData.ts
Note — two flavours of "engine type". Some of these are genuinely engine-pure: CameraTarget was deliberately invented so the Engine Camera can follow a target while naming zero game concepts (decision C5/D3). Others — PlayerStates, PlayerDirection, ContactPoints — physically live under engine/types/ but are named for the player and are consumed almost entirely by the game's Player. They are the project's documented "known residue": shared vocabulary parked in the engine folder. Where it matters below, the prose says honestly which is which — and points to the one place (the camera) that pointedly refuses to import the player's enum.

src/engine/types/cameraTarget.ts 25 LOC

This file IS the seam between the game's Player and the Engine Camera. It exports two things: a CameraFollowState enum (the camera's own vertical-follow modes, expressed in engine words) and a CameraTarget interface (the per-frame snapshot the camera reads). Nobody "constructs" it — the Game Director fills a fresh CameraTarget object each frame from its Player and hands it to Camera.Update. It imports nothing. Its lifecycle is the lifecycle of a value type: born in GameDirector.cameraTargetFromPlayer(), dead the moment Camera.Update returns.

Hard-won victory — C5 / D3 decouple. Before this file, camera.ts reached straight into the live Player — it read player.CurrentPosition, player.NextTranslation.x, player.IsTouching.ground, and switched on PlayerState. That made the engine camera un-reusable: it named game concepts. CameraTarget carries exactly those scalars and nothing else, so the camera's easing branches are preserved byte-for-byte while the camera now imports nothing from game/. A different game — or a multi-player follow — just fills the snapshot differently.

export const CameraFollowState = { GROUNDED, CLIMBING, FALLING, JUMPING } as const

The camera's four vertical-follow modes, in engine terms — note there is no RUNNING or IDLE here, because to the camera "standing still" and "walking" are the same grounded behaviour. The game maps its richer PlayerState onto these four. The as const freezes the literal string values so they double as a type. 

export const CameraFollowState = {
  GROUNDED: "grounded",
