  CLIMBING: "climbing",
  FALLING: "falling",
  JUMPING: "jumping",
} as const;

export type CameraFollowState =
  (typeof CameraFollowState)[keyof typeof CameraFollowState];
ModeCamera behaviour (per Engine Camera Update)
GROUNDEDRest at the baseline; while truly grounded, re-baseline baselineY to the player's Y so walking up terrain pans up.
CLIMBINGTrack the player upward with a climbLeadY lead (next handhold visible); releases any CameraSensor pin.
FALLINGFollow down only once the player drops past fallBufferY below the camera; leads slightly with fallLeadY; capped rate.
JUMPINGHold the baseline — deliberately does not chase the jump arc.

The same-named const + type pattern (line 13's comment calls it "the playerStates.ts idiom") means CameraFollowState.CLIMBING is a value and followState: CameraFollowState is a type — one declaration, both jobs.

export interface CameraTarget

The per-frame follow input. Five fields, each one the exact scalar the camera used to read off the live Player. The camelCase field names are deliberate (decision D15: data-shape interfaces use camelCase, unlike the PascalCase public members on classes). 

export interface CameraTarget {
  x: number;            // world X to follow (was player.CurrentPosition.x)
  y: number;            // world Y to follow (was player.CurrentPosition.y)
  velocityX: number;    // horizontal velocity -> look-ahead direction (was NextTranslation.x)
  followState: CameraFollowState; // vertical-follow mode (mapped from the game's state)
  isGrounded: boolean;  // truly on the ground -> grounded re-baseline (was IsTouching.ground)
}
FieldTypeConsumed in Camera.Update for…
xnumberBase of targetX before the eased look-ahead is added.
ynumberClimb/fall tracking target and the grounded re-baseline value.
velocityXnumberSign (vs MOVE_EPSILON) decides the look-ahead direction; below epsilon the look-ahead eases to 0.
followStateCameraFollowStateThe switch that selects vertical-follow behaviour.
isGroundedbooleanGuards the grounded re-baseline (only re-anchor Y when truly on the ground and no sensor pin).
Producer vs consumer. Producer: Game Director — cameraTargetFromPlayer(player) maps player.State through a switch into a CameraFollowState (CLIMBING / FALLING / JUMPING, else GROUNDED) and copies player.CurrentPosition, player.NextTranslation.x, player.IsTouching.ground. Consumer: Engine Camera — Update(target?: CameraTarget). Note the ?: the camera guards against the target not being ready yet and returns early. 
flowchart LR
  PS["Player.State (PlayerState)"] --> M["GameDirector.cameraTargetFromPlayer()"]
  POS["Player position / velocity / IsTouching.ground"] --> M
  M --> CT["CameraTarget snapshot"]
  CT --> CU["Camera.Update(target)"]
  CU --> EASE["follow easing (X look-ahead, Y by followState)"]

Modifying this: if the camera needs a new input (say a zoom hint), add a field here and read it in Camera.Update — then fill it in GameDirector.cameraTargetFromPlayer. Keep the field a plain scalar/enum; the whole point of C5 is that the camera never imports a game class, so never add a Player-typed field. To add a follow mode, extend CameraFollowState and add a case in both the camera's switch and the director's mapping switch.

src/engine/types/contactPoints.ts 34 LOC

A single default-exported interface ContactPoints: eight booleans describing which surfaces a GameObject is currently touching. It imports nothing. In practice the only thing that owns one is the game's Player (player.IsTouching), which it seeds in its constructor and rewrites every frame from shapecasts and ladder sensors. The player's state handlers and PlayerDebug read it. It carries no logic — it is the vocabulary the contact-detection code writes and the state machine reads.

export default interface ContactPoints

The fields fall into three groups, flagged by the section comments in the file.

export default interface ContactPoints {
  // ===== BASIC COLLISION DIRECTIONS =====
  ground: boolean;
  ceiling: boolean;
  leftSide: boolean;
  rightSide: boolean;
  // ===== SPECIAL PLATFORM TYPES =====
  edgePlatform: boolean;
  // ===== LADDER COLLISION ZONES =====
  ladderCore: boolean;
  ladderTop: boolean;
  ladderBottom: boolean;
}
FieldSet true when…Used by the player for…
groundDown-shapecast hits a floor.Enables jumping, resets coyote time, drives idle/run animation; also feeds CameraTarget.isGrounded.
ceilingUp-shapecast hits.Cancels upward velocity; prevents jumping through ceilings.
leftSideLeft-shapecast hits a wall.Blocks leftward movement (see applyHorizontalMovement); can enable wall slide.
rightSideRight-shapecast hits a wall.Blocks rightward movement; can enable wall slide.
edgePlatformStanding on a platform whose IsEdge is true.Edge-specific animations / mechanics.
ladderCoreInside a ladder's core sensor zone.Permits climbing up/down on directional input.
ladderTopAt a ladder's top sensor.Transition climb → stand on the platform above.
ladderBottomAt a ladder's bottom sensor.Transition climb → stand on the ground below.
Producer vs consumer. Producer: the Player writes every field. ground/ceiling/leftSide/rightSide come from four directional shapeCast calls (Object.assign(this.IsTouching, {…})), edgePlatform from the platform under the feet, and the three ladder flags from UpdateLadderState(top, core, bottom) — which the Game Director feeds from the ladder sensors. Consumers: the player's five state handlers (handlePlayer{Idle,Running,Jumping,Falling,Climbing}), applyHorizontalMovement, and PlayerDebug (read-only display).
Gotcha — ordering. IsTouching is mutated in place each frame (the player keeps one object for its whole life). Contact detection must run before the state handlers in Player.Update, or the FSM reads last frame's surface state. The booleans are an engine-neutral surface description, but only the player ever fills one today.

Modifying this: add a boolean here only when a new surface relationship exists (e.g. a movingPlatform contact). Then set it in the player's detection pass and consume it in a handler — and remember to seed it false in the player's IsTouching initializer, or it reads undefined on frame one.

src/engine/types/gameObjectType.ts 14 LOC

A default-exported const enum of the three physics collider shapes the engine supports: CUBE, SPHERE, CAPSULE. It imports nothing. It is the contract between every game entity (which picks a shape) and GameObject (which turns that string into a Rapier collider and a Three.js debug mesh).

const GameObjectType = { CUBE: "cube", SPHERE: "sphere", CAPSULE: "capsule" } as const

const GameObjectType = {
  CUBE: "cube",     // players, platforms, walls, slopes (rotated cubes)
  SPHERE: "sphere", // rolling enemies, projectiles, round objects
  CAPSULE: "capsule", // smooth-moving characters (won't catch on ledges)
} as const;

export default GameObjectType;
ValueStringRapier collider (in GameObject.createCollider)
CUBE"cube"ColliderDesc.cuboid(width/2, height/2) — also the default fall-through.
SPHERE"sphere"ColliderDesc.ball(width/2)
CAPSULE"capsule"ColliderDesc.capsule(height/2, width)
Hard-won victory — D5 / "D-lean". The file's header records a deliberate purge: only these three primitives survive. Slopes are built from rotated cubes and complex shapes from overlapped cubes, so polyline and convex-mesh colliders were removed entirely. That keeps GameObject.createCollider a tiny three-way switch and avoids the whole class of degenerate/auto-generated mesh-collider bugs.
Producer vs consumer. Producers: the game entities pick a shape and pass it into createObjectPhysics — e.g. Platform and the Player both pass GameObjectType.CUBE; Enemy, TrashCan, WinFlag, Teleporter, CameraSensor, LadderSensor each pick the shape that fits. Consumer: the engine's GameObject — createCollider (Rapier shape) and createObjectGraphicsDebug (the matching Three.js debug geometry).
Tip. GameObject.createCollider takes a plain gameObjectType: string, not the enum type. An unrecognised string is not an error — it falls through to CUBE. That is intentional (a missing shape becomes a box rather than a crash), but it means a typo'd shape name silently becomes a cube.

Modifying this: adding a fourth shape means three edits in lockstep — a new key here, a new case in GameObject.createCollider (Rapier ColliderDesc), and a new case in createObjectGraphicsDebug (Three.js geometry). Resist it unless rotated/overlapped cubes genuinely can't express the shape — that restraint is the whole D5 point.

src/engine/types/inputSource.ts 12 LOC

A default-exported interface InputSource with a single method — the seam between whatever produces input and the entities that consume it. It is the one behavioural interface in this folder (it declares a method, not data fields), and it imports only its sibling InputState. The keyboard Input device implements it today; a touch, gamepad, network, or replay source drops in by implementing the same one method, with no edits to the Player or any other entity.

export default interface InputSource { CaptureInto(state: InputState): void }

One method. CaptureInto is handed an entity's InputState bag and fills all nine intent booleans for the current frame, mutating the bag in place (see inputState.ts below). The interface owns no state of its own — it is purely the contract "given a snapshot, write this frame's intent into it". 

import InputState from "./inputState";

export default interface InputSource {
  // Fill the given InputState snapshot from this source for the current frame.
  CaptureInto(state: InputState): void;
}
Hard-won victory — depend on the seam, not the device. Player's inputDevice field is typed InputSource, not the concrete keyboard Input class. So the movement code calls this.inputDevice.CaptureInto(this.Input) against the interface, and a different producer — touch, gamepad, a recorded replay, an AI — is a one-file drop-in: write a class that implements InputSource and assign it. Nothing in the entity changes. This is the behavioural twin of InputState's "input-as-data" decouple: the data shape stays the same, and now the source of that data is an interface too — not a concrete class the entity has to name.
Producer vs consumer. Producer (implementer): Engine Input — the keyboard device implements InputSource and supplies CaptureInto. Consumer: the game's Player (and any future controllable entity) holds an inputDevice: InputSource and calls CaptureInto(this.Input) at the top of Update — depending on this interface, never on the concrete device.

Modifying this: resist widening it. A new input source is a new class that implements InputSource, not a change here. Only touch the interface if every source must grow a new capability (say a Rumble() back-channel), and then implement it in every producer. To add a new intent, edit InputState instead (a field there, a line in each CaptureInto).

src/engine/types/inputState.ts 18 LOC

A default-exported interface InputState: a plain per-frame snapshot of input intent. It imports nothing. It is the decoupling seam in Engine Events & Input: controllers read these booleans instead of polling the live keyboard device, so the source of input (keyboard today; network/replay/AI later) can change without touching movement code.

export default interface InputState

Nine booleans. The field names mirror the Input device's intent methods exactly — so a handler reads player.Input.isLeft (a value) where it once called input.IsLeft() (a method): same answer, one fewer pair of parens. 

export default interface InputState {
  // Horizontal intent (mutually-exclusive helpers mirror the input device)
  isLeft: boolean;
  isRight: boolean;
  isLeftRightCombo: boolean;
  isNeitherLeftRight: boolean;
  // Jump intent
  isJump: boolean;
  // Vertical intent (ladders)
  isUp: boolean;
  isDown: boolean;
  isUpDownCombo: boolean;
  isNeitherUpDown: boolean;
}
FieldFilled from Input…True when
isLeftIsLeft()Left held, right not.
isRightIsRight()Right held, left not.
isLeftRightComboIsLeftRightCombo()Both left+right held.
isNeitherLeftRightIsNeitherLeftRight()Neither held.
isJumpIsJump()Spacebar held.
isUpIsUp()Up held, down not.
isDownIsDown()Down held, up not.
isUpDownComboIsUpDownCombo()Both up+down held.
isNeitherUpDownIsNeitherUpDown()Neither held.
Hard-won victory — D10 "input-as-data". The Player's constructor comment marks this: each entity owns its own InputState bag and refreshes it from the device every frame (this.inputDevice.CaptureInto(this.Input) at the top of Player.Update). Because handlers read the bag, not the device, you could swap the producer for a replay file or a network packet without changing a single line of movement logic.
Producer vs consumer. Producer: Engine Input — CaptureInto(state) writes all nine fields, mutating the bag in place so existing references stay valid. Consumer: the game's Player owns the bag (seeded in initializePlayerAttributes with isNeitherLeftRight: true / isNeitherUpDown: true) and its state handlers read it.
Gotcha — mutated in place. CaptureInto overwrites the same object; it never allocates a new one. Don't stash a reference expecting an immutable snapshot of "this frame's input" — it will change next frame. If you need history (combos, buffering), copy the fields out.

Modifying this: a new intent means three coordinated edits — a field here, a matching Is* getter on Input, and a line in Input.CaptureInto. Then seed it in the player's Input initializer. Keep names mirroring the device method (drop the parens, lowercase the first letter).

src/engine/types/levelData.ts 35 LOC

The shape the GLB parser emits and the entity factories consume. Two interfaces: LevelEntity (one spawnable object) and LevelData (a dictionary of named entities). The default export is LevelData. It imports nothing. GLB is the single permanent level source, so this is the one data contract between ResourceLoader (engine) and the game's spawn pipeline.

interface LevelEntity

One GameObject to create — its size, transform, type string, and an optional metadata bag.

interface LevelEntity {
  width: number;   // X axis (horizontal)
  depth: number;   // Y axis in this coord system (into-screen; ~1 for 2D)
  height: number;  // Z axis in this coord system (vertical)
  position: number[]; // [x, y, z] = [horizontal, depth, vertical]
  rotation: number[]; // Euler [x, y, z] radians; usually [0, rotation, 0]
  type: string;    // "Platform", "Wall", "OneWayPlatform", "...Sensor", "Enemy", ...
  meta?: Record<string, string>; // parsed texture-name tokens, e.g. "floor=1 oneway=8"
}
FieldMeaning & how the factories read it
width / height / depthCollider extents. Factories pass { width: data.width, height: data.height } (and depth for walls) into the entity constructors.
positionSlot order [gameX, gameDepth, gameUp]. Factories read position[0] as x and position[2] as y; setCameraStart reads position[1] as depth.
rotationSlot order [-, gameRotation(z), -]. Factories pass -data.rotation[1] (the parser already negated once; see the victory below).
typeThe canonical type string — the same verbatim strings as game/types/entityType.ts. The factory registry is keyed by it.
meta?Generic key=value bag parsed from the mesh's texture name. The engine never interprets a key; the game does (see below).

interface LevelData { [entityName: string]: LevelEntity }

A dictionary keyed by unique entity name. The Game Director iterates Object.values(this.LevelData) to spawn everything. The key is for identity/debugging only — entities are matched by type, never by key. 

interface LevelData {
  [entityName: string]: LevelEntity;
}

export default LevelData;
Hard-won victory — the generic meta bag. The header notes this replaced the old generic value0-3 indices. Previously every entity carried four nameless numbers; now ResourceLoader parses the collision mesh's texture name (e.g. "floor=1 oneway=8") into a Record<string,string> and the game-side factories interpret keys per type (floor/edge/oneway for platforms, dir for ladders, cam/dest for camera/teleporter). The engine parser stays generic and never reads a game-specific key — preserving the engine/game seam through the level pipeline.
Producer vs consumer. Producer: Engine Resources — ResourceLoader.ParseLevel(glbPath) returns Promise<LevelData> via convertToLevelData (which also #n-disambiguates duplicate names so none are dropped). Consumers: the Game Director (LoadLevelData stores it and loops it) and the entity factories in game config, whose EntityFactory signature takes the row as data: any and reads data.meta through metaNum / metaCoords helpers.
Gotcha — axis slots, not names. position/rotation are positional arrays in the parser's fixed slot order, not {x,y,z} objects. The parser is the single place glTF axes map to game axes; the factories rely on that exact slot order ([0]=x, [2]=up). Reorder the slots in one place and you must reorder both.

Modifying this: to teach a new entity new authored data, add a key=value token in the level's texture name — no schema change needed here, meta is already open. Only touch this interface to add a genuinely new structural field (a new size axis, etc.), and then update both ResourceLoader.convertToLevelData (producer) and the factories (consumer).

src/engine/types/playerDirection.ts 19 LOC

A default-exported const of signed numeric direction constants. It imports nothing. Unlike the string enums, these are numbers because they are used arithmetically — multiplied into velocity, fed as shapecast directions, and used to flip sprites. NEUTRAL means no directional input.

const PlayerDirection = { NEUTRAL: 0, RIGHT: 1, LEFT: -1, UP: 1, DOWN: -1 } as const

const PlayerDirection = {
  NEUTRAL: 0, // no input / vertical-only checks
  RIGHT: 1,   // positive X
  LEFT: -1,   // negative X
  UP: 1,      // positive Y
  DOWN: -1,   // negative Y
} as const;

export default PlayerDirection;
ValueNumberAxisUsed for
NEUTRAL0—No input; the "other axis" arg in a single-axis shapecast.
RIGHT+1XRightward velocity / right-side shapecast / sprite faces right.
LEFT-1XLeftward velocity / left-side shapecast / sprite faces left.
UP+1YJump / climb-up / ceiling detection shapecast.
DOWN-1YFall / climb-down / ground detection shapecast.
Gotcha — overlapping numeric values. RIGHT === UP === 1 and LEFT === DOWN === -1. The axis is implied by which argument slot the value occupies, not by the value itself. That is exactly why Player.shapeCast(xDirection, yDirection) takes two args: shapeCast(NEUTRAL, DOWN) casts down, shapeCast(RIGHT, NEUTRAL) casts right. Read these as "a signed step along whichever axis the caller is probing", never as a single global direction.
Producer vs consumer. There is no separate producer — these are constants. Consumers are all game-side: Player seeds this.Direction = PlayerDirection.NEUTRAL and builds its four shapecasts from them; applyHorizontalMovement branches on player.Direction to block movement into a touched wall; and the five player state handlers set Direction from input.

Modifying this: these five cover a 2D platformer completely; you rarely add to them. If you ever need diagonals, prefer a {x,y} vector over a sixth constant — overloading one signed scalar for two axes doesn't extend to diagonals.

src/engine/types/playerStates.ts 24 LOC

The canonical same-named const + type file (every other enum here cites it as the idiom). It default-exports a PlayerStates const of five string states and also exports a PlayerState type. It imports nothing. Each state has a handler in game/entities/player/states/; the value drives animation, physics, input handling, and transitions through the generic StateMachine.

const PlayerStates = { IDLE, RUNNING, JUMPING, FALLING, CLIMBING } as const; export type PlayerState

const PlayerStates = {
  IDLE: "idle",
  RUNNING: "running",
  JUMPING: "jumping",
  FALLING: "falling",
  CLIMBING: "climbing",
} as const;

export type PlayerState = typeof PlayerStates[keyof typeof PlayerStates];

export default PlayerStates;

PlayerStates.IDLE is the value; PlayerState is the union type "idle" | "running" | "jumping" | "falling" | "climbing". That union is what makes StateMachine<Player, PlayerState> type-safe — only a real state can be assigned to player.State or used as a handler key.

StateMeaningTransitions to (per the file's comments)
IDLEStanding still on the ground.RUNNING (horizontal), JUMPING (jump), FALLING (walk off edge), CLIMBING (ladder + up/down).
RUNNINGMoving horizontally on the ground.IDLE (input released), JUMPING, FALLING (leave ground), CLIMBING.
JUMPINGAscending.FALLING (jump released / max jump time), IDLE/RUNNING (land), CLIMBING.
FALLINGDescending.IDLE/RUNNING (land), CLIMBING.
CLIMBINGOn a ladder.IDLE (reach top/bottom), FALLING (leave ladder), JUMPING (jump off ladder).
Producer vs consumer. Producer/owner: the Player — seeds this.State = PlayerStates.IDLE, registers a handler per state in its StateMachine, and the handlers reassign State to transition. Consumers: the five state handlers, the generic StateMachine (which dispatches by it), and the Game Director, which maps it to a CameraFollowState in cameraTargetFromPlayer.
Hard-won victory — the camera does NOT import this. It would have been trivial for the camera to switch (player.State). It deliberately doesn't — that's the C5/D3 decouple. The camera owns its own CameraFollowState and the Game Director performs the PlayerState → CameraFollowState mapping. So playerStates.ts stays purely the game's vocabulary even though it physically lives under engine/types/.
Tip — the mirrored idiom. game/types/entityType.ts and game/types/gameCollisionGroups.ts both copy this exact const + type shape. Its own comment notes EntityType "mirrors PlayerStates / CollisionGroups" — one const map keeps the spawn side and routing side from drifting. 
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> IDLE
  IDLE --> RUNNING
  IDLE --> JUMPING
  IDLE --> FALLING
  RUNNING --> IDLE
  RUNNING --> JUMPING
  RUNNING --> FALLING
  JUMPING --> FALLING
  FALLING --> IDLE
  FALLING --> RUNNING
  IDLE --> CLIMBING
  RUNNING --> CLIMBING
  CLIMBING --> IDLE
  CLIMBING --> FALLING
  CLIMBING --> JUMPING

Modifying this: add a state value here, add its handler under player/states/, register it in the player's StateMachine map, and decide its CameraFollowState mapping in the director (default falls to GROUNDED). Because PlayerState is a union, the compiler will flag any handler map that's missing the new key.

src/engine/types/userData.ts 10 LOC

The identity tag attached to every Rapier physics body. A default-exported interface UserData with two strings: type (shared routing flag) and name (per-instance id). It imports nothing. This is the bridge that lets the contact system route collisions by data instead of instanceof — because Rapier callbacks hand back raw colliders, not your classes.

export default interface UserData { type: string; name: string }

export default interface UserData {
  // TYPE flag — shared routing identity; many entities can share one type.
  type: string;
  // Per-INSTANCE identifier; defaults to the type until SetName overrides it.
  name: string;
}
FieldRoleSet / read by
typeShared routing TYPE flag — many entities can carry the same one, so a single contact rule matches all of them.Set by GameObject.SetType (and the spawn default); read by matchesType / isColliderType.
namePer-instance identifier to single out one entity. Defaults to type until SetName overrides it.Set by GameObject.SetName; read for debugging/logging.

The engine treats both as opaque strings — their meaning is assigned entirely by the game layer. Crucially, per-entity numeric metadata is not stored here; it lives as named typed fields on the entity classes, set at spawn from LevelEntity.meta (see levelData.ts above). userData is identity only.

// in GameObject.createObjectPhysics — the one place userData is born

this.PhysicsBody.userData = {
  type: name,
  name: name,
} as UserData;

At creation both fields default to the same incoming name string. The game then calls SetType(EntityType.WALL) (which sets both type and name to the new type) and optionally SetName(...) afterward to give a distinct per-instance id while leaving the routing type intact.

Hard-won victory — type-routing over instanceof. Rapier collision events return a raw Collider, not your GameObject subclass, so instanceof is unavailable at the dispatch point. UserData.type survives that boundary: physicsHelpers' matchesType(gameObject, type) and isColliderType(collider, type) read it, and the Contact Registry rules (declared in game config contactRules) are written as matchesType predicates. That's how the contact table fires "when an A touches a B" without a single hard-coded class check.
Producer vs consumer. Producer: the engine GameObject stamps it in createObjectPhysics and mutates it via SetType/SetName. Consumers: Engine Helpers (getUserData returns a safe { type:"", name:"" } default when the body is undefined; matchesType/isColliderType compare the flag) and, through them, the Contact Registry and the game's contactRules.
Gotcha — SetType resets name. SetType writes the new type to both fields (so a freshly typed entity has name === type by default). If you want a distinct instance id, call SetName after SetType, never before — otherwise the SetType call clobbers your name.

Modifying this: keep it to identity. The temptation to add numeric/gameplay fields here is the thing the design explicitly rejected — put those on the entity class, fed from meta. If you genuinely need a third opaque routing channel (e.g. a faction tag), add a string and a matching matches* helper in physicsHelpers; the engine must still treat it as an opaque string it never interprets.

The big picture. These nine types are the project's lingua franca. Three flow game → engine as plain snapshots (CameraTarget, InputState filled by the game's Player; UserData read by the engine's contact dispatch). One flows engine → game (LevelData from the parser into the factories). Four are shared vocabulary the game owns but the engine folder hosts (ContactPoints, GameObjectType, PlayerDirection, PlayerStates). And one — InputSource — is a behavioural seam: an engine-defined interface the keyboard Input implements and the Player depends on. The discipline that keeps the engine reusable is visible right here: every cross-boundary value is a scalar, string, or boolean, and the one cross-boundary contract is an interface — never a concrete class reference.

GameDirector & GameUtils — the conductor

GameDirector is the single game-layer object that owns every Donkey-Kong rule: it holds the typed entity arrays, wires the lifecycle event bus, runs the per-frame update order, drives the registry-based level pipeline, and spawns barrels off the frame clock. GameUtils is the thin static facade game code calls instead of reaching into engine helpers directly — re-exporting pure math/physics helpers and adding a handful of game-flavoured array/sensor utilities the director leans on each frame.

src/game/gameDirector.tssrc/game/gameUtils.ts

src/game/gameDirector.ts 607 LOC

The "central controller for game state, entities, levels, spawning." It is the one place that knows the game has a Player, barrels, ladders, trash cans (oil cans) and a win flag — the engine layer below it knows none of that. It is constructed once (by the game bootstrap) and grabs the Experience singleton in its constructor to cache the Scene, Time, Camera and ResourceLoader handles it needs. From there its life has four phases: constructor (cache systems + setupEventHandlers), Update (the sim pass — runs inside the fixed-timestep loop, driving player, enemies, level entities and ladder polling), RenderUpdate(alpha) (the per-frame pass — interpolates the moving entities by alpha and then follows the interpolated player with the camera), and Destroy (off its event handlers by ref, unload the level). Almost everything else hangs off events: it never polls "is the game over?" — it reacts to gameStart/gameOver/gameWin/gameReset on the event bus.

Its imports tell the whole story of the seam it straddles: from engine/ it pulls Experience, Camera, Time, ResourceLoader, GameObject, Emitter, the engine-pure CameraTarget/CameraFollowState and PlayerStates types, and the seeded-PRNG helpers seedRandom/randomRange (from mathHelpers); from game/ it pulls every concrete entity class (Player, Platform, Enemy, TrashCan, WinFlag, CameraSensor, LadderSensor, Teleporter), the GameUtils facade, and the two config tables that make it data-driven: ENTITY_FACTORIES (with FactoryContext) and the LEVEL_REGISTRY/FEEL_PROFILES registry.

type UpdatableEntity = { Update?(player: Player, trashCan?: TrashCan): void; }

A structural type used by the uniform level-entity update loop. Update is optional so an array of mixed level entities can be ticked with optional chaining (e.Update?.(...)) — anything without an Update is simply skipped instead of needing a runtime guard. The second arg is the trash can the enemy reads; entities that don't need it ignore it. This is the type-level expression of the engine's "uniform update contract".

type UpdatableEntity = {
  Update?(player: Player, trashCan?: TrashCan): void;
};

// typed entity arrays

The director's whole world is a set of strongly-typed arrays — the "derived query views" the rest of the game indexes by type. Player is a single instance (definite-assignment !, spawned from level data); the rest are arrays. Note the deliberate splits: Platforms vs Walls are both Platform[] but kept separate so the enemy can reverse on walls but ground on platforms; the three ladder sub-arrays (LadderTopSensors/LadderCoreSensors/LadderBottomSensors) are split because the player and barrel ask each set a different question.

public Player!: Player;
// One unified barrel array now — normal and crazy barrels are both Enemy,
// distinguished by their starting FSM state (D28 fold-in of the old CrazyEnemy).
public Enemies: Enemy[] = [];
public Platforms: Platform[] = [];
public Walls: Platform[] = [];
public TrashCans: TrashCan[] = [];
public WinFlags: WinFlag[] = [];
public Teleporters: Teleporter[] = [];
public CameraSensors: CameraSensor[] = [];
public LadderTopSensors: LadderSensor[] = [];
public LadderCoreSensors: LadderSensor[] = [];
public LadderBottomSensors: LadderSensor[] = [];
Hard-won victory (D28). There is no CrazyEnemy class and no second array. Normal and crazy barrels are both Enemy, distinguished only by their starting FSM state (ROLLING vs BOUNCING). Folding the old CrazyEnemy into one data-driven Enemy is what fixed the long-standing barrel/enemy bug noted in the latest commit — one array, one update loop, one cleanup path, no chance of the two flavours drifting apart. See enemy, where State === BOUNCING is literally the "is this crazy?" discriminator.

// level management + spawner fields

The remaining state falls into level-management and spawner buckets. CurrentLevelName is public so the dat.gui LevelDebug dropdown can bind to it with .listen() (it reflects F2 cycling live). currentLevelIndex drives the cycle math; levelLoaded gates the unload-before-load; isLoading is the re-entrancy lock and the Update freeze. The spawner counters are all in seconds (advanced by Time.Delta), not wall-clock.

public CurrentLevelName: string = LEVEL_REGISTRY[DEFAULT_LEVEL_INDEX].name;
private currentLevelIndex = -1;
private levelLoaded = false;
private isLoading = false;

private isSpawningEnemies = false;
private timeSinceLastSpawn = 0;
private currentSpawnInterval = 0;
private enemyCount = 0;

Line-level notes: currentLevelIndex = -1 starts "before" index 0, so the first switchToNextLevel after a boot at index 0 still computes correctly (boot sets it explicitly). playerHasBeenSpawned (declared just above, default false) is the flag that makes setPlayerStart construct the Player exactly once and merely teleport it on every subsequent level — so the singleton Player survives level switches.

constructor()

First seeds the gameplay PRNG deterministically (and logs the seed), then caches the engine subsystems off the Experience singleton, creates the empty GraphicsObject overlay holder, then wires the event bus. No physics body or entity is created here — the world is empty until resourcesReady fires and the default level loads.

// Seed gameplay randomness deterministically (same seed -> same barrel sequence).
seedRandom(RNG_SEED);
console.log(`[FireGame] RNG seed = ${RNG_SEED} (deterministic run)`);

this.experience = Experience.GetInstance();
this.scene = this.experience.Scene;
this.time = this.experience.Time;
this.camera = this.experience.Camera;
this.resources = this.experience.Resources;
this.GraphicsObject = new GameObject();

this.setupEventHandlers();
Note. Pulling everything from Experience.GetInstance() is the decoupling seam: the director never imports or constructs the renderer/physics/camera itself, it asks the singleton. That is how the same director could run under a test harness with stub systems.
Hard-won victory (deterministic runs). The module const RNG_SEED = 1337 is fed to seedRandom here at construction, so every play replays the identical barrel sequence — and resetGameState re-seeds with the same value, so a reset replays it again from the top. All gameplay randomness (spawn cadence, the barrel's take-ladder dice) now flows through the one seeded mulberry32 PRNG instead of the global unseeded Math.random, which is what makes a run reproducible. Swap to seedRandom(Date.now()) for varied runs.

private setupEventHandlers()

The director's nervous system — it listens on the event bus and never calls these flows directly. Each handler is a tiny stored arrow field (onResourcesReady, onGameStart, onGameOver, onGameWin, onGameReset, onSwitchLevel, onGameObjectRemoved) — and setupEventHandlers just registers each one by reference. Storing the refs is what lets Destroy remove exactly these listeners (and no one else's) from the shared bus. There is no wall-clock timer anywhere in the director any more — destroyed-barrel cleanup moved into Update (see GameUtils.CompactDestroyedObjects).

EventHandler does
resourcesReadyawait this.loadLevelByIndex(DEFAULT_LEVEL_INDEX) — boots the default level. Loading emits gameStart on success.
gameStartPhysics.SetPaused(false) + isSpawningEnemies = true — go live.
gameOverPhysics.SetPaused(true) + isSpawningEnemies = false — freeze, stop barrels.
gameWinIdentical freeze to gameOver — so the win screen holds and a stray barrel can't flip "Game Win!" to "Game Over!".
gameResetPhysics.SetPaused(false) + resetGameState().
switchLevelawait this.switchToNextLevel() — the F2 cycle.
gameObjectRemovedremovedGameObject.Destroy() — the single teardown sink for any entity anyone wants gone.
 
// Stored arrow fields capture `this`; they're only invoked on events.
private onGameObjectRemoved = (removed: GameObject) => {
  removed.Destroy();
};

// setupEventHandlers() — register every stored handler BY REFERENCE.
Emitter.on("resourcesReady", this.onResourcesReady);
Emitter.on("gameStart", this.onGameStart);
Emitter.on("gameOver", this.onGameOver);
Emitter.on("gameWin", this.onGameWin);
Emitter.on("gameReset", this.onGameReset);
Emitter.on("switchLevel", this.onSwitchLevel);
Emitter.on("gameObjectRemoved", this.onGameObjectRemoved);

Line-level notes: the gameObjectRemoved handler is the decoupling pivot — entities (or resetGameState/UnloadLevelData) emit "remove me" and the director is the only thing that actually calls Destroy(), so teardown is centralized. Registering each handler by its stored ref (instead of an inline closure) is the bit that makes Destroy able to off precisely these listeners off the shared bus — a bare Emitter.off("gameStart") would wipe other modules' handlers (e.g. App.vue's) too.

Hard-won victory (bus hygiene). Every handler is a captured-this arrow field, so the on here and the off in Destroy pass the same function reference. Registering all seven (including gameWin) means Destroy can later remove all seven — no leaked listener closing over a dead instance on HMR / unmount.

public Update()

The sim pass — run inside the engine's fixed-timestep loop (0..N times per frame, see Experience.onTick), so all movement integrates at the constant step. Camera and visual interpolation are not here — they run once per frame in RenderUpdate, decoupled from the sim rate. The order of the steps is load-bearing — several downstream reads depend on running after the player has moved this step.

flowchart TD
  A["isLoading? -> return"] --> B["Player.PhysicsBody ready? -> return"]
  B --> C["Player.Update()"]
  C --> E["trySpawn()"]
  E --> F["Enemies.forEach(e.Update(Player, firstTrashCan))"]
  F --> CD["GameUtils.CompactDestroyedObjects(Enemies) — swap-remove in place"]
  CD --> G["Platforms: e.Update?.(Player, firstTrashCan)"]
  G --> H["Player.UpdateLadderState(top, core, bottom)"]
if (this.isLoading) return;
if (!this.Player?.PhysicsBody) return;

this.Player.Update();

// Spawn + update enemies, then compact destroyed barrels IN PLACE (stable ref).
this.trySpawn();
const firstTrashCan = this.TrashCans[0];
this.Enemies.forEach((enemy) => enemy.Update(this.Player, firstTrashCan));
GameUtils.CompactDestroyedObjects(this.Enemies);

const updatableLevelEntities: UpdatableEntity[] = this.Platforms;
for (const e of updatableLevelEntities) {
  e.Update?.(this.Player, firstTrashCan);
}

this.Player.UpdateLadderState(
  GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggered(this.LadderTopSensors),
  GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggeredObjectFullyInside(this.LadderCoreSensors, this.Player),
  GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggered(this.LadderBottomSensors)
);

Line-level notes: the two guard returns matter — isLoading freezes all game logic while a level is being torn down/rebuilt (the Vue loading screen covers the canvas), and !this.Player?.PhysicsBody bails before the player exists (between boot and first spawn). firstTrashCan = this.TrashCans[0] is the oil can passed to both the enemies and the platform loop. GameUtils.CompactDestroyedObjects(this.Enemies) runs right after the enemy tick so a barrel flagged IsBeingDestroyed this step (hit the player, lit the can) is swept this frame — no array reallocation, the same array instance the spawner pushes into. The Platforms loop runs after Player.Update() so the one-way-platform toggle reads this step's player state. Ladder detection is the last step and is still polled manually (see the GameUtils helpers) — the comment flags it as a TODO to convert to sensor callbacks. The three arguments map to top/core/bottom: top & bottom use plain IsAnySensorTriggered, but the core uses the fully-inside variant so the player must be horizontally centred on the ladder to climb (the D9 idiom).

Hard-won victory (no per-frame allocations). Destroyed barrels used to be rebuilt into a fresh array on a 5-second setInterval (GameUtils.RemoveDestroyedObjects returned a new array, reassigned). Now CompactDestroyedObjects swap-removes in place every frame — promptly, with no GC churn, and keeping the array reference stable (which levelEntityArrays/teardown rely on). The whole director is now timer-free.
Note. WinFlag is conspicuously absent from the update loop — the comment records that it "no longer updates" because winning routes through the contact table (contactRules), not a per-frame shapecast. And the camera no longer ticks here at all: it moved to RenderUpdate so it follows the smooth, interpolated player rather than the stepped sim position.

public RenderUpdate(alpha: number)

The per-frame render pass, called by Experience.OnRenderUpdate once per rendered frame with the interpolation alpha (the fraction accumulator / dt through the next sim step). It lerps the moving entities between their last two sim states so motion is smooth above the sim rate, then follows the interpolated player with the camera. Render and sim rates are fully decoupled — buttery on a 144 Hz display, no judder on a 60 Hz one.

if (this.isLoading) return;
if (!this.Player?.PhysicsBody) return;

this.Player.InterpolateGraphics(alpha);
for (const enemy of this.Enemies) {
  enemy.InterpolateGraphics(alpha);
}

// Camera follows the interpolated visual position (C5 decouple: plain snapshot).
this.camera.Update(this.cameraTargetFromPlayer(this.Player));

Line-level notes: same two guards as Update (loading freeze, player-ready). InterpolateGraphics(alpha) on the player and each enemy lerps the mesh between the previous and current sim translation/rotation and writes each entity's RenderTranslation — the interpolated position other systems read. The camera Update runs last and after the player's interpolation, so cameraTargetFromPlayer can hand it the freshly-computed RenderTranslation.

Hard-won victory (render interpolation). Splitting the per-step sim from the per-frame render is what removes the old one-frame camera lag for good: the player is no longer "updated before the camera in the same frame" (the previous fix) — instead the sim integrates at a fixed step and the render pass interpolates toward it, and the camera follows that interpolated player. Teleports/resets still snap because TeleportToPosition collapses the interpolation state (prev = current = render = target), so they jump instead of sliding.

private cameraTargetFromPlayer(player: Player): CameraTarget

Maps the game Player into the engine's plain CameraTarget snapshot. The camera reads only these five scalars, never the game Player or PlayerState — so camera.ts imports nothing from game/. The switch translates the game's PlayerStates enum into the engine-neutral CameraFollowState (with GROUNDED as the default for idle/running). Called from RenderUpdate, it now reads the player's interpolated RenderTranslation (set by InterpolateGraphics a line earlier) so the camera tracks the smooth visual player, not the stepped sim position.

switch (player.State) {
  case PlayerStates.CLIMBING: followState = CameraFollowState.CLIMBING; break;
  case PlayerStates.FALLING:  followState = CameraFollowState.FALLING;  break;
  case PlayerStates.JUMPING:  followState = CameraFollowState.JUMPING;  break;
  default:                    followState = CameraFollowState.GROUNDED;
}

return {
  // Follow the INTERPOLATED render position (set by InterpolateGraphics just
  // before this in RenderUpdate), not the stepped sim position.
  x: player.RenderTranslation.x,
  y: player.RenderTranslation.y,
  velocityX: player.NextTranslation.x,
  followState,
  isGrounded: player.IsTouching.ground,
};
Hard-won victory (C5). This single method is the camera decouple. Before C5 the engine Camera read player.State, player.IsTouching.ground etc. directly and thus imported game types. Now the game builds the snapshot and hands it in, so a different game (or a multi-player follow) just fills the struct differently. The five fields are exactly the scalars the camera's vertical-follow easing used to read off the player, so the easing branching is preserved unchanged. See the Camera section for how followState drives the GROUNDED/CLIMBING/FALLING/JUMPING easing.

Level pipeline

Loading a level is a chain: loadLevelByIndex orchestrates — it now resources.ParseLevels the GLB first (before any teardown), validates the optional graphics asset, then unloads the old level and calls LoadLevelData (spawn), which iterates the parsed rows through the ENTITY_FACTORIES registry using a FactoryContext built by getFactoryContext. (LoadGLBLevel is no longer on this internal path — it survives only as a public convenience API.)

flowchart TD
  R["loadLevelByIndex(index)"] --> P["resources.ParseLevel(entry.source) -> LevelData<br/>(parse FIRST, before unload)"]
  P --> V["validate entry.graphics asset is loaded"]
  V --> U["UnloadLevelData (if levelLoaded)"]
  U --> L["LoadLevelData(levelData)"]
  L --> SP["setPlayerStart() + setCameraStart()"]
  SP --> CTX["getFactoryContext() aliases the typed arrays"]
  CTX --> LOOP["for each row: ENTITY_FACTORIES[type]?(data, ctx, type)"]
  LOOP --> RS["resetSpawner()"]
  RS --> OV["GraphicsObject overlay (if entry.graphics)"]
  OV --> FEEL["applyFeelProfile + camera.SetCameraFollow"]
  FEEL --> GS["emit gameStart"]
  GS -. on throw .-> RB["catch: roll back currentLevelIndex / CurrentLevelName"]

public async LoadGLBLevel(glbPath: string)

Thin adapter: hands the bundled GLB URL to the ResourceLoader and awaits parsed LevelData, then forwards to LoadLevelData. Levels are GLB/glTF only (Blender/Blockbench export); glbPath is normally the registry entry's source. No longer used on the internal load path — loadLevelByIndex now parses the GLB itself (so it can parse before unloading); this stays as a public one-call convenience (parse + spawn) for callers outside the registry flow.

const parsedLevelData = await this.resources.ParseLevel(glbPath);
await this.LoadLevelData(parsedLevelData);

public async LoadLevelData(levelData: LevelData)

The data-source-agnostic spawn pass — turns parsed rows into live entities. Stores the data, ensures a single ambient light exists (lazily created once, reused across levels), places the player and camera first (so sensors have a target), then runs the factory loop, then resets the spawn cadence.

this.LevelData = levelData;

if (!this.ambientLight) {
  this.ambientLight = new THREE.AmbientLight(0xffffff, 1);
  this.scene.add(this.ambientLight);
}

this.setPlayerStart();
this.setCameraStart();

const ctx = this.getFactoryContext();
for (const data of Object.values(this.LevelData)) {
  const factory = ENTITY_FACTORIES[data.type];
  if (factory) factory(data, ctx, data.type);
}

this.resetSpawner();

Line-level notes: the factory loop is the heart of the data-driven spawn — ENTITY_FACTORIES[data.type] resolves the right builder by the EntityType string the parser emitted; if (factory) silently skips rows with no factory (PlayerStart/CameraStart handled just above, plus GraphicsObject/Unknown). Each factory pushes into ctx's arrays — which alias the director's real query arrays (see getFactoryContext), so they're populated in place. resetSpawner() at the end means a fresh level starts the barrel cadence clean; whether it actually runs is gated separately by isSpawningEnemies.

Gotcha. Order matters: setPlayerStart() must run before the factory loop because some sensors (and the camera) are positioned/targeted relative to the player. The ambient light is created once and never per-level — UnloadLevelData removes it, and the next load recreates it.

private getFactoryContext(): FactoryContext

Builds the plain struct the factories push into. Critically these are the same array instances the director queries (trashCans[0] for the enemy aim, the ladder sub-arrays for the climb checks) — not copies — so pushing in a factory mutates the director's live state.

return {
  camera: this.camera,
  walls: this.Walls,
  platforms: this.Platforms,
  trashCans: this.TrashCans,
  winFlags: this.WinFlags,
  teleporters: this.Teleporters,
  cameraSensors: this.CameraSensors,
  ladderTopSensors: this.LadderTopSensors,
  ladderCoreSensors: this.LadderCoreSensors,
  ladderBottomSensors: this.LadderBottomSensors,
};

The only non-array member is camera, handed in because the camera-sensor factory needs to wire each sensor to the engine camera. See entityFactories for what each factory does with this context.

private levelEntityArrays(): GameObject[][]

The single list of every level-spawned entity array, returned as GameObject[][]. UnloadLevelData tears them all down by iterating this, so a new entity kind is wired in exactly three places — its typed field, the FactoryContext, and this list — and is never hand-listed in teardown again. The Player is deliberately absent (it survives level switches).

return [
  this.Enemies,
  this.TrashCans,
  this.WinFlags,
  this.CameraSensors,
  this.LadderTopSensors,
  this.LadderCoreSensors,
  this.LadderBottomSensors,
  this.Walls,
  this.Platforms,
  this.Teleporters,
];

public UnloadLevelData()

Tears down the current level. A local generic destroyAll<T extends GameObject> helper emits gameObjectRemoved for every entity (routing teardown through the central sink) then truncates the array via entities.length = 0 — and instead of hand-listing each array, it maps that helper over levelEntityArrays(), so every typed array is cleared from one place. Then the graphics overlay is destroyed and replaced with a fresh instance, and the ambient light is removed.

// emit removal for each entity, then empty the array in place (stable ref)
const destroyAll = <T extends GameObject>(entities: T[]) => {
  entities.forEach((entity) => Emitter.emit("gameObjectRemoved", entity));
  entities.length = 0;
};
this.levelEntityArrays().forEach((arr) => destroyAll(arr));

// Destroy() is idempotent now (latches IsBeingDestroyed), so swap in a fresh
// GraphicsObject for the next level rather than destroying-then-reusing the old one.
this.GraphicsObject.Destroy();
this.GraphicsObject = new GameObject();
if (this.ambientLight) {
  this.scene.remove(this.ambientLight);
  this.ambientLight = undefined;
}

Line-level notes: destroyAll is now <T extends GameObject>-generic (no more any[]) and is fed every array by levelEntityArrays(), so adding a new entity kind never means hand-editing teardown again. entities.length = 0 empties the array in place, preserving the same array instance — important because getFactoryContext handed those instances out as aliases; reassigning to [] would orphan the alias. The Player is not in levelEntityArrays() and is not destroyed here — it persists across level switches and is merely teleported by setPlayerStart. The ambient light is removed and nulled so the lazy re-create in LoadLevelData fires again on the next load.

Hard-won victory (idempotent Destroy). GameObject.Destroy() now latches IsBeingDestroyed on its first line and no-ops on a second call. That makes the long-lived GraphicsObject un-reusable after teardown (a re-load would hit a dead, permanently-latched object), so each unload replaces it with new GameObject() — a clean overlay holder per level, and no never-resetting destroy latch.

private setPlayerStart()  /  private setCameraStart()

Scan the level data for the spawn-point rows. setPlayerStart reads position[0] (X) and position[2] (the glTF Z that maps to the game's vertical) — if the player already exists it teleports, otherwise it constructs the one Player and flips playerHasBeenSpawned. setCameraStart reads all three components and teleports the camera (note the argument order (x, z, y) mirroring the camera's signature).

// setPlayerStart
if (this.playerHasBeenSpawned) {
  this.Player.TeleportToPosition(x, z);
  return;
}
this.Player = new Player({ width: 1.75, height: 4 }, { x, y: z });
this.playerHasBeenSpawned = true;

// setCameraStart
this.camera.TeleportToPosition(x, z, y);

Line-level notes: both loops return on the first matching row — there is exactly one spawn point of each kind. The axis swap (position[2] feeding the game's y) is the recurring glTF-Z-is-game-Y convention used throughout the factory registry too. The player is 1.75 × 4 world units — the DK box.

private resetGameState()  /  private resetSpawner()

resetGameState (fired by gameReset) returns the world to its level-start: re-seed the PRNG with RNG_SEED (so the reset replays the identical barrel sequence), teleport player and camera back to their initial positions, emit gameObjectRemoved for every barrel, douse every trash can (IsOnFire = false), then re-enable spawning and reset the cadence. resetSpawner just zeroes the three cadence counters.

// resetGameState
// Re-seed so a reset replays the identical sequence (deterministic restart).
seedRandom(RNG_SEED);
this.Player.TeleportToPosition(this.Player.InitialTranslation.x, this.Player.InitialTranslation.y);
this.camera.TeleportToPosition(this.camera.InitialPosition.x, this.camera.InitialPosition.y, this.camera.InitialPosition.z);
this.Enemies.forEach((enemy) => Emitter.emit("gameObjectRemoved", enemy));
this.TrashCans.forEach((trashCan) => (trashCan.IsOnFire = false));
this.isSpawningEnemies = true;
this.resetSpawner();

// resetSpawner
this.timeSinceLastSpawn = 0;
this.currentSpawnInterval = 0;
this.enemyCount = 0;

Line-level notes: dead barrels are flagged here but physically swept by the per-frame CompactDestroyedObjects in Update (and the next UnloadLevelData); reset doesn't wait. Setting currentSpawnInterval = 0 is significant — it re-arms the "opening throw" branch in trySpawn (the first barrel after a reset is again Kong's oil-can shot).

private async loadLevelByIndex(index: number)

The single load path. Boot, the F2 cycle, and the dat.gui level select all funnel through here. It applies the registry entry's per-level setup (feel profile, camera follow, optional graphics overlay), and is the only method that emits gameStart on a successful load. The ordering is the key: it parses the new GLB and validates the graphics asset BEFORE tearing down the current level, and on any failure it rolls back the index/name bookkeeping — so a failed switch is a no-op, never a teardown into emptiness.

const entry = LEVEL_REGISTRY[index];
if (!entry || this.isLoading) return;

this.isLoading = true;
Emitter.emit("loadingStarted");

// Remember the current level so a failed load can roll back to it.
const prevIndex = this.currentLevelIndex;
const prevName = this.CurrentLevelName;

try {
  if (!entry.source) {
    throw new Error(`level "${entry.name}" has no resolvable GLB source — is it in src/assets/levels?`);
  }

  // Parse the GLB FIRST, before tearing down the current level. A parse throw
  // (missing/corrupt GLB) leaves the old level fully intact — we catch + roll back.
  const parsedLevelData = await this.resources.ParseLevel(entry.source);

  // Validate the optional graphics overlay is present BEFORE the unload too, so a
  // missing/failed graphics item fails into the rollback while the old level is intact.
  if (entry.graphics && !this.resources.Items[entry.graphics]) {
    throw new Error(`level "${entry.name}" graphics asset "${entry.graphics}" failed to load`);
  }

  // Parse + validate succeeded — now it's safe to swap.
  if (this.levelLoaded) {
    this.UnloadLevelData();
  }

  this.currentLevelIndex = index;
  this.CurrentLevelName = entry.name;

  await this.LoadLevelData(parsedLevelData);

  if (entry.graphics) {
    await this.GraphicsObject.CreateObjectGraphics(this.resources.Items[entry.graphics]);
  }

  this.applyFeelProfile(entry.feelProfile);
  this.camera.SetCameraFollow(entry.cameraFollow);

  this.levelLoaded = true;
  Emitter.emit("gameStart");
} catch (error) {
  // Roll back to the last good level so a failed switch is a no-op.
  console.error(`Failed to load level "${entry.name}":`, error);
  this.currentLevelIndex = prevIndex;
  this.CurrentLevelName = prevName;
} finally {
  this.isLoading = false;
  Emitter.emit("loadingFinished");
}
Hard-won victory (failed-load rollback). Parse-and-validate happen before the unload, and the catch restores currentLevelIndex/CurrentLevelName to the values it snapshotted into prevIndex/prevName. The two throwing steps that used to live after teardown (a bad GLB, a missing graphics overlay) now both fire while the old level is still standing — so a broken registry row no longer drops the player into an empty world with mismatched bookkeeping. LoadLevelData's spawn pass is deterministic, so once parse+validate pass the swap is safe.
Hard-won victory (D5 — the re-entrancy guard). if (!entry || this.isLoading) return; plus isLoading = true is "one boolean, old-school". The GLB parse is async and callers are fire-and-forget, so without the guard a second load (mashing F2, or picking a level mid-load) would interleave teardown + respawn → double entities, a leaked ambient light, and a mismatched index. The same flag freezes Update (top of the frame loop) so logic doesn't tick against half-built state, and loadingStarted/loadingFinished bracket the window so the Vue loading screen covers the seam.
Gotcha (fail loud, then fail safe). Two deliberate error choices. (1) An empty entry.source (a levelUrl() miss) is thrown here rather than letting "" reach the loader — gltfLoader.load("") would fetch the HTML page and throw a confusing parse error far from the cause. (2) That throw is caught locally and only console.error'd — it must not reject out of this method, because the boot handler awaits it; an unhandled rejection there would skip gameStart and leave a frozen, physics-paused game. The finally always clears isLoading and emits loadingFinished, so the overlay never hangs even on a failed load. And because gameStart is inside the try after the load steps, a failed load never wrongly unpauses.
Note. This is the SINGLE place the game starts running. Switching out of a paused game-over or win screen by loading a level can't leave the new level frozen, because every entry point reaches gameStart here.

public LoadLevelByName(name: string)  /  private async switchToNextLevel()

Two thin entry points onto loadLevelByIndex. LoadLevelByName (used by the dat.gui level select) resolves the name to an index via LEVEL_REGISTRY.findIndex and fires-and-forgets the load (the isLoading guard serializes overlaps). switchToNextLevel (the F2 / switchLevel handler) cycles with modulo wrap.

// LoadLevelByName
const index = LEVEL_REGISTRY.findIndex((level) => level.name === name);
if (index >= 0) this.loadLevelByIndex(index);

// switchToNextLevel
const next = (this.currentLevelIndex + 1) % LEVEL_REGISTRY.length;
await this.loadLevelByIndex(next);

private applyFeelProfile(profile: FeelProfile)

Applies a feel profile to the current player by indexing the FEEL_PROFILES identity table (from levelRegistry) and calling the resolved setter (setDkAttributes or setCelesteAttributes) on the Player. Guards on the Player existing.

if (!this.Player) return;
FEEL_PROFILES[profile](this.Player);
Note. Feel is the single source of truth: it is set only here, from the loading level's registry entry — no live debug swap bypasses it. The comment flags a future "a trigger sets the feel" mechanic that would promote this to a public hook. See baseFeel for what the setters actually write.

Enemy spawning

private trySpawn()

Frame-driven barrel spawning, called once per frame from Update. It replaced an old wall-clock setInterval so there is now one clock (Time.Delta) and a fresh boot spawns without a reset. Bails immediately if spawning is off or physics is paused. The cadence: the first barrel is Kong's opening throw (a crazy barrel aimed at the oil can to light it); afterwards a normal/crazy mix every few seconds, every 8th one crazy.

if (!this.isSpawningEnemies || this.experience.Physics.IsPaused) return;

this.timeSinceLastSpawn += this.time.Delta;

// First barrel: Kong's opening throw — a crazy barrel aimed at the oil can.
if (this.currentSpawnInterval === 0) {
  this.timeSinceLastSpawn = 0;
  this.currentSpawnInterval = randomRange(3, 4); // 3-4 seconds
  this.spawnOilCanBarrel();
  return;
}

// Subsequent barrels: mostly normal, every 8th crazy.
if (this.timeSinceLastSpawn >= this.currentSpawnInterval) {
  this.timeSinceLastSpawn = 0;
  this.currentSpawnInterval = randomRange(2, 3); // 2-3 seconds
  this.enemyCount++;
  if (this.enemyCount % 8 === 0) {
    this.SpawnCrazyEnemy();
  } else {
    this.SpawnEnemy();
  }
}

Line-level notes: currentSpawnInterval === 0 is the one-shot opening-throw sentinel — it is only zero at boot/reset, fires the oil-can barrel, then sets a real 3–4s interval so it never re-enters. The interval values come from the seeded randomRange(3, 4) / randomRange(2, 3) (not the global Math.random), so the spawn cadence is part of the reproducible, seeded run. The cadence counters are advanced before the (async) spawn (timeSinceLastSpawn = 0 is set first) so a stutter frame can't double-spawn. The double-guard against Physics.IsPaused belts-and-suspenders with isSpawningEnemies (both are cleared on gameOver/gameWin).

private spawnOilCanBarrel()

The opening crazy barrel Kong hurls at the oil can to light it. Spawns at the normal throw point { x: -13, y: 50 } but aims its bounce drift toward the first trash can so it reaches and ignites it (igniting also consumes the barrel, via the contact table). Drift defaults rightward (aim = 0 → SpawnCrazyEnemy treats >= 0 as +1) when the can is directly below or absent.

const spawn = { x: -13, y: 50 };
const can = this.TrashCans[0];
let aim = 0;
if (can) aim = Math.sign(can.CurrentTranslation.x - spawn.x);
this.SpawnCrazyEnemy(spawn, aim);

public async SpawnEnemy(position = { x: -13, y: 50 })  /  public async SpawnCrazyEnemy(position = { x: -13, y: 50 }, bounceDirection = 1)

Both construct an Enemy, attach its graphics via CreateObjectGraphics(this.resources.Items.enemy), and push into this.Enemies. The difference is the constructor args: a normal barrel is new Enemy(1, position) (rolls, may take ladders); a crazy barrel is new Enemy(1, position, 0, true, bounceDirection) — the true sets the crazy flag (starts BOUNCING), and bounceDirection is its horizontal drift sign.

// SpawnEnemy
const enemy = new Enemy(1, position);
await enemy.CreateObjectGraphics(this.resources.Items.enemy);
this.Enemies.push(enemy);

// SpawnCrazyEnemy
const enemy = new Enemy(1, position, 0, true, bounceDirection);
await enemy.CreateObjectGraphics(this.resources.Items.enemy);
this.Enemies.push(enemy);
Note. These are public (and async) so the dat.gui debug can spawn barrels on demand. They are the runtime spawner — deliberately not entity factories (the factory registry is for level-data-placed entities only); the director keeps barrel spawning as an explicit step.

public Destroy()

Tears the director down: offs every event handler by reference, then unloads the level. There is no timer to clear any more.

// Remove EXACTLY our handlers (pass the ref) so we don't wipe other modules'
// listeners on the shared bus. gameWin is removed too now (it used to leak).
Emitter.off("resourcesReady", this.onResourcesReady);
Emitter.off("gameStart", this.onGameStart);
Emitter.off("gameOver", this.onGameOver);
Emitter.off("gameWin", this.onGameWin);
Emitter.off("gameReset", this.onGameReset);
Emitter.off("switchLevel", this.onSwitchLevel);

// Tear down level entities BEFORE removing gameObjectRemoved: UnloadLevelData EMITS
// gameObjectRemoved, and that handler is what turns it into the actual entity.Destroy().
this.UnloadLevelData();
Emitter.off("gameObjectRemoved", this.onGameObjectRemoved);
Hard-won victory (teardown hygiene). Two fixes here. (1) Every off now passes the stored handler ref (matching the on in setupEventHandlers), so it removes only this director's listeners — a bare Emitter.off("gameStart") would wipe other modules' handlers off the shared bus. (2) gameWin — which previously had no matching off and leaked a listener closing over the dead instance — is now removed with the rest.
Gotcha (order matters). UnloadLevelData() runs before Emitter.off("gameObjectRemoved", …) on purpose: unload emits gameObjectRemoved for every entity, and the onGameObjectRemoved handler is what actually calls each entity's Destroy(). Off it first and the teardown emit would reach nobody — leaking every entity's physics body and meshes on unmount / HMR. So: tear down, then off the sink.

Modifying this: to add a new level-data-placed entity, you almost never touch this file — add a factory in entityFactories and a typed array via getFactoryContext/the field block, and the factory loop picks it up. To add a new level, add a row to LEVEL_REGISTRY; everything funnels through loadLevelByIndex. To change game flow, add an Emitter.on(...) in setupEventHandlers (and a matching off in Destroy) rather than calling logic directly — the director's whole design is "react to events, never poll". To tune difficulty, the spawn cadence numbers live in trySpawn; the barrel physics live in enemy.

src/game/gameUtils.ts 121 LOC

The "game utilities facade — the single entry point for game-layer helpers; game code goes through here, not engine helpers directly." It is a class with only static members — never instantiated, no lifecycle. Two jobs: (1) re-export the engine's pure helpers (mathHelpers and physicsHelpers) under game-friendly names so the whole game has one import surface, and (2) hold a few game-flavoured helpers the director and enemy call directly — array sweeping and the sensor "is it triggered / fully inside?" polling. It imports RAPIER (for the Collider/Cuboid types), GameObject and GameSensor from the engine, plus the two helper modules.

static MoveTowardsPoint / CalculatePercentChance / IsColliderType

Three re-export aliases — no logic, just the facade surface. They point at the engine functions so game code calls GameUtils.MoveTowardsPoint(...) instead of importing mathHelpers directly.

public static MoveTowardsPoint = MathHelpers.moveTowards;     // move a value toward a target by a max step
public static CalculatePercentChance = MathHelpers.percentChance; // true with the given percent probability
public static IsColliderType = PhysicsHelpers.isColliderType; // does a collider match a given entity type

MoveTowardsPoint drives the player's accel/decel ramps; CalculatePercentChance rolls the barrel's take-ladder dice (enemy.ts DecideTakeLadder); IsColliderType is used all over the enemy collision callbacks to match by EntityType. See Helpers for the implementations.

public static RemoveDestroyedObjects<T extends GameObject>(existingArray: T[]): T[]

Returns a new array containing only the objects not flagged IsBeingDestroyed. Generic over any GameObject subtype so it preserves the element type. Returns: the filtered (kept) array.

const kept: T[] = [];
for (const object of existingArray) {
  if (!object.IsBeingDestroyed) {
    kept.push(object);
  }
}
return kept;

No longer called by the director. It used to run on a 5-second cleanup interval (this.Enemies = GameUtils.RemoveDestroyedObjects(this.Enemies)), but because it returns a fresh array — reassigning the field — it could only be used on arrays the director didn't alias out through a FactoryContext. The director now sweeps barrels every frame with the in-place CompactDestroyedObjects below (stable array ref); this filtering variant remains as a general-purpose utility.

public static CompactDestroyedObjects<T extends GameObject>(arr: T[]): void

Removes destroyed objects from an array in place via swap-remove, keeping the same array reference. Walks back-to-front, and for each entry flagged IsBeingDestroyed overwrites it with the last element and pop()s the tail. Returns nothing — it mutates the array. Cheaper than RemoveDestroyedObjects (no new array, no GC churn), and the stable reference is what lets long-lived systems keep holding the same array.

for (let i = arr.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
  if (arr[i].IsBeingDestroyed) {
    arr[i] = arr[arr.length - 1];
    arr.pop();
  }
}

Where the director calls it. Every frame in Update, right after the enemy tick — GameUtils.CompactDestroyedObjects(this.Enemies). This replaced the old 5-second wall-clock sweep: dead barrels are dropped promptly, and because the array instance never changes, levelEntityArrays() and the teardown path can keep relying on the stable Enemies reference. Swap-remove is order-unstable (it reorders survivors), which is harmless for the barrel pool.

Hard-won victory (allocation-free hot path). Sweeping on a per-frame loop with a fresh-array filter would allocate (and GC) an array every frame; in-place swap-remove does zero allocation and runs in one backward pass. It's part of the same Batch-2 push that hoisted the player/enemy predicates and scratch vectors out of the per-frame path — the hot loop no longer churns the heap.

public static IsObjectFullyInsideSensor<T extends RAPIER.Collider, U extends GameObject>(collider: T, gameObject: U): boolean

X-axis-only AABB containment: true when the object's left/right edges fall strictly inside the collider's left/right edges. Computes the object edges from CurrentTranslation.x ± CurrentSize.x / 2 and the collider edges from collider.translation().x ± (collider.shape as RAPIER.Cuboid).halfExtents.x.

return objectMinX > colliderMinX && objectMaxX < colliderMaxX;

Line-level notes: it casts collider.shape to RAPIER.Cuboid to read halfExtents — so it assumes the sensor collider is a box. This is the collider-based sibling of GameSensor.IsFullyInside (which works off a GameObject); it exists for Rapier intersection callbacks that hand you a raw Collider. The Enemy uses it in checkLadderIntersections to decide a barrel is centred enough on a ladder core to descend.

public static IsAnySensorTriggered<T extends GameSensor>(gameSensors: T[]): boolean

True if any sensor in the list currently has an intersection. For each sensor it skips ones without a collider or world, then uses Rapier's World.intersectionPairsWith with a callback that flips a local isIntersecting flag; returns true on the first hit.

for (const sensor of gameSensors) {
  if (!sensor.PhysicsBody?.collider(0) || !sensor.Physics?.World) continue;

  let isIntersecting = false;
  sensor.Physics.World.intersectionPairsWith(
    sensor.PhysicsBody.collider(0),
    () => { isIntersecting = true; }
  );
  if (isIntersecting) return true;
}
return false;

Where the director calls it. In Update's ladder block, for the LadderTopSensors and LadderBottomSensors arms of Player.UpdateLadderState(top, core, bottom) — top/bottom only care that the player touches the sensor at all.

public static IsAnySensorTriggeredObjectFullyInside<T extends GameObject, U extends GameSensor>(gameSensors: U[], gameObject: T): boolean

The stricter sibling: true only when a sensor is both intersecting and the given object is fully inside it (sensor.IsFullyInside(gameObject)). Same intersection scan as above, with the extra containment gate before returning true.

if (isIntersecting && sensor.IsFullyInside(gameObject)) {
  return true;
}

Where the director calls it. The core arm of the ladder check — GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggeredObjectFullyInside(this.LadderCoreSensors, this.Player). The player must be horizontally centred on the ladder core before climbing is allowed, which is what stops you from grabbing a ladder while only clipping its edge.

Hard-won victory (D9 — the "fully inside" climb gate). The ladder climb path relies on continuous polling of the core sensor's containment, not on enter/exit events. As the GameSensor comment puts it, "the D9 ladder 'fully inside' climb path depends on that continuous polling." Both this helper (for the player) and Enemy.checkLadderIntersections (for barrels) implement the same idiom — touch isn't enough, you must be horizontally contained.
Note (TEMPORARY). Both IsAnySensorTriggered* helpers are explicitly marked TEMPORARY — remove once Player uses callbacks. They are per-frame intersection polls; the intended end state is event-driven OnSensorEnter/Exit callbacks on the sensors. They work today and the climb gate genuinely needs continuous containment, so the poll stays for now — but it is the one place in the player's ladder logic that hasn't moved to the event model yet.

Modifying this: if a new helper references any game concept (entity types, the player, sensors), add it here — if it is pure number/Rapier math with no game knowledge, add it to the engine helpers and re-export it here as a one-line alias. Keep the facade total: game code should never import mathHelpers/physicsHelpers directly. When the player finally moves to sensor callbacks, the two IsAnySensorTriggered* polls (and their call sites in GameDirector.Update) are the ones to delete.

App.vue — the Vue UI shell & wiring

App.vue is the single Vue component in the app and the one place where the engine Singleton and the entire game layer are assembled. It owns presentation — the loading overlay, a status line, a Reset button, and the WebGL canvas — while the game only ever signals through the event bus. Every line of its <script setup> is a deliberate ordering decision: listeners come up before the engine, and asset loading is kicked off dead last.

src/App.vue

src/App.vue 188 LOC

This is the root component mounted by main.ts via createApp(App).mount('#app') — there is no router and no other component, so App.vue is the application shell. Its job is threefold: (1) hold two DOM refs (the canvas and a status paragraph), (2) own the loading-overlay and game-status reactive state, and (3) inside a single onMounted hook, wire the engine Experience Singleton to the game's GameDirector, contact rules, debug sinks, input bindings, and the sim/render update hooks — plus a symmetric onUnmounted that tears the whole graph down. It is intentionally the only file that knows about both the engine and the game by name — the seam where the two halves are bolted together. Everything else talks through the Emitter bus.

import { onMounted, onUnmounted, ref } from "vue"; // + Experience, Emitter, GameDirector, InputBindings, registerContactRules, CollisionLogger, LevelDebug, DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS, GAME_ASSETS, LEVEL_NODE_TYPES

The import list reads like a wiring manifest. From the engine: Experience (the Singleton it boots) and Emitter (the bus it listens on). From the game: GameDirector (the central controller it constructs), InputBindings (F-key → game-event mapper), registerContactRules (declares the collision table into the engine), CollisionLogger + LevelDebug (game-owned debug plug-ins), DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS (the wireframe palette), and the two newest seam pieces — GAME_ASSETS (the boot asset manifest handed to Configure) and LEVEL_NODE_TYPES (the level-node→EntityType vocabulary injected into the engine's GLB parser). The fact that only this file imports from both ./engine and ./game is the whole decoupling story in one glance — and the engine no longer hardcodes a single game asset path or level-node name. 

import { onMounted, onUnmounted, ref } from "vue";
import Experience from "./engine/core/experience.ts";
import Emitter from "./engine/events/eventBus.ts";
import GameDirector from "./game/gameDirector.ts";
import InputBindings from "./game/config/inputBindings.ts";
import registerContactRules from "./game/config/contactRules.ts";
import CollisionLogger from "./game/debug/collisionLogger.ts";
import LevelDebug from "./game/debug/levelDebug.ts";
import DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS from "./game/config/debugColors.ts";
import GAME_ASSETS from "./game/config/assetManifest.ts";
import LEVEL_NODE_TYPES from "./game/config/levelNodeTypes.ts";

const webglRef = ref<HTMLCanvasElement | null>(null); const statusRef = ref<HTMLElement | null>(null); const isResetButtonVisible = ref<boolean | null>(null);

Three template refs / reactive flags. webglRef is bound to the <canvas> via ref="webglRef"; its .value is null until the DOM mounts, which is exactly why Experience.Configure(webglRef.value, GAME_ASSETS) lives inside onMounted (line 48) and not at module scope. statusRef points at the status paragraph whose .innerText the win/over/reset handlers write directly. isResetButtonVisible drives the v-if on the Reset button — it starts null (falsy → hidden) and flips true on win/over. 

const webglRef = ref<HTMLCanvasElement | null>(null);
const statusRef = ref<HTMLElement | null>(null);

const isResetButtonVisible = ref<boolean | null>(null);
Note. The status text is poked imperatively through statusRef.value.innerText rather than a reactive string bound with {{ }}. It works because there's exactly one writer per state and the DOM node is guaranteed present inside the handlers; each writer guards with if (statusRef.value).

const isLoading = ref(true); const loadProgress = ref<{ loaded: number; total: number; item: string } | null>(null);

The loading-overlay state, and the most carefully-reasoned pair of refs in the file. isLoading starts true so the overlay covers the screen before any code runs — the boot asset load is already underway visually by the time Vue paints. loadProgress is the per-item count that drives a real progress bar; it is deliberately null except during the initial multi-asset boot load. 

// Loading screen: starts up so it covers initial asset loading, then the engine/
// game emit loadingStarted/loadingFinished around every level (GLB) load. The
// presentation layer (Vue) owns the overlay; the game just signals via events.
// `loadProgress` drives a real bar during the initial multi-asset load
// (loadingProgress events); it's cleared for level loads (a single GLB parse has
// no per-item count) so the overlay falls back to a plain "Loading…".
const isLoading = ref(true);
const loadProgress = ref<{ loaded: number; total: number; item: string } | null>(
  null
);

The shape { loaded, total, item } matches the engine's loadingProgress event payload exactly (see eventBus.ts's EngineEventMap and ResourceLoader.StartLoading, which emits a seed { loaded: 0, total, item: "" } and then advances loaded per asset). Two distinct loads share this one overlay: the multi-asset boot load (textures + GLB models, which has a per-item count → progress bar) and a per-level GLB load (a single opaque parse, no count → spinner).

let webglExperience: Experience | null = null; let gameDirector: GameDirector | null = null; // + seven named handlers

New to this batch: the engine Singleton and the game director are lifted to script scope (not buried as consts inside onMounted) so the new onUnmounted hook can reach them and tear them down on an HMR reload or route change. Inside onMounted they're assigned to these refs and captured in non-null locals (exp, director) used for the rest of the wiring. 

// Engine/game instances — script-scoped so onUnmounted can tear them down (HMR /
// route change). Inside onMounted they're used via non-null locals.
let webglExperience: Experience | null = null;
let gameDirector: GameDirector | null = null;

Sitting beside them are seven named event-handler consts — onLoadingStarted, onLoadingFinished, onLoadingProgress, onGameWin, onGameOver, onGameReset, onGameStart. Naming them is what lets onUnmounted remove exactly these from the shared bus by reference: a bare Emitter.off("gameWin") would wipe every module's gameWin listener, and anonymous handlers can't be removed at all (they'd double-register on hot-reload and fire twice). Their bodies appear in steps 1 and 7 below.

onMounted(async () => { ... });

The entire boot-and-wire sequence. It is async because Experience.Configure awaits Rapier init. The ordering inside is not incidental — it encodes two hard-won "listener exists before the event can fire" guarantees. The flow: 

sequenceDiagram
  participant V as "App.vue onMounted"
  participant E as "Experience (Singleton)"
  participant G as GameDirector
  participant R as ResourceLoader
  V->>V: "Emitter.on(loadingStarted / Finished / Progress)  [FIRST]"
  V->>E: GetInstance()
  V->>E: "await Configure(webglRef.value, GAME_ASSETS)"
  V->>R: "Resources.RegisterLevelTypes(LEVEL_NODE_TYPES)"
  V->>E: "registerContactRules(Physics.Contacts)"
  V->>E: "Debug.RegisterCollisionLogSink(new CollisionLogger())"
  V->>E: "Debug.SetPhysicsTypeColors(DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS)"
  V->>G: "new GameDirector()  (subscribes resourcesReady, gameStart...)"
  V->>V: "new InputBindings(Input)"
  V->>E: "Debug.RegisterModule(new LevelDebug(director))"
  V->>E: "OnGameUpdate = () => { inputBindings.Update(); director.Update() }  [per sim step]"
  V->>E: "OnRenderUpdate = (alpha) => director.RenderUpdate(alpha)  [per frame]"
  V->>V: "Emitter.on(gameWin / gameOver / gameReset / gameStart)"
  V->>R: "Resources.StartLoading()  [LAST]"
  R-->>G: "resourcesReady -> loadLevelByIndex(DEFAULT)"
