Step 1 — loading listeners FIRST (lines 67–71). The three loading handlers are named consts at script scope (shown here with their bodies); onMounted registers them by reference before Configure is even called:

// Named handlers at script scope so onUnmounted can off EXACTLY these:
const onLoadingStarted = () => {
  isLoading.value = true;
  loadProgress.value = null; // level loads are a single GLB parse — no per-item bar
};
const onLoadingFinished = () => {
  isLoading.value = false;
};
const onLoadingProgress = (p: { loaded: number; total: number; item: string }) => {
  loadProgress.value = p;
};

// ...inside onMounted, FIRST — before Configure — so a fast boot load can't fire
// loadingFinished before we're listening:
Emitter.on("loadingStarted", onLoadingStarted);
Emitter.on("loadingFinished", onLoadingFinished);
Emitter.on("loadingProgress", onLoadingProgress);

loadingStarted raises the overlay and clears loadProgress (so per-level loads show the plain spinner, since a single GLB parse reports no item count). loadingFinished drops the overlay. loadingProgress stores the latest count, which makes the bar appear. These are emitted by GameDirector.loadLevelByIndex (the loadingStarted/loadingFinished bracket, the latter in its finally) and by ResourceLoader (the loadingProgress stream). App.vue never emits any of them — it is a pure consumer here.

Hard-won victory — listeners before Configure. Configure awaits async init; if the boot assets happened to resolve inside that await window and someone emitted loadingFinished before these handlers were attached, the overlay would never lift (mitt does not buffer or replay). Subscribing first makes the race impossible. The same logic, one step larger, governs StartLoading() being last.

Step 2 — boot the engine Singleton (lines 74–78).

// Non-null local for use here; the script-scoped ref is for onUnmounted.
const exp = Experience.GetInstance();
webglExperience = exp;

// The game supplies its own asset manifest; the engine names none of these.
await exp.Configure(webglRef.value, GAME_ASSETS);

GetInstance() returns the lazily-created Experience Singleton; Configure is its real (async) constructor — it builds Debug, Sizes, Time, Input, Resources, the Three.js Scene, Camera, Renderer, and awaits Physics.Configure() (Rapier init), then subscribes the engine's resize and tick handlers. Note the second argument: Configure(canvas, resources) now takes the game's GAME_ASSETS manifest and feeds it straight into the ResourceLoader it constructs — the engine no longer hardcodes a randy/enemy/dkGraphicsData import list, so engine/ ships without naming a single game asset. Passing webglRef.value is the reason this whole block is inside onMounted: the canvas element only exists once Vue has mounted the DOM. The instance is also stashed in the script-scoped webglExperience ref (via the exp local) so onUnmounted can later call its Destroy().

Reusability win — the manifest is injected, not baked in. GAME_ASSETS (see assetManifest.ts) is a plain Resource[] the game owns; the engine just loads whatever it's handed. The same engine can boot a different game by passing a different manifest — the old hardcoded ?url imports inside resourceLoader.ts are gone.

Step 3 — the game declares its rules into the engine (lines 82–88). Four injections, each pushing a game concept into an engine subsystem that names nothing game-specific:

exp.Resources.RegisterLevelTypes(LEVEL_NODE_TYPES);

registerContactRules(exp.Physics.Contacts);
exp.Debug.RegisterCollisionLogSink(new CollisionLogger());
exp.Debug.SetPhysicsTypeColors(DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS);

RegisterLevelTypes(LEVEL_NODE_TYPES) (see levelNodeTypes.ts) hands the engine's GLB parser the game's level-node-name→EntityType vocabulary, so the engine can classify the nodes it finds in a level file without ever mirroring the game's EntityType enum — it must run before StartLoading() kicks off the first level load. registerContactRules (see contactRules.ts) populates the engine's Physics.Contacts registry with this game's six cross-entity rules (Enemy↔Player = gameOver, Enemy↔TrashCan, Teleporter, CameraSensor, WinFlag) — the engine dispatches physics contacts into the table but never names a game concept. RegisterCollisionLogSink(new CollisionLogger()) hands the engine Debug coordinator a game-owned formatter implementing the CollisionLogSink interface; Physics routes enter/exit log calls to it (no-op when no sink). SetPhysicsTypeColors(DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS) injects the entity-type→color map (see debugColors.ts) which PhysicsDebug consults to tint each collider's wireframe.

Decision code D3 — the engine never names a type. The injection comment above these calls is tagged (D3). The wireframe palette is keyed by game EntityType strings, but the engine's PhysicsDebug must not import the game's enum. So the game injects the Map<string, THREE.Color> and the engine looks each collider's userData.type up in it opaquely, falling back to gray for unlisted types. D3 prevents the engine layer from ever depending on a game-defined name — the bug it forestalls is a circular/leaky dependency that would make engine/ un-reusable.

Step 4 — construct the game (lines 92–94), built AFTER Configure.

const director = new GameDirector();
gameDirector = director;
const inputBindings = new InputBindings(exp.Input);

GameDirector's constructor calls Experience.GetInstance() and caches Scene, Time, Camera, Resources — so it must run after Configure has created those subsystems. Critically, its constructor also runs setupEventHandlers(), which subscribes the resourcesReady listener that boots the default level. That subscription is the reason StartLoading() is deferred to the very end. InputBindings takes the engine Input device and converts F-key press edges into game events (F1→gameReset, F2→switchLevel, F3→manualCameraControl). The director is also captured in the script-scoped gameDirector ref so onUnmounted can call its Destroy().

Step 5 — register the debug level dropdown (line 97).

// Debug level select (dat.gui) — no-op store when debug is inactive.
exp.Debug.RegisterModule(new LevelDebug(director));

LevelDebug is a DebugModule that adds a dat.GUI "🗺️ Level" folder bound directly to GameDirector.CurrentLevelName. It is passed the director so its dropdown can call LoadLevelByName. Per Debug.RegisterModule, when debug is inactive the module is merely stored (its Init never runs); when active the folder is built immediately. App.vue doesn't branch on debug state — it always registers, and the engine decides.

Step 6 — the sim and render hooks (lines 99–107). Two late-bound seams now, not one: OnGameUpdate runs once per fixed sim step, OnRenderUpdate once per rendered frame.

exp.OnGameUpdate = () => {
  inputBindings.Update();
  director.Update();
};
exp.OnRenderUpdate = (alpha: number) => {
  director.RenderUpdate(alpha);
};

These are the late-bound seams that keep the engine free of game names. Experience's onTick now runs a fixed-step simulation loop: it calls this.OnGameUpdate?.() once per fixed step (so the sim rate is decoupled from frame rate), then — after the loop — computes an interpolation factor alpha = accumulator / dt and calls this.OnRenderUpdate?.(alpha) exactly once per frame, just before Renderer.Update(). The split maps cleanly onto the director: Update() is the SIM pass (input edges, player, enemies, spawn, ladder), while RenderUpdate(alpha) is the per-frame pass that interpolates each body's graphics between its previous and current sim positions and then moves the camera to follow the interpolated player. Order inside OnGameUpdate still matters: inputBindings.Update() runs first so an F-key press this step (e.g. F1→gameReset) is emitted before director.Update() ticks the player/enemies.

Note. App.vue assigns both hooks but never calls them. The engine's Time "tick" event drives the loop; App.vue only supplies the two function bodies. This is the inversion that lets engine/core/experience.ts ship without importing a single game file.
Why two hooks — smooth motion at any frame rate. Splitting a per-step OnGameUpdate from a per-frame OnRenderUpdate(alpha) is what lets physics tick at a fixed rate while rendering interpolates between steps — a rolling barrel slides smoothly instead of snapping a step at a time. App.vue's only job here is to wire the right pass to each hook; the interpolation math lives in GameDirector.RenderUpdate and the engine loop.

Step 7 — game-status handlers (defined at script scope, registered here — lines 48–64, 109–112). Four more named handlers translate game-flow events into UI changes; onMounted subscribes them by reference (so onUnmounted can later off exactly these):

const onGameWin = () => {
  if (statusRef.value) statusRef.value.innerText = "Game Win!";
  isResetButtonVisible.value = true;
};
const onGameOver = () => {
  if (statusRef.value) statusRef.value.innerText = "Game Over!";
  isResetButtonVisible.value = true;
};
const onGameReset = () => {
  if (statusRef.value) statusRef.value.innerText = "";
  isResetButtonVisible.value = false;
};
// A (re)start — boot or a level switch — clears any lingering game-over/win UI.
const onGameStart = () => {
  if (statusRef.value) statusRef.value.innerText = "";
  isResetButtonVisible.value = false;
};

// ...inside onMounted, after the update hooks:
Emitter.on("gameWin", onGameWin);
Emitter.on("gameOver", onGameOver);
Emitter.on("gameReset", onGameReset);
Emitter.on("gameStart", onGameStart);

gameWin and gameOver write the banner and reveal the Reset button. gameReset and gameStart both clear the banner and hide the button — the difference is semantic: gameReset is the player re-arming the current level, while gameStart fires on every successful level load (boot, F2 switch, debug select — emitted from GameDirector.loadLevelByIndex on success). Listening to gameStart is what stops a fresh level from inheriting the previous level's "Game Over!" text. None of these events originates here; they come from contactRules.ts (gameOver/gameWin), GameDirector (gameStart), and InputBindings/the Reset button (gameReset).

Step 8 — kick off asset loading LAST (line 116).

// Kick off asset loading LAST — everything that listens for the boot
// ("resourcesReady" → GameDirector) and per-asset progress is now wired.
exp.Resources.StartLoading();
Hard-won victory — StartLoading() dead last. ResourceLoader deliberately does not auto-start in its constructor (its own comment explains why). If loading began before GameDirector existed, a fast load could emit resourcesReady into zero listeners — mitt drops it — and the boot would hang on the loading screen forever. By starting only after the director (resourcesReady) and the progress listeners (step 1) are all subscribed, the completion event is guaranteed to land. This is the macro version of the step-1 race fix.
Gotcha — ordering is load-bearing top to bottom. Moving any of these calls is dangerous: listeners must precede Configure; RegisterLevelTypes must follow Configure (it reaches into Resources) yet precede StartLoading (the first level load needs the vocabulary in place); GameDirector must follow Configure (it reads subsystems) yet precede StartLoading (it owns the resourcesReady listener); the update hooks (OnGameUpdate/OnRenderUpdate) can be assigned any time before the first tick fires but are placed after the game objects exist. Treat onMounted as a fixed recipe.

const handleButtonClicked = () => { ... };

The Reset button's @click handler. It emits gameReset (which GameDirector listens for to unpause physics and call resetGameState()), then also clears the status text and hides itself locally. 

// Reset button: clear win/over status and re-arm the game
const handleButtonClicked = () => {
  Emitter.emit("gameReset");

  if (statusRef.value) {
    statusRef.value.innerText = "";
  }

  isResetButtonVisible.value = false;
};

Note the slight redundancy: emitting gameReset already triggers the gameReset handler from step 7, which clears the same status and button. Doing it inline as well is harmless and makes the click feel instant regardless of listener order. This is the only event App.vue emits.

const barWidth = () => string;

Computes the progress-bar fill percentage as a CSS width string. Returns "0%" when there is no progress payload or total is zero (guarding a divide-by-zero / NaN), otherwise loaded / total * 100 + "%". 

// Loading-bar fill percent from loaded/total (empty -> 0%)
const barWidth = () => {
  if (!loadProgress.value || !loadProgress.value.total) return "0%";
  return (loadProgress.value.loaded / loadProgress.value.total) * 100 + "%";
};

It is a plain function (not a computed), called from the template's :style binding; Vue re-invokes it whenever loadProgress changes because it reads that ref. The empty guard is what shows a 0%-wide bar the instant StartLoading emits its seed { loaded: 0, total } event.

<template> — canvas, status/reset, loading overlay

The template is three sibling blocks: an absolutely-positioned overlay div (title, status, Reset), the canvas, and the loading overlay. Vue owns all of this presentation; the game never touches the DOM. 

<div class="absolute w-full h-full">
  <p class="text-white">firePlatformerEngine</p>
  <p ref="statusRef" class="text-white"></p>
  <button
    v-if="isResetButtonVisible"
    @click="handleButtonClicked"
    class="text-green-500 mt-2 ml-2 px-3 py-1 border rounded-md"
  >
    Reset
  </button>
</div>
<canvas ref="webglRef" class=""></canvas>

The first <p> is a static title; the second carries ref="statusRef" and is written imperatively by the handlers. The Reset button is gated by v-if="isResetButtonVisible" so it only exists in the DOM after win/over. The <canvas ref="webglRef"> is the surface handed to Experience.Configure — its class is empty because the engine's Sizes/Renderer drive its dimensions. 

<!-- Loading overlay — covers the canvas during any level/asset load: progress bar while initial assets load, plain "Loading…" while a level GLB parses. -->
<div
  v-if="isLoading"
  class="absolute inset-0 flex flex-col items-center justify-center gap-3 bg-black z-50"
>
  <template v-if="loadProgress">
    <p class="text-white text-sm tracking-widest">
      Loading {{ loadProgress.loaded }} / {{ loadProgress.total }}
      <span v-if="loadProgress.item"> · {{ loadProgress.item }}</span>
    </p>
    <div class="w-64 h-2 rounded bg-white/20">
      <div
        class="h-full rounded bg-white transition-all duration-150"
        :style="{ width: barWidth() }"
      ></div>
    </div>
  </template>
  <p v-else class="text-white text-2xl tracking-widest animate-pulse">
    Loading…
  </p>
</div>

The overlay is gated by v-if="isLoading" (a full-screen black z-50 layer over the canvas). Inside, a v-if="loadProgress" / v-else picks the presentation: when a count exists (boot load) it renders "Loading N / total · item" plus the filled bar driven by :style="{ width: barWidth() }"; otherwise (a per-level GLB parse, where loadProgress was nulled by the loadingStarted handler) it shows the pulsing "Loading…" spinner text. The item suffix is itself gated by v-if="loadProgress.item" so the seed item: "" event doesn't render a dangling separator.

Event surface — what App.vue listens to vs. emits

EventDirectionEffect / source
loadingStartedlistenraise overlay, null loadProgress → spinner. From GameDirector.loadLevelByIndex.
loadingFinishedlistendrop overlay. From GameDirector.loadLevelByIndex (finally).
loadingProgresslistenstore count → show/advance bar. From ResourceLoader.
gameWinlistenbanner "Game Win!" + show Reset. From contactRules (WinFlag).
gameOverlistenbanner "Game Over!" + show Reset. From contactRules (Enemy↔Player).
gameResetlisten & emitclear banner + hide Reset (listen); Reset button emits it.
gameStartlistenclear stale banner/button on every level load. From GameDirector.

onUnmounted(() => { ... });

The teardown hook, symmetric with the onMounted recipe. It removes exactly the seven named listeners this component registered — by reference, so it can't disturb other modules sharing the bus — then destroys the game and the engine, in that order. This is the path that finally exercises the engine subsystems' Destroy() at all; it fires on an HMR reload or, if App.vue ever became a mounted/unmounted child, on a route change. 

onUnmounted(() => {
  Emitter.off("loadingStarted", onLoadingStarted);
  Emitter.off("loadingFinished", onLoadingFinished);
  Emitter.off("loadingProgress", onLoadingProgress);
  Emitter.off("gameWin", onGameWin);
  Emitter.off("gameOver", onGameOver);
  Emitter.off("gameReset", onGameReset);
  Emitter.off("gameStart", onGameStart);

  gameDirector?.Destroy();
  webglExperience?.Destroy();
});

Order matters: gameDirector?.Destroy() runs before webglExperience?.Destroy() so the director can off its own bus handlers and unload its level data while the engine subsystems it borrows still exist; only then does the engine tear down its own loop, renderer, and physics world. The optional chaining (?.) guards the case where a half-failed Configure left one of the script-scoped refs null.

Hard-won victory — symmetric teardown, no leaks. Naming the handlers (script scope) and offing each by reference is what makes a clean unmount possible: a bare Emitter.off("gameWin") would wipe every module's gameWin listener, and the old anonymous handlers couldn't be removed at all — they'd survive a hot-reload and double-fire on the next mount. Pairing every Emitter.on in onMounted with an Emitter.off here, plus gameDirector.Destroy() + webglExperience.Destroy(), closes the leak the previous version openly warned about.

Modifying this: To add a new HUD element reacting to gameplay, define a fresh game event in eventBus.ts's GameEventMap, emit it from the game side, and add one Emitter.on here that flips a ref the template renders — do not reach into game objects from Vue. To wire a new engine↔game seam (another contact-rule set, debug sink, or update participant), add it to the onMounted recipe in the right slot: injection calls after Configure, anything subscribing to resourcesReady before StartLoading(), and listeners before Configure when they must catch boot events. Keep StartLoading() the final statement of onMounted. And whenever you add an Emitter.on here, define it as a named handler and add the matching Emitter.off (by that same reference) to onUnmounted so the listener tears down cleanly.

Game Config — the data that defines the game

Seven tiny files form the engine's control panel. None of them holds per-frame logic; together they declare what entities exist (entityFactories.ts), what happens when two of them touch (contactRules.ts), which levels are playable and how each one feels (levelRegistry.ts), which keys drive game flow (inputBindings.ts), what color each entity's debug wireframe is (debugColors.ts), which assets to load at boot (assetManifest.ts), and how a level-node's name maps to a game type (levelNodeTypes.ts). The folder is the seam where the generic engine/ layer (which names zero game concepts) meets the game/ layer: almost every gameplay tuning knob lives here, so you can re-skin the Donkey-Kong rules without ever opening an engine file. The last two are recent additions — the asset manifest and the level-node vocabulary the engine used to hardcode now live here too, so a different game reuses engine/ unchanged and just hands in its own.

src/game/config/entityFactories.ts src/game/config/contactRules.ts src/game/config/levelRegistry.ts src/game/config/inputBindings.ts src/game/config/debugColors.ts src/game/config/assetManifest.ts src/game/config/levelNodeTypes.ts

src/game/config/entityFactories.ts 219 LOC

This file IS the spawn table: a const Record<string, EntityFactory> keyed by the EntityType string (see Game Types — entityType). It replaces the old GameDirector per-type methods (importWalls, importPlatforms, importLadderSensors, …) with one map, so the level loader can iterate the parsed rows and dispatch by data alone. Nobody constructs it — it is a module-level singleton object, default-exported as ENTITY_FACTORIES and imported by GameDirector (gameDirector.ts:23). It imports THREE (only to build the Vector3 a camera sensor stores), GameObject as the factory return type, Camera (handed to the camera-sensor factory), and the seven concrete game entity classes it constructs (Platform, TrashCan, WinFlag, CameraSensor, LadderSensor, Teleporter) plus the EntityType identity table. Its lifecycle is "imported once, read forever" — the only runtime action is GameDirector.LoadLevelData calling ENTITY_FACTORIES[data.type](data, ctx, data.type) once per level-data row (gameDirector.ts:249–252).

Note — only level-data-placed types live here. The runtime barrel spawner (Enemy, normal & crazy — see Game Entities — Enemy) and the non-entity load steps (ambient light, the optional graphics overlay) are NOT factories; GameDirector keeps them as explicit steps. The keys of ENTITY_FACTORIES therefore are the exact list of types the GLB loader can place into a level.

export interface FactoryContext

The bag of typed query-arrays a factory pushes into, plus the engine camera the camera-sensor factory needs. These are derived views: the same array instances GameDirector holds and queries elsewhere (this.Walls, this.Platforms, the ladder sub-arrays). Each factory appends the entity it built to the matching array as a side effect, so the loop populates the director's collections in place. 

export interface FactoryContext {
  camera: Camera; // engine camera handed to the camera-sensor factories

  walls: Platform[];
  platforms: Platform[];
  trashCans: TrashCan[];
  winFlags: WinFlag[];
  teleporters: Teleporter[];
  cameraSensors: CameraSensor[];
  ladderTopSensors: LadderSensor[];
  ladderCoreSensors: LadderSensor[];
  ladderBottomSensors: LadderSensor[];
}

The ladders are split into three arrays (top/core/bottom) rather than one because the game queries them separately: GameDirector.Update polls LadderTopSensors and LadderBottomSensors with IsAnySensorTriggered but checks LadderCoreSensors with the fully-inside test (the hard-won climb behavior, D9, that lives in Game Entities — ladderSensor). GameDirector.getFactoryContext (gameDirector.ts:262–275) builds this struct by aliasing its own public arrays.

type EntityFactory = (data: any, ctx: FactoryContext, type: string) => GameObject

The shape every factory satisfies. data is one LevelEntity row from the GLB parse (see Engine Types — levelData); ctx is the FactoryContext to push into; type is the resolved EntityType string for this row — passed so the shared platform and ladder factories can branch on which variant they're building. The return value is the constructed GameObject.

Gotcha — data: any is deliberate, not sloppy. The old import* callbacks received (data: any) verbatim, and the level rows carry a loose meta bag read untyped. Keeping any here is a true no-op versus the original behavior, not new looseness. If you tighten it to LevelEntity, every data.meta?.[...] read still works, but you inherit the optional-chaining ceremony — the comment flags this on purpose.

function metaNum(meta, key, fallback = 0): number  ·  function metaCoords(meta, key): number[]

The metadata coercers. The GLB parser hands each row a generic meta: Record<string,string> parsed from the mesh's texture name — the engine never interprets a key. The game owns that interpretation here, so each factory reads only the keys meaningful to its type. metaNum reads one numeric value (meta.floor "1" -> 1) with a finite-number guard and a fallback; metaCoords splits a "_"-separated list (meta.cam "3.5_2_-1" -> [3.5, 2, -1]), substituting 0 for any garbage component. 

function metaNum(meta, key, fallback = 0): number {
  const n = Number(meta?.[key]);
  if (Number.isFinite(n)) return n;
  return fallback;
}

function metaCoords(meta, key): number[] {
  const parts = (meta?.[key] ?? "").split("_");
  const result: number[] = [];
  for (const c of parts) {
    const n = Number(c);
    if (Number.isFinite(n)) result.push(n);
    else result.push(0);
  }
  return result;
}

These replace the old value0–value3 positional indices. The win is legibility at the call site: metaNum(data.meta, "floor") says what it reads, where value0 said nothing. Both helpers swallow missing/garbage input rather than throwing, so a hand-authored texture name that's slightly wrong degrades to a sensible default instead of crashing the level load.

Note — the axis remap is shared by every factory. The 2D world uses the glTF row's position[0] as horizontal X and position[2] as vertical Y, while position[1] is the unused depth. Rotation is always taken as -rotation[1] (negated Y-euler). Width/height/depth pass straight through — ResourceLoader already emits height = gameUp(glTF Y) and depth = gameDepth(glTF Z), so there is no second remap here.

const createCameraSensor: EntityFactory = (data, ctx) => { … }

Builds a CameraSensor (see Game Entities — cameraSensor) from the row, reads its cam metadata as a coordinate triple, stores that as the zone's anchor Vector3, and pushes into ctx.cameraSensors. This is the one factory that uses ctx.camera — it is forwarded into the sensor's constructor so the sensor can later call camera.SetBaselinePosition in its ApplyCameraOnEnter. 

const sensor = new CameraSensor(
  { width: data.width, height: data.height },
  { x: data.position[0], y: data.position[2] },
  -data.rotation[1],
  ctx.camera
);
const cam = metaCoords(data.meta, "cam");
sensor.SetCameraPositionData(
  new THREE.Vector3(cam[0] ?? 0, cam[1] ?? 0, cam[2] ?? 0)
);
ctx.cameraSensors.push(sensor);

The ?? 0 guards are belt-and-suspenders: metaCoords already pads short lists with 0, but if the cam key is entirely absent the array is [0] and the missing components read as undefined. The trigger rule (Player enters → reposition camera) lives in contactRules.ts, not here — this factory only wires the data.

const createWall: EntityFactory = (data, ctx) => { … }

Builds a solid Platform with depth, tags it SetType(EntityType.WALL), and pushes into ctx.walls. Walls reuse the Platform class but carry the WALL type flag so collision routing and the debug palette can tell them apart from floors. 

const wall = new Platform(
  { width: data.width, height: data.height, depth: data.depth },
  { x: data.position[0], y: data.position[2] },
  -data.rotation[1]
);
wall.SetType(EntityType.WALL);
ctx.walls.push(wall);

const createPlatform: EntityFactory = (data, ctx, type) => { … }

Handles BOTH PLATFORM and ONE_WAY_PLATFORM — the only factory registered under two keys. It branches on type === EntityType.ONE_WAY_PLATFORM to set the constructor's isOneWay flag, then reads three metadata keys and pushes into ctx.platforms. 

const isOneWay = type === EntityType.ONE_WAY_PLATFORM;
const platform = new Platform(
  { width: data.width, height: data.height, depth: data.depth },
  { x: data.position[0], y: data.position[2] },
  -data.rotation[1],
  isOneWay
);

platform.SetPlatformFloorLevel(metaNum(data.meta, "floor"));
platform.SetEdgePlatform(metaNum(data.meta, "edge"));
platform.SetOneWayEnablePoint(metaNum(data.meta, "oneway") || data.position[2]);

ctx.platforms.push(platform);

Three texture-name keys drive platform behavior: floor sets the floor index enemies/players reason about, edge (> 0) marks an edge where enemies turn around, and oneway sets the Y above which a one-way platform becomes solid. The || data.position[2] fallback is the load-bearing bit: when no oneway override is authored (so metaNum returns 0, which is falsy), the enable point defaults to the platform's own placement Y — exactly what Platform's constructor already assumed. The Platform.Update one-way toggle then reads this each frame against the player's feet (see Game Entities — platform).

const createLadderSensor: EntityFactory = (data, ctx, type) => { … }

Builds one LadderSensor, sets its climb dir from metadata, re-tags it with the specific variant via SetType(type), then pushes into the matching sub-array. The type parameter is what lets one factory serve all three ladder keys. 

const sensor = new LadderSensor(
  { width: data.width, height: data.height },
  { x: data.position[0], y: data.position[2] },
  -data.rotation[1]
);
sensor.SetLadderValue(metaNum(data.meta, "dir"));
sensor.SetType(type);

if (type === EntityType.LADDER_TOP_SENSOR) ctx.ladderTopSensors.push(sensor);
else if (type === EntityType.LADDER_CORE_SENSOR) ctx.ladderCoreSensors.push(sensor);
else if (type === EntityType.LADDER_BOTTOM_SENSOR) ctx.ladderBottomSensors.push(sensor);
Gotcha — the constructor's type is overwritten. LadderSensor's constructor builds its physics body under the generic EntityType.LADDER_SENSOR name (ladderSensor.ts:25), but this factory immediately calls SetType(type) to stamp the specific top/core/bottom variant onto its userData.type. That is why the debug palette (debugColors.ts) lists all four ladder keys — the three specific colors are the ones actually matched at runtime, with the generic LADDER_SENSOR color kept as a sensible fallback.

const createTrashCan / createWinFlag: EntityFactory = (data, ctx) => { … }

The two simplest factories. Each builds its entity from width/height/depth + remapped position + -rotation[1] and pushes into ctx.trashCans / ctx.winFlags respectively. Neither reads any metadata. The trash can is the oil can a barrel must fall into; the win flag is the goal zone. Both behaviors are declared in contactRules.ts, not here.

Note. ctx.trashCans order matters downstream: GameDirector reads TrashCans[0] as "the" oil can the barrels aim for (gameDirector.ts:169, :514). Authoring two cans in a level would leave the second purely decorative.

const createTeleporter: EntityFactory = (data, ctx) => { … }

Builds a Teleporter sensor and seeds its destination from the dest coordinate metadata (dest=<x>_<y>), then pushes into ctx.teleporters. 

const teleporter = new Teleporter(
  { width: data.width, height: data.height },
  { x: data.position[0], y: data.position[2] },
  -data.rotation[1]
);
const dest = metaCoords(data.meta, "dest");
teleporter.SetTeleportPosition(dest[0] ?? 0, dest[1] ?? 0);
ctx.teleporters.push(teleporter);
Hard-won victory — the silently-dropped teleporter. There was never an importTeleporters method, so a Blender-placed teleporter vanished on load: teleporters[] was declared and destroyed but never filled. The factory map made the gap obvious — every other sensor had a factory — and wiring this one closes it. A teleporter you place in the editor now actually teleports, with its target read from the texture name like every other authored value.

const ENTITY_FACTORIES: Record<string, EntityFactory>

The registry itself. Keyed off the EntityType const so the spawn side and the routing side (contactRules.ts) share one identity table and cannot drift. A row whose type is not a key here is not a placeable entity: PlayerStart/CameraStart are handled separately as spawn points, and GraphicsObject/Unknown are simply skipped by the loader's if (factory) guard. 

const ENTITY_FACTORIES: Record<string, EntityFactory> = {
  [EntityType.CAMERA_SENSOR]: createCameraSensor,
  [EntityType.WALL]: createWall,
  [EntityType.PLATFORM]: createPlatform,
  [EntityType.ONE_WAY_PLATFORM]: createPlatform,
  [EntityType.LADDER_TOP_SENSOR]: createLadderSensor,
  [EntityType.LADDER_CORE_SENSOR]: createLadderSensor,
  [EntityType.LADDER_BOTTOM_SENSOR]: createLadderSensor,
  [EntityType.TRASH_CAN]: createTrashCan,
  [EntityType.WIN_FLAG]: createWinFlag,
  [EntityType.TELEPORTER]: createTeleporter,
};
flowchart TD
  A["GameDirector.LoadLevelData(levelData)"] --> B["ctx = getFactoryContext()"]
  B --> C["for each row in Object.values(LevelData)"]
  C --> D{"ENTITY_FACTORIES[row.type] ?"}
  D -- "yes" --> E["factory(row, ctx, row.type)"]
  D -- "no (PlayerStart / Graphics / Unknown)" --> F["skip"]
  E --> G["new EntityClass(...) from row + meta"]
  G --> H["ctx.<array>.push(entity)"]
  H --> I["GameDirector arrays populated in place"]

Modifying this: to make a new GLB-placed entity type, (1) add its identity string to entityType.ts, (2) write a const createX: EntityFactory that builds the entity and pushes it into a context array, (3) add the key to ENTITY_FACTORIES, and (4) if the type needs its own collection, add an array to FactoryContext and alias it in GameDirector.getFactoryContext. To add a new authored value to an existing type, give it a texture-name key and read it with metaNum/metaCoords — no engine change. Do NOT add the runtime barrel here; enemies are spawned by GameDirector, not placed in level data.

src/game/config/contactRules.ts 68 LOC

This file IS the declarative cross-entity contact table — the data half of the engine's ContactRegistry (see Architecture — Contacts and Engine Entities — contactRegistry). It answers "when an A touches a B, what happens?" without a single hard-coded if/else in the physics loop. It default-exports one function, registerContactRules(contacts), called exactly once at startup from App.vue (App.vue:50) with webglExperience.Physics.Contacts, AFTER the engine is configured. It imports the engine ContactRegistry + its ContactMatch type, matchesType from physicsHelpers, the global Emitter, the EntityType table, and the four entity classes it casts to (Player, Teleporter, CameraSensor, TrashCan). Note the deliberate division of labor: an entity's OWN movement reactions (an enemy reversing at a wall) live with the entity; only cross-entity consequences live here.

const byType = (type: string): ContactMatch => (go: GameObject) => matchesType(go, type)

A tiny curried predicate factory. byType(EntityType.ENEMY) returns a ContactMatch that answers "does this GameObject carry the Enemy type flag?" — delegating to matchesType, which reads the opaque userData.type off the Rapier body (physicsHelpers.ts:18). Matching by type string rather than instanceof is essential: Rapier callbacks return a raw Collider, not a class instance, so type-based routing survives where instanceof cannot. 

const byType = (type: string): ContactMatch => (go: GameObject) =>
  matchesType(go, type);

export default function registerContactRules(contacts: ContactRegistry)

Registers all five of this game's rules into the registry via contacts.Add(a, b, phase, run). Each Add pushes a { a, b, phase, run } record; later, Physics calls contacts.Dispatch(phase, x, y) for every contact event, and the registry tests each rule two-sided — (x,y) then (y,x) via an else if — so a rule fires once regardless of which body moved, and the handler always receives args in the rule's own (a, b) order (contactRegistry.ts:37–49). That two-sidedness is why the rules below can be written from one perspective only.

a (side 1)b (side 2)phaseHandler effect
ENEMYPLAYERenterEmitter.emit("gameOver")
ENEMYTRASH_CANentercan.IsOnFire = true; Emitter.emit("gameObjectRemoved", enemy)
TELEPORTERPLAYERsensorEnterplayer.TeleportToPosition(dest.x, dest.y)
CAMERA_SENSORPLAYERsensorEntersensor.ApplyCameraOnEnter()
WIN_FLAGPLAYERsensorEnterEmitter.emit("gameWin")

Rule 1 — Enemy touches Player → game over. A solid-body enter rule. Two-sided dispatch is the point: it fires whether the player ran into the barrel or the barrel rolled into the player. The handler emits a pure signal; GameDirector listens for gameOver and pauses physics + stops spawning (gameDirector.ts:112), while App.vue shows "Game Over!". 

contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.ENEMY),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "enter",
  () => Emitter.emit("gameOver")
);

Rule 2 — Enemy falls into the oil can → enemy destroyed, can lit. The only rule that uses both handler args. It casts b to TrashCan to flip IsOnFire (a public boolean on the can, trashCan.ts:8), then emits gameObjectRemoved carrying the enemy — which GameDirector listens for and calls Destroy() on (gameDirector.ts:136). This is how the opening crazy barrel Kong throws lights the can. 

contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.ENEMY),
  byType(EntityType.TRASH_CAN),
  "enter",
  (enemy, can) => {
    (can as TrashCan).IsOnFire = true;
    Emitter.emit("gameObjectRemoved", enemy);
  }
);

Rule 3 — Player enters a Teleporter → teleport to destination. A sensorEnter rule. The handler reads the teleporter's Destination getter (a RAPIER.Vector2, teleporter.ts:45) and calls player.TeleportToPosition(dest.x, dest.y). The teleporter class owns only the destination + sensor; the "who triggers it and what happens" rule is here, by design. 

contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.TELEPORTER),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "sensorEnter",
  (teleporter, player) => {
    const dest = (teleporter as Teleporter).Destination;
    (player as Player).TeleportToPosition(dest.x, dest.y);
  }
);

Rule 4 — Player enters a CameraSensor → reposition the camera. Calls the sensor's ApplyCameraOnEnter(), which pins the camera's rest anchor to the zone's authored cam=x_y (cameraSensor.ts:50). On a non-follow level the camera eases to that exact spot; on a follow level only the Y is used (the vertical baseline the follow eases around). Note the handler uses only the first arg. 

contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.CAMERA_SENSOR),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "sensorEnter",
  (sensor) => (sensor as CameraSensor).ApplyCameraOnEnter()
);

Rule 5 — Player reaches the WinFlag → win. Emits gameWin. The comment in GameDirector notes the win flag "no longer updates — it wins via the contact table, not a per-frame shapecast" (gameDirector.ts:174–175): moving this into a data rule deleted a whole per-frame query. 

contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.WIN_FLAG),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "sensorEnter",
  () => Emitter.emit("gameWin")
);
Gotcha — enter vs sensorEnter must match the entity. Solid bodies (Enemy, Player, TrashCan) fire enter/exit; sensor bodies (Teleporter, CameraSensor, WinFlag — all GameSensor subclasses with setAsSensor(true)) fire sensorEnter/sensorExit. A rule registered under the wrong phase never fires. The sensors above also call enableContactEvents() in their constructors so they participate in dispatch even though they define no callback of their own.

Modifying this: add a cross-entity behavior by adding one contacts.Add(...) call — pick the two byType predicates, the correct phase, and a handler that emits an event (preferred — keeps this file thin) or casts and mutates. Never put per-frame movement reactions here. Because matching is by type string, a brand-new entity only needs its EntityType registered to be routable. If a rule "doesn't fire," check the phase first (solid vs sensor) and that both bodies enabled contact events.

src/game/config/levelRegistry.ts 82 LOC

This file IS the single source of truth for which levels are playable and how each one is set up. It exports LEVEL_REGISTRY (the ordered list of level entries), FEEL_PROFILES + its FeelProfile type, and DEFAULT_LEVEL_INDEX. GameDirector imports all of these (gameDirector.ts:24–29) and funnels boot, the F2 cycle, and the dat.gui level select through one load path. It imports the two feel-profile setters (setDkAttributes, setCelesteAttributes) and uses Vite's import.meta.glob to bundle level GLBs. No instances, no lifecycle — pure module-level data resolved once at import.

const levelGlbUrls = import.meta.glob("../../assets/levels/*.glb", { query: "?url", import: "default", eager: true })

Auto-discovery of level art. Level GLBs live in src/assets/levels (NOT public/) so Vite bundles and content-hashes them. The eager glob collects every *.glb in that folder as its bundled URL into a Record<string,string>. The loop below flattens the full glob paths to bare filenames so a registry row can reference a level by name. 

const levelGlbUrls = import.meta.glob("../../assets/levels/*.glb", {
  query: "?url",
  import: "default",
  eager: true,
}) as Record<string, string>;

const LEVEL_SOURCES: Record<string, string> = {};
for (const path of Object.keys(levelGlbUrls)) {
  const url = levelGlbUrls[path];
  const fileName = path.split("/").pop();
  if (fileName) LEVEL_SOURCES[fileName] = url;
}
Warning — eager glob bundles EVERY GLB. The glob is eager, so every .glb in src/assets/levels ships in the build whether or not a LEVEL_REGISTRY row references it. Delete unused level GLBs from that folder to stay lean — the source comment flags this explicitly.

function levelUrl(fileName: string): string

Resolves a bare filename (e.g. "TestLevel-Slopes.glb") to its bundled hashed URL via LEVEL_SOURCES. On a miss it logs a loud console.error listing the available filenames and returns the empty string. 

function levelUrl(fileName: string): string {
  const url = LEVEL_SOURCES[fileName];
  if (!url) {
    console.error(
      `Level GLB "${fileName}" not found in src/assets/levels. Available: ${
        Object.keys(LEVEL_SOURCES).join(", ") || "(none)"
      }`,
    );
    return "";
  }
  return url;
}
Hard-won victory — fail loud, fail early. Returning "" on a miss pairs with a guard in GameDirector.loadLevelByIndex (gameDirector.ts:395–399): an empty source throws before reaching the loader. Without it, gltfLoader.load("") fetches the HTML index page and throws a confusing parse error far from the real cause — a typo'd filename now surfaces as a clear "not found in src/assets/levels" instead.

export const FEEL_PROFILES = { dk, celeste } as const;   export type FeelProfile = keyof typeof FEEL_PROFILES

The feel-profile name → setter table. dk maps to setDkAttributes, celeste to setCelesteAttributes — each a function that mutates a Player's movement tuning (max speed, jump power, gravity, apex hang…; see Game Attributes). GameDirector.applyFeelProfile resolves a level's string against this table: FEEL_PROFILES[profile](this.Player) (gameDirector.ts:468). One identity table means data and setter can't drift — and FeelProfile being keyof typeof makes an unknown profile name a compile error in a registry row. 

export const FEEL_PROFILES = {
  dk: setDkAttributes,
  celeste: setCelesteAttributes,
} as const;

export type FeelProfile = keyof typeof FEEL_PROFILES;

interface LevelEntry { name; source; feelProfile; cameraFollow; graphics? }

The shape of one playable level. name doubles as the dat.gui label and GameDirector's CurrentLevelName; source is the bundled GLB URL (set via levelUrl(...)) that ResourceLoader.ParseLevel consumes; feelProfile is the FeelProfile applied on load; cameraFollow chooses follow-the-player vs a fixed single-screen view (passed to camera.SetCameraFollow); graphics? is an optional ResourceLoader.Items key for a detailed-art overlay mesh.

FieldTypeConsumed by
namestringdat.gui label · CurrentLevelName · LoadLevelByName lookup
sourcestring (bundled URL)LoadGLBLevel → ResourceLoader.ParseLevel
feelProfileFeelProfileapplyFeelProfile → FEEL_PROFILES[profile](Player)
cameraFollowbooleancamera.SetCameraFollow(entry.cameraFollow)
graphics?stringGraphicsObject.CreateObjectGraphics(Items[graphics]) — skipped if omitted

export const LEVEL_REGISTRY: LevelEntry[]  ·  export const DEFAULT_LEVEL_INDEX = 0

The playable levels in cycle order. Index 0 is the boot level (DEFAULT_LEVEL_INDEX); F2 / switchLevel advances with (currentLevelIndex + 1) % LEVEL_REGISTRY.length (gameDirector.ts:457), wrapping around. Currently two rows, both DK feel: a follow-camera slopes test and a fixed-camera test. Both omit graphics — the overlay mechanism is kept and ready but unused until a level pairs simple collision with a detailed art mesh. 

export const LEVEL_REGISTRY: LevelEntry[] = [
  {
    name: "Slopes Testing",
    source: levelUrl("TestLevel-Slopes.glb"),
    feelProfile: "dk",
    cameraFollow: true,
  },
  {
    name: "Camera Testing",
    source: levelUrl("TestLevel-Camera.glb"),
    feelProfile: "dk",
    cameraFollow: false,
  },
];

export const DEFAULT_LEVEL_INDEX = 0;
Note — feel is the single source of truth. GameDirector applies the entry's feelProfile only in loadLevelByIndex; no live debug swap bypasses it. Switching levels with F2 thus also swaps the player's feel, deterministically.

Modifying this: add a level by exporting the .glb from the editor into src/assets/levels and adding a LEVEL_REGISTRY row that references it via levelUrl("file.glb"). To author a new feel, write a setter in game/attributes and register it in FEEL_PROFILES — the FeelProfile type then accepts its name. Change the boot level by editing DEFAULT_LEVEL_INDEX. Keep the folder pruned: every GLB there is bundled even if unreferenced.

src/game/config/inputBindings.ts 45 LOC

This file IS the bridge from the engine's raw F-key device state to this game's flow events. The engine Input (see Engine Events & Input) exposes only HELD-state booleans and names zero game events; this class watches IsF1Pressed/IsF2Pressed/IsF3Pressed each frame, detects the press edge (false→true since last frame), and emits the matching game event once per press. It is constructed in App.vue (new InputBindings(webglExperience.Input), App.vue:65) and its Update() is called first each frame inside OnGameUpdate, before gameDirector.Update() (App.vue:71–74). It imports just the global Emitter and the Input type.

constructor(input: Input)

Stores the engine Input reference and zero-initializes the three "was pressed last frame" flags (wasF1Pressed, wasF2Pressed, wasF3Pressed). These remembered booleans are the entire state needed for edge detection. 

constructor(input: Input) {
  this.input = input;

  this.wasF1Pressed = false;
  this.wasF2Pressed = false;
  this.wasF3Pressed = false;
}

public Update()

Per-frame edge detector. For each F-key: if it is held this frame AND was not held last frame, emit the event; then store this frame's held-state into the was* flag. The "store after the test" is what converts a held key into a single edge — without it, holding F2 would emit switchLevel every frame and the level would cycle wildly. 

public Update() {
  // F1 -> gameReset
  if (this.input.IsF1Pressed && !this.wasF1Pressed) {
    Emitter.emit("gameReset");
  }
  this.wasF1Pressed = this.input.IsF1Pressed;

  // F2 -> switchLevel
  if (this.input.IsF2Pressed && !this.wasF2Pressed) {
    Emitter.emit("switchLevel");
  }
  this.wasF2Pressed = this.input.IsF2Pressed;

  // F3 -> manualCameraControl
  if (this.input.IsF3Pressed && !this.wasF3Pressed) {
    Emitter.emit("manualCameraControl");
  }
  this.wasF3Pressed = this.input.IsF3Pressed;
}
KeyEvent emittedPrimary listener
F1gameResetGameDirector (unpause + resetGameState) · App.vue (clear status)
F2switchLevelGameDirector.switchToNextLevel (cycle registry)
F3manualCameraControlcamera debug toggle (engine side)
Note — F4 exists but isn't bound here. Input tracks IsF4Pressed (input.ts:35), but this file binds only F1–F3; F4 is a spare device flag with no game event yet. Adding a binding is a one-block copy.

Modifying this: to bind another key, add a was<Key>Pressed field, init it in the constructor, and add the three-line edge-detect block in Update() emitting your event (which must be declared in eventBus.ts's GameEventMap). This is also the natural home for a future rebinding layer — every game shortcut routes through one file.

src/game/config/debugColors.ts 22 LOC

This file IS the dev-only wireframe palette: a Map<string, THREE.Color> keyed by EntityType string. It is default-exported as DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS and injected into the engine once at startup from App.vue: webglExperience.Debug.SetPhysicsTypeColors(DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS) (App.vue:59). It imports only THREE (for Color) and the EntityType table. The one place to tweak the palette; PhysicsDebug looks up each collider's userData.type in this map while rebuilding the wireframe each frame.

Hard-won victory — D3: the engine stays type-blind. The engine names zero game entity types, yet the physics debug view needs a per-type color. The fix (decision D3) is dependency inversion: the GAME owns this palette and injects it; Debug.SetPhysicsTypeColors forwards it opaquely to PhysicsDebug.SetTypeColors (physicsDebug.ts:36), which stores it in a generic Map<string, THREE.Color> commented "injected by the game (D3: engine stays type-blind)" (physicsDebug.ts:29). The engine never learns that "Player" is white — it just renders whatever map it was handed. This replaced Rapier's built-in debug render, whose palette could only tell sensors from solids.

const DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS = new Map<string, THREE.Color>([ … ])

Thirteen entries — one per visualizable type. Any collider whose userData.type is not a key here falls back to PhysicsDebug's DEFAULT_COLOR (gray #777777, physicsDebug.ts:20), so an unmapped/new type still draws, just colorless. 

const DEBUG_TYPE_COLORS = new Map<string, THREE.Color>([
  [EntityType.PLAYER, new THREE.Color("#ffffff")],              // white
  [EntityType.WALL, new THREE.Color("#888888")],                // gray
  [EntityType.PLATFORM, new THREE.Color("#33dd55")],            // green
  [EntityType.ONE_WAY_PLATFORM, new THREE.Color("#b6ff3a")],    // lime
  [EntityType.ENEMY, new THREE.Color("#ff3b3b")],               // red
  [EntityType.TRASH_CAN, new THREE.Color("#ff9a3b")],           // orange
  [EntityType.WIN_FLAG, new THREE.Color("#ffe23b")],            // gold
  [EntityType.TELEPORTER, new THREE.Color("#c44bff")],          // purple
  [EntityType.CAMERA_SENSOR, new THREE.Color("#3bb0ff")],       // blue
  [EntityType.LADDER_SENSOR, new THREE.Color("#ff8a44")],       // amber
  [EntityType.LADDER_TOP_SENSOR, new THREE.Color("#ffcf6b")],   // light amber
  [EntityType.LADDER_CORE_SENSOR, new THREE.Color("#ff8a44")],  // amber
  [EntityType.LADDER_BOTTOM_SENSOR, new THREE.Color("#cc6a22")],// dark amber
]);
Note — why four ladder keys. Ladder sensors are constructed under the generic LADDER_SENSOR name but immediately re-typed to top/core/bottom by entityFactories.createLadderSensor (SetType(type)). So at render time the three specific colors (light/amber/dark) are what actually paint the ladder bands — handy for eyeballing the top-grab and bottom-mount zones — while the generic LADDER_SENSOR amber stays as a fallback for any sensor whose type wasn't specialized.
Tip — safe to inject when debug is off. Debug.SetPhysicsTypeColors is a no-op store when debug is inactive; PhysicsDebug only reads the map while rendering, which only runs when active (dev build or #debug in the URL). So App.vue can always inject unconditionally.

Modifying this: recolor a type by editing its hex; add a row for any new EntityType you want visible (else it draws gray). This is purely a dev-visualization knob — nothing in gameplay reads it, so experiment freely. If a new entity's wireframe shows up gray, it means its userData.type isn't a key here (or wasn't set via SetType).

src/game/config/assetManifest.ts 21 LOC

This file IS the game's boot asset manifest — the list of art the engine must load before the first frame. It default-exports GAME_ASSETS: Resource[] and is handed to the engine in App.vue as the one argument to Configure: await webglExperience.Configure(webglRef.value, GAME_ASSETS) (App.vue:48). It imports the engine's Resource type (the contract row) and the three Vite ?url asset imports it names. The engine's ResourceLoader no longer hardcodes a single line of this — it just loads whatever URLs the manifest gives it and stores each under its name; a different game supplies its own manifest and reuses engine/ unchanged.

Hard-won victory — the engine stopped naming game art. ResourceLoader used to import the randy / enemy / dkGraphicsData URLs itself and carry a bundled-asset map. That made the generic loader know three Donkey-Kong asset names. Moving the manifest here (reusability mile, #5) inverts the dependency: the GAME declares its assets and injects them via Configure, and the engine names zero of them — the same inversion the contact table and the debug palette already use.

const GAME_ASSETS: Resource[]

Three rows, each a { name, type, path } matching the engine's Resource interface (resourceLoader.ts:29). name is the key the game later fetches the loaded asset by (Resources.Items[name]) — "randy" the player sprite sheet, "enemy" the barrel model, "dkGraphicsData" the level-art overlay; type picks the engine loader (texture vs gltfModel); path is the Vite-resolved bundled URL. The ?url imports are how Vite turns each asset into a content-hashed build URL rather than an inlined blob. 

import type { Resource } from "../../engine/resources/resourceLoader";

import randyUrl from "../../assets/textures/randySpriteSheet.png?url";
import enemyUrl from "../../assets/models/enemy.glb?url";
import dkGraphicsUrl from "../../assets/models/dkGraphicsData.glb?url";

const GAME_ASSETS: Resource[] = [
  { name: "randy", type: "texture", path: randyUrl },
  { name: "enemy", type: "gltfModel", path: enemyUrl },
  { name: "dkGraphicsData", type: "gltfModel", path: dkGraphicsUrl },
];

export default GAME_ASSETS;

Modifying this: add an asset by adding a ?url import and one { name, type, path } row — then read it back anywhere via Resources.Items["yourName"]. The engine never needs to change. Keep name unique; it is the lookup key. To swap art for a re-skin, point the paths at different files — no engine file is touched.

src/game/config/levelNodeTypes.ts 29 LOC

This file IS the level-node vocabulary: the map from a GLB node's lower-cased name token to the game type its factory spawns. It default-exports LEVEL_NODE_TYPES: Record<string, string> and is injected into the engine GLB parser from App.vue: webglExperience.Resources.RegisterLevelTypes(LEVEL_NODE_TYPES) (App.vue:53), run BEFORE the first level loads. It imports only the EntityType identity table, and every value is sourced from it so this map cannot drift from the spawn side (entityFactories.ts) or the routing side (contactRules.ts).

Hard-won victory — the GLB parser is now type-blind. ResourceLoader used to carry a static CANONICAL_TYPES mirror of EntityType hand-synced inside the engine. That mirror is gone (reusability mile, #5): the parser now reads an injected instance field (this.levelTypes) populated by RegisterLevelTypes (resourceLoader.ts:176), and lookupCanonicalType / warnUnknownType resolve against it. The engine never names "OneWayPlatform" again — the GAME owns the vocabulary and hands it in. (resourceLoader.ts:433 resolves a node token via this.levelTypes[token]; :442 walks the injected map's values to warn on an unknown node.)

const LEVEL_NODE_TYPES: Record<string, string>

Keyed by the lower-cased node-name token Blender authors emit; valued by an EntityType string (plus the two spawn points and the parser-skipped "GraphicsObject"). Only level-PLACEABLE types, the spawn points, and the graphics overlay belong here — runtime-only types like the barrel Enemy spawn are NOT placed from a level node, so they have no token. Both graphics and graphicsobject map to the same skipped overlay name so either authoring spelling resolves. 

const LEVEL_NODE_TYPES: Record<string, string> = {
  wall: EntityType.WALL,
  platform: EntityType.PLATFORM,
  onewayplatform: EntityType.ONE_WAY_PLATFORM,
  trashcan: EntityType.TRASH_CAN,
  winflag: EntityType.WIN_FLAG,
  teleporter: EntityType.TELEPORTER,
  camerasensor: EntityType.CAMERA_SENSOR,
  laddertopsensor: EntityType.LADDER_TOP_SENSOR,
  laddercoresensor: EntityType.LADDER_CORE_SENSOR,
  ladderbottomsensor: EntityType.LADDER_BOTTOM_SENSOR,
  playerstart: EntityType.PLAYER_START,
  camerastart: EntityType.CAMERA_START,
  graphics: "GraphicsObject",
  graphicsobject: "GraphicsObject",
};

export default LEVEL_NODE_TYPES;
Note — the spawn points and graphics are NOT factories. playerstart/camerastart resolve to types here so the parser can tag those nodes, but GameDirector consumes them as spawn points, not via ENTITY_FACTORIES; "GraphicsObject" nodes are skipped by the loader's if (factory) guard. So this map's keys are a SUPERSET of the factory keys — it covers everything the parser must recognize, placeable or not.

Modifying this: to make a new node name placeable, add a token: EntityType.X row here (token lower-cased, value from entityType.ts) and a matching factory in entityFactories.ts. Because the value comes from EntityType, the parser, the spawn table, and the contact rules all stay in lock-step. A node whose token isn't a key here resolves to Unknown and is skipped (with a warning) — never silently mistyped.

Feel — tunable movement profiles

"Feel" is the complete set of scalars that decide how heavy, snappy, floaty, or forgiving the player character is — run speed, ground/air accel, the jump launch impulse, rise/fall gravity, terminal velocity, coyote time, jump buffering, climb pacing, and animation cadence. Three tiny pure functions own it: baseFeel seeds the values shared by every character, then setDkAttributes / setCelesteAttributes stamp two distinct personalities on top. Feel is the single source of truth and is applied only at level load, from the registry, via GameDirector.applyFeelProfile — see GameDirector and levelRegistry.

src/game/attributes/baseFeel.ts src/game/attributes/setCelesteAttributes.ts src/game/attributes/setDkAttributes.ts
Note — where these scalars live and how they're consumed. None of these three files contains any logic; each one just writes public fields on a Player instance. The fields are declared in the "Player attributes" block of player.ts (e.g. public JumpPower!: number;), and they are read every frame by the player state handlers: handlePlayerRunning, handlePlayerJumping, handlePlayerFalling, handlePlayerClimbing, and the shared applyHorizontalMovement. So to understand what a number does, you read it in the setter and feel it in the handler.
Tip — the unit convention. player.NextTranslation is a velocity (world-units per second); updateTranslation() in player.ts multiplies it by Time.Delta to get the per-frame move. So a "speed" tunable (MaxGroundSpeed, MaxFallSpeed, MaxClimbSpeed, JumpPower) is a target velocity, and an "accel/decel/gravity" tunable (GroundAcceleration, FallAcceleration, RiseGravity, …) is fed in as rate * Time.Delta — i.e. units / second² — the maximum step a velocity is allowed to change this frame. Every ramp goes through GameUtils.MoveTowardsPoint(current, target, maxStep) (= MathHelpers.moveTowards): nudge current toward target by at most maxStep, snap exactly on arrival. 
flowchart TD
  A["GameDirector.loadLevelByIndex(index)"] --> B["entry = LEVEL_REGISTRY[index]"]
  B --> C["applyFeelProfile(entry.feelProfile)"]
  C --> D["FEEL_PROFILES[profile](this.Player)"]
  D --> E["setDkAttributes(player) OR setCelesteAttributes(player)"]
  E --> F["applyBaseFeel(player)  // shared seed first"]
  F --> G["profile overrides on top  // horizontal + jump arc"]
  G --> H["Player fields mutated in place"]
  H --> I["state handlers read fields every frame"]

src/game/attributes/baseFeel.ts 39 LOC

The default movement attribute set — the values shared by every feel profile, so the common DK/Celeste numbers live in exactly one place and each profile only writes its differences on top. It exports a single pure function, applyBaseFeel, which takes a Player and assigns the shared climbing scalars, the jump/coyote/buffer runtime flags & counters, the shared timers, the coyote-time window, and the sprite-animation pacing divisor. It imports only the Player type (for the parameter signature) — it owns no behaviour and is never constructed; it is called, always first, by the two profile setters.

Note — what base feel deliberately does NOT set. Read it carefully: base feel never touches MaxGroundSpeed, the ground/air accel-decel pairs, or any jump-arc scalar (JumpPower, RiseGravity, FallAcceleration, MaxFallSpeed, ApexHang*, MinJumpTime, BufferJumpRange). Those are profile-only — the things that actually make DK feel like DK and Celeste feel like Celeste. Base feel owns the parts that are genuinely identical between the two: ladder climbing, the coyote/buffer bookkeeping flags, and animation pacing. Consequence: calling applyBaseFeel alone would leave a player unable to walk or jump (those fields would be undefined) — it is an ingredient, never a complete recipe.

const applyBaseFeel = (player: Player) => void

Purpose. Seed the shared half of a feel profile onto player. Parameter. player — the live Player whose public attribute fields are mutated in place. Returns. nothing; it is a side-effecting setter. The body is three thematic blocks.

Climbing (shared)
player.MaxClimbSpeed = 4;       // top vertical speed on a ladder
player.ClimbAcceleration = 30;  // how fast you reach MaxClimbSpeed
player.ClimbDeceleration = 8;   // how fast you stop when you let go

Read these in handlePlayerClimbing: when up/down is held the target is player.Direction * MaxClimbSpeed ramped at ClimbAcceleration * Time.Delta; when neutral the target is 0 ramped at ClimbDeceleration * Time.Delta. Ladders feel the same in both games, so these are shared. MaxClimbSpeed also doubles as the nudge velocity used to pop the player off the top/bottom of a ladder back into IDLE.

Jump / coyote / buffer runtime state (shared)
// Early-jump-ended latch (variable height): reset every jump entry & on ground.
player.EndedJumpEarly = false;

// Coyote-jump runtime flags/counters
player.CoyoteAvailable = false;
player.CoyoteCount = 0;

// Buffer-jump runtime flags/counters (the RANGE differs per profile)
player.BufferJumpAvailable = false;
player.GroundWithinBufferRange = false;
player.WasBufferJumpUsed = false;
player.BufferJumpCount = 0;

// Jump and fall timers (shared)
player.TimeJumpWasEntered = 0;
player.TimeFallWasEntered = 0;

player.CoyoteTime = 0.033; // 2 frames

These are runtime values, not personality knobs — seeding them here guarantees every flag holds a real boolean and every counter a real number before the first frame runs, so the handlers never branch on undefined. Their meanings, traced through the handlers:

  • EndedJumpEarly — the variable-height latch. Set true in handlePlayerJumping when jump is released after MinJumpTime; reset to false on every fresh launch (idle/running/coyote) and on the ground (idle/running). When true, rise gravity is multiplied by JumpEndedEarlyGravityModifier so a tapped jump peaks lower.
  • CoyoteAvailable / CoyoteCount — grace-jump state. Set CoyoteAvailable = true while grounded (idle/running); the FALLING handler lets you still jump if Time.Elapsed < TimeFallWasEntered + CoyoteTime.
  • Buffer quartet — GroundWithinBufferRange is set by the player's downward shapecast (true when ground is within BufferJumpRange and you're descending); BufferJumpAvailable arms when you're in range with jump released; WasBufferJumpUsed + BufferJumpCount are the debounce/telemetry so one press counts once.
  • TimeJumpWasEntered / TimeFallWasEntered — timestamps (Time.Elapsed seconds) stamped on entering JUMPING/FALLING; the coyote and MinJumpTime windows are measured against them.
  • CoyoteTime = 0.033 — the actual grace window, ~2 frames at 60 fps. This one is a tunable, and it's shared: both games give the same 2-frame forgiveness.
Animation pacing (shared)
player.AnimationScalingFactor = 1.6;

The divisor that ties run-cycle speed to horizontal velocity. In handlePlayerRunning it feeds SpriteAnimator.ChangeAnimationTiming(1 / (|NextTranslation.x| / AnimationScalingFactor)) — i.e. AnimationScalingFactor / |vx|. Larger |vx| → smaller per-frame hold → faster legs; the factor sets the overall relationship so the run cycle reads right at each game's top speed (the wall-hit branch reuses MaxGroundSpeed in the same formula to keep the cycle moving while pinned against a wall).

Modifying this: put a value here only if DK and Celeste genuinely agree on it (climbing, coyote window, animation pacing) — anything that should differ between feels belongs in a profile setter instead. If you add a new shared runtime flag, seed it here too so the handlers always see a defined value on frame one. Remember base feel runs first inside each profile, so a profile can always override anything you set here.

src/game/attributes/setCelesteAttributes.ts 47 LOC

The "Celeste" personality: fast, twitchy, high-flying, very floaty at the apex — a precision platformer feel. It exports setCelesteAttributes(player), which calls applyBaseFeel(player) first (the shared seed) and then overrides the horizontal-movement and jump-arc scalars with big, snappy numbers. It imports the Player type and applyBaseFeel. It is referenced by name only through the FEEL_PROFILES table in levelRegistry (key "celeste"); a level opts into it via feelProfile: "celeste".

const setCelesteAttributes = (player: Player) => void

Purpose. Apply the Celeste feel. Parameter. player — mutated in place. Returns. nothing. First line is always the shared base; everything below is a Celeste override. 

applyBaseFeel(player);

// Horizontal
player.MaxGroundSpeed = 25;
player.GroundAcceleration = 120;
player.GroundDeceleration = 30;
player.AirAcceleration = 120;   // air == ground here (no air difference)
player.AirDeceleration = 30;

Top horizontal speed is 25 (more than double DK's), and acceleration is a steep 120 — you snap to full speed almost instantly. Air control is set equal to ground control, so mid-air steering is just as responsive as on the floor; applyHorizontalMovement picks the air pair when its airborne flag is true (jumping/falling pass true). The comment notes these are deliberately equal as a starting point — diverge them in the Feel Lab for floatier or draggier air. 

// Jump arc
player.MaxFallSpeed = 80;       // terminal fall velocity
player.FallAcceleration = 440;  // fall half of the arc
player.RiseGravity = 360;       // rise half (lighter than fall = floaty ascent)
player.JumpEndedEarlyGravityModifier = 3;
player.ApexHangThreshold = 20;  // |vy| window for apex float
player.ApexHangMult = 0.5;
player.JumpPower = 128;         // upward launch velocity
player.BufferJumpRange = 4;
player.MinJumpTime = 0.06;

Tracing each through the handlers:

  • JumpPower = 128 — the launch impulse written straight into NextTranslation.y on jump entry (idle/running/coyote). A huge initial velocity.
  • RiseGravity = 360 — subtracted from vy each frame during JUMPING (NextTranslation.y -= riseGravity * Time.Delta). The apex emerges where this brings vy to zero — there is no fixed apex timer.
  • FallAcceleration = 440 — fall ramps toward -MaxFallSpeed at this rate in FALLING. It is much larger than RiseGravity, which is the asymmetry that produces the signature snappy Celeste arc: you rise lazily and drop hard.
  • MaxFallSpeed = 80 — terminal velocity; also used in idle/running as the constant downward velocity that keeps the player glued to slopes.
  • ApexHangThreshold = 20 / ApexHangMult = 0.5 — near the top, while |vy| < 20, rise gravity is halved, giving a generous hang. The window (20) is wide, so the float is pronounced.
  • JumpEndedEarlyGravityModifier = 3 — release jump early (after MinJumpTime) and rise gravity triples, cutting the jump short. Mutually exclusive with the apex hang in the handler's if / else if.
  • BufferJumpRange = 4 — a generous landing window; pressing jump up to 4 units above the ground still fires on touchdown.
  • MinJumpTime = 0.06 — the variable-height floor; a jump always lasts at least 60 ms even on the fastest tap.
Hard-won victory — the "2× time-scaled" arc. The header comment records that these numbers were not hand-guessed: they were tuned in slow-motion (the Feel Lab), then time-scaled ~2× to feel snappy on the real clock. The rule preserves apex height while halving airtime: velocities ×2, accelerations ×4, jump times ×0.5. The original labbed values were JumpPower 64 / RiseGravity 90 / Fall 110 / MaxFall 40 / ApexHang 10×0.5 / MinJumpTime 0.12. This works because apex height h = v² / (2g) is invariant under v → 2v, g → 4g (4v² / 8g = v² / 2g), while time-to-apex t = v / g → 2v / 4g halves. Same arc shape, twice as responsive.

Modifying this: change values below the applyBaseFeel(player) line only — never remove that first call or you lose climbing/coyote/animation defaults. Keep FallAcceleration > RiseGravity if you want to preserve the rise-floaty / fall-snappy character. To add a brand-new feel knob, declare the field on Player, set it in baseFeel (if shared) or in both profile setters, and read it in a handler.

src/game/attributes/setDkAttributes.ts 49 LOC

The "Donkey Kong" personality and the default the engine actually ships with: slow, heavy, deliberate, with a short controlled hop — the original arcade feel. It exports setDkAttributes(player), structurally identical to the Celeste setter (applyBaseFeel first, then overrides) but with much smaller numbers. It is referenced through FEEL_PROFILES.dk in levelRegistry (both shipped levels use feelProfile: "dk"), and it is also imported directly by player.ts, where the constructor calls setDkAttributes(this) in initializePlayerAttributes() as a construction-time default.

Gotcha — DK feel is applied twice, and that's intentional. The Player constructor runs setDkAttributes(this) so a freshly-built player is never half-initialised (it can move on frame zero even before a level finishes loading). Then GameDirector.loadLevelByIndex calls applyFeelProfile(entry.feelProfile), which re-applies the registry's chosen profile to the same player. For a DK level this is a harmless re-stamp; for a Celeste level the load-time call overrides the constructor's DK seed. The load-time application — not the constructor — is the authoritative one. See GameDirector.applyFeelProfile.

const setDkAttributes = (player: Player) => void

Purpose. Apply the DK feel. Parameter. player — mutated in place. Returns. nothing. 

applyBaseFeel(player);

// Horizontal
player.MaxGroundSpeed = 11.8;   // less than half Celeste's 25
player.GroundAcceleration = 50;
player.GroundDeceleration = 20;
player.AirAcceleration = 50;
player.AirDeceleration = 20;

// Jump arc
player.MaxFallSpeed = 40;
player.FallAcceleration = 80;
player.RiseGravity = 72;
player.JumpEndedEarlyGravityModifier = 3;
player.ApexHangThreshold = 4;   // narrow window = barely-there hang
player.ApexHangMult = 0.5;
player.JumpPower = 24;          // a short controlled hop
player.BufferJumpRange = 1.5;   // tight landing window
player.MinJumpTime = 0.05;

Same fields, same handler paths as Celeste — only the magnitudes change, and that difference is the entire point. MaxGroundSpeed = 11.8 is a measured-feeling arcade walk; JumpPower = 24 with RiseGravity = 72 gives a short hop that clears a barrel but not much more. The apex window is narrow (ApexHangThreshold = 4) so DK gets only the faintest hang — heavy, not floaty — and BufferJumpRange = 1.5 demands a more precise pre-land press than Celeste's forgiving 4. The early-release modifier (3), apex multiplier (0.5), and the shared coyote/climb values are identical to Celeste, so the mechanics are the same; only the tuning differs.

Hard-won victory — same time-scale recipe, DK numbers. Like Celeste, DK's arc was labbed slow then time-scaled ~2× (velocities ×2, accelerations ×4, jump times ×0.5; same apex height, half the airtime). The recorded originals were JumpPower 12 / RiseGravity 18 / Fall 20 / MaxFall 20 / ApexHang 2×0.5 / MinJumpTime 0.1. Doubling JumpPower 12 → 24 and quadrupling RiseGravity 18 → 72 keeps the identical hop height while making the controls feel responsive on the real frame clock.

Modifying this: this is the live default, so test changes here against the actual game, not just the Feel Lab. Keep the constructor seed and the registry profile in sync conceptually — if you want a different construction-time default, change the call in player.ts initializePlayerAttributes(), but the real authority is the level's feelProfile.

Base vs DK vs Celeste — every tunable, side by side

"—" means base feel does not set that field (it is profile-only). Where base feel sets a value, DK and Celeste inherit it unless they explicitly override (none of them override the shared ones today).

FieldbaseFeelDKCelestePhysical meaning & where it's read
Horizontal movement — applyHorizontalMovement
MaxGroundSpeed—11.825Top horizontal speed (target velocity). Celeste is >2× faster.
GroundAcceleration—50120How fast you reach top speed on the floor.
GroundDeceleration—2030How fast you stop on the floor (neutral input).
AirAcceleration—50120Mid-air steering accel (here = ground, by design).
AirDeceleration—2030Mid-air stop accel (here = ground, by design).
Jump arc — handlePlayerJumping / handlePlayerFalling
JumpPower—24128Upward launch velocity stamped into vy on jump entry.
RiseGravity—72360Decel applied while rising; apex is where it zeroes vy.
FallAcceleration—80440Accel toward terminal velocity while falling (> rise = snappy).
MaxFallSpeed—4080Terminal fall velocity; also the slope-glue downward velocity.
JumpEndedEarlyGravityModifier—33Rise-gravity multiplier when jump released early (variable height).
ApexHangThreshold—420|vy| window for the apex float. Wide = floaty (Celeste).
ApexHangMult—0.50.5Rise-gravity multiplier inside the apex window.
MinJumpTime—0.050.06Minimum jump duration; the variable-height floor (seconds).
Forgiveness — coyote & buffer
CoyoteTime0.0330.0330.033Post-ledge grace window to still jump (~2 frames). Shared.
BufferJumpRange—1.54Pre-land distance at which a jump press is remembered.
Climbing — handlePlayerClimbing (all shared)
MaxClimbSpeed444Top vertical speed on a ladder.
ClimbAcceleration303030How fast you reach climb speed.
ClimbDeceleration888How fast you stop climbing.
Presentation
AnimationScalingFactor1.61.61.6Divisor tying run-cycle speed to |vx|. Shared.
Runtime flags / counters / timers (seeded in baseFeel, mutated by handlers)
EndedJumpEarlyfalsefalsefalseVariable-height latch.
CoyoteAvailable / CoyoteCountfalse / 0↑↑Coyote grace state & tally.
BufferJumpAvailable / GroundWithinBufferRange / WasBufferJumpUsed / BufferJumpCountfalse / false / false / 0↑↑Buffer-jump arming, range flag, debounce, tally.
TimeJumpWasEntered / TimeFallWasEntered0 / 0↑↑Entry timestamps for the MinJump / coyote windows.
Note — feel is the single source of truth, applied only at level load. GameDirector.applyFeelProfile(profile) is the one place feel is set: it looks the profile up in FEEL_PROFILES and calls the setter on the current player — FEEL_PROFILES[profile](this.Player). There is no live debug swap that bypasses it; the dat.gui PlayerDebug can nudge individual fields for experimentation, but the authoritative profile is whatever the loading level's registry row declares. The method comment even flags the future hook: a "trigger sets the feel" mechanic would promote applyFeelProfile from private to a public entry point.
Gotcha — load order matters. applyFeelProfile guards on this.Player existing, and inside loadLevelByIndex it runs after setPlayerStart() has spawned (or teleported) the player and after the GLB has parsed — so the player object is always present when the profile is stamped. The whole load path is serialized by the isLoading re-entrancy lock (decision code D5): without it, mashing F2 or the level dropdown mid-load could interleave teardown and respawn and apply a profile to a half-built player. Feel application rides safely inside that single-flight window.

Player — body, movement & sprites

Randy, the hero. Player is the one entity that fuses a Rapier kinematic character controller, a Three.js animated sprite, the generic finite-state-machine, and the tunable "feel" attributes into a single GameObject subclass. It owns its movement State, snapshots input as data each frame, drives itself through five state handlers, and feeds the contact table from BOTH sides of every collision. The two helpers beside it are deliberately tiny: applyHorizontalMovement is the shared accel/decel/clamp the run/jump/fall handlers all call, and spriteAnimations is the flat lookup table of sprite-sheet frames keyed by state + facing.

src/game/entities/player/player.ts src/game/entities/player/applyHorizontalMovement.ts src/game/entities/player/spriteAnimations.ts

src/game/entities/player/player.ts 508 LOC

This file IS the playable character. It extends GameObject (see Engine Entities), so it inherits the rigid-body/mesh marriage, the transform sync, the collision-group helpers and Destroy; what it adds on top is everything Donkey-Kong-specific about a controllable hero. It is constructed by the entity factories (the game's spawn registry), never by the engine. Its imports tell the whole story: engine singletons it caches (Time, the input device — held behind the InputSource interface, not the concrete keyboard device — ResourceLoader, Debug), the generic StateMachine it runs on, the five handlePlayer* state functions it dispatches to (see Player States), SpriteAnimator + the local SpriteAnimations table for rendering, setDkAttributes for feel (see Feel), GameUtils for collider-type checks, the local CollisionGroups/EntityType vocabulary, and Platform so shapecasts can read named fields. (It no longer imports Emitter — it does not announce its own removal; see Destroy().)

Lifecycle: the constructor wires attributes → sprite → character-controller → physics body → sprite mesh → collision groups → debug. Each frame the owner (the GameDirector) calls Update(), which snapshots input, syncs the mesh, runs one FSM tick, moves the kinematic body, and re-senses ground/walls. Destroy() frees the character controller and sprite animator, then defers to GameObject.Destroy() — it deliberately does not emit its own removal (see below).

Note on the names. NextTranslation is a velocity in units/second, not a position delta — updateTranslation() multiplies it by Time.Delta to get the per-frame move. CurrentPosition is the live body translation copied out for handlers/debug, and CurrentTranslation (inherited from GameObject) is the same value mirrored onto the mesh. Keep the trio straight and the rest of the file reads cleanly.

public State / Direction / CurrentPosition / NextTranslation / IsTouching / CurrentFloor (public state fields)

The public surface is wide because the state handlers in Player States mutate it directly — the Player object IS the handlers' shared blackboard. The load-bearing fields:

FieldTypeMeaning
StatePlayerStateCurrent FSM state string (idle/running/jumping/falling/climbing). Handlers reassign it to transition.
DirectionnumberSigned facing/move axis from PlayerDirection (-1/0/+1): feeds velocity, shapecast direction, and sprite flip.
CurrentPositionRAPIER.Vector2Live body translation, refreshed at the top of updateTranslation().
NextTranslationRAPIER.Vector2Desired velocity (x = horizontal, y = vertical) the handlers write; consumed by the character controller.
IsTouchingContactPointsPer-frame contact booleans: ground/ceiling/leftSide/rightSide/edgePlatform/ladderTop/ladderCore/ladderBottom.
CurrentFloornumberFloor level of the platform currently underfoot (read from the Platform we stand on).
InitialTranslationRAPIER.Vector2(inherited from GameObject) the spawn coordinate, cached by createObjectPhysics for respawn/teleport math.

Below those sit two clusters of feel attributes — climb (MaxClimbSpeed, ClimbAcceleration, ClimbDeceleration), ground/air (MaxGroundSpeed shared, plus separate Ground*/Air* accel/decel), and the jump arc/timing block (JumpPower, RiseGravity, FallAcceleration, MaxFallSpeed, apex-hang, coyote/buffer counters and timers). All of them are declared here with the definite-assignment ! and filled in by setDkAttributes, not in this file — the seam that lets the Feel Lab swap a whole profile (DK vs Celeste) without touching the player.

public constructor(size: { width: number; height: number }, position: { x: number; y: number })
