Builds the entire player from a bounding-box size and a spawn point. There are no other parameters — feel and identity come from elsewhere. Order matters here; the sequence is deliberate. 

super();

this.initializePlayerAttributes();
this.setSpriteAnimator();
this.setCharacterController();

this.createObjectPhysics(
  EntityType.PLAYER,
  GameObjectType.CUBE,
  size,
  position,
  0,
  RAPIER.RigidBodyDesc.kinematicPositionBased().lockRotations()
);

this.createSpriteGraphics();

this.setCollisionGroup(CollisionGroups.PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX, 0);
this.setCollisionMask(CollisionGroups.PLATFORM | CollisionGroups.ENEMY, 0);

Line-level notes:

  • super() runs GameObject.initializeAttributes() — grabs Experience, Scene, Physics and zeroes the transform vectors — before any player code touches this.experience.
  • initializePlayerAttributes() must run first because it both caches singletons and builds the FSM and IsTouching map the rest of the constructor and every frame rely on.
  • setSpriteAnimator() runs before createSpriteGraphics() because the latter reads the SpriteMaterial the animator produced.
  • setCharacterController() creates the KCC before createObjectPhysics makes the body — the controller is world-attached and independent of the body, so it can be built first.
  • createObjectPhysics (in GameObject) is handed EntityType.PLAYER as the routing name/type, GameObjectType.CUBE so the collider becomes a cuboid, and a kinematicPositionBased().lockRotations() body — kinematic because the player is driven by the character controller, not by forces, and rotation-locked so a humanoid never tips over.
  • createSpriteGraphics() must come after createObjectPhysics so there is a body translation to sync the new sprite mesh to.
  • The two collision calls run after the body exists (both early-return if PhysicsBody is missing): the player's collider 0 is tagged group PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX and masked to collide with PLATFORM | ENEMY only.
// Enable collision events on the bounding box so it feeds the contact system
if (this.PhysicsBody) {
  this.PhysicsBody.collider(0).setActiveEvents(RAPIER.ActiveEvents.COLLISION_EVENTS);
}

if (this.experience.Debug.IsActive) {
  this.Debug = this.experience.Debug;
  this.Debug.RegisterModule(new PlayerDebug(this));
}

GameObject.enablePhysicsEvents() only auto-arms colliders that define OnCollisionEnter/Exit callbacks. The player deliberately defines none — it routes through the declarative contact table instead — so the constructor must arm collision events on collider 0 by hand, otherwise Rapier would emit nothing and Enemy↔Player death would never fire. The debug block is the engine's inversion seam: the game hands its PlayerDebug module to the engine's debug coordinator; the engine never imports it.

Hard-won victory (collider/death rework). The header comment on the collision block records that the old 62.5% hit-box sensor and its per-frame poll were deleted. Death now fires from the full bounding box via the contact table's "any touch registers" rule (Enemy↔Player ⇒ game over). One collider, one source of truth — re-add a smaller sensor only if a stomp mechanic ever needs it.

private initializePlayerAttributes(): void

Caches the engine singletons, seeds the per-entity input snapshot, sets the starting state/direction, applies the DK feel profile, builds the FSM, and zeroes the physics/contact fields. This is where the FSM state map and the IsTouching shape are defined. 

this.Time = this.experience.Time;
this.inputDevice = this.experience.Input;
this.Resources = this.experience.Resources;

// Per-entity input snapshot, refreshed from the device each frame (D10:
// input-as-data — a network/replay/AI source could fill this instead).
this.Input = { isLeft: false, isRight: false, /* ...nine flags... */ isNeitherUpDown: true };

this.State = PlayerStates.IDLE;
this.Direction = PlayerDirection.NEUTRAL;

setDkAttributes(this);

this.fsm = new StateMachine<Player, PlayerState>(this, {
  [PlayerStates.IDLE]: handlePlayerIdle,
  [PlayerStates.RUNNING]: handlePlayerRunning,
  [PlayerStates.FALLING]: handlePlayerFalling,
  [PlayerStates.JUMPING]: handlePlayerJumping,
  [PlayerStates.CLIMBING]: handlePlayerClimbing,
});
D10 — input as data. The player does not read the live keyboard inside its handlers. It owns an InputState struct and the device fills it each frame via CaptureInto. Decoupling intent (the nine booleans) from the device is the single seam that lets a network packet, a replay file, or an AI controller drive the exact same handlers — see Engine Input. The seam is now explicit in the type system too: inputDevice is typed InputSource — the one-method interface { CaptureInto(state: InputState): void } — not the concrete keyboard Input class. The player depends only on the interface, so a touch / gamepad / network / replay source drops in with zero Player changes.

Note setDkAttributes(this) mutates the player in place, writing every feel field declared above. The FSM is constructed with this as the host and a frozen map from state string → handler; the StateMachine never owns the state, the player does (the host's State field is the source of truth). The tail seeds physics constants and the contact map: 

this.ColliderOffset = 0.01;
// Increased threshold significantly for better slope detection and prevent micro-falling
this.ColliderOffsetThreshold = this.ColliderOffset + 0.015;
this.CurrentPosition = new RAPIER.Vector2(0, 0);
this.NextTranslation = new RAPIER.Vector2(0, 0);
this.CurrentFloor = 0;

this.IsTouching = {
  ground: false, ceiling: false, leftSide: false, rightSide: false,
  edgePlatform: false, ladderCore: false, ladderTop: false, ladderBottom: false,
};

ColliderOffset (0.01) is the KCC skin gap; ColliderOffsetThreshold (0.025) is the shapecast time-of-impact cutoff that counts as "touching" — bumped above the raw offset specifically to keep the player glued to the ground on downslopes and stop one-pixel micro-falls.

private setSpriteAnimator(): void

Creates the sprite-sheet animator from the loaded randy texture, declares the sheet layout, sets the opening animation, and registers the resulting material on the base class. 

this.SpriteAnimator = new SpriteAnimator(this.Resources.Items.randy as THREE.Texture, 4, 6);
this.SpriteAnimator.State = SpriteAnimations.IDLE_RIGHT;
this.setMaterial(this.SpriteAnimator.Material, 4);

The 4, 6 are the sheet's tile grid — 4 columns × 6 rows = 24 tiles — which the animator uses to compute UV offsets (see Engine Rendering → SpriteAnimator). The initial State is set directly (the very first frame), and setMaterial(..., 4) stores the animator's material on GameObject with a sprite scale of 4 for createSpriteGraphics to apply.

private createSpriteGraphics(): void

Builds the actual THREE.Sprite mesh from the animator's material, scales it, adds it to the scene, and snaps it onto the body's transform.

this.mesh = new THREE.Sprite(this.material as THREE.SpriteMaterial);
if (this.spriteScale) {
  this.mesh.scale.set(this.spriteScale, this.spriteScale, this.spriteScale);
}
this.scene.add(this.mesh);
this.syncGraphicsToPhysics();

A THREE.Sprite always faces the camera — exactly right for a 2D billboarded character in a 3D WebGL scene. The trailing syncGraphicsToPhysics() (from GameObject) copies the body's translation onto the fresh mesh so it appears at the spawn point on frame zero, not at the origin.

private setCharacterController(): void

Creates the player's own Rapier KinematicCharacterController and tunes its slope/step behaviour. The KCC is what turns a desired velocity into a collision-aware move. 

this.CharacterController = this.Physics.World.createCharacterController(this.ColliderOffset);
this.CharacterController.enableSnapToGround(0.02);
this.CharacterController.enableAutostep(0.5, 0.2, true);
this.CharacterController.setMaxSlopeClimbAngle((45 * Math.PI) / 180);
this.CharacterController.setMinSlopeSlideAngle((30 * Math.PI) / 180);
CallEffect
createCharacterController(0.01)KCC with a 0.01 skin gap (= ColliderOffset).
enableSnapToGround(0.02)Snap down to ground within 0.02 units — kills micro-falling on slopes.
enableAutostep(0.5, 0.2, true)Step up ledges ≤ 0.5 tall / ≥ 0.2 wide, including onto dynamic bodies.
setMaxSlopeClimbAngle(45°)Refuse to climb slopes steeper than 45°.
setMinSlopeSlideAngle(30°)Auto-slide down slopes shallower than 30°.

This controller is created here (not in Physics) precisely because it is the player's alone, and it must be removed by hand in Destroy() — Rapier does not free it when the body goes.

public Update(): void

The per-frame entry point the GameDirector calls. Five steps in a fixed order; reordering them breaks things.

if (this.IsBeingDestroyed) {
  return;
}

// Refresh this entity's input snapshot before the state handlers read it.
this.inputDevice.CaptureInto(this.Input);

this.syncGraphicsToPhysics();
this.updatePlayerState();
this.updateTranslation();
this.detectCollisions();
flowchart TD
  A["Player.Update()"] --> B{"IsBeingDestroyed?"}
  B -- yes --> Z["return"]
  B -- no --> C["inputDevice.CaptureInto(Input)"]
  C --> D["syncGraphicsToPhysics() (mesh = last body pose)"]
  D --> E["updatePlayerState()"]
  E --> E1["fsm.Update() -> handlePlayer*(player)"]
  E1 --> E2["SpriteAnimator.Update(Time.Delta)"]
  E2 --> F["updateTranslation() (move KCC + diff contact edges)"]
  F --> G["detectCollisions() (reset + shapecast IsTouching)"]
  • Destroy guard first — a removed player must not run a frame against a freed body.
  • Capture input next — the snapshot must be fresh before the handlers read this.Input.
  • Sync before state — the mesh shows last frame's resolved pose while the new one is computed.
  • State → translation → detect — handlers write NextTranslation, the KCC consumes it, then shapecasts refresh IsTouching for next frame's handlers.
Gotcha (one-frame contact lag). detectCollisions() runs after the handlers, so IsTouching the handlers read is always last frame's sensing. That is fine — and intended — but it's why "just landed" logic checks the previous frame's ground flag.

private updatePlayerState(): void

Two lines: tick the FSM, then advance the sprite animation by the frame's delta.

this.fsm.Update();
this.SpriteAnimator.Update(this.Time.Delta);

fsm.Update() calls exactly the handler registered for the current State string (handlers[host.State]?.(host)) — one dispatch, no re-entry. If a handler reassigns player.State, the new handler runs next frame, not this one. The sprite advance is separate and unconditional: handlers pick which animation via SpriteAnimator.ChangeState(...), and this line plays whatever is current.

private updateTranslation(): void

The heart of locomotion: read the body's position, scale the handler's desired velocity by delta, ask the KCC what move is actually possible against the world, apply it, then feed the KCC's own blocked-by contacts into the contact table as proper enter/exit edges. This is the second half of the engine's two-sided contact dispatch. 

const position = this.PhysicsBody!.translation();
this.CurrentPosition.x = position.x;
this.CurrentPosition.y = position.y;

// Desired translation scaled by the (fixed) timestep — reuse a scratch object.
this.scratchDesired.x = this.NextTranslation.x * this.Time.Delta;
this.scratchDesired.y = this.NextTranslation.y * this.Time.Delta;

// Predicate is hoisted (this.moveFilter) — don't collide with sensors or active
// OneWayPlatforms; we DO collide with enemies (they stop the player).
this.CharacterController.computeColliderMovement(
  this.PhysicsBody!.collider(0),
  this.scratchDesired,
  undefined,
  undefined,
  this.moveFilter
);

// Apply the actual translation to the next kinematic translation (scratch reuse).
const correctiveMovement = this.CharacterController.computedMovement();
this.scratchNext.x = this.CurrentTranslation.x + correctiveMovement.x;
this.scratchNext.y = this.CurrentTranslation.y + correctiveMovement.y;
this.PhysicsBody!.setNextKinematicTranslation(this.scratchNext);

computeColliderMovement takes the desired move and clips it against obstacles, returning the corrective (actually-possible) move via computedMovement(). The two undefined slots are filter flags/groups the author found unreliable; the work is done by moveFilter — now a hoisted bound field (created once, not a fresh closure per frame) that lets the player pass through sensors and active one-way platforms but be stopped by everything solid, including enemies (deliberately: a roaming enemy halts the player). The move is staged in reused scratchDesired/scratchNext vectors and committed with setNextKinematicTranslation on top of CurrentTranslation — no per-frame allocation (see the allocation-free callout below). 

if (!this.Physics.IsPaused) {
  this.currentContacts.clear();
  const numCollisions = this.CharacterController.numComputedCollisions();
  for (let i = 0; i < numCollisions; i++) {
    const collision = this.CharacterController.computedCollision(i);
    if (!collision?.collider) {
      continue;
    }
    const other = this.Physics.GetGameObjectFromCollider(collision.collider);
    if (other && !other.IsBeingDestroyed) {
      this.currentContacts.add(other);
    }
  }

  // New contacts this frame → "enter".
  for (const other of this.currentContacts) {
    if (!this.previousContacts.has(other)) {
      this.Physics.Contacts.Dispatch("enter", this, other);
    }
  }
  // Contacts gone since last frame → "exit".
  for (const other of this.previousContacts) {
    if (!this.currentContacts.has(other) && !other.IsBeingDestroyed) {
      this.Physics.Contacts.Dispatch("exit", this, other);
    }
  }

  // Swap the two sets (no allocation); next frame clears `current` before refill.
  const swap = this.previousContacts;
  this.previousContacts = this.currentContacts;
  this.currentContacts = swap;
}
Hard-won victory — the KCC two-sided dispatch, now as contact EDGES. A kinematic character keeps a skin gap from solids it moves into, so Rapier fires no collision event for the player-as-mover (confirmed by runtime logging: ccBlockedBy=[Enemy] yet zero Rapier events for the player). Without this, running into an enemy would never trigger game-over — only the enemy bumping into a standing player would. So we ask the controller what it was blocked by and dispatch those contacts ourselves, making the same declarative rules (Enemy↔Player ⇒ gameOver) fire in BOTH directions. But numComputedCollisions() is a per-frame currently-touching set — dispatching "enter" from it every frame would re-fire game-over on every resting frame. So we diff it against last frame's set using two reused Set<GameObject>s: new contacts dispatch "enter", vanished ones dispatch "exit" (skipping IsBeingDestroyed objects), then the two sets swap. Now an "enter" rule fires once on contact and there is a real "exit" edge. (The contact table only has enter/sensorEnter rules today, so "exit" is a no-op there for now — but the edge is real, and a future rule can use it.) See Engine Physics / Contacts for the table.
Gotcha (pause guard). The whole diff is wrapped in if (!Physics.IsPaused) so the game-over freeze screen does zero contact bookkeeping. With the edge diff this is belt-and-braces — a resting contact no longer re-fires "enter" on its own — but skipping it while paused keeps the frozen frame from churning the sets at all.
Hard-won victory — an allocation-free hot path (the GC-stutter fix). Everything the player does every frame used to allocate: a fresh desired-move object and next-translation object here, two inline predicate closures (one in computeColliderMovement, one per castShape ×4), plus a pair of cast-origin/direction objects per cast. That garbage piled up until the GC paused mid-jump — visible stutter. Now the two predicates are hoisted to bound arrow fields (moveFilter, shapeCastFilter) created once, and four scratch vectors (scratchDesired, scratchNext, scratchCastOrigin, scratchCastDir) are reused across the KCC move and all four casts. Rapier reads the vectors synchronously, so reuse is safe — and the per-frame hot path now allocates nothing.

private detectCollisions(): void

Clears last frame's contact flags, then re-senses ground/walls/ceiling by shapecasting. Two lines:

this.resetCollisions();
this.getShapeCastCollisions();

Note the division of labour: the solid contact flags in IsTouching (ground/walls/ceiling/ edge) are sensed here by shapecast, while the KCC's numComputedCollisions() (for game-rule dispatch) is handled separately in updateTranslation. Two mechanisms, two jobs.

private resetCollisions(): void

Resets all eight IsTouching flags to false each frame — but in place.

// Use assign instead of replacing with a JS Object, fixes issue with dat.gui
Object.assign(this.IsTouching, {
  ground: false, ceiling: false, leftSide: false, rightSide: false,
  edgePlatform: false, ladderCore: false, ladderTop: false, ladderBottom: false,
});
Gotcha (dat.gui live binding). It uses Object.assign rather than this.IsTouching = {...} on purpose. The PlayerDebug panel binds dat.gui controllers to the original object reference; replacing it with a new object would leave the GUI pointed at a stale struct that never updates. Mutating in place keeps the live debug bindings valid.

public UpdateLadderState(top: boolean, core: boolean, bottom: boolean): void

Pushes the three ladder-zone flags into IsTouching from outside. Parameters map 1:1 onto ladderTop/ladderCore/ladderBottom. No return value. 

this.IsTouching.ladderTop = top;
this.IsTouching.ladderCore = core;
this.IsTouching.ladderBottom = bottom;

Unlike ground/wall sensing, ladder contact is detected by external ladder sensors (see LadderSensor), and the GameDirector relays their state into the player through this setter. The comment flags it as a stopgap "until ladder sensors use proper callbacks" — for now the director is the courier. The climbing handler reads these three flags to decide enter/exit and to clamp at the rails.

Ordering dependency. Because detectCollisions() → resetCollisions() wipes all eight flags (including the ladder trio) each Update(), the director must call UpdateLadderState(...) after the player's update each frame, or the climb flags will be zero by the time the handlers read them.

private getPlatform(collider): Platform | undefined / private isActiveOneWayPlatform(collider): boolean

Two small adapters that turn an anonymous Rapier collider into typed game data.

private getPlatform(collider: RAPIER.Collider): Platform | undefined {
  const go = this.Physics.GetGameObjectFromCollider(collider);
  if (go instanceof Platform) return go;
  return undefined;
}

private isActiveOneWayPlatform(collider: RAPIER.Collider): boolean {
  return this.getPlatform(collider)?.IsOneWayActive ?? false;
}

getPlatform resolves a collider back to its owning GameObject via the physics registry, and narrows to Platform so callers can read named fields (FloorLevel, IsEdge, IsOneWayActive) instead of poking at anonymous userData. isActiveOneWayPlatform is the typed replacement for the old userData.value3 check — a one-way platform you can currently jump up through. Both feed the shapecast predicate and the KCC filter.

private getShapeCastCollisions(): void

Casts the player's own collider shape a hair in each of the four directions and converts near hits into the ground/wall/ceiling/edge/buffer flags. Direction is encoded as a signed axis pair from PlayerDirection (down = (0,-1), left = (-1,0), etc.). 

const shapeCasts = {
  down:  this.shapeCast(PlayerDirection.NEUTRAL, PlayerDirection.DOWN),
  left:  this.shapeCast(PlayerDirection.LEFT,    PlayerDirection.NEUTRAL),
  right: this.shapeCast(PlayerDirection.RIGHT,   PlayerDirection.NEUTRAL),
  up:    this.shapeCast(PlayerDirection.NEUTRAL, PlayerDirection.UP),
};

Ground — a down hit within the touch threshold that is not a wall and not an active one-way platform:

if (downCast &&
    downCast.time_of_impact <= this.ColliderOffsetThreshold &&
    GameUtils.IsColliderType(downCast.collider, EntityType.WALL) === false &&
    this.isActiveOneWayPlatform(downCast.collider) === false) {
  this.IsTouching.ground = true;

  const platform = this.getPlatform(downCast.collider);
  if (platform) this.CurrentFloor = platform.FloorLevel;
  else this.CurrentFloor = 0;
  if (platform) this.IsTouching.edgePlatform = platform.IsEdge;
  else this.IsTouching.edgePlatform = false;
}

When grounded, the player also reads CurrentFloor and edgePlatform straight off the Platform it is standing on — named fields, no userData spelunking. The edgePlatform flag is what lets the running handler zero vertical velocity right at a ledge so the player walks off cleanly into FALLING instead of catching on the lip.

Buffer-jump range — a down hit that is close-ish (within BufferJumpRange) while descending, but not yet grounded, latches GroundWithinBufferRange so a jump pressed just before landing still fires:

if (!this.IsTouching.ground &&
    downCast &&
    downCast.time_of_impact <= this.BufferJumpRange &&
    this.NextTranslation.y <= 0) {
  this.GroundWithinBufferRange = true;
} else {
  this.GroundWithinBufferRange = false;
}

Walls and ceiling — left/right/up hits within the threshold set the matching flag, but each ignores one-way platforms (you should never be wall-stopped or head-bonked by a pass-through platform):

// left/right/up each:
if (cast &&
    cast.time_of_impact <= this.ColliderOffsetThreshold &&
    GameUtils.IsColliderType(cast.collider, EntityType.ONE_WAY_PLATFORM) === false) {
  this.IsTouching.leftSide /* rightSide / ceiling */ = true;
}

private shapeCast(xDirection: number, yDirection: number): RAPIER.ShapeCastHit | null

Casts collider 0's shape from a tiny offset of the body origin, in the given direction, and returns the first solid hit. The predicate is what makes it see through non-solids to the real ground — and it, plus the cast origin/direction, are now reused fields so the four casts per frame allocate nothing. 

// Reuse scratch vectors (Rapier reads them synchronously) so the 4 casts/frame
// allocate nothing.
this.scratchCastOrigin.x =
  this.CurrentTranslation.x + this.ColliderOffset * xDirection;
this.scratchCastOrigin.y =
  this.CurrentTranslation.y + this.ColliderOffset * yDirection;
this.scratchCastDir.x = xDirection;
this.scratchCastDir.y = yDirection;

// HOISTED predicate (this.shapeCastFilter) penetrates sensors / active one-way
// platforms / enemies to find solid ground.
const hit = this.Physics.World.castShape(
  this.scratchCastOrigin,
  0,
  this.scratchCastDir,
  this.PhysicsBody!.collider(0).shape,
  0.0, 1000, true,
  undefined, undefined,                 // filterFlags, filterGroups
  this.PhysicsBody!.collider(0),        // exclude collider 0
  this.PhysicsBody,                     // exclude the whole player body
  this.shapeCastFilter
);
return hit;
  • The small ColliderOffset * direction nudge to the cast origin fixes a quirk where an unoffset X-cast collides with the shape's own inner wall.
  • The player's own collider and body are excluded explicitly (the two args before the predicate) so a cast never reports the player hitting itself.
  • shapeCastFilter (a hoisted bound field, not a fresh closure per cast) runs for every candidate, returning false to skip — so the cast tunnels through sensors (camera/ladder), through active one-way platforms, and through enemies (an enemy must never be treated as standable ground), and reports only the solid surface beneath.

public TeleportToPosition(targetX: number, targetY: number): void inherited

Defined on GameObject, not on Player — included here because it is how the game relocates Randy (respawn, level start, teleporters). It computes the delta from CurrentTranslation to the target and hard-sets the body translation, bypassing the character controller entirely. 

public TeleportToPosition(targetX: number, targetY: number) {
  const deltaX = targetX - this.CurrentTranslation.x;
  const deltaY = targetY - this.CurrentTranslation.y;
  this.TeleportRelative(deltaX, deltaY);  // setTranslation(current + delta)
}
Note. Because this calls setTranslation (an instantaneous warp) rather than setNextKinematicTranslation (the interpolated kinematic move), it skips collision resolution — correct for a respawn, but it is the caller's job to pick a non-overlapping target.

public Destroy(): void

Tears the player down in the right order. Note what it pointedly does not do anymore: announce itself.

// NOTE: do NOT emit "gameObjectRemoved" here. The director's handler for that
// event calls .Destroy(), so self-emitting was unbounded recursion. Whatever
// removes the player owns the emit (the same way enemies are removed).
this.Physics.World.removeCharacterController(this.CharacterController);
this.SpriteAnimator.Destroy();
super.Destroy();
  • removeCharacterController — frees the KCC created in setCharacterController; Rapier won't reclaim it with the body.
  • SpriteAnimator.Destroy() — disposes the sprite texture/material (GPU memory).
  • super.Destroy() — latches IsBeingDestroyed (now idempotent: a second Destroy() early-returns), removes the mesh, disposes geometry/material, unregisters from the contact system, and removes the rigid body (and its collider) last.
Hard-won fix (no more self-emit recursion). The player used to call Emitter.emit("gameObjectRemoved", this) on its way out. But the GameDirector's handler for that very event calls .Destroy() — so a player emitting its own removal re-entered Destroy() unbounded. The emit (and the now-unused Emitter import) were removed: whoever removes the player owns the emit, exactly like enemies. The base class's IsBeingDestroyed latch is the belt-and-braces guard that makes any stray double-Destroy() a no-op.

Modifying this: add a new movement state by writing a handlePlayerX function (see Player States), adding a PlayerStates.X constant, and registering the pair in the FSM map in initializePlayerAttributes — nothing else here changes. To retune the character physically (snap, step, slope), edit setCharacterController; to retune feel (speeds, gravity, jump), do NOT touch this file — change a profile in Feel. To add a new sensed contact, add a flag to ContactPoints, reset it in resetCollisions, and set it from a new branch in getShapeCastCollisions. Any teardown you add (a new world resource) belongs in Destroy() before super.Destroy().

src/game/entities/player/applyHorizontalMovement.ts 47 LOC

One pure-ish helper function, shared by three state handlers — running, jumping, falling (see Player States). It is the single place horizontal velocity is accelerated, decelerated, clamped, and wall-stopped, so the three airborne/grounded states stay consistent without duplicating the math. It imports only Player (the blackboard it mutates), PlayerDirection (the signed axis), and GameUtils.MoveTowardsPoint (the framerate-independent stepper). It has no lifecycle of its own; the handlers call it once per frame.

const applyHorizontalMovement = (player: Player, airborne = false): boolean

Accelerate player.NextTranslation.x toward the directional target speed (or decelerate toward 0 when neutral), then hard-stop on a wall. Parameters: player — the entity to move; airborne — selects the air accel/decel pair instead of the ground pair (the running handler omits it → ground; jumping and falling pass true → air). Returns true if a wall was hit this frame, which the running handler uses to keep its wall animation playing. 

let accelRate = player.GroundAcceleration;
if (airborne) accelRate = player.AirAcceleration;
let decelRate = player.GroundDeceleration;
if (airborne) decelRate = player.AirDeceleration;

let targetSpeed = 0;
let acceleration = 0;

if (player.Direction === PlayerDirection.NEUTRAL) {
  targetSpeed = 0;
  acceleration = decelRate;
} else {
  targetSpeed = player.Direction * player.MaxGroundSpeed;
  acceleration = accelRate;
}

player.NextTranslation.x = GameUtils.MoveTowardsPoint(
  player.NextTranslation.x,
  targetSpeed,
  acceleration * player.Time.Delta
);

const hitWall =
  (player.IsTouching.leftSide  && player.Direction === PlayerDirection.LEFT) ||
  (player.IsTouching.rightSide && player.Direction === PlayerDirection.RIGHT);
if (hitWall) {
  player.NextTranslation.x = 0;
}

return hitWall;
  • Air vs ground only differ on accel/decel — top speed is shared (MaxGroundSpeed is the target for both). Air control therefore tunes how quickly you reach/lose speed mid-jump, not how fast you can ultimately go, so a jump never out-runs a sprint.
  • Neutral decelerates, input accelerates — when Direction is NEUTRAL the target is 0 at the decel rate (coast to a stop); otherwise the target is Direction * MaxGroundSpeed (the sign IS the facing) at the accel rate.
  • Framerate independence — MoveTowardsPoint (= mathHelpers.moveTowards) steps toward the target by acceleration * Time.Delta and snaps exactly to it when within one step, so the curve is identical at 30 or 144 fps.
  • Wall stop is directional — velocity is zeroed only when the touched wall is on the side you're pushing into; a left wall doesn't freeze rightward escape. Returning hitWall lets the caller keep the run cycle animating against a wall instead of snapping to idle.
Tip. Because target speed is read live from player.MaxGroundSpeed every call, sliding that one value in the Feel Lab changes both ground and air top speed instantly — there is no separate air cap to forget.

Modifying this: to give air its own top speed, add a MaxAirSpeed attribute and branch targetSpeed on airborne. To add ice/momentum, swap the NEUTRAL branch's decel for a friction term. Keep it returning hitWall — the running handler depends on that boolean.

src/game/entities/player/spriteAnimations.ts 40 LOC

A pure data table — no logic, no imports. It maps each state + facing to a list of sprite-sheet tile indices and a matching list of per-frame durations, exactly the shape SpriteAnimator.ChangeState expects ({ indicies, timing }, see Engine Rendering). The player initializes with IDLE_RIGHT, and each state handler calls player.SpriteAnimator.ChangeState(SpriteAnimations.X) to swap clips. indicies keeps its typo on purpose — it matches the field name in the animator.

const SpriteAnimations = { IDLE_LEFT, IDLE_RIGHT, RUN_LEFT, RUN_RIGHT, JUMP_LEFT, JUMP_RIGHT, FALL_LEFT, FALL_RIGHT }

Eight clips. Tile indices are 0-based, read left-to-right top-to-bottom on the 4×6 randy sheet (the 4, 6 passed to the SpriteAnimator constructor). timing[i] is how many seconds indicies[i] holds; the arrays are parallel and equal length.

Clipindiciestiming (s)Notes
IDLE_LEFT[1, 2][5, 0.1]Long hold then a quick blink — a 5-second breathing idle.
IDLE_RIGHT[5, 6][0.1, 5]Mirror of idle-left (blink-then-hold); the opening state.
RUN_LEFT[8, 9, 10, 11][0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25]4-frame run cycle; base timing is reshaped live (below).
RUN_RIGHT[12, 13, 14, 15][0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25]4-frame run cycle, facing right.
JUMP_LEFT[17, 18][0.25, 0.25]2-frame ascend pose.
JUMP_RIGHT[21, 22][0.25, 0.25]2-frame ascend pose, right.
FALL_LEFT[17, 18][0.25, 0.25]Reuses the jump frames — fall shares jump art.
FALL_RIGHT[21, 22][0.25, 0.25]Reuses jump-right frames.
 
RUN_LEFT: {
  indicies: [8, 9, 10, 11],
  timing: [0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25],
},
RUN_RIGHT: {
  indicies: [12, 13, 14, 15],
  timing: [0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25],
},
Note (jump ≡ fall art). FALL_* intentionally points at the same tiles as JUMP_*. They are distinct entries so a future fall animation can diverge without touching the jump handler — for now Randy holds the same pose through the whole arc.
Tip (the 0.25 is a baseline, not the final speed). The run handler doesn't play the run cycle at a flat 0.25s/frame — it calls SpriteAnimator.ChangeAnimationTiming(1 / (|NextTranslation.x| / AnimationScalingFactor)) so the legs cycle faster as Randy moves faster and slow as he creeps. The table's timing is just the unscaled reference; AnimationScalingFactor lives in Feel.

Modifying this: add a clip by appending a { indicies, timing } entry keyed by STATE_FACING and pointing its handler at it via ChangeState. Keep the two arrays the same length (the animator indexes them in lockstep). If you re-author the sheet, the only coupling is the 4, 6 grid in setSpriteAnimator and these raw tile numbers — change both together.

Player States — the FSM bodies

Five tiny pure-function handlers — one per PlayerState — are the flesh on the bones of the generic StateMachine. Each runs once per frame for the player's current state: it reads the per-entity input snapshot and the shapecast contact flags, writes a desired NextTranslation, picks a sprite, and — when a condition trips — reassigns player.State to transition next frame. This is where coyote-time, the jump buffer, variable jump height, apex-hang, and ladder snapping actually live.

src/game/entities/player/states/handlePlayerIdle.ts src/game/entities/player/states/handlePlayerRunning.ts src/game/entities/player/states/handlePlayerJumping.ts src/game/entities/player/states/handlePlayerFalling.ts src/game/entities/player/states/handlePlayerClimbing.ts
How these get called. Each handler is a plain (player: Player) => void registered in the player's FSM map in initializePlayerAttributes(). The generic StateMachine<Player, PlayerState> (see FSM Architecture — StateMachine) dispatches exactly one handler per fsm.Update(), keyed on player.State. A handler that reassigns player.State does not re-dispatch this frame — the new state runs next frame. All the tuning numbers (JumpPower, RiseGravity, CoyoteTime, …) come from the Feel (Attributes) profiles; the handlers never hard-code feel. 
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> IDLE
  IDLE --> RUNNING: isLeft or isRight
  IDLE --> FALLING: not ground and vy <= 0
  IDLE --> JUMPING: isJump and BufferJumpAvailable
  IDLE --> CLIMBING: ladderCore and up/down
  RUNNING --> IDLE: slow and neutral input
  RUNNING --> FALLING: not ground
  RUNNING --> JUMPING: isJump and BufferJumpAvailable
  RUNNING --> CLIMBING: ladderCore and up/down
  JUMPING --> FALLING: ceiling or apex (vy <= 0)
  FALLING --> IDLE: ground and slow/neutral
  FALLING --> RUNNING: ground and moving/input
  FALLING --> JUMPING: coyote window
  CLIMBING --> IDLE: leave ladder or reach top/bottom
Gotcha — the enum doc oversells. playerStates.ts comments claim CLIMBING -> JUMPING and JUMPING -> CLIMBING. Read the bodies: neither handler implements those edges. CLIMBING only ever exits to IDLE; JUMPING only ever exits to FALLING. The diagram above reflects the code, not the comment.

src/game/entities/player/states/handlePlayerIdle.ts 104 LOC

The resting state — standing still on the ground — and the engine's initial state (Player seeds State = PlayerStates.IDLE). It is also the grounded "hub": every grounded reset (coyote refill, early-jump latch clear, buffer refill) lives here and in Running. Imports Player (the host), PlayerStates (transition targets), SpriteAnimations (the frame table) and PlayerDirection (signed facing). No physics import — it nudges the body purely through player.NextTranslation, which Player.updateTranslation() later feeds to the kinematic character controller.

const handlePlayerIdle = (player: Player) => void

Runs while standing. The order of the four transition checks is deliberate: a slope/edge guard, then fall, then run, then jump, then climb — and only if none fire does the body do its grounded housekeeping and pick an idle sprite.

1. The vertical-direction edge guard (not a transition). Before any state change, it catches the case where the body still carries upward/zero vertical intent while the player presses a horizontal key:

if (
  player.NextTranslation.y >= 0 &&
  (player.Input.isLeft || player.Input.isRight)
) {
  player.NextTranslation.y = -player.MaxFallSpeed;
  return;
}

It slams vertical velocity to terminal-fall (-MaxFallSpeed) and returns — staying IDLE for one frame so the body is pressed firmly down before the next frame re-evaluates ground contact. This prevents a one-frame "float" when leaving a state with residual y >= 0.

2. Transition to FALLING — walked off something while not rising:

if (!player.IsTouching.ground && player.NextTranslation.y <= 0) {
  player.TimeFallWasEntered = player.Time.Elapsed;
  player.State = PlayerStates.FALLING;
  return;
}

Stamping TimeFallWasEntered here is what arms coyote time — the FALLING handler measures the coyote window against this timestamp.

3. Transition to RUNNING — any horizontal press:

if (player.Input.isLeft || player.Input.isRight) {
  player.State = PlayerStates.RUNNING;
  return;
}

4. Transition to JUMPING — the buffered-jump launch:

if (player.Input.isJump && player.BufferJumpAvailable) {
  // Launch impulse + reset the early-end latch so every jump starts at full
  // height (a buffered jump fired on landing skips the grounded reset below).
  player.NextTranslation.y = player.JumpPower;
  player.EndedJumpEarly = false;
  player.TimeJumpWasEntered = player.Time.Elapsed;
  player.State = PlayerStates.JUMPING;
  return;
}

Note the gate is BufferJumpAvailable, not raw "is grounded". The grounded reset further down sets that flag true each frame the player is on the ground and not holding jump, so a fresh press launches. Setting EndedJumpEarly = false here matters because a buffered jump can fire on the landing frame and skip the grounded reset below — the comment spells out exactly that race.

5. Transition to CLIMBING — on a ladder core, pressing up/down, but not pressing "off the end":

if (
  player.IsTouching.ladderCore &&
  (player.Input.isUp || player.Input.isDown) &&
  !(player.IsTouching.ladderTop && player.Input.isUp) &&
  !(player.IsTouching.ladderBottom && player.Input.isDown)
) {
  player.NextTranslation.x = 0;
  player.NextTranslation.y = 0;
  player.State = PlayerStates.CLIMBING;
  return;
}

The two negated clauses stop you from "grabbing" a ladder by pushing up at its very top or down at its very bottom (you'd just walk off). The ladder flags (ladderCore/Top/Bottom) are not shapecast here — they are pushed in each frame by GameDirector via Player.UpdateLadderState() from the Ladder Sensor. Zeroing both axes freezes the body the instant it latches on.

The grounded body (only reached if no transition fired):

// In a grounded state, give coyote and reset early jump gravity
player.CoyoteAvailable = true;
player.EndedJumpEarly = false;
if (!player.Input.isJump) {
  player.BufferJumpAvailable = true;
}

// Simple max gravity in non-vertical state to fix downward movement on slopes, maintain touching ground
player.NextTranslation.y = -player.MaxFallSpeed;

This is the canonical grounded reset, mirrored verbatim in Running: refill coyote, clear the early-end latch, and re-arm the buffer jump only when jump is released (so holding jump can't auto-rebuffer). The constant -MaxFallSpeed downward push keeps the kinematic controller's snap-to-ground glued to slopes instead of micro-hopping.

Sprite selection. A switch (player.Direction) picks IDLE_LEFT/IDLE_RIGHT directly for LEFT/RIGHT. The default (NEUTRAL) branch is the interesting one — it inspects the current sprite state to choose which idle orientation to settle into:

default:
  const currentState = player.SpriteAnimator.State;
  // If already in idle state, no need to change
  if (
    currentState === SpriteAnimations.IDLE_LEFT ||
    currentState === SpriteAnimations.IDLE_RIGHT
  ) {
    break;
  }
  // Transitioned into idle state, match with correct idle orientation
  if (
    currentState === SpriteAnimations.RUN_LEFT ||
    currentState === SpriteAnimations.FALL_LEFT
  ) {
    player.SpriteAnimator.ChangeState(SpriteAnimations.IDLE_LEFT);
  } else if (
    currentState === SpriteAnimations.RUN_RIGHT ||
    currentState === SpriteAnimations.FALL_RIGHT
  ) {
    player.SpriteAnimator.ChangeState(SpriteAnimations.IDLE_RIGHT);
  }
  break;

With no horizontal input, Direction stays NEUTRAL, so the sprite must inherit the last facing from whatever animation was playing — a run/fall to the left settles to IDLE_LEFT, etc. Already-idle is a no-op so the loop never restarts. 

stateDiagram-v2
  state "IDLE" as I
  I --> FALLING: not ground and vy <= 0
  I --> RUNNING: isLeft or isRight
  I --> JUMPING: isJump and BufferJumpAvailable
  I --> CLIMBING: ladderCore and up/down (not off-end)
  I --> I: grounded — refill coyote/buffer, vy = -MaxFallSpeed
Hard-won victory — buffered-jump full height. Resetting EndedJumpEarly = false at the launch site (not only in the grounded reset) fixes a subtle bug: a jump buffered while still airborne fires on the landing frame and would otherwise skip the grounded reset, inheriting a stale "ended early" latch and peaking short. Every jump now starts un-cut.

Modifying this: keep the transition checks ordered and each terminated by return — they are early-exits, so a new check's position decides its priority. The grounded reset block must stay after all transitions; moving it above them would refill coyote/buffer on a frame you intend to leave the ground. If you add a transition (e.g. a crouch), copy the return discipline and decide deliberately where it sits relative to jump and climb.

src/game/entities/player/states/handlePlayerRunning.ts 126 LOC

Horizontal locomotion on the ground. It shares IDLE's transition set and grounded reset, but adds the real horizontal movement (delegated to applyHorizontalMovement), speed-scaled animation timing, and a run-off-the-edge special case. The only extra import over IDLE is applyHorizontalMovement (the shared accel/decel + wall-stop).

const handlePlayerRunning = (player: Player) => void

1. Transition to FALLING — left the ground:

if (!player.IsTouching.ground) {
  if (player.IsTouching.edgePlatform) {
    player.NextTranslation.y = 0;
  }
  player.TimeFallWasEntered = player.Time.Elapsed;
  player.State = PlayerStates.FALLING;
  return;
}

Same coyote-arming timestamp as IDLE. The extra edgePlatform guard zeroes vertical velocity at the lip of an edge platform so the transition into FALLING starts from a clean y = 0 rather than the residual -MaxFallSpeed the grounded body would otherwise have left.

2. Transition to IDLE — effectively stopped and not steering:

const isMovingSlowly =
  Math.abs(player.NextTranslation.x) <= player.MaxGroundSpeed * 0.01;
const isIdleInput =
  player.Input.isNeitherLeftRight || player.Input.isLeftRightCombo;

if (isMovingSlowly && isIdleInput) {
  player.NextTranslation.x = 0;
  player.State = PlayerStates.IDLE;
  return;
}

It waits for the body to decelerate below 1% of top speed (MaxGroundSpeed * 0.01) before handing back to IDLE — so releasing the key doesn't snap to idle mid-slide; the run animation keeps playing while applyHorizontalMovement bleeds off speed. isLeftRightCombo (both held) counts as "no steering" too. The hard x = 0 snaps the last sub-1% creep to a dead stop.

3. Transition to JUMPING and 4. Transition to CLIMBING are character-for-character identical to IDLE (same buffered-launch block with the EndedJumpEarly = false reset, same ladder grab with both axes zeroed). They are duplicated rather than shared because each state owns its own transition table.

Grounded body. Same reset as IDLE:

player.CoyoteAvailable = true;
player.EndedJumpEarly = false;
if (!player.Input.isJump) {
  player.BufferJumpAvailable = true;
}

Input & animation. LEFT/RIGHT set Direction and the matching run sprite. The neutral branch keeps the run animation facing the last direction by reading the current sprite state:

else if (isIdleInput) {
  player.Direction = PlayerDirection.NEUTRAL;
  const currentState = player.SpriteAnimator.State;
  if (
    currentState === SpriteAnimations.JUMP_LEFT ||
    currentState === SpriteAnimations.FALL_LEFT
  ) {
    player.SpriteAnimator.ChangeState(SpriteAnimations.RUN_LEFT);
  }
  if (
    currentState === SpriteAnimations.JUMP_RIGHT ||
    currentState === SpriteAnimations.FALL_RIGHT
  ) {
    player.SpriteAnimator.ChangeState(SpriteAnimations.RUN_RIGHT);
  }
}

This covers the "just landed from a jump/fall while still pressing both keys or neither" case — it converts a JUMP/FALL sprite into the correct-facing RUN sprite so the decel slide animates as running.

Speed-scaled animation timing. The run anim plays faster the faster you move:

player.SpriteAnimator.ChangeAnimationTiming(
  1 / (Math.abs(player.NextTranslation.x) / player.AnimationScalingFactor)
);

The comments spell out the math: the denominator scales the per-frame hold time relative to horizontal speed; the 1 / numerator inverts it so a larger |vx| yields a shorter hold (faster animation). AnimationScalingFactor (1.6 in the shared base feel) is the divisor that tunes the overall pace.

Gravity (Y axis). The edge-platform exception again:

if (player.IsTouching.edgePlatform) {
  // Exception to simple gravity when about to run off an edge
  player.NextTranslation.y = 0;
} else {
  player.NextTranslation.y = -player.MaxFallSpeed;
}

On a normal floor the constant downward push keeps ground contact on slopes; on an edge platform it holds y = 0 so the player can stand at the very lip without the snap-down nudging them off.

Movement (X axis) and the wall-animation trick.

if (applyHorizontalMovement(player)) {
  // Play running animation even though hitting a wall ...
  player.SpriteAnimator.ChangeAnimationTiming(
    1 / (player.MaxGroundSpeed / player.AnimationScalingFactor)
  );
}

applyHorizontalMovement (no airborne flag, so ground accel/decel) returns true when it stopped you dead on a wall. Running uses that to re-time the animation as if at full speed — even though |vx| just got zeroed by the wall — so the sprite keeps "running into the wall" instead of freezing on the first frame. The author's own comment admits this lives here only to avoid duplicating the input check in the animation section above. 

stateDiagram-v2
  state "RUNNING" as R
  R --> FALLING: not ground
  R --> IDLE: slow and neutral input
  R --> JUMPING: isJump and BufferJumpAvailable
  R --> CLIMBING: ladderCore and up/down (not off-end)
  R --> R: accel/decel via applyHorizontalMovement, speed-scaled anim
Note — top speed is shared, control isn't. See applyHorizontalMovement: ground vs air use separate accel/decel constants but the same MaxGroundSpeed ceiling, so air control can be tuned floatier/draggier in the Feel Lab without changing how fast you can ultimately move.

Modifying this: the IDLE threshold (MaxGroundSpeed * 0.01) is the knob for "how stopped before idle"; raise it for a snappier stop, lower it for a longer slide. If you change the grounded reset, change IDLE's identically — they are intentional twins. The two ChangeAnimationTiming calls are a known mild duplication; consolidate only if you also fold the wall-hit detection up into the animation block.

src/game/entities/player/states/handlePlayerJumping.ts 93 LOC

The rising half of the jump arc. Entered only from IDLE/RUNNING (buffered jump) or FALLING (coyote jump) — in all three the launch site sets NextTranslation.y = player.JumpPower and stamps TimeJumpWasEntered. This handler does not set the impulse; it spends it. It owns variable jump height (early-release), apex-hang, and the ceiling/apex exits. Imports add applyHorizontalMovement for air steering.

const handlePlayerJumping = (player: Player) => void

1. Transition to FALLING — ceiling bonk:

if (player.IsTouching.ceiling && player.NextTranslation.y >= 0) {
  player.NextTranslation.y = 0;
  player.TimeFallWasEntered = player.Time.Elapsed;
  player.State = PlayerStates.FALLING;
  return;
}

Hitting a ceiling while still moving up kills upward velocity and drops to FALLING immediately (stamping the fall timestamp, though coyote won't matter from a ceiling). The y >= 0 guard avoids re-triggering on a ceiling brush while already descending.

2. Transition to FALLING — the natural apex:

// Natural apex — rise gravity has decelerated the launch impulse to zero. The
// peak emerges from physics now, not a fixed MaxJumpTime timer.
if (player.NextTranslation.y <= 0) {
  player.TimeFallWasEntered = player.Time.Elapsed;
  player.State = PlayerStates.FALLING;
  return;
}

This is the only way a jump ends upward travel: rise gravity (below) bleeds vy down to zero and the very frame it crosses to <= 0, the state flips to FALLING. The comment is explicit that the apex is emergent — there is no fixed jump-duration timer. Consequence: FALLING is always entered at the apex with vy <= 0, which is why FALLING's old "early release" branch was dead code (it documents this).

Variable height — the early-end latch:

// Variable height — releasing jump past the MinJumpTime floor latches an
// "early end", which boosts rise gravity below so the player peaks lower.
if (
  !player.Input.isJump &&
  player.Time.Elapsed > player.TimeJumpWasEntered + player.MinJumpTime
) {
  player.EndedJumpEarly = true;
}

Releasing jump after the MinJumpTime floor (0.05s) sets the latch; a tap shorter than that still gets the minimum arc. This does not transition — it stays JUMPING and lets the apex check end it once the (now heavier) rise gravity zeroes vy.

Body — lock out coyote/buffer while airborne:

player.CoyoteAvailable = false;
player.BufferJumpAvailable = false;

You can't coyote- or buffer-jump out of a jump; those refill only on the ground (IDLE/RUNNING).

Input & animation. LEFT/RIGHT set Direction and the jump sprite. The neutral/combo branch converts an idle/run sprite into the matching jump sprite so a jump from standstill still shows the jump pose facing the right way.

The rise-gravity model (the heart of the feel):

let riseGravity = player.RiseGravity;
if (player.EndedJumpEarly) {
  riseGravity *= player.JumpEndedEarlyGravityModifier;
} else if (Math.abs(player.NextTranslation.y) < player.ApexHangThreshold) {
  riseGravity *= player.ApexHangMult;
}

player.NextTranslation.y -= riseGravity * player.Time.Delta;

Three mutually exclusive regimes, in priority order:

ConditionMultiplierEffect
EndedJumpEarlyJumpEndedEarlyGravityModifier (3)Cut jump falls away fast — variable height
|vy| < ApexHangThreshold (4)ApexHangMult (0.5)Held jump floats briefly at the top — apex hang
otherwise1×Plain RiseGravity deceleration

The else if ordering is load-bearing: a cut jump gets no hang (the comment: "The two are mutually exclusive — a cut jump gets no hang"). Decelerating per-Delta rather than per-frame keeps the arc frame-rate independent.

Movement (X axis): applyHorizontalMovement(player, true) — the true selects the air accel/decel constants.

stateDiagram-v2
  state "JUMPING" as J
  [*] --> J: entry sets vy = JumpPower, stamps TimeJumpWasEntered
  J --> FALLING: ceiling and vy >= 0
  J --> FALLING: apex (vy <= 0)
  J --> J: rise gravity (early-cut x3, apex-hang x0.5), air steer
Hard-won victory — physics-emergent apex. The peak is no longer a fixed MaxJumpTime timer; it's wherever RiseGravity brings vy to zero. That made apex-hang and variable height fall out of one gravity multiplier each, and it guarantees FALLING is always entered with vy <= 0 — the invariant the FALLING handler relies on.

Modifying this: all four jump-feel numbers (RiseGravity, JumpEndedEarlyGravityModifier, ApexHangThreshold, ApexHangMult, plus JumpPower set at the launch sites) live in the Feel profiles — tune there, not here. Keep the early-end check as else if against apex-hang; flattening them to two ifs would let a cut jump also hang. Don't add a duration timer — the apex is meant to emerge from gravity.

src/game/entities/player/states/handlePlayerFalling.ts 124 LOC

The descending half — reached from the JUMPING apex/ceiling or from walking off an edge (IDLE/RUNNING). It owns coyote time (jump shortly after leaving ground) and the jump buffer bookkeeping (count/grant a jump queued just before landing), plus terminal-velocity gravity. Adds GameUtils for MoveTowardsPoint and applyHorizontalMovement for air steering.

const handlePlayerFalling = (player: Player) => void

1. Transition to IDLE or RUNNING — landing:

if (player.IsTouching.ground) {
  if (
    (player.Input.isNeitherLeftRight || player.Input.isLeftRightCombo) &&
    Math.abs(player.NextTranslation.x) < player.MaxGroundSpeed * 0.01
  ) {
    player.State = PlayerStates.IDLE;
  } else {
    player.State = PlayerStates.RUNNING;
  }
  // Reset gating mechanism for counting buffer jumps
  player.WasBufferJumpUsed = false;
  return;
}

On touchdown it picks IDLE only if you're both not steering and already slow (same 1%-of-top-speed test as Running); otherwise RUNNING so a moving landing keeps its momentum and run animation. WasBufferJumpUsed = false re-arms the buffer-jump counter gate for the next airborne stretch.

2. Transition to JUMPING — coyote time:

if (
  player.Input.isJump &&
  player.CoyoteAvailable &&
  player.Time.Elapsed < player.TimeFallWasEntered + player.CoyoteTime
) {
  player.CoyoteCount += 1;
  // Launch impulse + reset the early-end latch so every jump starts un-cut.
  player.NextTranslation.y = player.JumpPower;
  player.EndedJumpEarly = false;
  player.CoyoteAvailable = false;
  player.TimeJumpWasEntered = player.Time.Elapsed;
  player.State = PlayerStates.JUMPING;
  return;
}

This is the whole of coyote time: if jump is pressed within CoyoteTime (0.033s ≈ 2 frames) of leaving the ground — where TimeFallWasEntered was stamped by IDLE/RUNNING — and the single-use CoyoteAvailable flag (refilled only on the ground) is still set, it launches a full JUMPING just as if grounded. It consumes the flag (CoyoteAvailable = false), stamps the jump timer, resets the early-end latch, and bumps a debug counter.

Buffer-jump bookkeeping (only if not landing/coyote-ing):

// Check if ground is within buffer range
if (player.GroundWithinBufferRange) {
  // Give buffer jump
  if (!player.Input.isJump) {
    player.BufferJumpAvailable = true;
  }
}

// Count buffer jumps
if (
  player.BufferJumpAvailable &&
  player.Input.isJump &&
  !player.WasBufferJumpUsed
) {
  player.WasBufferJumpUsed = true;
  player.BufferJumpCount++;
}

GroundWithinBufferRange is computed in Player.getShapeCastCollisions() — true when a downward shapecast finds ground within BufferJumpRange (1.5) while descending. When ground is that close and jump is released, it pre-arms BufferJumpAvailable so the very next IDLE/RUNNING frame (after landing) launches instantly. The second block is purely the debug counter, gated by WasBufferJumpUsed so one queued press counts once.

Note — where the buffered jump actually fires. FALLING arms the buffer; the launch itself happens in IDLE/RUNNING's isJump && BufferJumpAvailable block on the landing frame. The two halves live in different states by design.

Coyote expiry:

if (player.Time.Elapsed >= player.TimeFallWasEntered + player.CoyoteTime) {
  player.CoyoteAvailable = false;
}

Once the window passes, the flag is cleared — which also gates gravity below.

Input & animation. LEFT/RIGHT set facing + fall sprite; the neutral/combo branch derives facing from the current sprite (idle/run/jump, left vs right) and picks FALL_LEFT/FALL_RIGHT to match.

Gravity (Y axis) — terminal-velocity accel:

// Variable jump height now lives entirely in the JUMPING state ...
// (The old early-release branch here was dead: FALLING is always entered at
// the apex with y <= 0, so its `y > 0` guard never fired.)
if (!player.CoyoteAvailable) {
  player.NextTranslation.y = GameUtils.MoveTowardsPoint(
    player.NextTranslation.y,
    -player.MaxFallSpeed,
    player.FallAcceleration * player.Time.Delta
  );
}

Gravity only applies after the coyote window closes (!CoyoteAvailable) — during the 2-frame grace the player hangs so a coyote jump launches from the same height they left. After that it MoveTowardsPoints vy toward -MaxFallSpeed at FallAcceleration per Delta. The comment documents that the historical early-release gravity branch was removed as dead code once the apex invariant from JUMPING made y > 0 here impossible.

Movement (X axis): applyHorizontalMovement(player, true) — air constants again.

stateDiagram-v2
  state "FALLING" as F
  [*] --> F: entry stamps TimeFallWasEntered (arms coyote)
  F --> IDLE: ground and slow/neutral
  F --> RUNNING: ground and moving/input
  F --> JUMPING: isJump and CoyoteAvailable within CoyoteTime
  F --> F: arm/count buffer, expire coyote, accel toward -MaxFallSpeed
Gotcha — coyote gates gravity, not just the jump. The if (!player.CoyoteAvailable) wrapper around the fall accel means the player genuinely floats (no downward accel) for the coyote window. If you ever make coyote consume differently, remember it also pauses gravity — otherwise a coyote jump would launch from a slightly-lower spot.

Modifying this: coyote tuning is two numbers — CoyoteTime (window) and the one-shot CoyoteAvailable flag refilled in IDLE/RUNNING. Buffer tuning is BufferJumpRange (sensed in Player.getShapeCastCollisions). Don't reintroduce a y > 0 gravity branch here; the apex invariant guarantees it's unreachable.

src/game/entities/player/states/handlePlayerClimbing.ts 68 LOC

Vertical movement on a ladder. The simplest handler: one exit (back to IDLE), vertical accel/decel via MoveTowardsPoint, and the ladder-snap nudge that pops you onto the top platform or off the bottom. Entered from IDLE/RUNNING's ladder-grab block (which zeroes both axes on entry). Imports only PlayerStates, PlayerDirection and GameUtils — notably no SpriteAnimations, so climbing does not change the sprite (it keeps whatever was last set).

const handlePlayerClimbing = (player: Player) => void

The exit helpers — "at an end and pressing toward/idle":

const atLadderBottom =
  player.IsTouching.ladderBottom &&
  (player.Input.isDown ||
    player.Input.isNeitherUpDown ||
    player.Input.isUpDownCombo);

const atLadderTop =
  player.IsTouching.ladderTop &&
  (player.Input.isUp ||
    player.Input.isNeitherUpDown ||
    player.Input.isUpDownCombo);

You're "at the bottom" if the bottom sensor is touching and you're pressing down, pressing nothing vertical, or pressing both (i.e. anything that isn't actively climbing up). Symmetric at the top. The ladder flags come from the Ladder Sensor via Player.UpdateLadderState().

1. Transition to IDLE — the only exit, with the ladder snap:

if (!player.IsTouching.ladderCore || atLadderBottom || atLadderTop) {
  if (atLadderTop) {
    player.NextTranslation.y = player.MaxClimbSpeed;
  } else {
    player.NextTranslation.y = -player.MaxClimbSpeed;
  }
  player.IsTouching.ground = true;
  player.State = PlayerStates.IDLE;
  return;
}

Three triggers: you left the ladder core entirely, or you reached the bottom, or the top. The snap is the NextTranslation.y nudge — at the top it pushes up (+MaxClimbSpeed) to clear you onto the platform; otherwise it pushes down (-MaxClimbSpeed) to seat you on the ground below. Forcing IsTouching.ground = true the same frame hands a clean grounded state to IDLE so it doesn't immediately bounce you into FALLING. There is no JUMPING or FALLING exit from CLIMBING despite the enum comment.

Direction from input:

if (player.Input.isUp) {
  player.Direction = PlayerDirection.UP;
} else if (player.Input.isDown) {
  player.Direction = PlayerDirection.DOWN;
} else {
  player.Direction = PlayerDirection.NEUTRAL;
}

Here Direction is reused as a vertical sign (UP = +1, DOWN = -1, NEUTRAL = 0) — the same numeric enum the horizontal code uses for X, exploited for Y.

Vertical movement:

let targetSpeed = 0;
let acceleration = 0;

if (
  player.Direction === PlayerDirection.UP ||
  player.Direction === PlayerDirection.DOWN
) {
  targetSpeed = player.Direction * player.MaxClimbSpeed;
  acceleration = player.ClimbAcceleration * player.Time.Delta;
} else {
  targetSpeed = 0;
  acceleration = player.ClimbDeceleration * player.Time.Delta;
}

player.NextTranslation.y = GameUtils.MoveTowardsPoint(
  player.NextTranslation.y,
  targetSpeed,
  acceleration
);

Pressing up/down accelerates toward Direction * MaxClimbSpeed at ClimbAcceleration; releasing decelerates to a stop at the gentler ClimbDeceleration (8 vs 30 in base feel) so you ease to rest on the ladder rather than stopping dead. The signed-Direction trick makes one MoveTowardsPoint handle both up and down. No X movement and no gravity are applied — climbing fully overrides the normal physics. 

stateDiagram-v2
  state "CLIMBING" as C
  [*] --> C: entry from IDLE/RUNNING, both axes zeroed
  C --> IDLE: not ladderCore — snap down (-MaxClimbSpeed)
  C --> IDLE: atLadderTop — snap up (+MaxClimbSpeed), ground = true
  C --> IDLE: atLadderBottom — snap down, ground = true
  C --> C: accel toward Direction*MaxClimbSpeed, else decel to 0
Hard-won victory — the top snap + forced ground. Setting NextTranslation.y = +MaxClimbSpeed and IsTouching.ground = true together on a top exit is what makes dismounting a ladder land you cleanly on the platform instead of flickering CLIMBING -> FALLING -> CLIMBING at the lip.
Gotcha — no sprite update. CLIMBING never calls SpriteAnimator.ChangeState (it doesn't even import the table), so the on-ladder pose is whatever was last set before grabbing. If you want a dedicated climb animation, add a climb entry to Sprite Animations and a ChangeState call here — mind the up/down/neutral facing.

Modifying this: climb feel is three shared base-feel numbers (MaxClimbSpeed, ClimbAcceleration, ClimbDeceleration). The ladder geometry (top/core/bottom) is owned by the Ladder Sensor and pushed in via Player.UpdateLadderState(), not sensed here — fix mis-snapping at the edges there, not in this handler. Keep the IsTouching.ground = true on exit or you'll reintroduce the lip flicker.

Tip — reading the whole player loop. These handlers only write intent into player.NextTranslation and flags. The actual move (kinematic computeColliderMovement, snap-to-ground, two-sided contact dispatch) and the shapecast contact sensing that fills IsTouching both happen in Player's updateTranslation() / detectCollisions(), run right after fsm.Update() each frame. Cross-reference FSM Architecture — StateMachine for the dispatch and Feel (Attributes) for every number quoted above.

Enemy (barrels) — rolling, descending, bouncing

The Enemy is the Donkey-Kong barrel: a dynamic sphere that rolls the girders, decides whether to take a ladder, and — in its crazy flavour — bounces girder-to-girder down the screen. There is exactly one Enemy class (the old CrazyEnemy was folded in, D28): normal vs crazy is just the starting FSM state plus a bounce-drift sign. The barrel's own movement reactions (reverse at walls, edge-turn, bounce) live in its collision callbacks; the cross-entity verdicts (kill the Player, ignite the oil can) deliberately do not — they live in the contact table, which is what finally fixed the barrel/enemy bug.

src/game/entities/enemy/enemy.tssrc/game/entities/enemy/enemyStates.tssrc/game/entities/enemy/states/handleEnemyRolling.tssrc/game/entities/enemy/states/handleEnemyDescending.tssrc/game/entities/enemy/states/handleEnemyBouncing.ts

src/game/entities/enemy/enemy.ts 334 LOC

A DK barrel entity, subclass of the engine GameObject. It is constructed at runtime by the GameDirector spawner (SpawnEnemy / SpawnCrazyEnemy → new Enemy(...)), pushed into the director's single Enemies[] array, and ticked once per frame by director.Update via enemy.Update(player, firstTrashCan). It imports the engine clock Time (delta drives the bounce integration), the generic StateMachine (its three action states), the GameUtils facade (collider-type checks, the take-ladder dice, the fully-inside test), the EntityType/CollisionGroups tables, and the LadderSensor/TrashCan/Player classes it touches. Lifecycle: constructor (build the sphere physics body + arm collisions) → per-frame Update (poll ladders, run the current state handler, sync graphics) → Destroy (inherited from GameObject, fired through the gameObjectRemoved event).

The defining architectural choice is at the top of the file: normal and crazy barrels are one class, discriminated by state.

// Enemy — a DK barrel, two flavors picked at spawn (D28: data-driven, old CrazyEnemy folded in here).
// NORMAL barrel → starts ROLLING; rolls the girders and may take ladders (ROLLING ⇄ DESCENDING).
// CRAZY barrel → starts BOUNCING; vertically-tossed wild barrel, bounces girder-to-girder down the screen, never takes ladders.
// A crazy barrel never leaves BOUNCING and a normal never enters it, so State === BOUNCING is the "is this crazy?" discriminator.
Hard-won victory (D28 + the contact table). There used to be a separate CrazyEnemy class and hard-coded if (other is Player) gameOver() / if (other is TrashCan) ignite() logic inside the enemies' own collision callbacks. Two problems compounded: the two classes drifted, and the death/ignite branches fired from inside entity callbacks where a barrel-vs-barrel or barrel-vs-enemy contact could mis-route. The latest refactor (commit "Replace collision routing with contact table, finally fix barrel/enemy bug") did two things at once: (1) folded CrazyEnemy into this one data-driven Enemy (D28), so there is one class, one array, one update loop; and (2) moved every cross-entity verdict out to the declarative contactRules table, matched by EntityType. Now this file owns only the barrel's reaction to the world (walls, girders, ladders); who-kills-whom is a separate, two-sided table. Because the enemy's collision mask excludes ENEMY, barrels never even generate contacts with each other — the old bug is structurally impossible.

// Feel constants (module-scope)

Eight tuning numbers split into a "roll" group (normal barrel) and a "bounce" group (crazy barrel). They are module-private — not part of the per-level feel profile — because barrel difficulty is global, not per-character.

ConstantValueMeaning
GROUND_SPEED14Horizontal roll speed along a girder.
ROLL_FALL_SPEED9.8Downward velocity pinned every frame while rolling so the barrel hugs girders/slopes. Coincides with gravity but is hand-applied, not engine gravity.
LADDER_DESCEND_FACTOR0.65Ladder-descent speed = GROUND_SPEED * 0.65 ≈ 9.1 — a touch slower than rolling.
LADDER_BOTTOM_COOLDOWN1.0Seconds the ladder-bottom trigger is suppressed after a descent ends, so the barrel doesn't oscillate in/out of descent at the ladder foot.
LADDER_TAKE_CHANCE0.75Chance to take a ladder once the oil can is lit. While unlit the barrel ALWAYS takes ladders (see DecideTakeLadder).
BOUNCE_GRAVITY80Downward accel integrated each frame for the crazy bounce arc (hand-applied, predictable).
BOUNCE_MAX_FALL40Terminal fall speed of a bouncing barrel.
BOUNCE_IMPULSE28Upward velocity kicked on each girder contact — sets the bounce height.
BOUNCE_DRIFT_SPEED8Horizontal drift while bouncing (gentler than a roll so the toss reads as mostly vertical). Flips at walls.

// instance fields

The fields divide cleanly along the same normal/crazy seam, plus the FSM plumbing and the per-frame ladder inputs the handlers read. State and the four Is*/Did* flags are public because the state handlers (free functions in states/) and the engine StateMachine read and reassign them from outside the class.

private time!: Time; // engine clock; delta drives bounce integration

// Roll movement (NORMAL barrel)
private groundSpeed!: number;
private direction!: number;
private ladderSensorValue!: number;

// Bounce movement (CRAZY barrel)
private bounceVelocityY!: number; // current vertical velocity, integrated by gravity
private bounceDirection!: number; // horizontal drift sign (+1 right / -1 left)

// FSM state (replaces the old performSpecialRoll flag — DESCENDING ⇔ "taking
// the ladder"). Public so the engine StateMachine can read/dispatch it.
public State!: EnemyState;
private fsm!: StateMachine<Enemy, EnemyState>;

// Per-frame inputs the handlers read. Refreshed at the top of Update.
public IsGrounded!: boolean;       // on a girder (event-owned by the collision callbacks)
public IsInsideLadder!: boolean;   // fully inside a ladder core this frame
public IsAtLadderBottom!: boolean; // touching a ladder bottom (cooldown-gated)
public DidJudgeLadder!: boolean;   // rolled the take-ladder dice for this entry

private ladderBottomCooldown!: number;  // anti-oscillation timer at the ladder foot
private currentTrashCan?: TrashCan;     // the oil can, stashed each frame for the decision

// Hoisted scratch + callback so the per-frame hot path allocates nothing (these
// multiply with on-screen barrel count, the exact time you least want GC churn).
private scratchVel = { x: 0, y: 0 };
private onLadderIntersection = (otherCollider: RAPIER.Collider) => {
  /* the ladder-poll callback — shown in full under checkLadderIntersections below */
};

Line-level notes: IsGrounded is "event-owned" — it is set/cleared only by the collision callbacks (enter sets it on a girder, exit clears it), never polled, so it reflects real contact state. direction and bounceDirection are independent: a normal barrel only ever uses direction, a crazy one only bounceDirection. ladderSensorValue caches the climb direction of the ladder the barrel is currently inside, so SnapDirectionToLadder can face the roll the right way when the descent ends. The last two fields are the hot-path allocation killers: scratchVel is a single reused velocity object the three setLinvel movers write into, and onLadderIntersection is the ladder-poll callback hoisted to a bound field (created once at construction) so checkLadderIntersections stops minting a fresh closure every frame. Both matter because they multiply with the on-screen barrel count.

constructor(size: number, position: { x: number; y: number }, rotation: number = 0, crazy: boolean = false, bounceDirection: number = 1)

Builds the physics body and arms collisions. Parameters: size (sphere diameter, used for both width and height); position (spawn point); rotation (radians, default 0); crazy (the D28 flavour switch — true starts the barrel in BOUNCING); bounceDirection (the crazy barrel's horizontal drift sign, ignored by normal barrels). No return value.

super();

this.createObjectPhysics(
  EntityType.ENEMY,
  GameObjectType.SPHERE,
  { width: size, height: size },
  position,
  rotation,
  RAPIER.RigidBodyDesc.dynamic()
);

this.initializeEnemyAttributes(crazy, bounceDirection);

// Collides with platforms and the player box. (Defining the collision
// callbacks below auto-arms contact events — no enableContactEvents needed.)
this.setCollisionGroup(CollisionGroups.ENEMY);
this.setCollisionMask(
  CollisionGroups.PLATFORM | CollisionGroups.PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX
);

Line-level notes: GameObjectType.SPHERE maps to a Rapier ball collider — the barrel is literally a rolling sphere. The body is RigidBodyDesc.dynamic(), so Rapier integrates it (though the handlers override velocity every frame with setLinvel). The collision group is ENEMY and the mask is PLATFORM | PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX — so a barrel collides with girders/walls and the player box, but not with other barrels (no ENEMY bit in the mask) and not with sensors. That mask exclusion is half of why the barrel/enemy bug is gone (the other half is moving the verdicts to the contact table).

Note (auto-armed events). The constructor never calls enableContactEvents(). Because this class defines OnCollisionEnter/OnCollisionExit, GameObject.enablePhysicsEvents sees the callbacks during createObjectPhysics and turns on COLLISION_EVENTS for the collider automatically. The contact-table rules then ride the same event stream (see Physics.handleCollisionEvents, which calls both the entity callback and Contacts.Dispatch).

private initializeEnemyAttributes(crazy: boolean, bounceDirection: number)

Seeds every field and constructs the state machine. Parameters: the same crazy / bounceDirection the constructor received. Returns nothing.

this.time = this.experience.Time;

this.groundSpeed = GROUND_SPEED;
this.direction = 1;
this.ladderSensorValue = 0;

this.bounceVelocityY = 0; // starts falling under integrated gravity
if (bounceDirection >= 0) this.bounceDirection = 1;
else this.bounceDirection = -1;

this.IsGrounded = true; // assume grounded at spawn
this.IsInsideLadder = false;
this.IsAtLadderBottom = false;
this.DidJudgeLadder = false;
this.ladderBottomCooldown = 0;

// The barrel flavor is chosen here by its starting state and never changes.
if (crazy) this.State = EnemyStates.BOUNCING;
else this.State = EnemyStates.ROLLING;
this.fsm = new StateMachine<Enemy, EnemyState>(this, {
  [EnemyStates.ROLLING]: handleEnemyRolling,
  [EnemyStates.DESCENDING]: handleEnemyDescending,
  [EnemyStates.BOUNCING]: handleEnemyBouncing,
});

Line-level notes: bounceDirection is normalised to exactly ±1 here — the spawner passes Math.sign(...) which can be 0 (oil can directly below), and this collapses 0 to +1 (rightward). The barrel flavour is decided once, by the starting state, and the FSM map is wired with all three handlers regardless of flavour — a normal barrel simply never reaches the BOUNCING entry and a crazy one never reaches ROLLING/DESCENDING. This mirrors the Player's movement FSM exactly (same StateMachine generic, host owns State).

Collision callbacks — the barrel's reaction to the world

These two methods are the barrel's own movement reactions: reverse at walls, track grounded, bounce off girders. They are NOT where the game-rule verdicts live (kill player / ignite can) — those are in the contact table. The split is the whole point: the entity knows how to move through its world; it does not know it can kill anything.

public OnCollisionEnter(other: GameObject): void

Fired by Physics on every solid collision start. Branches first on flavour (State === BOUNCING), then on what was hit. Parameter: the other GameObject. Returns nothing.

const otherCollider = other.PhysicsBody?.collider(0);
if (!otherCollider) return;

// CRAZY barrel: bounce off girders, flip drift at walls. Never grounds/rolls.
if (this.State === EnemyStates.BOUNCING) {
  if (GameUtils.IsColliderType(otherCollider, EntityType.WALL)) {
    this.bounceDirection = this.bounceDirection * -1;
    return;
  }

  if (
    GameUtils.IsColliderType(otherCollider, EntityType.PLATFORM) ||
    GameUtils.IsColliderType(otherCollider, EntityType.ONE_WAY_PLATFORM)
  ) {
    // Only bounce when coming DOWN onto a girder (ignore contacts while
    // already rising), so a single landing produces one clean kick.
    if (this.bounceVelocityY <= 0) {
      this.bounceVelocityY = BOUNCE_IMPULSE;
    }
  }
  return;
}

// NORMAL barrel below — wall reverse + grounded tracking.

// Wall — reverse direction.
if (GameUtils.IsColliderType(otherCollider, EntityType.WALL)) {
  this.direction = this.direction * -1;
  return;
}

// One-way platform — grounded only when landing on top of it.
if (GameUtils.IsColliderType(otherCollider, EntityType.ONE_WAY_PLATFORM)) {
  if (this.PhysicsBody!.translation().y > otherCollider.translation().y) {
    this.IsGrounded = true;
  }
  return;
}

// Solid platform — grounded.
if (GameUtils.IsColliderType(otherCollider, EntityType.PLATFORM)) {
  this.IsGrounded = true;
  return;
}

Line-level notes: collisions hand you a raw Rapier Collider, not the class instance, so identity is checked by GameUtils.IsColliderType(collider, EntityType.X) (which reads collider.parent().userData.type) — never instanceof. The crazy bounce-kick is gated on bounceVelocityY <= 0: only re-kick when the barrel is descending onto the girder, so one landing yields one clean upward impulse instead of a jitter while it is still rising. The one-way-platform ground check compares the barrel's translation().y against the platform's — a barrel only "lands" on a one-way girder it is above, so it can rise through it from below.

public OnCollisionExit(other: GameObject): void

Fired when a solid contact ends. Clears grounded and, while rolling, turns the barrel around so it doesn't walk off the girder edge. Parameter: the other GameObject.

// Crazy barrels don't ground or edge-turn — nothing to do on exit.
if (this.State === EnemyStates.BOUNCING) return;

const otherCollider = other.PhysicsBody?.collider(0);
if (!otherCollider) return;

if (otherCollider.isSensor()) return;

if (
  GameUtils.IsColliderType(otherCollider, EntityType.ONE_WAY_PLATFORM) ||
  GameUtils.IsColliderType(otherCollider, EntityType.PLATFORM)
) {
  this.IsGrounded = false;

  if (this.State === EnemyStates.ROLLING) {
    this.direction = this.direction * -1;
  }
}

Line-level notes: the edge-turn is State === ROLLING-gated, NOT fired while DESCENDING. That is essential — taking a ladder intentionally leaves the platform downward, and if the exit handler flipped direction every time a descending barrel passed off a girder it would scramble the roll direction it resumes with. The isSensor() early-out means leaving a ladder sensor never affects grounded/direction (ladders are handled by the poll, not by collision events).

Gotcha (edge-turn coupling). IsGrounded and the wall/edge direction flips are split across two sources: walls flip on OnCollisionEnter, platform edges flip on OnCollisionExit. A barrel that rolls into a wall reverses immediately; a barrel that rolls off an open ledge reverses when it loses the platform. Both feed the same direction field the rolling handler reads next frame.

State-handler helpers — the verbs the handlers call

The free-function state handlers in states/ stay tiny by delegating the actual physics to these public methods on the Enemy.

public DecideTakeLadder(): boolean

Rolls the take-ladder dice. Returns: true if the barrel should descend the ladder it is currently inside. Always true while the oil can is unlit (the relentless DK opening); otherwise a flat LADDER_TAKE_CHANCE (0.75) roll.

if (!this.currentTrashCan?.IsOnFire) return true;
return GameUtils.CalculatePercentChance(LADDER_TAKE_CHANCE);

Line-level notes: currentTrashCan is the oil can the director passes into Update each frame. Until the very first crazy barrel lights it (IsOnFire flipped by the contact table), every barrel descends every ladder — the wave of barrels pours straight down. Once lit, barrels start branching probabilistically, which is what spreads them across the girders. CalculatePercentChance is the mathHelpers percentChance re-export (Math.random() < p).

public SnapDirectionToLadder()

Faces the roll the way the ladder sensor points. Used at the moment a descent ends (and at its start) so the barrel resumes rolling in a sensible direction.

if (this.ladderSensorValue > 0) {
  this.direction = 1;
} else if (this.ladderSensorValue < 0) {
  this.direction = -1;
}

ladderSensorValue was cached in checkLadderIntersections from the LadderSensor.Direction of whatever core the barrel was inside (positive = right, negative = left). A 0 value leaves direction untouched.

public RollHorizontally()

The per-frame normal-barrel roll. Sets the collision mask based on grounded-ness, then pins the velocity: horizontal by direction, vertical to a constant downward pull.

let mask: number = CollisionGroups.PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX;
if (this.IsGrounded) {
  mask = CollisionGroups.PLATFORM | CollisionGroups.PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX;
}
this.setCollisionMask(mask);

let vx = -this.groundSpeed;
if (this.direction >= 0) vx = this.groundSpeed;
this.scratchVel.x = vx;
this.scratchVel.y = -ROLL_FALL_SPEED;
this.PhysicsBody!.setLinvel(this.scratchVel, true);

Line-level notes: the velocity is fully authored every frame — the barrel never coasts on Rapier's integrator. y: -ROLL_FALL_SPEED is a hand-applied downward pull (≈ gravity) that keeps the sphere pressed onto the girders and sliding down slopes, but it is deterministic, not engine gravity.
