The mask is rewritten each frame: while grounded it keeps PLATFORM so the barrel rests on girders; the moment it is airborne (IsGrounded false) PLATFORM drops from the mask, so a barrel that has rolled off an edge falls cleanly without snagging on the girder lip below until it next lands. PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX is always kept so the barrel can hit the player no matter what.

public DescendLadder()

The per-frame ladder-descent move: drop platform collisions and travel straight down.

// Drop PLATFORM so the barrel passes through girders; ALWAYS keep the player
// box so it can still kill the player while descending.
this.setCollisionMask(CollisionGroups.PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX);
this.scratchVel.x = 0;
this.scratchVel.y = -this.groundSpeed * LADDER_DESCEND_FACTOR;
this.PhysicsBody!.setLinvel(this.scratchVel, true);

Line-level notes: removing PLATFORM from the mask is how the barrel passes through the girders it crosses on the way down a ladder — the same trick the player uses to climb. Keeping PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX means a descending barrel still kills the player it lands on. Horizontal velocity is hard-zeroed so the descent is perfectly vertical regardless of the roll direction it came in with.

public Bounce()

The per-frame crazy-barrel arc. Integrates the hand-rolled gravity, clamps to terminal speed, and applies horizontal drift. Unlike the roll/descend methods it never touches the mask — a crazy barrel always keeps PLATFORM so it bounces off girders.

this.bounceVelocityY -= BOUNCE_GRAVITY * this.time.Delta;
if (this.bounceVelocityY < -BOUNCE_MAX_FALL) {
  this.bounceVelocityY = -BOUNCE_MAX_FALL;
}

let vx = -BOUNCE_DRIFT_SPEED;
if (this.bounceDirection >= 0) vx = BOUNCE_DRIFT_SPEED;
this.scratchVel.x = vx;
this.scratchVel.y = this.bounceVelocityY;
this.PhysicsBody!.setLinvel(this.scratchVel, true);

Line-level notes: this is the one place time.Delta matters — bounceVelocityY is integrated frame-rate-independently (-= BOUNCE_GRAVITY * Δt), whereas the roll's downward speed is a flat constant. The upward kick is not here; it is applied in OnCollisionEnter when the bouncing barrel lands on a girder (bounceVelocityY = BOUNCE_IMPULSE). So Bounce only ever pulls down and clamps; the bounce up is event-driven. Drift sign flips at walls (also in OnCollisionEnter), so the barrel weaves down the screen.

Frame update

private checkLadderIntersections()

Refreshes this frame's ladder inputs (IsInsideLadder, IsAtLadderBottom, ladderSensorValue) by polling sensor overlaps with the hoisted onLadderIntersection callback — deliberately NOT event-driven (D9, the "fully inside" climb idiom). Runs the bottom-cooldown timer too. The method body itself is now just the cooldown tick, the two flag resets, and the poll call; every per-overlap test lives in the bound field.

if (this.ladderBottomCooldown > 0) {
  this.ladderBottomCooldown -= this.time.Delta;
}

this.IsInsideLadder = false;
this.IsAtLadderBottom = false;

// Poll intersections with the hoisted callback (this.onLadderIntersection) — no
// per-frame closure allocation. (Not event-driven: the "fully inside" climb
// idiom, D9, needs continuous polling.)
this.Physics.World.intersectionPairsWith(
  this.PhysicsBody!.collider(0),
  this.onLadderIntersection
);

The per-overlap work lives in onLadderIntersection, a bound arrow field built once in the field initializer (so this is already captured) rather than a closure re-created on every checkLadderIntersections call:

private onLadderIntersection = (otherCollider: RAPIER.Collider) => {
  const ladder = this.Physics.GetGameObjectFromCollider(otherCollider);

  // Fully inside a ladder core with a climb direction — a descent candidate.
  if (
    GameUtils.IsColliderType(otherCollider, EntityType.LADDER_CORE_SENSOR) &&
    ladder instanceof LadderSensor &&
    ladder.Direction !== 0 &&
    GameUtils.IsObjectFullyInsideSensor(otherCollider, this)
  ) {
    this.ladderSensorValue = ladder.Direction;
    this.IsInsideLadder = true;
  }

  // At the ladder bottom — flag it (the DESCENDING handler ends the descent here)
  // AND arm the anti-oscillation cooldown immediately, on first contact in ANY
  // state, so a descent through an overlapping core+bottom sensor completes.
  if (
    GameUtils.IsColliderType(otherCollider, EntityType.LADDER_BOTTOM_SENSOR) &&
    this.ladderBottomCooldown <= 0
  ) {
    this.IsAtLadderBottom = true;
    this.ladderBottomCooldown = LADDER_BOTTOM_COOLDOWN;
  }
};

Line-level notes: World.intersectionPairsWith walks every sensor the barrel's collider currently overlaps (the barrel is a solid, but sensors register intersections against solids). Physics.GetGameObjectFromCollider resolves the raw collider back to its owning GameObject so the instanceof LadderSensor + ladder.Direction reads work. Two distinct conditions: a core hit only counts when the barrel is IsObjectFullyInsideSensor (horizontally contained — the D9 gate, the same one the player's climb uses via GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggeredObjectFullyInside); a bottom hit is gated by the cooldown.

Allocation-free per-barrel hot path. Both halves of the barrel's per-frame work used to allocate: checkLadderIntersections passed an inline (otherCollider) => {…} closure to intersectionPairsWith (a fresh function object every frame), and the three movers (RollHorizontally, DescendLadder, Bounce) each built a fresh { x, y } literal for setLinvel. Now the closure is the hoisted onLadderIntersection field and the velocity is the single reused scratchVel — Rapier reads both synchronously, so reuse is safe. With dozens of barrels on screen those were the allocations that fed GC churn at the worst moment; the hot path now mints nothing per frame.
Hard-won victory (the bottom cooldown armed on entry, not on exit). The cooldown is set true + armed on the first bottom-sensor contact in any state — including while a barrel is still rolling toward the ladder — not only when a descent ends. The source comment spells out why: arm the cooldown immediately, "on first contact in ANY state, so a descent through an overlapping core+bottom sensor completes." When a ladder's core and bottom sensors overlap, a barrel that began descending would otherwise see IsAtLadderBottom on its very first descent frame, immediately flip back to ROLLING, and never actually descend. Arming the cooldown the instant the bottom sensor is touched suppresses that spurious bottom flag long enough for the descent to take.

public Update(player: Player, trashCan?: TrashCan)

The per-frame entry point, called by GameDirector.Update as enemy.Update(this.Player, firstTrashCan). Parameters: player (the uniform update contract — but the enemy deliberately ignores it), trashCan (the oil can it reads for the ladder decision). Returns nothing.

// Uniform update contract: the enemy ignores the player (it doesn't track or
// chase) and reads the trash can (the oil can) for its ladder decision.
void player;

if (this.IsBeingDestroyed) {
  return;
}

// Stash this frame's inputs, refresh ladder state, run the current state.
// Crazy (BOUNCING) barrels never take ladders, so skip the poll for them.
this.currentTrashCan = trashCan;
if (this.State !== EnemyStates.BOUNCING) {
  this.checkLadderIntersections();
}
this.fsm.Update();

this.syncGraphicsToPhysics();

Line-level notes: void player; is an explicit "I am intentionally not using this argument" — the barrel doesn't chase, unlike a smarter enemy might, but it still conforms to the shared Update(player, trashCan?) signature so the director can tick it in the same loop as everything else. The IsBeingDestroyed guard short-circuits a barrel that has already been flagged for teardown (e.g. it hit the player or fell in the can) so it doesn't run a frame on a half-dead body. Crazy barrels skip checkLadderIntersections entirely — they never descend, so the poll would be wasted work. fsm.Update() dispatches to exactly one of the three handlers; syncGraphicsToPhysics() (inherited) then copies the body's translation/rotation onto the Three.js sprite.

sequenceDiagram
  participant D as GameDirector.Update
  participant E as Enemy.Update
  participant P as checkLadderIntersections
  participant F as StateMachine
  participant H as state handler
  D->>E: Update(player, firstTrashCan)
  E->>E: currentTrashCan = trashCan
  alt State != BOUNCING
    E->>P: poll ladder sensors (D9 fully-inside)
  end
  E->>F: fsm.Update()
  F->>H: handlers[State](enemy)
  H->>H: RollHorizontally / DescendLadder / Bounce
  E->>E: syncGraphicsToPhysics()

Modifying this: tune barrel feel with the module constants at the top (roll speed, ladder odds, bounce height/drift) — they are global, not per-level. To add a new barrel behaviour, add a state to enemyStates and a handler in states/, then wire it into the fsm map in initializeEnemyAttributes; do not add a subclass (the D28 lesson was one class, many states). Never add "kills X / wins Y" logic to OnCollisionEnter — that belongs in the contact table; this file is strictly the barrel's reaction to the world. If you give barrels a new sensor interaction, follow checkLadderIntersections (poll, don't event) only if it needs the D9 fully-inside semantics; otherwise prefer a contact rule.

src/game/entities/enemy/enemyStates.ts 17 LOC

The enemy's state enum — a frozen string-const map plus a derived union type, mirroring PlayerStates and CollisionGroups. It is imported by enemy.ts (the State field, the FSM map keys) and by all three state handlers (to assign transitions). String values (not numeric) so debug logs and the StateMachine's Record<S,...> handler map read clearly.

const EnemyStates = {
  // Rolling along a girder; at an unjudged ladder it decides whether to descend. -> DESCENDING
  ROLLING: "rolling",

  // Taking a ladder — straight down, passing through platforms. -> ROLLING at the bottom sensor
  DESCENDING: "descending",

  // Crazy barrel: bounces girder-to-girder, never takes ladders. Terminal (marks it as crazy).
  BOUNCING: "bouncing",
} as const;

// Type for enemy state values — ensures only valid states are used.
export type EnemyState = (typeof EnemyStates)[keyof typeof EnemyStates];

export default EnemyStates;

The header comment ties the file to the project's decision log: "Enemy state machine for the barrel enemy (DK normal/crazy); game-side per ADR-0003 / D3." The D3 / ADR-0003 rule is that game-specific FSMs live in the game layer (here), driven by the engine-pure generic StateMachine — the engine never names "rolling" or "bouncing". as const freezes the values to string literals so EnemyState is the exact union "rolling" | "descending" | "bouncing", which is what makes the FSM handler map exhaustively type-checked.

stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> ROLLING : normal barrel
  [*] --> BOUNCING : crazy barrel (D28)
  ROLLING --> DESCENDING : inside ladder core (D9) && DecideTakeLadder()
  DESCENDING --> ROLLING : at ladder bottom sensor
  BOUNCING --> BOUNCING : terminal (never leaves)
Note. BOUNCING is terminal — there is no transition out of it, which is precisely why State === EnemyStates.BOUNCING doubles as the "is this a crazy barrel?" test throughout enemy.ts. A normal barrel only ever ping-pongs between ROLLING and DESCENDING.

Modifying this: add a value here first (with a comment naming its entry/exit transitions), then add the matching handler to the FSM map in enemy.ts. The union type updates automatically from as const, so a forgotten handler is a compile error.

src/game/entities/enemy/states/handleEnemyRolling.ts 31 LOC

The ROLLING state handler — a free function (enemy: Enemy) => void matching the StateMachine handler shape. The engine FSM calls it once per frame while enemy.State === ROLLING. It owns the per-frame roll and the once-per-entry ladder decision; the wall-reverse and edge-turn that also belong to rolling live in the enemy's collision callbacks, not here.

const handleEnemyRolling = (enemy: Enemy) => void

Decide-then-act. If the barrel is fully inside an un-judged ladder, roll the take-ladder dice exactly once; if it takes, snap direction, switch to DESCENDING, and begin the descent this same frame. Otherwise just roll.

// Change state
if (enemy.IsInsideLadder) {
  // Decide once per continuous occupancy of the ladder core.
  if (!enemy.DidJudgeLadder) {
    enemy.DidJudgeLadder = true;

    if (enemy.DecideTakeLadder()) {
      enemy.SnapDirectionToLadder();
      enemy.State = EnemyStates.DESCENDING;
      enemy.DescendLadder(); // begin the descent on this same frame
      return;
    }
  }
} else {
  // Off the ladder — re-arm the decision for the next ladder entered.
  enemy.DidJudgeLadder = false;
}

// Handle Rolling state
enemy.RollHorizontally();

Line-level notes: DidJudgeLadder is the "decide once" latch — without it the dice would be re-rolled every frame the barrel sits inside the core, so a barrel that declined would eventually take the ladder by repeated coin-flips. It is set true on the first judged frame and only re-armed (false) once the barrel is off the ladder again, so each continuous occupancy gets exactly one decision. Calling DescendLadder() right after setting State = DESCENDING means the velocity flips to straight-down on the same frame the decision is made (no one-frame stall), and the return skips the trailing RollHorizontally() so the two move methods never fight over the velocity in one frame.

Gotcha (transition is next-frame for the handler, this-frame for the move). Reassigning enemy.State here does not re-dispatch within this fsm.Update() — the StateMachine reads State once per update, so the DESCENDING handler first runs next frame. The handler bridges that gap by calling DescendLadder() itself, so the physics already reflect the descent before handleEnemyDescending takes over.

src/game/entities/enemy/states/handleEnemyDescending.ts 21 LOC

The DESCENDING state handler. Runs each frame while the barrel is taking a ladder. It watches for the ladder-bottom sensor and, on reaching it, lands and resumes rolling; otherwise it keeps descending.

const handleEnemyDescending = (enemy: Enemy) => void

If IsAtLadderBottom (set by the cooldown-gated poll), re-ground, switch back to ROLLING, and roll this same frame. Otherwise keep descending straight down.

// Change state
if (enemy.IsAtLadderBottom) {
  // The cooldown was already armed when the bottom sensor fired (in the
  // enemy's ladder poll); just land and resume rolling on this same frame.
  enemy.IsGrounded = true; // landed at the ladder foot, back on the girder
  enemy.State = EnemyStates.ROLLING;
  enemy.RollHorizontally();
  return;
}

// Handle Descending state
enemy.DescendLadder();

Line-level notes: the handler manually sets IsGrounded = true because at the ladder foot the platform-collision callback may not have re-fired yet (the barrel had PLATFORM dropped from its mask during descent) — without it the very next RollHorizontally() would think the barrel is airborne and drop PLATFORM again. It calls RollHorizontally() in the same frame (which restores PLATFORM to the mask and starts horizontal motion) so the resume is seamless, then returns to skip the trailing DescendLadder(). The anti-oscillation cooldown that keeps this from immediately re-triggering a descent was already armed back when the bottom sensor first fired in checkLadderIntersections — see the victory callout there.

src/game/entities/enemy/states/handleEnemyBouncing.ts 10 LOC

The BOUNCING state handler — the crazy barrel's whole per-frame life. It is the simplest handler in the game: a single delegation, because the bounce arc (integrated gravity + clamp + drift) lives in enemy.Bounce and the reactions (girder kick, wall drift-flip) live in the collision callbacks. There is no transition out — BOUNCING is terminal.

const handleEnemyBouncing = (enemy: Enemy) => void

Apply this frame's bounce step. No state check, no ladder logic.

const handleEnemyBouncing = (enemy: Enemy) => {
  enemy.Bounce();
};

Why so thin: the discriminator State === BOUNCING short-circuits the ladder poll in Update (crazy barrels never descend) and routes every girder/wall response through OnCollisionEnter's bouncing branch. So by the time this handler runs, the only thing left to do per frame is integrate the arc — which is exactly one call. This is the payoff of the D28 fold-in: the "crazy" behaviour is one extra handler plus a couple of branches, not a whole parallel class.

Modifying this: if you want crazy barrels to eventually settle into rolling, this is where you'd add the exit transition (e.g. when bounce energy decays past a threshold, set enemy.State = EnemyStates.ROLLING). Today the design intent is a wild barrel that bounces its whole life, so the handler stays a one-liner.

World Entities — platforms, sensors, teleporter, trash can, win flag

These six classes are the level furniture: the solid floors and one-way girders the player walks on, the invisible trigger zones that move the camera and mark ladders, the teleporter, the oil can the enemies dive into, and the goal flag. Each is a thin game subclass of one of the two engine base classes — GameObject (solid) or GameSensor (pass-through) — that adds a little named state and almost no behavior. The behavior that reacts to these zones lives elsewhere: in the declarative contact table (Game Config — contactRules) and in the per-frame loop in Game Director. None of them are constructed directly; they are all built by the factory registry (Game Config — entityFactories) from parsed GLB level rows.

src/game/entities/platform.ts src/game/entities/cameraSensor.ts src/game/entities/ladderSensor.ts src/game/entities/teleporter.ts src/game/entities/trashCan.ts src/game/entities/winFlag.ts
Note — the two base classes, in one breath. GameObject (see Engine Entities — gameObject) owns the Rapier rigid body + collider, the THREE mesh, the cached CurrentTranslation/CurrentSize mirrors, collision-group helpers, and the OnCollisionEnter/Exit + OnSensorEnter/Exit override hooks. GameSensor (see Engine Entities — gameSensor) extends it with two things only: setAsSensor(true) (turns the collider non-solid and arms KINEMATIC_FIXED active-collision-types so a fixed sensor still notices the kinematic player) and IsFullyInside(other) (X-axis AABB containment). The split below is exact: Platform and TrashCan are solid GameObjects; CameraSensor, LadderSensor, Teleporter and WinFlag are GameSensors. 
flowchart TD
  GO["GameObject (engine: body + collider + mesh)"]
  GS["GameSensor (engine: setAsSensor + IsFullyInside)"]
  GO --> GS
  GO --> Platform
  GO --> TrashCan
  GS --> CameraSensor
  GS --> LadderSensor
  GS --> Teleporter
  GS --> WinFlag
Tip — how a zone turns into an action. A sensor entity here defines no callbacks of its own. Instead its constructor calls enableContactEvents() to arm Rapier collision events, and the rule "Player enters X → do Y" is registered once in Game Config — contactRules. The engine's Physics.Contacts registry (Engine Entities — contactRegistry) matches the two colliders by their type flag and fires the rule's handler. That is why these files are so short: they own data (a destination, a camera anchor, a direction, a fire flag) and expose one action — the table owns the when.

src/game/entities/platform.ts 90 LOC

The solid world geometry: every floor girder, every wall, and the special "one-way" girders you can jump up through from below but stand on from above. It is the only entity in this section with a real per-frame Update. One class covers three placed types (Platform, OneWayPlatform, and Wall) — the factory decides which by passing the isOneWayPlatform flag and, for walls, calling SetType(EntityType.WALL) afterward (see Game Config — entityFactories, createPlatform/createWall). It imports GameObject (base), GameObjectType (collider-shape enum — always CUBE here), CollisionGroups (puts it on the PLATFORM layer), EntityType (routing strings), and Player (its Update reads the player's Y and state). Lifecycle: constructed by the factory, driven each frame from GameDirector.Update, destroyed with the level via GameObject.Destroy().

private isOneWayPlatform: boolean private oneWayEnablePoint: number public FloorLevel = 0 public IsEdge = false public IsOneWayActive = false

The named, typed metadata that replaced the old anonymous userData.value0/1/3 bag. isOneWayPlatform is the immutable kind flag set in the constructor. oneWayEnablePoint is the Y threshold above which a one-way platform turns solid. FloorLevel (which girder this is, 1..N) and IsEdge (does an enemy turn around here) are read by enemy floor logic. IsOneWayActive is the live toggle — true means "currently pass-through-from-below". The comment is load-bearing: it is only ever true for one-way platforms; a regular platform never flips it. 

public FloorLevel: number = 0;
public IsEdge: boolean = false;
// For a one-way platform: is it currently pass-through-from-below active?
// (Only ever true for one-way platforms — regular platforms never toggle it.)
public IsOneWayActive: boolean = false;

constructor(size: { width; height; depth }, position: { x; y }, rotation: number, isOneWayPlatform = false)

Builds the solid body. Params: size (the depth component is accepted but the 2D collider only uses width/height), position in game X/Y, rotation in radians (slopes are rotated cubes), and the isOneWayPlatform kind flag (default false → a normal solid platform/wall). After super() wires the engine singletons, the body of the constructor: 

// The Y above which a one-way platform becomes solid (= the platform's own
// height); the player passes up through it from below.
this.oneWayEnablePoint = position.y;

// Determine platform's routing type
let objectName: string = EntityType.PLATFORM;
if (isOneWayPlatform) objectName = EntityType.ONE_WAY_PLATFORM;

// Cuboid collider — this is a cuboid/sphere engine (slopes = rotated cubes).
this.createObjectPhysics(objectName, GameObjectType.CUBE, size, position, rotation);

this.isOneWayPlatform = isOneWayPlatform;
this.SetType(objectName);

// Set collision groups and masks
this.setCollisionGroup(CollisionGroups.PLATFORM);
this.setCollisionMask(CollisionGroups.DEFAULT);

Line-level notes:

  • Default enable point = position.y. The threshold defaults to the platform's own centre Y. The factory immediately overrides it with SetOneWayEnablePoint(metaNum(meta,"oneway") || data.position[2]), so an author can tune the pass-through line in Blender; absent that meta it falls back to the same Y.
  • objectName picks the routing string (Platform vs OneWayPlatform), then SetType(objectName) stamps it onto the body's userData.type so the contact table and collision logger can match it. Walls call SetType(EntityType.WALL) afterward in the factory — same class, different flag.
  • CUBE is hard-coded. The comment ties back to the engine decision that this is a cuboid/sphere-only physics world (see Engine Types — gameObjectType, the D5 lean): no polyline/convex colliders, slopes are just rotated cubes.
  • Group PLATFORM, mask DEFAULT. It lives on the platform layer and collides with everything that lists it — the player bounding box and enemies both mask in the platform bit (see Game Types — gameCollisionGroups). The group/mask helpers preserve the other half of the bitfield, so order (group then mask) is safe here.

public SetPlatformFloorLevel(value?: number) public SetEdgePlatform(value?: number) public SetOneWayEnablePoint(value: number)

Three small setters the factory calls right after construction from the texture-name meta bag. SetPlatformFloorLevel(value ?? 0) stores the floor index. SetEdgePlatform coerces a number to a boolean — IsEdge = (value ?? 0) > 0 — because the meta arrives as a number (edge=1) but the field is a flag. SetOneWayEnablePoint overwrites the constructor's default threshold. 

public SetEdgePlatform(value?: number) {
  this.IsEdge = (value ?? 0) > 0;
}

public Update(player: Player)

The heart of the one-way mechanic, called every frame for every platform from GameDirector.Update (it pushes this.Platforms into a uniform UpdatableEntity[] loop and calls e.Update?.(this.Player, firstTrashCan)). It mutates IsOneWayActive based on where the player's feet are and whether the player is climbing. 

public Update(player: Player) {
  // Exit early if not a OneWayPlatform
  if (!this.isOneWayPlatform) {
    return;
  }

  // Solid once the player's feet rise above the enable point; pass-through below.
  if (player.CurrentTranslation.y - player.CurrentSize.y / 2 > this.oneWayEnablePoint) {
    this.IsOneWayActive = false;
  } else {
    this.IsOneWayActive = true;
  }

  // While the player is climbing, force this platform to pass-through so the
  // player can move up/down a ladder without landing on one-way platforms.
  if (player.State === "climbing") {
    this.IsOneWayActive = true;
  }
}
  • Early-out for non-one-way platforms. Regular platforms and walls share this class but must never toggle, so the guard returns before touching IsOneWayActive. This is why the field's comment promises it is "only ever true for one-way platforms".
  • Feet, not centre. The comparison uses player.CurrentTranslation.y - player.CurrentSize.y / 2 — the bottom edge of the player. Once the feet clear oneWayEnablePoint the platform becomes solid (IsOneWayActive = false) so the player lands; while the feet are still below it, it stays pass-through so the jump-up doesn't bonk.
  • Climbing override. The player.State === "climbing" string compare forces pass-through regardless of Y, so a player on a ladder never snags on a one-way girder mid-climb.
Gotcha — ordering is mandatory. GameDirector.Update calls Player.Update() first, then the platform loop, precisely so this Update reads this frame's player position and state (the inline comment at the call site spells it out). If you ever reorder the director loop and update platforms before the player, the one-way toggle goes one frame stale and the player will clip or bonce on girders.
Note — who consumes IsOneWayActive. This Update only sets the flag; the actual "let the player pass / stand" decision is made by the player/physics collision path that reads it (see Player — states). The platform is a pure state holder per frame. 
sequenceDiagram
  participant GD as GameDirector.Update
  participant P as Player.Update
  participant PL as Platform.Update
  GD->>P: Player.Update() (writes this frame pos + State)
  GD->>PL: Platform.Update(player)
  PL->>PL: feet > enablePoint ? solid : pass-through
  PL->>PL: State == "climbing" ? force pass-through

Modifying this: to add a new authored knob (say a moving platform's speed), add a private field, a SetX setter, and read the meta in createPlatform — do not revive the old value0/1 index hop. To change one-way feel, the only tuning surface is oneWayEnablePoint (per-instance via the oneway= meta) and the feet-vs-centre comparison in Update. Keep the non-one-way early-return first or you will accidentally make solid platforms pass-through.

src/game/entities/cameraSensor.ts 57 LOC

An invisible trigger zone that re-anchors the camera when the player walks into it. It is a GameSensor that holds two things: a PositionData anchor (authored in Blender as cam=x_y_z on the mesh's texture) and a reference to the engine Camera. The "Player enters → adjust camera" rule lives in the contact table (Game Config — contactRules); this class only exposes the action. It imports THREE (for the Vector3 anchor), RAPIER (to pass an explicit fixed rigid-body desc), GameSensor, GameObjectType, Camera (engine), and EntityType. Built by createCameraSensor in the factory registry, which hands it the shared ctx.camera and fills PositionData from the meta.

public PositionData: THREE.Vector3 private camera: Camera

PositionData is the camera anchor to ease to on enter (public so the factory's SetCameraPositionData and the action method can touch it). camera is the engine camera this zone repositions — injected through the constructor rather than pulled from the Experience singleton, which keeps the sensor honest about its one dependency.

constructor(size: { width; height }, position: { x; y }, rotation = 0, camera: Camera)

Builds the sensor body and arms it for the contact table. 

this.PositionData = new THREE.Vector3(0, 0, 0);
this.camera = camera;

this.createObjectPhysics(
  EntityType.CAMERA_SENSOR,
  GameObjectType.CUBE,
  { width: size.width, height: size.height },
  position,
  rotation,
  RAPIER.RigidBodyDesc.fixed()
);

this.setAsSensor(true);
// Take part in the contact table even though we define no callback here.
this.enableContactEvents();

// Uncomment to visualize camera sensors in debug mode
// this.createObjectGraphicsDebug("yellow", 0.1);
  • Explicit RAPIER.RigidBodyDesc.fixed(). Passed as the sixth arg to createObjectPhysics (which also defaults to fixed) — a self-documenting "this never moves" for trigger zones.
  • setAsSensor(true) then enableContactEvents(). The order matters: setAsSensor (from GameSensor) makes the collider non-solid and sets KINEMATIC_FIXED active types so the kinematic player is noticed by this fixed sensor; enableContactEvents() (from GameObject) arms COLLISION_EVENTS on the collider. Because the class defines no OnSensorEnter override, the engine's auto-arm in enablePhysicsEvents would not turn events on — so the explicit enableContactEvents() is what lets this table-driven sensor fire at all.
  • Debug mesh commented out. createObjectGraphicsDebug("yellow", 0.1) is left as a one-line uncomment to visualize the zone.

public SetCameraPositionData(newPositionData: THREE.Vector3)

Stores the anchor. Called by the factory after parsing cam=x_y_z into a THREE.Vector3(camX, camY, camZ).

public ApplyCameraOnEnter()

The single action this zone performs, invoked by the contact rule ((sensor as CameraSensor).ApplyCameraOnEnter()). It pins the camera's rest anchor: 

public ApplyCameraOnEnter() {
  // Pins the camera's rest anchor to this zone's authored x,y. On a non-follow
  // level the camera eases to that spot on both axes (fixed/zone camera); on a
  // follow level only the Y is used (pins the vertical baseline the follow eases
  // around). Author the spot as `cam=x_y` on the sensor's texture.
  this.camera.SetBaselinePosition(this.PositionData.x, this.PositionData.y);
}

It forwards PositionData.x and .y to Camera.SetBaselinePosition (see Engine Camera — camera), which sets baselineX/baselineY and flips hasSensorBaseline = true. The Z component of PositionData is intentionally unused here — the 2D camera anchor is X/Y only. On a zone (non-follow) level the camera eases to both axes; on a follow level only Y is consumed and X keeps tracking the player.

Note — decoupling seam. The sensor never touches the camera's transform directly; it calls one public method. That is the same C5 "camera is engine-pure" discipline used in the director: the camera consumes plain scalars and snapshots, never game objects.

Modifying this: to add a per-zone zoom or follow-mode switch, add fields + setters here and a second line in ApplyCameraOnEnter calling a new public camera method — keep the camera ignorant of CameraSensor. Remember an enter-only rule: there is no ApplyCameraOnExit, so two overlapping zones will leave the last-entered anchor pinned.

src/game/entities/ladderSensor.ts 44 LOC

Marks a ladder region. The smallest entity here: a GameSensor carrying a single numeric Direction. Unlike the other sensors, it is not event-driven and does not call enableContactEvents() — ladder detection is the hard-won "fully inside" polling path (D9), read every frame by GameDirector. One class serves three roles — top, core, and bottom — distinguished only by the EntityType string the factory stamps via SetType, and sorted into three separate director arrays (LadderTopSensors / LadderCoreSensors / LadderBottomSensors). Imports RAPIER (fixed body desc), GameSensor, GameObjectType, EntityType.

Hard-won victory — D9, the "fully inside" climb gate. Ladder mounting is deliberately kept out of the contact/event system. The file's header comment states it plainly: ladder detection is "the hard-won 'fully inside' climb behavior (D9), polled by GameDirector / read by the player + enemy. That path is preserved and deferred — this sensor defines no contact callbacks, so it arms no physics events." The bug D9 prevents: an enemy or player brushing the edge of a ladder collider and snapping into climb state. Instead, GameDirector.Update polls GameUtils.IsAnySensorTriggeredObjectFullyInside(this.LadderCoreSensors, this.Player), which only returns true when GameSensor.IsFullyInside confirms the player's whole X extent sits inside the core sensor's X extent. Top/bottom use the looser IsAnySensorTriggered.

public Direction: number = 0

Climb direction: positive = right, negative = left, 0 = neutral. Read by the player's climbing state and by enemy ladder logic to bias horizontal travel while on the ladder.

constructor(size: { width; height }, position: { x; y }, rotation = 0)

Builds a cuboid sensor and marks it as a sensor — and notably stops there.

// Cuboid sensor — this is a cuboid/sphere engine. (Ladders were always boxes
// anyway; the polyline path was never reached.)
this.createObjectPhysics(
  EntityType.LADDER_SENSOR,
  GameObjectType.CUBE,
  { width: size.width, height: size.height },
  position,
  rotation,
  RAPIER.RigidBodyDesc.fixed()
);

this.setAsSensor(true);

// Uncomment to visualize ladder sensors in debug mode
// this.createObjectGraphicsDebug("blue", 0.5);
  • No enableContactEvents(). This is the deliberate difference from CameraSensor/Teleporter/WinFlag. Because the class also defines no OnSensorEnter/Exit, the engine's auto-arm leaves COLLISION_EVENTS off — the sensor fires no physics callbacks at all, by design. It is detected only by the per-frame polling helpers.
  • Stamped LADDER_SENSOR at build, re-typed by the factory. createObjectPhysics sets the generic type; createLadderSensor then calls sensor.SetType(type) with the specific top/core/bottom EntityType and pushes it into the matching array.

public SetLadderValue(value?: number)

Sets Direction = value ?? 0 from the dir= meta. Positive right, negative left, 0 neutral — the same coercion-with-fallback shape used by the platform setters.

flowchart TD
  LT["LadderTopSensor (loose: IsAnySensorTriggered)"]
  LC["LadderCoreSensor (strict: IsFullyInside / D9)"]
  LB["LadderBottomSensor (loose: IsAnySensorTriggered)"]
  GD["GameDirector.Update polls all three"]
  GD --> LT
  GD --> LC
  GD --> LB
  GD --> UP["Player.UpdateLadderState(top, coreInside, bottom)"]

Modifying this: if you ever convert ladders to event-driven (the director comment says "will convert to sensor callbacks later"), you would add enableContactEvents() here and a contact rule — but you must replace the strict IsFullyInside gate, or you reintroduce the edge-brush bug D9 fixed. To add a third climb axis or speed, extend Direction into a richer field and read it in the player's climbing state.

src/game/entities/teleporter.ts 48 LOC

A pass-through zone that sends whatever enters it to a stored destination. A GameSensor that owns one RAPIER.Vector2 destination and exposes it through a read-only Destination getter. The rule "Player enters Teleporter → teleport to its destination" lives in the contact table; this class just holds the data. Imports RAPIER (the Vector2 type and fixed body desc), GameSensor, GameObjectType, EntityType. Built by createTeleporter, which parses dest=x_y from the meta. Notably, a SPHERE collider — the only round trigger in this set.

Note — newly wired. The factory comment records that there was originally no importTeleporters step, so a Blender-placed teleporter was silently dropped (teleporters[] was declared and destroyed but never filled). createTeleporter fixed that, mirroring the other sensors.

private positionData: RAPIER.Vector2

The destination entrants are sent to. Private — the only read path is the Destination getter, the only write path is SetTeleportPosition.

constructor(size: { width; height }, position: { x; y }, rotation = 0)

this.createObjectPhysics(
  EntityType.TELEPORTER,
  GameObjectType.SPHERE,
  { width: size.width, height: size.height },
  position,
  rotation,
  RAPIER.RigidBodyDesc.fixed()
);

this.positionData = new RAPIER.Vector2(0, 0);

this.setAsSensor(true);
// Take part in the contact table even though we define no callback here.
this.enableContactEvents();
this.SetTeleportPosition(0, 0);

this.createObjectGraphicsDebug("teal", 0.1);
  • GameObjectType.SPHERE → a ball collider (radius = width/2 in GameObject.createCollider). Teleporters read more naturally as a circular pad.
  • setAsSensor(true) + enableContactEvents() — same table-driven arming as CameraSensor: non-solid trigger, COLLISION_EVENTS on, no callback here.
  • SetTeleportPosition(0, 0) is redundant-but-harmless — it re-creates the zero vector just assigned two lines up; the real destination is set later by the factory.
  • Debug mesh is on here (not commented) — createObjectGraphicsDebug("teal", 0.1) always draws a faint teal sphere, unlike the other sensors whose debug draw is commented out.

public SetTeleportPosition(x: number, y: number) public get Destination(): RAPIER.Vector2

SetTeleportPosition replaces positionData with a fresh new RAPIER.Vector2(x, y) (the factory feeds it dest[0]/dest[1] from metaCoords). Destination is the read-only getter the contact rule uses: 

// from contactRules.ts — Player enters Teleporter -> teleport to its destination
(teleporter, player) => {
  const dest = (teleporter as Teleporter).Destination;
  (player as Player).TeleportToPosition(dest.x, dest.y);
}

Player.TeleportToPosition is the inherited GameObject.TeleportToPosition (computes a delta from CurrentTranslation and calls PhysicsBody.setTranslation). So the teleporter never moves the player itself — it just surrenders the coordinate to the rule, which moves the entrant.

Modifying this: to teleport enemies too, you do not change this file — you add a second contact rule pairing TELEPORTER with ENEMY in Game Config — contactRules. To add a one-shot/cooldown, hold a flag here and check it inside the rule handler.

src/game/entities/trashCan.ts 31 LOC

The oil can the Donkey-Kong barrels dive into — a solid GameObject (not a sensor) so enemies physically fall in. Its only state is IsOnFire, flipped true by the contact table when an enemy lands in it. Imports GameObject, GameObjectType, CollisionGroups, EntityType. Built by createTrashCan; the director keeps a list and hands TrashCans[0] to every enemy each frame as the target to aim for.

public IsOnFire: boolean

True once an enemy fell in and lit it. Set in the constructor to false, flipped to true by the contact rule, and reset to false for all cans on level (re)load by GameDirector (this.TrashCans.forEach(c => (c.IsOnFire = false))). It drives the can's burning visual / behavior.

constructor(size: { width; height; depth }, position: { x; y }, rotation: number)

this.createObjectPhysics(
  EntityType.TRASH_CAN,
  GameObjectType.CUBE,
  size,
  position,
  rotation
);

this.IsOnFire = false;

// Collide with the default world so enemies can fall in.
this.setCollisionGroup(CollisionGroups.DEFAULT);
this.setCollisionMask(CollisionGroups.DEFAULT);
  • No setAsSensor. It is solid: enemies collide with it and drop inside, which is the whole gag. It also defines no OnCollisionEnter override, so the engine auto-arm leaves events off — instead the ENEMY × TRASH_CAN rule in the contact table does the work (and the engine arms events for any collider named in a registered rule).
  • Group and mask are both DEFAULT (all bits). Unlike the platform (which sits on its own PLATFORM bit), the can lives on the default world layer so enemies — which mask the default world — register the contact and fall in.

// the ignite rule (contactRules.ts)

The TrashCan has no method that lights itself; the rule does. From Game Config — contactRules: 

// Enemy falls into a TrashCan -> enemy destroyed, can catches fire.
contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.ENEMY),
  byType(EntityType.TRASH_CAN),
  "enter",
  (enemy, can) => {
    (can as TrashCan).IsOnFire = true;
    Emitter.emit("gameObjectRemoved", enemy);
  }
);

Two effects on one solid-collision "enter": the can's IsOnFire flips, and the engine emits gameObjectRemoved on the event bus (Engine Events — eventBus) to despawn the enemy. The TrashCan itself stays put. Separately, the director passes TrashCans[0] into enemy.Update(player, firstTrashCan) so the descending barrel knows where to aim (see Enemy — states).

Note — solid vs sensor, the whole reason this is a GameObject. Every other trigger in this section is a pass-through GameSensor. The trash can is the exception because the desired physics is a real collision — the barrel must land in the can — and the ignite is keyed on a solid-collision "enter" phase, not a "sensorEnter".

Modifying this: to make the fire hurt the player, add a contact rule pairing TRASH_CAN with PLAYER and guard it on IsOnFire inside the handler — leave this class a plain state holder. If you want multiple cans the enemies target, the director currently aims at index 0 only; generalize the enemy targeting logic in Game Director, not this file.

src/game/entities/winFlag.ts 31 LOC

The level goal. A pass-through GameSensor zone: walk into it and the level is won. The win is triggered by the contact table (Player enters WinFlag → emit "gameWin"), not by any logic in this file. Imports just GameObjectType, GameSensor, EntityType — the leanest entity here. Built by createWinFlag. It has no fields, no setters, and (as the director comment notes) no Update.

Hard-won victory — the win moved into the contact table. The header comment is the receipt: the WinFlag "was a solid box that self-detected with a per-frame 4-direction shapecast; that proof-of-concept hack is gone now that the contact system exists." Reaching the goal is now a single "sensorEnter" rule instead of four raycasts every frame, every flag, forever. You walk into the goal rather than bonking a solid wall.

constructor(size: { width; height; depth }, position: { x; y }, rotation: number)

this.createObjectPhysics(
  EntityType.WIN_FLAG,
  GameObjectType.CUBE,
  size,
  position,
  rotation
);

this.setAsSensor(true);
// Take part in the contact table even though we define no callback here.
this.enableContactEvents();

Identical sensor-arming pattern to CameraSensor and Teleporter: setAsSensor(true) makes it non-solid (you pass through the goal zone), enableContactEvents() arms COLLISION_EVENTS so the table-matched rule can fire even though no callback is overridden. No debug mesh, no extra state.

// the win rule (contactRules.ts)

// Player reaches the WinFlag goal zone -> win the level.
contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.WIN_FLAG),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "sensorEnter",
  () => Emitter.emit("gameWin")
);

The handler ignores both colliders — reaching the flag is binary — and simply emits gameWin on the event bus. The phase is "sensorEnter" (the sensor-intersection enter), which is why the flag must be a sensor with events armed. Whatever listens for gameWin (the game shell / director) handles the actual level transition.

Modifying this: to require collecting a key first, gate the emit inside the rule handler on a game-state flag rather than adding logic here. To make a goal that only the player triggers (already the case via byType(PLAYER)) or that also reacts to enemies, edit the contact rule, not this class. This file should stay a zero-behavior marker.

Tip — the shared shape of all six. Constructor → createObjectPhysics (body + collider + userData.type) → arm (solid groups, or setAsSensor + enableContactEvents) → optional setters the factory calls from meta. Reactions live in the contact table; per-frame state (only Platform) lives in Update; teardown is the inherited GameObject.Destroy() the director calls on level unload. Add a new world entity by copying this shape and registering it in Game Config — entityFactories (and a rule in Game Config — contactRules if it should do something).

Game Debug & Types — collision log, level/player panels, type enums

The game half of the debug story. Three of these files are the game-owned modules that plug into the engine's game-blind Debug coordinator (the D3 seam): a big dat.GUI player inspector, a console collision/sensor log sink, and a one-line level dropdown. The other two are the tiny identity tables every entity is built from — the EntityType string vocabulary and the Rapier CollisionGroups bitmasks. None of these files is imported by anything under engine/; the game always pushes knowledge into the engine, never the reverse.

src/game/debug/playerDebug.ts src/game/debug/collisionLogger.ts src/game/debug/levelDebug.ts src/game/types/entityType.ts src/game/types/gameCollisionGroups.ts
Where these get wired. All registration is dependency injection into the engine Debug coordinator, done once after Experience.Configure():
  • PlayerDebug — registered by the Player itself in its constructor: this.Debug.RegisterModule(new PlayerDebug(this)) (player.ts), guarded by Debug.IsActive.
  • CollisionLogger — registered in App.vue: Debug.RegisterCollisionLogSink(new CollisionLogger()).
  • LevelDebug — registered in App.vue: Debug.RegisterModule(new LevelDebug(gameDirector)).
EntityType and CollisionGroups are plain const tables imported all over game/ (factories, entities, contact rules) — no lifecycle.

src/game/debug/playerDebug.ts 396 LOC

The big dat.GUI player inspector: a nested folder tree of live readouts (state, position, velocity, contact flags, ladder sensors, jump assists, timers), a Feel Lab of live-writable tuning sliders, a click-to-teleport noclip mode, and a "dump feel values" action. It implements the engine's DebugModule interface (Init(ui) / Destroy()) so the engine can drive it without ever importing it. It is constructed and registered by the Player in its constructor (only when Debug.IsActive), and handed a reference to that same Player so every control binds to live fields.

Imports: three (raycasting for teleport), dat.gui (the panel type passed to Init), Experience (singleton — for the canvas element and the camera the raycaster needs), and the DebugModule interface from engine/debug/debug.ts.

Hard-won victory — the structural Player forward type. The file declares its own local type Player = {…} instead of importing the real class. The header comment says exactly why: Player → Experience → Debug → PlayerDebug → Player would be a circular import. By describing only the shape it touches, PlayerDebug stays a leaf. Note the deliberate casing split called out in the comment: top-level fields mirror the Player class (PascalCase — State, CurrentFloor, JumpPower…), while nested data-shape fields stay camelCase to match the real underlying types (IsTouching.ground, the Rapier vector CurrentTranslation.x/y/z).

constructor(player: Player)

Stores the live player reference into the private player field. That single object is what every folder.add(...) call below binds against, so the GUI mutates and reads the real Player in place. clickHandler starts null (no teleport listener attached).

private player: Player; // the player whose live state we mirror
private clickHandler: ((e: MouseEvent) => void) | null = null;

constructor(player: Player) {
  this.player = player;
}

public Init(ui: dat.GUI): void

Called by the engine Debug coordinator (immediately on register if the panel is active, see RegisterModule). Builds the entire 🎮 Player Debug folder tree against the live player. Takes the root dat.GUI as its only argument; returns nothing.

public Init(ui: dat.GUI) {
  const player = this.player;
  const experience = Experience.GetInstance();
  const folder = ui.addFolder("🎮 Player Debug");
  folder.close();

  folder.add(player, "State").name("Current State").listen();
  folder.add(player, "CurrentFloor").name("Current Floor").listen();

The key idiom throughout is .listen(): dat.gui then polls the bound field every frame and repaints the control, so a read-only readout like State tracks the live Player with zero per-frame code on our side. The root folder is created .close()d so it does not clutter the panel until expanded. experience is resolved once here only because the noclip handler needs the canvas and camera.

The body lays out these sub-folders, all bound to the same player object:

FolderControls (label → bound field)Mode
(root)Current State → State, Current Floor → CurrentFloorread (.listen())
🚀 Noclip / TeleportEnable Teleport → local noclipConfig.enabledtoggle (.onChange)
🏃 MovementDirection → Direction; Position X/Y → CurrentPosition.x/y; Velocity X/Y → NextTranslation.x/yread, .step(0.001)
💥 CollisionsGround/Ceiling/Left Wall/Right Wall/Edge Platform → IsTouching.{ground,ceiling,leftSide,rightSide,edgePlatform}read (bool)
🪜 LaddersTop/Core/Bottom Sensor → IsTouching.{ladderTop,ladderCore,ladderBottom}read (bool)
🦘 Jump MechanicsCoyote Available/Count, Buffer Jump Available/Count → matching Player fieldsread
⏱️ TimersElapsed Time → Time.Elapsed; Jump/Fall Entered Time → TimeJumpWasEntered/TimeFallWasEnteredread
🎚️ Feel Labnested Jump (Y) / Horizontal (X) / Assists / Climb sliders + Dump actionwrite (.min/.max/.step)

The 💥 Collisions and 🪜 Ladders folders mirror the player's ContactPoints flags (see engine/types/contactPoints.ts) — these are exactly the eight booleans the FSM reads each frame, so watching them here is the fastest way to debug a state-transition bug.

Tip — Feel Lab sliders edit live. The Feel Lab folders (🦘 Jump (Y), 🏃 Horizontal (X), 🅰️ Assists, 🪜 Climb) call .add(player, "JumpPower").min(0).max(200).step(0.5).listen() on writable Player fields. Because the movement handlers (baseFeel / setDkAttributes / setCelesteAttributes) read these fields fresh every frame, dragging a slider changes the game feel instantly. The base profile is still applied on level load from the level registry (the single source of truth) — the Lab only lets you tune on top of the loaded profile, then dump the result.
Note — the Feel Lab slider ranges. Each slider's .min/.max/.step encodes the sane tuning envelope: e.g. JumpPower 0–200 step 0.5, RiseGravity/FallAcceleration 1–800 step 1, JumpEndedEarlyGravityModifier ("Early-Release Mult") 1–8 step 0.1, ApexHangMult 0.05–1 step 0.05, CoyoteTime 0–0.2 step 0.005, BufferJumpRange 0–8 step 0.1, MaxClimbSpeed 0–15. There is intentionally no live profile-swap dropdown here — feel is registry/level-driven so it can't silently diverge from the source of truth.

public Destroy(): void

The DebugModule teardown hook (called by Debug.Destroy() over its registered modules). Only job: drop the teleport click listener so a leftover noclip handler can't fire after the panel is gone.

public Destroy() {
  this.removeClickToTeleport();
}

Note it does not tear down the dat.gui folders — the coordinator destroys the whole Ui in one call, so per-folder cleanup would be redundant.

private dumpFeelValues(): void

Bound to the 📋 Dump Feel Values button. Builds a paste-ready block of player.Field = value; assignments from the player's current (possibly Lab-tuned) feel, logs it, and copies it to the clipboard. Purpose: when you nail a feel in the Lab, this is how you capture it back into source.

const block = [
  "// ── baseFeel.ts (shared) ──",
  `player.MaxClimbSpeed = ${p.MaxClimbSpeed};`,
  // …CoyoteTime, AnimationScalingFactor…
  "",
  "// ── profile overrides (setDkAttributes.ts / setCelesteAttributes.ts) ──",
  `player.MaxGroundSpeed = ${p.MaxGroundSpeed};`,
  // …through JumpPower, BufferJumpRange, MinJumpTime…
].join("\n");

console.log("🎚️ Current feel values:\n" + block);
const copy = navigator.clipboard?.writeText(block);

The block is split by where each field belongs in source — the shared baseFeel.ts values vs the per-profile setters — so you paste each half into the right file. The clipboard write is defensive: navigator.clipboard?. optional-chains (absent in insecure contexts), and the returned promise's .catch falls back to "copy from the log above" when the browser blocks programmatic clipboard access. This is the seam that closes the loop with Game Feel.

private setupClickToTeleport(player: Player, experience: Experience): void

Activated by the noclip toggle's .onChange(true). Installs a window click listener that raycasts the click onto the player's Z-plane and teleports the player to the hit point. The classic level-debug "fly anywhere" tool.

this.removeClickToTeleport();         // ensure only one listener ever

this.clickHandler = (event: MouseEvent) => {
  const canvas = experience.TargetElement;
  if (!canvas) return;

  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();
  const x = event.clientX - rect.left;
  const y = event.clientY - rect.top;
  if (x < 0 || x > rect.width || y < 0 || y > rect.height) return;

  const mouse = new THREE.Vector2(
    (x / rect.width) * 2 - 1,
    -(y / rect.height) * 2 + 1,
  );
  const raycaster = new THREE.Raycaster();
  raycaster.setFromCamera(mouse, experience.Camera.Instance);

  const playerZ = player.CurrentTranslation.z ?? 0;
  const plane = new THREE.Plane(new THREE.Vector3(0, 0, 1), -playerZ);
  const worldPosition = new THREE.Vector3();
  raycaster.ray.intersectPlane(plane, worldPosition);

  player.TeleportToPosition(worldPosition.x, worldPosition.y);
};
window.addEventListener("click", this.clickHandler);

Line-level notes:

  • Bounds guard — clicks outside the canvas rect are ignored, so clicking the dat.gui panel itself doesn't teleport.
  • NDC conversion — the canvas-local pixel is mapped to normalized device coords ([-1,1], Y flipped) for raycaster.setFromCamera.
  • The Z-plane — this is a 2D game on a fixed Z, so the ray is intersected with the plane z = playerZ (normal (0,0,1), constant -playerZ). CurrentTranslation.z ?? 0 defends against an undefined Z. The result feeds player.TeleportToPosition(x, y) — the same teleport the level loader uses for respawns.
  • Self-cleanup — it calls removeClickToTeleport() first so toggling on twice never stacks two listeners.

private removeClickToTeleport(): void

Detaches the active click listener (if any) and nulls clickHandler. Called from the noclip .onChange(false), from Destroy(), and defensively at the top of setupClickToTeleport.

private removeClickToTeleport() {
  if (this.clickHandler) {
    window.removeEventListener("click", this.clickHandler);
    this.clickHandler = null;
  }
}

Storing the exact handler reference is what makes removeEventListener work — an anonymous re-creation could never be removed. This is why clickHandler is a field, not a local.

Modifying this: to surface a new readout, add the field to the local type Player first (or TypeScript rejects the .add), then a folder.add(player, "Field").name("…").listen() in the matching sub-folder. For a new tuning slider, mirror the Feel Lab pattern (.min/.max/.step/.listen()) and add a line to dumpFeelValues so the dump stays complete. Keep top-level fields PascalCase and nested data-shape fields camelCase to match the real Player.

src/game/debug/collisionLogger.ts 74 LOC

The game-owned console sink for collision and sensor events. It implements the engine's CollisionLogSink interface and is registered in App.vue via Debug.RegisterCollisionLogSink(new CollisionLogger()). The engine Physics step, while iterating Rapier's contact/intersection events, calls back through Debug.LogCollisionEvent / LogSensorEvent — which forward to this sink. This is the D3 decoupling in action: the engine knows only the shape of a log sink; the game owns the formatting.

Imports: Experience (singleton — lazily, only to read the clock for timestamps), GameObject (the engine base type both colliding objects are), and the CollisionLogSink interface from engine/debug/debug.ts. 

sequenceDiagram
    participant R as Rapier events
    participant PH as Physics.Update()
    participant D as Debug (engine)
    participant CL as CollisionLogger (game)
    R->>PH: contact / intersection pair
    PH->>PH: dispatch contact rules + callbacks
    Note over PH: if (debug.LogCollisions)
    PH->>D: LogCollisionEvent(obj1, obj2, "enter")
    D->>CL: forwarded opaquely
    CL->>CL: console.log colored line

public LogCollisions = false; public LogSensors = false;

The two enable flags, both off by default. The engine Debug coordinator proxies these via get/set that delegate to the registered sink (get LogCollisions() { return this.logSink?.LogCollisions ?? false }), and Physics gates every log call on them (if (this.debug && this.debug.LogCollisions)). So flipping logging on means setting experience.Debug.LogCollisions = true (from the console or a future checkbox), which writes straight through to this field. When no sink is registered the getter returns false, so logging is a guaranteed no-op.

public LogCollisionEvent(obj1: GameObject, obj2: GameObject, eventType: "enter" | "exit"): void

Prints one colored line for a solid-collision enter/exit. Early-returns when LogCollisions is off (cheap when disabled). Parameters are the two engine GameObjects Rapier reported and the phase. No return.

if (!this.LogCollisions) return;

const timestamp = this.getTimestamp();
let color = "#ff6666";                 // exit = red
if (eventType === "enter") color = "#00ff00"; // enter = green
let symbol = "↔️";
if (eventType === "enter") symbol = "💥";

console.log(
  `%c${symbol} [${timestamp}] COLLISION ${eventType.toUpperCase()}: %c${
    obj1.constructor.name
  } %c↔ %c${obj2.constructor.name}`,
  `color: ${color}; font-weight: bold;`,
  "color: #4fc3f7; font-weight: bold;",   // obj1 = blue
  "color: #999;",                         // ↔ = gray
  "color: #ffb74d; font-weight: bold;"    // obj2 = orange
);

The %c markers are the browser console's per-span CSS mechanism: each %c consumes the next style string argument, so the line is multicolor (green/red verb, blue obj1, gray arrow, orange obj2). The crucial detail is obj1.constructor.name / obj2.constructor.name — it logs the runtime class name (Player, Enemy, Platform, TrashCan…), not the EntityType string. That's deliberate: it reads cleanly without any lookup table, and the class names happen to line up with the EntityType values.

public LogSensorEvent(obj1: GameObject, obj2: GameObject, eventType: "enter" | "exit"): void

Identical structure for sensor (intersection) events, gated on LogSensors. Different palette/symbols so sensor traffic is visually distinct from solid collisions: enter = 📡 cyan #00ddff, exit = ⚪ magenta #ff88ff; obj1 purple, obj2 green.

if (!this.LogSensors) return;
// …
let color = "#ff88ff";
if (eventType === "enter") color = "#00ddff";
let symbol = "⚪";
if (eventType === "enter") symbol = "📡";

Separating sensors from collisions matters because the game leans on sensors heavily (ladder top/core/bottom, camera zones, teleporters) — without the color split a ladder climb would drown the console in indistinguishable lines.

private getTimestamp(): string

Returns the elapsed engine-clock time as a string like "12.34s". Lazily resolves the Experience singleton on first use and caches it.

if (!this.experience) {
  try {
    this.experience = Experience.GetInstance();
  } catch {
    return "0.00s";
  }
}
return `${this.experience.Time.Elapsed.toFixed(2)}s`;

The lazy + try/catch pattern is the safety: CollisionLogger can be constructed before Experience is fully wired, and GetInstance() throws if the singleton isn't ready. Rather than couple construction order, it returns "0.00s" until the engine clock exists, then caches the reference forever after. It reads Time.Elapsed (the engine wall-clock, see engine/core/time) so timestamps line up with everything else in the loop.

Modifying this: to add a third category (say, "trigger" events), add another Log… flag + method to both this class and the CollisionLogSink interface, then call it from Physics behind its flag — the engine forwards opaquely, so it never needs to know what the category means. To label lines by EntityType instead of class name, swap obj.constructor.name for a getUserData(obj.PhysicsBody).type read.

src/game/debug/levelDebug.ts 30 LOC

The smallest module here: a single 🗺️ Level folder with one dropdown that loads any registry level. It implements DebugModule and is registered in App.vue with a reference to the live GameDirector. The whole trick is that the dropdown binds directly to GameDirector.CurrentLevelName with .listen(), so there is no per-frame sync code at all.

Imports: dat.gui, the DebugModule interface, LEVEL_REGISTRY from the level registry (to build the option list), and — critically — GameDirector as a type-only import (import type). The header comment flags this: a value import would create the cycle GameDirector → … → LevelDebug → GameDirector. Type-only erases at compile time, so no cycle.

constructor(director: GameDirector)

Stashes the director reference; the dropdown both reads and writes through it.

public Init(ui: dat.GUI): void

Builds the folder and the single bound dropdown.

const folder = ui.addFolder("🗺️ Level");
folder.open();

folder
  .add(director, "CurrentLevelName", LEVEL_REGISTRY.map((level) => level.name))
  .name("Level (F2 cycles)")
  .listen()
  .onChange((name: string) => director.LoadLevelByName(name));

Four lines doing four things:

  • The option list — LEVEL_REGISTRY.map(l => l.name) turns the registry into the dropdown's choices, so adding a level row automatically adds a menu entry. No duplicate list to maintain.
  • The binding — .add(director, "CurrentLevelName", …) binds the control to the director's public field. Picking an option writes that field.
  • .listen() — dat.gui polls CurrentLevelName every frame and repaints the dropdown. So when you press F2 (the switchLevel event → switchToNextLevel → loadLevelByIndex sets CurrentLevelName), the menu's selection updates itself — no callback wiring from the director back to the GUI.
  • .onChange — picking an item calls director.LoadLevelByName(name), the public registry-name load path (it findIndexes the registry and funnels into the same loadLevelByIndex guarded loader). The folder is opened by default since switching levels is a common debug action.
Gotcha — the dropdown sets the field, then .onChange loads. Both directions move through CurrentLevelName. When you pick: dat.gui writes CurrentLevelName, then fires onChange → LoadLevelByName. When F2 fires: the director sets CurrentLevelName itself and .listen() reflects it without re-firing onChange (that only fires on user input), so there's no double-load. The isLoading re-entrancy guard in loadLevelByIndex (decision D5) backstops any overlap if you mash the menu mid-load.
Note — no live feel control here. The header comment is explicit: feel is registry-driven and applied only on level load (the single source of truth), so there is deliberately no per-level feel dropdown in this folder. Live feel tweaking lives only in PlayerDebug's Feel Lab.

Modifying this: to add a level you touch only LEVEL_REGISTRY — this file rebuilds its options from it. Keep GameDirector a type-only import. If you add more level-scoped debug controls, hang them off this same folder rather than reaching into the director's internals.

src/game/types/entityType.ts 29 LOC

The single identity vocabulary for every entity. Each entity carries a string type (stamped into its Rapier collider's userData), and this const map is the canonical list of those strings. It's a pure data file — no class, no lifecycle — imported by the spawn side (entityFactories), the routing side (contactRules, the GameDirector spawn loop), and the debug palette (debugColors).

Hard-won victory — why strings, not instanceof. The header comment nails it: "Rapier callbacks return a raw Collider (not a class), so type-based routing survives where instanceof can't." When a contact fires, all you get back from Rapier is a collider handle; you can't ask "is this a Player?" with instanceof. Stamping a type string onto userData at spawn lets the contact table and debug layers route purely on data. One const map keeps the spawn side and the routing side from drifting — and the trailing warning is real: these values are the exact strings level data and the engine's resourceLoader emit, so changing a value silently breaks routing.

const EntityType = { … } as const; export type EntityType = typeof EntityType[keyof typeof EntityType];

The const-object-plus-derived-union pattern (the same shape used by PlayerStates and CollisionGroups, called out in the comment). The object gives you named constants at runtime (EntityType.PLAYER); the derived type gives you a compile-time union of the values ("Player" | "Wall" | …) so a function can demand a valid entity name. Default export is the object; named export is the type.

MemberValue (the literal string)Maps to
PLAYER"Player"the player entity
WALL"Wall"solid wall (Platform-class, no one-way)
PLATFORM"Platform"solid girder
ONE_WAY_PLATFORM"OneWayPlatform"jump-through platform
ENEMY"Enemy"barrel (normal & crazy are one class — D28)
TRASH_CAN"TrashCan"the oil can
WIN_FLAG"WinFlag"level-win goal
LADDER_TOP_SENSOR"LadderTopSensor"top-of-ladder sensor
LADDER_CORE_SENSOR"LadderCoreSensor"ladder-body sensor
LADDER_BOTTOM_SENSOR"LadderBottomSensor"bottom-of-ladder sensor
LADDER_SENSOR"LadderSensor"generic ladder sensor type
CAMERA_SENSOR"CameraSensor"camera-zone trigger
TELEPORTER"Teleporter"teleport pad
PLAYER_START"PlayerStart"spawn marker (consumed, not an entity)
CAMERA_START"CameraStart"camera-init marker (consumed)

The strings are exactly the GLB object/collection names the level editor exports and the resourceLoader reads off the glTF — that's why a member's value is load-bearing and must not change casually. The last two (PLAYER_START, CAMERA_START) are spawn markers the GameDirector handles directly in setPlayerStart/setCameraStart rather than through a factory, which is why the factory loop simply skips rows whose type has no registered factory.

Note — one ENEMY type, two behaviors (D28). There is a single ENEMY member even though the game has "normal" and "crazy" barrels. The D28 refactor folded the old CrazyEnemy into one Enemy class distinguished only by its starting FSM state — so the identity vocabulary needs just one entry. See Game Entities: Enemy.

Modifying this: to introduce a new entity, add a member here, give it a factory in entityFactories, a wireframe color in debugColors, and (if it interacts) a rule in contactRules. Keep the value string identical to the name your level editor exports, or the spawn loop will silently skip it.

src/game/types/gameCollisionGroups.ts 30 LOC

The Rapier collision-group bitmask vocabulary. Each entity sets a membership group ("what I am") and a filter mask ("what I collide with"); Rapier only generates a contact when both sides agree. This file is the const table of those bits, imported by Player, Enemy, Platform, and TrashCan, which feed them to GameObject.setCollisionGroup/setCollisionMask.

Note — Rapier's group/mask rule. The header comment states it exactly: a contact occurs when (a.group & b.mask) AND (b.group & a.mask) are both non-zero. Both directions must agree — A must want B and B must want A. Combine multiple groups or masks with bitwise OR (PLATFORM | ENEMY).

const CollisionGroups = { … } as const;

The bit layout, written in 0b… binary for clarity:

ConstantBitValueBelongs to
PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX00b…0001the Player's collider 0 (main solid box)
(free)10b…0010was PLAYER_HIT_BOX — removed
ENEMY20b…0100every barrel (Enemy)
PLATFORM30b…1000platforms, one-way platforms, walls
DEFAULTall0b1111…1111collide-with-everything mask

And here is who declares what (read straight from the entities):

Entitygroup (membership)mask (filter)
Player (collider 0)PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOXPLATFORM | ENEMY
Enemy (rolling, grounded)ENEMYPLATFORM | PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX
Enemy (descending ladder)ENEMYPLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX only
Platform / WallPLATFORMDEFAULT
TrashCan (oil can)DEFAULTDEFAULT

The pairing is symmetric and intentional: Player wants PLATFORM | ENEMY and both Platform (via DEFAULT) and Enemy (which lists PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX) want the Player back, so all those contacts fire. The interesting dynamic case is the Enemy toggling its own mask at runtime: while rolling a girder it keeps PLATFORM in the mask (so it rides the floor), but when it takes a ladder (DescendLadder) it drops PLATFORM and keeps only PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX — so it falls straight through girders yet can still kill the player on the way down.

Hard-won victory — Bit 1 left deliberately free. The comment marks bit 1 as "was PLAYER_HIT_BOX (the player's 62.5% hit sensor), removed when death detection moved to the full bounding box via the contact table." The old design had a separate smaller sensor collider for death; once the contact table made "any touch of the Enemy registers death" trivial, that sensor and its per-frame poll became dead code. The bit isn't reused — leaving the gap documents the history and avoids accidentally colliding semantics with a brand-new group. See Player collider setup.
Gotcha — these are 16-bit groups packed into Rapier's 32-bit field. GameObject.setCollisionGroup/setCollisionMask (in engine/entities/gameObject) pack group into the low 16 bits and mask into the high 16: setting the group does collisionGroups(collisionGroups | (currentMask << 16)) after reading the current mask back via >> 16; setting the mask mirrors it with & 0xffff. That's why every constant here stays inside 16 bits (and why DEFAULT is 0xffff, not 0xffffffff) — the upper half is reserved for the mask. Set group and mask in either order; each setter preserves the other half.

Modifying this: add a new group on the next free bit (bit 1 is available, but consider leaving it as the documented gap and using bit 4) and stay within 16 bits. Then update the relevant entity's setCollisionGroup/setCollisionMask calls on both sides of any new interaction — a one-sided mask change produces a silent no-contact, the classic Rapier filtering bug. Remember this only controls whether Rapier generates a contact; what happens on contact is the contact table's job (contactRules).

Cookbook — how to extend the engine

This is the hands-on companion to Game Config — the data that defines the game. The whole point of the engine/game split is that almost every gameplay change is a data edit in a config file, not a new code path. Below are six step-by-step recipes — add an entity, add a level, add a player state, tune the feel, add an input, add a contact rule — each one grounded line-by-line in the five files that drive the game: entityFactories.ts, levelRegistry.ts, inputBindings.ts, contactRules.ts, and baseFeel.ts. Every recipe lists which files to touch in order, the exact additions, and the one gotcha that will bite you if you skip a step.

src/game/config/entityFactories.ts src/game/config/levelRegistry.ts src/game/config/inputBindings.ts src/game/config/contactRules.ts src/game/attributes/baseFeel.ts
Note — the design these recipes lean on. Three identity tables are the spine of the game: EntityType (the routing string every entity carries), PlayerStates (the FSM states), and CollisionGroups (the Rapier bit masks). Each is a single const map so the spawn side and the routing side can never drift. The recipes below mostly amount to "add a row to one of these tables, then wire the one or two places that consume it."

Recipe — add a new entity type touches 5+ files

Goal: introduce a brand-new level-placeable thing (we'll use a Spike hazard that kills the player on touch). An entity needs an identity (its EntityType string), a way for the GLB parser to recognise its cube (the resourceLoader's canonical-type mirror), a class that builds its physics body, a factory that the level loader dispatches to, optionally its own collision group, and a contact rule that says what happens when it's touched. Do them in this order so each step compiles against the one before it. 

flowchart LR
  A["Blender cube named "Spike""] --> B["resourceLoader.parseScene"]
  B -->|CANONICAL_TYPES lookup| C["LevelData row type=Spike"]
  C --> D["GameDirector.LoadLevelData"]
  D -->|"ENTITY_FACTORIES[Spike]"| E["createSpike factory"]
  E --> F["new Spike(...)"]
  F --> G["contactRules: Spike+Player = gameOver"]

1. src/game/types/entityType.ts — add the identity string

Add one line to the EntityType const. The value is the verbatim string everything routes on, so pick it once and never change it. 

const EntityType = {
  PLAYER: "Player",
  WALL: "Wall",
  // ...existing...
  TELEPORTER: "Teleporter",
  SPIKE: "Spike",            // <-- NEW
  PLAYER_START: "PlayerStart",
  CAMERA_START: "CameraStart",
} as const;

2. src/engine/resources/resourceLoader.ts — mirror it in CANONICAL_TYPES

The engine deliberately cannot import the game's EntityType (it names zero game concepts), so the parser keeps a hand-synced copy. Add the lower-cased mesh-name token mapping to the canonical string. The parser takes the first _-delimited token of a cube's name, lower-cases it, and looks it up here. 

private static readonly CANONICAL_TYPES: Record<string, string> = {
  wall: "Wall",
  platform: "Platform",
  onewayplatform: "OneWayPlatform",
  // ...existing...
  teleporter: "Teleporter",
  spike: "Spike",            // <-- NEW: a cube named "Spike" (or "Spike_2") resolves here
  playerstart: "PlayerStart",
  camerastart: "CameraStart",
  graphics: "GraphicsObject",
  graphicsobject: "GraphicsObject",
};
Gotcha — the silent-drop. Skip this step and your Spike cube resolves to no type: lookupCanonicalType returns undefined, parseScene treats the leaf mesh as a mis-named cube, calls warnUnknownType, and skips it entirely. The entity just never appears. This mirror in Engine Resources — resourceLoader is the "known residue" of the engine/game split: it must be kept in sync by hand whenever you add a level-placeable type.

3. src/game/entities/spike.ts — the entity class

Patterned exactly on Teleporter (a sensor that defines no callback of its own and joins the contact table). It extends GameSensor, builds its physics body with the new EntityType.SPIKE routing flag, flags itself a sensor, and arms contact events so the declarative table can see it. 

import * as RAPIER from "@dimforge/rapier2d-compat";
import GameObjectType from "../../engine/types/gameObjectType";
import GameSensor from "../../engine/entities/gameSensor";
import EntityType from "../types/entityType";

// Spike - kills the player on contact. The "who dies" rule lives in the
// declarative contact table (contactRules.ts), not here.
export default class Spike extends GameSensor {
  constructor(
    size: { width: number; height: number },
    position: { x: number; y: number },
    rotation: number = 0
  ) {
    super();

    this.createObjectPhysics(
      EntityType.SPIKE,
      GameObjectType.CUBE,
      size,
      position,
      rotation,
      RAPIER.RigidBodyDesc.fixed()
    );

    this.setAsSensor(true);
    // Take part in the contact table even though we define no callback here.
    this.enableContactEvents();

    this.createObjectGraphicsDebug("red", 0.4);
  }
}
Hard-won victory — enableContactEvents(). GameObject only auto-arms collision events for entities that define an OnCollisionEnter/OnSensorEnter callback (enablePhysicsEvents). A contact-table-only entity defines none, so without this call Rapier emits no event for it and your contact rule never fires. Teleporter hit exactly this and the comment there spells it out: "Take part in the contact table even though we define no callback here."

4. (optional) src/game/types/gameCollisionGroups.ts — a collision group

A sensor like Spike can skip this — sensors use the default group (collides/intersects with everything), the same way Teleporter sets no group. You only need a group bit if the entity is a solid the player must physically stop against. If so, claim the free bit (bit 1 is open — it used to be PLAYER_HIT_BOX) and remember the player must be told to collide with it. 

const CollisionGroups = {
  PLAYER_BOUNDING_BOX: 0b0000000000000001, // Bit 0
  SPIKE:               0b0000000000000010, // Bit 1 (was free) — only if SOLID
  ENEMY:               0b0000000000000100, // Bit 2
  PLATFORM:            0b0000000000001000, // Bit 3
  DEFAULT:             0b1111111111111111,
} as const;
Gotcha — collision is two-way. Collision occurs only when (a.group & b.mask) AND (b.group & a.mask). Giving Spike its own group does nothing unless the player's mask includes it. The player sets setCollisionMask(CollisionGroups.PLATFORM | CollisionGroups.ENEMY, 0) in its constructor — you'd have to OR in | CollisionGroups.SPIKE there too. For a kill-on-touch hazard a sensor (no group) is simpler and avoids this entirely.

5. src/game/config/entityFactories.ts — the factory + registry row

Write a factory matching the EntityFactory signature (data, ctx, type) => GameObject and register it under the EntityType.SPIKE key. Mirror the existing one-liners (axis mapping position[0]/position[2], the -rotation[1] negation, sizes passed straight through). Read any per-entity metadata off data.meta with the metaNum/metaCoords helpers. 

import Spike from "../entities/spike";

// FROM nothing — brand-new hazard.
const createSpike: EntityFactory = (data) => {
  const spike = new Spike(
    { width: data.width, height: data.height },
    { x: data.position[0], y: data.position[2] },
    -data.rotation[1]
  );
  return spike;
};

const ENTITY_FACTORIES: Record<string, EntityFactory> = {
  [EntityType.CAMERA_SENSOR]: createCameraSensor,
  // ...existing...
  [EntityType.TELEPORTER]: createTeleporter,
  [EntityType.SPIKE]: createSpike,   // <-- NEW
};
Note — the ctx arg. Spike isn't queried by index anywhere (unlike trashCans[0] or the ladder sub-arrays), so its factory pushes into no FactoryContext view and ignores ctx. If you do need the director to iterate your entities, add a typed array to FactoryContext, push the new entity into it inside the factory, and alias it in GameDirector.getFactoryContext (and tear it down in UnloadLevelData). See Game Director for both.

6. src/game/config/contactRules.ts — what touching it does

Spike is a sensor, so it fires the "sensorEnter" phase. Declare the rule the same way the WinFlag and Teleporter rules are declared — match both sides byType, pick the phase, run the reaction. 

// Player touches a Spike -> game over.
contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.SPIKE),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "sensorEnter",
  () => Emitter.emit("gameOver")
);

Step 7 — the GLB. In the level editor (BlockBench/Blender) drop a cube, name it Spike (or Spike_2 for a second instance — the parser strips the suffix), and export the .glb into src/assets/levels. The parser reads the cube's name for its type and its texture name for metadata; you don't touch any loader code. See the add a level recipe below for the drop-in mechanics.

Recipe — add a playable level touches 1 file + 1 GLB

Levels are GLB-only and the whole pipeline is data: drop a .glb into src/assets/levels and add one row to LEVEL_REGISTRY. GameDirector boots index 0, F2 cycles, and the dat.gui level dropdown selects — all three funnel through the same loadLevelByIndex, so one row is genuinely all you add.

1. Drop the GLB — auto-discovery does the rest

GLBs under src/assets/levels are Vite-bundled (hashed), not in public/. An eager import.meta.glob collects every *.glb as its bundled URL into a filename→URL map, which levelUrl() resolves against. 

const levelGlbUrls = import.meta.glob("../../assets/levels/*.glb", {
  query: "?url",
  import: "default",
  eager: true,
}) as Record<string, string>;
