Warning — the glob is eager. EVERY .glb in the folder is bundled into the build whether or not a registry row references it. Delete unused level GLBs to stay lean — an orphaned 4 MB level still ships.

2. src/game/config/levelRegistry.ts — add a LEVEL_REGISTRY row

Each row is a LevelEntry: a name (the dat.gui label and GameDirector.CurrentLevelName value), a source (the bundled URL via levelUrl("file.glb")), a feelProfile (a key of FEEL_PROFILES — see the tune the feel recipe), a cameraFollow boolean, and an optional graphics overlay key. 

export const LEVEL_REGISTRY: LevelEntry[] = [
  {
    name: "Slopes Testing",
    source: levelUrl("TestLevel-Slopes.glb"),
    feelProfile: "dk",
    cameraFollow: true,
  },
  // ...existing rows...
  {
    name: "My New Level",                    // <-- NEW ROW
    source: levelUrl("MyNewLevel.glb"),
    feelProfile: "celeste",
    cameraFollow: true,
    // graphics: "dkGraphicsData",  // optional detailed-art overlay (a ResourceLoader.Items key)
  },
];

Append it and you're done — it joins the F2 cycle automatically (switchToNextLevel does (currentLevelIndex + 1) % LEVEL_REGISTRY.length). To boot into it instead, change DEFAULT_LEVEL_INDEX.

Gotcha — every level needs a PlayerStart. GameDirector.setPlayerStart scans the LevelData for a PlayerStart row and only then constructs the Player. No PlayerStart cube → the player is never spawned → Update's guard if (!this.Player?.PhysicsBody) return; freezes the whole game with no error. Include a CameraStart too, or the camera sits at its default origin.
Hard-won victory — fail loud on a missing GLB (D5 neighbour). If levelUrl() can't resolve a filename it logs the available list and returns "". loadLevelByIndex checks for that empty source and throws a clear error before handing it to the loader — otherwise gltfLoader.load("") fetches the HTML page and throws a baffling parse error far from the real cause (a typo'd filename). The surrounding try/catch/finally still closes the Vue loading overlay so the screen never hangs.

Recipe — add a player state touches 3 files

The player movement FSM is the generic StateMachine (see Architecture — Finite State Machine) driving a handler-per-state map. Adding a state — say DASHING — is three edits: the state constant, a handler file, and the registration + at least one transition into it.

1. src/engine/types/playerStates.ts — add the constant

const PlayerStates = {
  IDLE: "idle",
  RUNNING: "running",
  JUMPING: "jumping",
  FALLING: "falling",
  CLIMBING: "climbing",
  DASHING: "dashing",      // <-- NEW
} as const;

2. src/game/entities/player/states/handlePlayerDashing.ts — the handler

A handler is a plain (player: Player) => void. It runs once per frame while the player is in this state; it reads player.Input/player.IsTouching, writes player.NextTranslation, drives the sprite, and reassigns player.State to transition. Pattern it on handlePlayerIdle: guard-clause transitions first (each with an early return), then the in-state behaviour. 

import Player from "../player";
import PlayerStates from "../../../../engine/types/playerStates";

const handlePlayerDashing = (player: Player) => {
  // Transition out: dash ended -> fall back to falling/idle.
  if (player.IsTouching.ground) {
    player.State = PlayerStates.IDLE;
    return;
  }

  // In-state behaviour: drive horizontal velocity hard in the facing direction.
  const dir = player.Direction; // LEFT/RIGHT/NEUTRAL
  player.NextTranslation.x = dir * player.MaxGroundSpeed * 2;

  // ...sprite animation via player.SpriteAnimator.ChangeState(...)...
};

export default handlePlayerDashing;

3. src/game/entities/player/player.ts — register + transition IN

Import the handler and add it to the FSM map (the player constructs its StateMachine in initializePlayerAttributes). Then add a transition into DASHING from an existing handler — e.g. in handlePlayerRunning when a dash key is pressed. 

import handlePlayerDashing from "./states/handlePlayerDashing";

this.fsm = new StateMachine<Player, PlayerState>(this, {
  [PlayerStates.IDLE]: handlePlayerIdle,
  [PlayerStates.RUNNING]: handlePlayerRunning,
  [PlayerStates.FALLING]: handlePlayerFalling,
  [PlayerStates.JUMPING]: handlePlayerJumping,
  [PlayerStates.CLIMBING]: handlePlayerClimbing,
  [PlayerStates.DASHING]: handlePlayerDashing,   // <-- NEW
});
Gotcha — a state with no handler silently freezes. StateMachine.Update dispatches via this.handlers[this.host.State]?.(this.host) — that optional chaining means a state whose key is missing from the map is a no-op, not a crash. Forget step 3 and the player enters DASHING and just stops updating forever, with no error. Every PlayerStates member must have a map entry.
Note — transitions take effect NEXT frame. StateMachine.Update reads State once per frame. A handler that sets player.State = PlayerStates.DASHING does not re-dispatch into the dash handler within the same frame — the new state runs on the following Update. This is by design and is why every handler does its state write last and returns.
Tip — new feel knobs go in the attribute profiles. If your dash reads a new tunable (player.DashSpeed, say), declare it on Player (public DashSpeed!: number;) and seed it in baseFeel or a profile (next recipe) — the ! definite-assignment fields are undefined until something sets them, and a handler reading undefined produces NaN velocity.

Recipe — tune the feel baseFeel.ts + a profile

"Feel" is a set of numeric attributes written onto the Player by a profile. The layering is: every profile calls applyBaseFeel(player) FIRST (shared values), then overrides on top. baseFeel.ts holds the values common to DK and Celeste (climb speed/accel/decel, the coyote/buffer runtime flags, CoyoteTime, AnimationScalingFactor); setDkAttributes / setCelesteAttributes hold the per-game divergences (see Game Feel). 

flowchart TD
  A["level loads"] --> B["GameDirector.applyFeelProfile(entry.feelProfile)"]
  B --> C["FEEL_PROFILES[profile](player)"]
  C --> D["applyBaseFeel(player) — shared values"]
  D --> E["DK/Celeste overrides on top"]

Tune a SHARED value — edit baseFeel.ts

Values that should change for both profiles live here. For example, to give every profile a snappier climb, bump MaxClimbSpeed: 

const applyBaseFeel = (player: Player) => {
  // Climbing (shared)
  player.MaxClimbSpeed = 4;        // <-- e.g. raise to 6
  player.ClimbAcceleration = 30;
  player.ClimbDeceleration = 8;
  // ...coyote/buffer flags, CoyoteTime, AnimationScalingFactor...
  player.CoyoteTime = 0.033; // 2 frames
  player.AnimationScalingFactor = 1.6;
};

Tune a PER-PROFILE value — edit setDkAttributes.ts

Values that differ per game live in the profile, after the applyBaseFeel(player) call. To make the DK jump higher, raise JumpPower: 

const setDkAttributes = (player: Player) => {
  applyBaseFeel(player);            // shared first
  // DK overrides (differ from base feel)
  player.MaxGroundSpeed = 11.8;
  player.JumpPower = 24;            // <-- raise for a higher jump
  player.RiseGravity = 72;         // lighter rise = floatier ascent
  player.MaxFallSpeed = 40;
  // ...
};

Add a NEW profile — register it in FEEL_PROFILES

Write a setter (call applyBaseFeel first), then add it to the FEEL_PROFILES table in levelRegistry.ts. The FeelProfile type is keyof typeof FEEL_PROFILES, so a level row's feelProfile is then type-checked against the new key automatically. 

import setFloatyAttributes from "../attributes/setFloatyAttributes";

export const FEEL_PROFILES = {
  dk: setDkAttributes,
  celeste: setCelesteAttributes,
  floaty: setFloatyAttributes,   // <-- NEW; now usable as feelProfile: "floaty"
} as const;
Hard-won victory — feel is single-source, applied only on load. GameDirector.applyFeelProfile runs exactly once, in loadLevelByIndex, from the loading level's registry entry — there is deliberately no live debug swap that bypasses it. That's why the registry row is the one true place a level's feel is decided; a value you change in the Feel Lab at runtime is never the persisted truth.
Gotcha — ordering: profile beats base. Because the profile calls applyBaseFeel first and overrides after, a value set in BOTH places resolves to the profile's. A value set ONLY in baseFeel (and not overridden) is the effective value. If a new attribute reads as its base value when you expected the override, check you actually re-assigned it below the applyBaseFeel(player) line in the profile.

Recipe — add an input key input.ts + inputBindings.ts

Two layers handle input. The engine Input device exposes raw HELD-state booleans and names no game concept. inputBindings.ts (the game) watches the flow F-keys, detects the press edge, and emits a game event once per press. This recipe adds a new flow key — F4 → "togglePause" — through both layers. (Movement keys like arrows and Space go a different path: they're captured into InputState via CaptureInto and read by the player handlers directly — see Engine Events & Input.)

1. src/engine/input/input.ts — expose the held-state flag

The F4 flag already exists in this codebase (IsF4Pressed, with its F4 entry in the keys[] table) — for a genuinely new key you'd add all three pieces: the public field, its false init in the constructor, and a Key table entry whose keyCode is the layout-independent KeyboardEvent.code. 

public IsF4Pressed: boolean;   // field

this.IsF4Pressed = false;      // init in constructor

{                              // entry in the keys[] table
  keyCode: "F4",
  isPressed: (eventResult: boolean) => {
    this.IsF4Pressed = eventResult;
  },
},

2. src/game/config/inputBindings.ts — edge-detect and emit

Add a wasF4Pressed latch (init false in the constructor), then in Update emit the event on the false→true edge and store this frame's value. The existing F1/F2/F3 blocks are the exact template. 

private wasF4Pressed: boolean;

constructor(input: Input) {
  this.input = input;
  this.wasF1Pressed = false;
  // ...
  this.wasF4Pressed = false;   // <-- NEW
}

public Update() {
  // ...F1/F2/F3...

  // F4 -> togglePause
  if (this.input.IsF4Pressed && !this.wasF4Pressed) {
    Emitter.emit("togglePause");
  }
  this.wasF4Pressed = this.input.IsF4Pressed;   // remember for next frame
}
Gotcha — forget the latch and it fires every frame. The whole reason this file exists is that engine Input only tells you a key is held, not that it was just pressed. The && !this.wasF4Pressed guard plus this.wasF4Pressed = ... assignment is the edge detector. Drop the assignment and "togglePause" emits 60×/second while the key is down.

3. Listen for the event — GameDirector (or App.vue)

inputBindings.Update() runs each frame from App.vue's OnGameUpdate hook (right before gameDirector.Update()). The emit goes onto the global mitt bus; subscribe wherever the reaction belongs, the way GameDirector.setupEventHandlers subscribes to switchLevel / gameReset. 

Emitter.on("togglePause", () => {
  const p = this.experience.Physics;
  p.SetPaused(!p.IsPaused);
});

Recipe — add a contact rule contactRules.ts

The contact table is the declarative "when an A touches a B, do this" layer over the engine's physics dispatch (see Architecture — The Contact Table). registerContactRules(contacts) runs ONCE at startup in App.vue, after Configure, against webglExperience.Physics.Contacts. Each rule is one contacts.Add(matchA, matchB, phase, handler) call. There are no if/else chains — you add a row.

contacts.Add(a, b, phase, run) — the four args

Match each side with the byType helper (which wraps matchesType(go, type)). Pick the phase. Write the run handler — it receives the two matched GameObjects in the rule's declared (a, b) order, so cast to the concrete class to read typed fields.

phasefires whenused by
"enter"two SOLID bodies begin touchingEnemy↔Player = gameOver; Enemy↔TrashCan = ignite
"exit"two solid bodies stop touching(available, none declared)
"sensorEnter"a body enters a SENSORTeleporter, CameraSensor, WinFlag
"sensorExit"a body leaves a sensor(available, none declared)

Example — a solid contact that emits an event

The simplest rule: match both sides by type, fire on solid "enter", emit a bus event the game already listens for.

// Enemy touches Player -> game over. Two-sided.
contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.ENEMY),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "enter",
  () => Emitter.emit("gameOver")
);

Example — a handler that reads typed state off the matched entity

When the reaction needs data from the entity, cast the GameObject arg. The handler args arrive in the rule's own declared order regardless of which body actually moved.

// Player enters a Teleporter sensor -> teleport to its destination.
contacts.Add(
  byType(EntityType.TELEPORTER),
  byType(EntityType.PLAYER),
  "sensorEnter",
  (teleporter, player) => {
    const dest = (teleporter as Teleporter).Destination;
    (player as Player).TeleportToPosition(dest.x, dest.y);
  }
);
Hard-won victory — two-sided dispatch. ContactRegistry.Dispatch tests each rule as both (x, y) and (y, x) with an else if, so a rule fires whether the player ran into the enemy or the enemy rolled into the player — "any touch registers" — yet fires once, not twice, and the handler always gets args in your declared order. You never write the rule twice.
Gotcha — match the phase to the body kind. A sensor entity emits "sensorEnter"/"sensorExit", a solid emits "enter"/"exit". Declare a Teleporter rule with "enter" and it never fires — the Teleporter is a sensor. When in doubt, check whether the entity's class calls setAsSensor(true).
Gotcha — both entities must have contact events armed. A rule can only fire if Rapier actually emits an event for the pair. Callback-defining entities are auto-armed; contact-table-only entities must call enableContactEvents() in their constructor (Teleporter, and the Spike in the first recipe). And the player side works in BOTH directions only because the kinematic controller's own blocked-by collisions are fed back into the table as "enter" contacts in Player.updateTranslation — without that, a player-as-mover collision fires no Rapier event at all.
Note — where rules do NOT belong. An entity's own movement reaction (an enemy reversing at a wall, a one-way platform toggling) lives with the entity, not in this table. The contact table is for cross-entity outcomes: who dies, wins, teleports, ignites.

Reference — File Index, Event Catalog & Decision Log

The fold-out reference card for the whole engine: every source file at a glance, every signal that crosses the mitt bus, and the Rosetta Stone for the (C5)/(D3)/(D28) decision codes scattered through the comments.

all 63 source filessrc/engine/events/eventBus.tsdecision log

The complete file index 66 files · ~7,000 LOC

Every TypeScript / Vue source file, its layer, its size, and a one-line statement of what it does. The golden rule to keep in your head while reading: anything under src/engine/ never imports from src/game/ — the arrows of dependency only ever point game → engine.

Engine layer — src/engine/ (game-agnostic, 28 files)

FileLOCPurpose
core/experience.ts122The singleton that constructs, owns and wires every engine subsystem; runs the per-frame onTick.
core/time.ts45requestAnimationFrame loop; tracks delta/elapsed; emits tick.
core/sizes.ts33Tracks viewport width/height/pixelRatio; emits resize.
events/eventBus.ts45The typed mitt emitter + the engine/game event map (single source of event truth).
input/input.ts290Keyboard device; snapshots raw key state into a plain InputState intent bag each frame.
rendering/renderer.ts58THREE.WebGLRenderer lifecycle: setup, per-frame draw, resize, dispose.
rendering/spriteAnimator.ts139Sprite-sheet flip-book animator: slices a sheet and drives UV offset/frame timing.
camera/camera.ts332Engine-pure follow camera; consumes a plain CameraTarget snapshot, never the Player (C5).
physics/physics.ts237The Rapier2D world: async WASM init, the stepped sim, and draining collision/sensor events into the contact table.
entities/gameObject.ts435Base entity: a rigid body + collider + THREE mesh kept in sync every frame; the engine's workhorse class.
entities/gameSensor.ts38Sensor-collider wrapper exposing continuous intersection state (the D9 "fully inside" path leans on this).
entities/contactRegistry.ts50The generic (typeA,typeB) → handler contact table; dispatches two-sided, names no game concept.
entities/stateMachine.ts17The tiny generic FSM: a state → handler map; the host owns the current state.
resources/resourceLoader.ts555Boot asset loader (DRACO GLB + textures) and the GLB level parser that emits LevelData.
helpers/mathHelpers.ts21Pure math: moveTowards, percentChance, ranges/easing primitives.
helpers/physicsHelpers.ts27Rapier identity helpers: getUserData, matchesType, isColliderType.
debug/debug.ts160Debug coordinator: URL-hash gate, dat.gui + stats.js, and the module/sink/palette injection seams.
debug/cameraDebug.ts167dat.gui camera inspector / gizmos and manual-control wiring.
debug/physicsDebug.ts179Rapier debug wireframe overlay, colored via the game-injected type→color map (D3).
types/cameraTarget.ts25CameraTarget snapshot + CameraFollowState enum (the C5/D3/D15 decoupling shape).
types/contactPoints.ts34Contact-surface vocabulary (which side touched: top/bottom/left/right + points).
types/gameObjectType.ts14Collider-shape enum: CUBE/SPHERE/CAPSULE only (D5 lean; slopes are rotated cubes).
types/inputState.ts18The plain per-frame input-intent bag the device fills and entities read.
types/inputSource.ts10The InputSource interface (one method, CaptureInto) — Player depends on this, not the concrete keyboard Input, so touch/gamepad/replay sources drop in.
types/levelData.ts35The parsed-level record shape the GLB parser produces and the factories consume.
types/playerDirection.ts19LEFT/RIGHT facing enum.
types/playerStates.ts24The player FSM state enum: IDLE/RUNNING/JUMPING/FALLING/CLIMBING.
types/userData.ts10The collider userData shape: an entity-type tag + a back-reference to the owning object.

Game layer — src/ & src/game/ (Donkey-Kong rules, 38 files)

FileLOCPurpose
main.ts5Vue entry point: createApp(App).mount("#app").
App.vue169The Vue UI shell: boot wiring, the loading overlay, and the status/reset-button presentation.
game/gameDirector.ts546The conductor: owns entity arrays, the level pipeline, the spawner, and the game-state event handlers.
game/gameUtils.ts108Static helpers: RemoveDestroyedObjects and the sensor-triggered query predicates.
game/config/contactRules.ts68Registers the concrete contact reactions (Enemy→Player = gameOver, barrel→can, winFlag…) into the engine registry.
game/config/debugColors.ts22The EntityType → wireframe color palette injected into engine Debug (D3).
game/config/entityFactories.ts219The EntityType → factory map that builds each entity from a LevelData row.
game/config/inputBindings.ts45The F-key bindings → game events (reset, switchLevel, manual camera).
game/config/levelRegistry.ts82LEVEL_REGISTRY rows + FEEL_PROFILES + DEFAULT_LEVEL_INDEX.
game/config/assetManifest.ts21The game's boot asset manifest (randy/enemy/dkGraphicsData URLs), handed to Experience.Configure so the engine names no game asset.
game/config/levelNodeTypes.ts27Level-node name token → game type, injected into the engine GLB parser via Resources.RegisterLevelTypes (replaced the old hand-synced EntityType mirror).
game/attributes/baseFeel.ts39The default movement "feel" attribute set (speeds, accel, jump, gravity, coyote, buffer…).
game/attributes/setCelesteAttributes.ts47Celeste-style feel overrides applied over the base.
game/attributes/setDkAttributes.ts49Donkey-Kong-style feel overrides applied over the base.
game/entities/player/player.ts491The player entity: body, FSM wiring, per-frame input snapshot, ladder state, teleport.
game/entities/player/applyHorizontalMovement.ts47The shared accel/decel/clamp horizontal-move helper the ground/air states call.
game/entities/player/spriteAnimations.ts40The per-state sprite-sheet animation config (which frames each state plays).
…/player/states/handlePlayerIdle.ts104Idle FSM state body + its transitions out.
…/player/states/handlePlayerRunning.ts126Running FSM state body + transitions.
…/player/states/handlePlayerJumping.ts93Jumping FSM state body (jump impulse, buffer) + transitions.
…/player/states/handlePlayerFalling.ts124Falling FSM state body (coyote time, landing) + transitions.
…/player/states/handlePlayerClimbing.ts68Climbing FSM state body (ladder snap, vertical move) + transitions.
game/entities/enemy/enemy.ts331The barrel enemy — normal & crazy folded into one class, chosen at spawn (D28).
game/entities/enemy/enemyStates.ts17The barrel FSM state enum (rolling/descending/bouncing).
…/enemy/states/handleEnemyRolling.ts31Rolling-along-girders state body.
…/enemy/states/handleEnemyDescending.ts21Descending-a-ladder state body.
…/enemy/states/handleEnemyBouncing.ts10The crazy-barrel vertical-bounce state body.
game/entities/platform.ts90Solid / one-way platforms + walls; the per-frame one-way enablePoint toggle.
game/entities/cameraSensor.ts57A camera trigger zone that moves the camera toward a target on entry.
game/entities/ladderSensor.ts44Ladder top/core/bottom sensor with a climb direction (D9 fully-inside).
game/entities/teleporter.ts48A teleport zone carrying a destination position.
game/entities/trashCan.ts31The oil can: holds the IsOnFire state a barrel ignites via the contact table.
game/entities/winFlag.ts31The win trigger — fires gameWin through the contact table (no per-frame shapecast).
game/debug/collisionLogger.ts74The game-owned collision-log sink registered into engine Debug.
game/debug/levelDebug.ts30The dat.gui level dropdown bound to GameDirector.CurrentLevelName.
game/debug/playerDebug.ts396The dat.gui live player inspector (every feel value + live readouts, .listen() reflected).
game/types/entityType.ts29The EntityType enum, mapped from GLB folder names by the parser.
game/types/gameCollisionGroups.ts30The Rapier membership/filter bitmask groups deciding which colliders may touch.

Modifying this: when you add a file, place it on the correct side of the seam. If it would force an import … from "../game/…" inside src/engine/, stop — invert it instead (inject the game concept as data or a callback, the way contact rules and the debug palette already do).

The event catalog 14 signals

Every event that crosses the mitt bus, who fires it, who hears it, and its payload. The map is declared in eventBus.ts as two halves — EngineEventMap (must never name a game concept) and GameEventMap — combined as EngineEventMap & GameEventMap. Listeners are global, so naming discipline is the architecture here.

EventPayloadEmitted byListened by
tickvoidTime every RAF frame time.ts:33Experience.onTick → physics/game/render experience.ts:98
resizevoidSizes on window resize sizes.ts:23Experience.onResize → Camera + Renderer experience.ts:82
resourcesReadyvoidResourceLoader when boot assets finish resourceLoader.ts:118/140GameDirector → loads default level gameDirector.ts:100; loader self-offs resourceLoader.ts:524
resourceLoadFailedstring (resource name)ResourceLoader on a failed asset resourceLoader.ts:129reserved — no listener yet
loadingProgress{ loaded, total, item }ResourceLoader per item resourceLoader.ts:81/110/132App.vue → progress bar App.vue:40
loadingStartedvoidGameDirector around level loads gameDirector.ts:389App.vue → show overlay App.vue:31
loadingFinishedvoidGameDirector (in finally) gameDirector.ts:444App.vue → hide overlay App.vue:37
gameStartvoidGameDirector on a successful load gameDirector.ts:435GameDirector (unpause + spawn) :106 & App.vue (clear UI) :102
gameOvervoidcontactRules — Enemy→Player contactRules.ts:28GameDirector (pause + stop spawn) :112 & App.vue ("Game Over!") :84
gameWinvoidcontactRules — winFlag contactRules.ts:66GameDirector (freeze) :119 & App.vue ("Game Win!") :76
gameResetvoidApp.vue reset button :118 & inputBindings (F-key) :29GameDirector (resetGameState) :125 & App.vue (clear UI) :92
switchLevelvoidinputBindings (F2) inputBindings.ts:35GameDirector → switchToNextLevel gameDirector.ts:131
manualCameraControlvoidinputBindings inputBindings.ts:41Camera → toggle manual control camera.ts:95
gameObjectRemovedGameObjectcontactRules (consumed barrel) :38, GameDirector (unload/reset) :281/356, Player :480GameDirector → entity.Destroy() gameDirector.ts:136
Hard-won victory — listen before you emit. Both App.vue and GameDirector register their listeners before Resources.StartLoading() is called last. A fast boot could otherwise fire resourcesReady/loadingFinished before anyone was subscribed, leaving the loading overlay stuck or the game un-booted. Ordering is the whole trick.
Why a bus at all. Because listeners are resolved by string, an emitter never holds a reference to its listener — that is exactly what lets the engine emit tick without importing Experience, and lets contactRules declare gameOver without the physics world knowing what "game over" means.

Modifying this: add new events to the correct half of the map in eventBus.ts (engine-pure vs game). Keep payloads plain data. Anything you Emitter.on inside a long-lived object must be paired with an Emitter.off in its Destroy (see Experience, Camera, GameDirector) or you leak listeners across level loads.

The decision-log decoder the Rosetta Stone

The comments carry terse tags like (C5 decouple) or (D3) that point at the author's private decision log. Each marks a deliberate, hard-won choice. Here is every code that appears in the source, every place it shows up, and the principle it encodes — so the comments stop being cryptic.

CodePrincipleAppears at
C5 Camera decouple. The engine Camera consumes a plain CameraTarget data snapshot each frame and reads only scalars from it — it never imports the game Player or PlayerStates. The game maps its Player → CameraTarget (PlayerState → CameraFollowState) in cameraTargetFromPlayer. A different game, or a multiplayer follow, just fills the snapshot differently. cameraTarget.ts:1, gameDirector.ts:160,189, cameraTarget.ts:18
D3 The engine never names a game type. Where the engine needs game knowledge it takes it as injected data: PhysicsDebug colors colliders from a game-supplied EntityType → color map; CameraTarget is plain data, not a Player; the enemy FSM lives game-side. Dependency inversion across the whole seam (also tracked as ADR-0003). App.vue:58, physicsDebug.ts:29, cameraTarget.ts:2, enemyStates.ts:1
D5 Stay lean — prefer the boring primitive. Two faces: (a) collider shapes are CUBE/SPHERE/CAPSULE only; slopes are built from rotated/overlapped cubes and the polyline/convex paths were purged; (b) re-entrancy is guarded with a single old-school boolean (isLoading) rather than a queue/lock library. gameObjectType.ts:1, gameDirector.ts:382
D9 The "fully inside" ladder climb. Grabbing/climbing a ladder requires the climber to be fully inside the ladder-core sensor, which depends on continuous intersection polling (not a one-shot enter event). Both the player and the barrel descend logic rely on it; it's why GameSensor exposes live intersection state. ladderSensor.ts:8, gameSensor.ts:7, enemy.ts:265
D10 Per-entity input snapshot. Each entity holds its own InputState snapshot, refreshed from the device once per frame, rather than every state handler reaching into the global input device. Decouples consumers from the keyboard and makes states trivially testable / AI-swappable. player.ts:138
D15 Data-shape interfaces use camelCase fields. Plain "bag of scalars" interfaces that cross the seam (like CameraTarget) are written as camelCase data shapes, distinguishing them from class-style APIs. cameraTarget.ts:18
D28 Data-driven enemy — one class, two flavors. The old separate CrazyEnemy class was folded into Enemy. Normal vs crazy barrels are now the same class, distinguished only by their starting FSM state and a bounceDirection drift chosen at spawn. This collapse is what let the contact table cleanly fix the barrel/enemy collision bug. gameDirector.ts:49, enemy.ts:44
ADR-0003 Game-side state machines. Architecture Decision Record backing D3: entity FSMs (player, enemy) live in the game layer and are built on the engine's generic StateMachine primitive — the engine supplies the mechanism, the game supplies the states. enemyStates.ts:1
Reading the tags in context. When you hit a (C5) or (D3) in a comment, it's the author flagging "this shape is deliberate, don't 'simplify' it back into a coupling." The cleanest demonstration of the whole philosophy is the contact table + camera pair: physics fires a generic event, the game decides what it means, and neither side imports the other.

Modifying this: if you introduce a new deliberate constraint, give it the next code and a one-line note at the point of use — future-you (and this table) will thank you. If you're about to violate one of these (e.g. importing a game type into the engine), that's the signal to inject data instead.
